topics.title: Basic Block Diagram of a Computer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Block Diagram of a Computer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Basic Block Diagram of a Computer:** A generalized functional representation of any stored-program digital computer, consisting of three primary functional units — the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, the **Memory Unit**, and the **Input/Output (I/O) Unit** — interconnected through a common set of signal pathways called the **System Bus**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme course *PBRUT404 – Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems*, this block diagram is the **architectural ancestor** of every modern microcontroller. The desktop PC on your desk and the tiny ARM Cortex-M0 inside an IoT sensor node are both *direct descendants* of this same generalized model.

The three primary blocks are:

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit)** — Often called the "brain." Internally subdivided into the *Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)*, the *Control Unit (CU)*, and *Registers*.
2. **Memory Unit** — Stores both *instructions* (program code) and *data* (operands and results). Categorized into Primary (RAM, ROM) and Secondary (HDD, SSD) memory.
3. **I/O Unit** — The interface between the computer and the external world (keyboard, display, sensors, actuators).

These three units communicate via the **System Bus**, which itself contains three sub-buses: the **Data Bus**, the **Address Bus**, and the **Control Bus**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Office" Model

Imagine a fully automated **smart office building**:

| Computer Block | Smart Office Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU** | The Office Manager (sitting at the desk, making decisions) | Executes instructions, makes calculations |
| **Memory** | The Filing Cabinets & Notepads (on the manager's desk and in storage) | Stores documents, plans, and records |
| **I/O Devices** | The Reception Desk, Phones, Printers, and Mailroom | Brings in new tasks (input) and delivers results (output) |
| **System Bus** | The Intercom + Pneumatic Tube Network + Internal Telephone System | Carries instructions, addresses, and control signals |

When a new project arrives at the reception (I/O), the manager (CPU) consults the filing cabinet (memory), processes the paperwork (ALU), and routes the result back out (output). The **bus** is the office's internal communication infrastructure that makes this all possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Embedded Systems:** In a *microcontroller*, the CPU, Memory (Flash + SRAM), and I/O peripherals are all integrated onto a **single silicon chip**. This is the System-on-Chip (SoC) concept. Understanding the block diagram helps you later understand *why* microcontrollers have specific bus architectures (e.g., ARM's AHB/APB bus matrix).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Central Processing Unit (CPU) — Internal Structure

The CPU is the *active* element of the computer. It is responsible for **fetching**, **decoding**, and **executing** instructions. Internally, it consists of three subunits:

#### 2.1.1 Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)
- Performs all **arithmetic operations** (`+`, `-`, `×`, `÷`).
- Performs all **logical operations** (`AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT`).
- Performs **shift** and **rotate** operations on binary data.
- Sets the **Flag Register** (also called *Program Status Word* or *Condition Code Register*) based on the result — flags like *Zero (Z)*, *Carry (C)*, *Sign (N)*, and *Overflow (V)*.

#### 2.1.2 Control Unit (CU)
- The "traffic controller" of the CPU.
- Fetches instructions from memory.
- **Decodes** the opcode to determine the required operation.
- Generates the precise sequence of **control signals** (timing and synchronization pulses) that orchestrate the ALU, registers, and bus transfers.
- Does *not* process data itself — it *directs* data movement and processing.

#### 2.1.3 Registers (The CPU's Scratchpad)
- **Program Counter (PC):** Holds the memory address of the *next* instruction to be fetched. After every fetch, it is incremented (or loaded with a new address for branches).
- **Instruction Register (IR):** Holds the *currently executing* instruction.
- **Memory Address Register (MAR):** Holds the memory address to be accessed (read or write).
- **Memory Data Register (MDR)** (also called *Memory Buffer Register, MBR*): Temporarily holds the data being transferred to/from memory.
- **Accumulator (ACC):** The primary working register for ALU operations.
- **Stack Pointer (SP):** Points to the top of the stack in memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "draw and explain the CPU," always draw the ALU, CU, and Registers as three distinct sub-blocks *inside* the CPU block, and clearly show the **internal CPU bus** connecting them.

### 2.2 The Memory Unit

Memory is the *passive* storage element. It is classified along two dimensions:

**By Function:**
- **Primary Memory:** Directly accessible by the CPU. Fast but limited in size. Examples: *RAM* (volatile, read/write) and *ROM* (non-volatile, read-only).
- **Secondary Memory:** Used for bulk, long-term storage. Slow but high capacity. Examples: HDD, SSD, SD card.

**By Access Pattern:**
- *Random Access Memory (RAM):* Any location can be accessed in equal time.
- *Sequential Access Memory (SAM):* Data is accessed in a linear sequence (e.g., magnetic tape).
- *Direct Access Memory:* A hybrid (e.g., HDD with movable read head).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Microcontrollers (KTU Emphasis):** In an embedded system, the *program* typically resides in **Flash ROM** (non-volatile), while *runtime data* lives in **SRAM** (volatile). This dual-memory model is a direct application of the basic block diagram.

### 2.3 The Input/Output (I/O) Unit

I/O devices enable communication between the computer and the outside world.

- **Input Devices:** Keyboard, mouse, scanner, sensors (temperature, accelerometer, etc.).
- **Output Devices:** Monitor, printer, LEDs, motors, speakers.
- **I/O Controllers / Interface Circuits:** Bridge the speed and signal mismatch between the slow I/O devices and the fast CPU. Examples: UART, SPI, I²C controllers, GPIO ports.

### 2.4 The System Bus — The Communication Backbone

A *bus* is a shared communication pathway consisting of multiple wires. The system bus has three components:

| Sub-Bus | Direction | Width (typical) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Bus** | Bidirectional | 8, 16, 32, or 64 bits | Carries the *actual data* (operand values, instruction opcodes, memory contents) being transferred. |
| **Address Bus** | Unidirectional (CPU → Memory/I/O) | 16, 20, 24, 32 bits | Carries the *memory or I/O address* of the location to be accessed. |
| **Control Bus** | Bidirectional | 10–20+ lines | Carries *timing and control signals* (Read/Write, Interrupt Request, Clock, Reset, Bus Request/Grant). |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Addressability — The Most Tested Formula in KTU Exams**

The fundamental relationship between **address bus width** and **addressable memory** is:

$$
N_{\text{locations}} = 2^{n}
$$

Where:
- $N_{\text{locations}}$ is the number of uniquely addressable memory locations.
- $n$ is the number of bits in the address bus.

The corresponding **maximum memory capacity** (in bytes, assuming byte-addressable memory) is:

$$
C_{\text{max}} = 2^{n} \times w \;\; \text{(bits)}
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
C_{\text{max}} \;\; (\text{bytes}) = 2^{n} \quad \text{(if } w = 8 \text{ bits, i.e., byte-addressable)}
$$

The **maximum data transfer per memory access** is bounded by the data bus width:

$$
D_{\text{max}} = 2^{d} - 1
$$

Where $d$ is the number of bits in the data bus (giving the range of distinct values).

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of addressable locations | $N$ | $2^{n}$ | locations |
| Max memory capacity (byte-addressable) | $C$ | $2^{n}$ | bytes |
| Max memory capacity (bit) | $C$ | $2^{n} \times 8$ | bits |
| Address range | $0$ to $2^{n}-1$ | — | decimal integer |
| Data per transfer | $D$ | $d$ bits | bits/bytes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Mnemonic:** *"Address bus says HOW MANY, data bus says HOW BIG."* — The address bus width ($n$) determines *how many* memory locations exist; the data bus width ($d$) determines *how big* each transfer is.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the block diagram is **not academic nostalgia** — it is the foundation of:
- **Microcontroller Selection:** Choosing an MCU with the right address bus width (e.g., 16-bit vs 32-bit) for your application's memory needs.
- **Performance Analysis:** A *wider* data bus means higher throughput per cycle. A *wider* address bus means more memory can be attached.
- **Bus Arbitration:** In multi-master systems (e.g., DMA + CPU both wanting the bus), the control bus signals (Bus Request, Bus Grant) prevent data corruption.
- **Embedded Memory Mapping:** When you write `*(uint32_t*)0x40021000 = 1;` in an STM32 program, you are explicitly using the address bus to select a peripheral register.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle (Instruction Execution)

The CPU operates in a continuous loop called the **Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle**. This is the most important process in any computer — and therefore, a frequent KTU Part A question. Below is the *exhaustive* step-by-step execution for a single instruction, say `ADD A, B` (add contents of memory location B to the accumulator).

**Assumptions:**
- Program Counter (PC) initially holds address $A_{i}$ of the next instruction.
- The instruction is stored at memory location $A_{i}$.
- The operand is at memory location $A_{i+1}$.

---

#### **STEP 1 — Fetch the Opcode (the operation code part of the instruction)**

$$
\text{Step 1a:} \quad \text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC}
$$
*Logic: The CPU first copies the address of the next instruction (held in PC) into the Memory Address Register. This address is then placed on the **address bus**.*

$$
\text{Step 1b:} \quad \text{Address Bus} \leftarrow \text{MAR}, \quad \text{Control Bus} \leftarrow \text{READ signal}
$$
*Logic: The address bus carries the address $A_{i}$ to the memory, and the control bus carries the READ signal to instruct memory to perform a read operation.*

$$
\text{Step 1c:} \quad \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1
$$
*Logic: Simultaneously, the Program Counter is incremented so it now points to the next sequential memory location, ready for the next fetch.*

$$
\text{Step 1d:} \quad \text{MDR} \leftarrow \text{Memory}[A_{i}]
$$
*Logic: The memory places the contents of location $A_{i}$ (the opcode) onto the data bus, and the CPU latches this value into the Memory Data Register.*

$$
\text{Step 1e:} \quad \text{IR} \leftarrow \text{MDR}
$$
*Logic: The fetched opcode is transferred from the MDR into the Instruction Register, where it will be decoded in the next phase.*

---

#### **STEP 2 — Decode the Opcode**

$$
\text{Step 2a:} \quad \text{CU} \leftarrow \text{IR}_{\text{opcode}}
$$
*Logic: The Control Unit examines the opcode bits held in the IR. Using hardwired logic or a microprogram, it determines which operation is required (e.g., ADD) and which internal control signals to activate.*

---

#### **STEP 3 — Fetch the Operand (if required)**

$$
\text{Step 3a:} \quad \text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC} \quad (= A_{i+1})
$$
*Logic: For an instruction that requires an operand from memory, the next address (already pre-incremented PC) is placed in the MAR.*

$$
\text{Step 3b:} \quad \text{Address Bus} \leftarrow \text{MAR}, \quad \text{Control Bus} \leftarrow \text{READ}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3c:} \quad \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1
$$

$$
\text{Step 3d:} \quad \text{MDR} \leftarrow \text{Memory}[A_{i+1}]
$$

$$
\text{Step 3e:} \quad \text{Operand Register} \leftarrow \text{MDR}
$$
*Logic: The operand value is moved into a temporary register accessible to the ALU.*

---

#### **STEP 4 — Execute the Operation**

$$
\text{Step 4a:} \quad \text{ALU} \leftarrow \text{ACC}, \quad \text{ALU} \leftarrow \text{Operand Register}
$$
*Logic: The ALU is given both inputs — the current accumulator value and the fetched operand.*

$$
\text{Step 4b:} \quad \text{ACC} \leftarrow \text{ALU}(\text{ACC} + \text{Operand Register})
$$
*Logic: The ALU performs the ADD operation, and the result is written back into the Accumulator, completing the execution.*

$$
\text{Step 4c:} \quad \text{Flag Register} \leftarrow \text{Status bits from ALU}
$$
*Logic: The ALU reports condition flags (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow) which the control unit latches into the Flag/Status Register for use by subsequent conditional branch instructions.*

---

#### **STEP 5 — Return to STEP 1**

The CPU now loops back to Step 1 to fetch the *next* instruction, and the cycle repeats indefinitely until a HALT or RESET is encountered.

> [!NOTE]
> **In Modern Microcontrollers:** This cycle is implemented in *pipelined* form (e.g., ARM Cortex-M3 uses a 3-stage pipeline: Fetch → Decode → Execute), allowing multiple instructions to be in different stages simultaneously — but the underlying logic is identical.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Memory Capacity vs Address Bus Width

| Address Bus Width ($n$) | Locations ($2^{n}$) | Max Capacity (byte-addressable) | Decimal Equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 bits | $2^{8}$ | 256 bytes | 256 B |
| 10 bits | $2^{10}$ | 1,024 bytes | 1 KB |
| 12 bits | $2^{12}$ | 4,096 bytes | 4 KB |
| 16 bits | $2^{16}$ | 65,536 bytes | 64 KB |
| 20 bits | $2^{20}$ | 1,048,576 bytes | 1 MB |
| 24 bits | $2^{24}$ | 16,777,216 bytes | 16 MB |
| 32 bits | $2^{32}$ | 4,294,967,296 bytes | 4 GB |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** Memorize the *powers of 2* from $2^{10}$ to $2^{32}$. These appear in nearly every memory-related exam question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A computer has a 16-bit address bus and an 8-bit data bus. Find (i) the number of addressable memory locations, (ii) the maximum directly addressable memory capacity, and (iii) the address range.

**Solution:**

**(i) Number of addressable locations:**

$$
N = 2^{n} = 2^{16}
$$

Computing $2^{16}$ step by step:

$$
2^{16} = 2^{10} \times 2^{6} = 1024 \times 64 = 65{,}536 \text{ locations}
$$

**(ii) Maximum memory capacity (byte-addressable, $w = 8$):**

$$
C = 2^{n} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

**(iii) Address range:**

$$
\text{From } 0 \text{ to } (2^{16} - 1) = 65{,}535
$$

Written in hexadecimal for completeness: `0x0000` to `0xFFFF`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (KTU often asks this):** The **Intel 8086** processor had a 20-bit address bus, giving it exactly $2^{20} = 1\text{ MB}$ of addressable memory — which is why old DOS systems could not use more than 1 MB without special memory models.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram of a Computer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU["CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT CPU"]
        ALU["ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT ALU"]
        CU["CONTROL UNIT CU"]
        REG["REGISTERS PC IR MAR MDR ACC SP"]
        IBUS["INTERNAL CPU BUS"]
        ALU --- IBUS
        CU --- IBUS
        REG --- IBUS
    end

    MEM["MEMORY UNIT RAM ROM CACHE"]
    IO["INPUT OUTPUT UNIT KEYBOARD DISPLAY SENSORS ACTUATORS"]

    DBUS["DATA BUS BIDIRECTIONAL"]
    ABUS["ADDRESS BUS CPU TO MEMORY IO"]
    CBUS["CONTROL BUS READ WRITE INT CLK RESET"]

    CPU -- DBUS --- DBUS
    CPU -- ABUS --- ABUS
    CPU -- CBUS --- CBUS

    DBUS --- MEM
    ABUS --- MEM
    CBUS --- MEM

    DBUS --- IO
    ABUS --- IO
    CBUS --- IO
```

### 4.2 System Bus Architecture — Detailed Sub-Bus View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU_BLOCK["CPU SUBSYSTEM"]
        direction TB
        CU1["CONTROL UNIT"]
        ALU1["ALU"]
        REG1["REGISTER FILE PC IR ACC MAR MDR"]
    end

    subgraph BUS_GROUP["SYSTEM BUS"]
        direction TB
        DLINE["DATA BUS 8 16 32 64 BITS WIDE"]
        ALINE["ADDRESS BUS UNIDIRECTIONAL n BITS"]
        CLINE["CONTROL BUS READ WRITE INT CLOCK RESET"]
    end

    subgraph MEM_BLOCK["MEMORY SUBSYSTEM"]
        direction TB
        ROM1["ROM NON VOLATILE PROGRAM"]
        RAM1["RAM VOLATILE DATA"]
    end

    subgraph IO_BLOCK["I O SUBSYSTEM"]
        direction TB
        INP["INPUT DEVICES KEYBOARD SENSOR"]
        OUTP["OUTPUT DEVICES DISPLAY LED MOTOR"]
        IOC["I O CONTROLLER UART SPI I2C GPIO"]
    end

    CPU_BLOCK --> DLINE
    CPU_BLOCK --> ALINE
    CPU_BLOCK --> CLINE
    DLINE --> MEM_BLOCK
    ALINE --> MEM_BLOCK
    CLINE --> MEM_BLOCK
    DLINE --> IO_BLOCK
    ALINE --> IO_BLOCK
    CLINE --> IO_BLOCK
    IO_BLOCK --> IOC
```

### 4.3 Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START(["CPU POWER ON"]) --> F1["STEP 1 MAR EQUALS PC"]
    F1 --> F2["STEP 2 PLACE PC ON ADDRESS BUS"]
    F2 --> F3["STEP 3 ISSUE READ SIGNAL ON CONTROL BUS"]
    F3 --> F4["STEP 4 MEMORY PUTS DATA ON DATA BUS"]
    F4 --> F5["STEP 5 MDR LATCHES DATA FROM DATA BUS"]
    F5 --> F6["STEP 6 IR EQUALS MDR OPCODE LATCHED"]
    F6 --> F7["STEP 7 PC INCREMENTED PC EQUALS PC PLUS 1"]
    F7 --> D1["STEP 8 CU DECODES OPCODE IN IR"]
    D1 --> D2{"STEP 9 OPERAND NEEDED"}
    D2 -- YES --> E1["STEP 10 FETCH OPERAND FROM MEMORY REPEAT STEPS 1 TO 6"]
    D2 -- NO --> E2["STEP 11 SKIP TO EXECUTE"]
    E1 --> E3["STEP 12 ALU PERFORMS OPERATION"]
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> E4["STEP 13 WRITE RESULT TO ACC OR DESTINATION"]
    E4 --> E5["STEP 14 UPDATE FLAG REGISTER"]
    E5 --> F1
```

### 4.4 Microcontroller Mapping — From Block Diagram to Silicon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEN["GENERIC COMPUTER BLOCK DIAGRAM"]
        GCPU["CPU"]
        GMEM["MEMORY"]
        GIO["I O"]
        GBUS["SYSTEM BUS"]
    end

    subgraph MCU["MICROCONTROLLER SINGLE CHIP SoC"]
        MCPU["ARM CORTEX Mx CORE"]
        MFLASH["FLASH ROM PROGRAM MEMORY"]
        MSRAM["SRAM DATA MEMORY"]
        MPER["PERIPHERALS GPIO UART ADC TIMER"]
        MINT["INTERNAL BUS MATRIX AHB APB"]
    end

    GCPU -. "INTEGRATED INTO" .-> MCPU
    GMEM -. "INTEGRATED INTO" .-> MFLASH
    GMEM -. "INTEGRATED INTO" .-> MSRAM
    GIO -. "INTEGRATED INTO" .-> MPER
    GBUS -. "INTEGRATED INTO" .-> MINT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Notice how the generic computer block diagram and the modern microcontroller are *topologically identical* — only the *physical packaging* differs. The CPU, memory, I/O, and bus still exist; they are just etched onto a single die.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a computer. Draw the basic block diagram of a computer and explain the function of each block.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A computer is an electronic data processing device that accepts input, processes it under the control of a stored program, produces output, and stores the results.

> **Block Diagram (1 mark):** *[Draw a clean block diagram with CPU, Memory, I/O, and System Bus with its three sub-buses — as shown in Section 4.1]*

> **Functions (1 mark):**
> - **CPU:** The brain that executes instructions; contains ALU (computations) and CU (control signals).
> - **Memory:** Stores program instructions and data; provides fast access for the CPU.
> - **I/O Unit:** Provides the interface for entering data and receiving results.
> - **System Bus:** The communication pathway comprising data, address, and control buses.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the three types of system buses with their functions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **System Bus** is the shared communication highway connecting the CPU, memory, and I/O. It consists of:
>
> 1. **Data Bus (1 mark):** Carries the actual data — operands, opcodes, and results. It is **bidirectional** (data flows both ways between CPU and memory/I/O). Its width (e.g., 8, 16, 32 bits) determines how much data is transferred per cycle.
>
> 2. **Address Bus (1 mark):** Carries the memory or I/O address specifying *where* data is to be read from or written to. It is **unidirectional** (only the CPU drives it). Its width $n$ determines the addressable memory size as $2^n$.
>
> 3. **Control Bus (1 mark):** Carries timing and command signals such as **Read/Write**, **Interrupt Request**, **Clock**, **Reset**, and **Bus Request/Grant**. It coordinates and synchronizes all bus activities.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q.A. (a)** Draw the basic block diagram of a computer and explain the functions of the CPU, memory, and I/O units. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the three types of system buses. A computer has a 24-bit address bus and a 16-bit data bus. Calculate (i) the number of addressable memory locations, (ii) the maximum memory capacity in bytes, and (iii) the address range. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**[Block Diagram — 3 marks]**
Draw a clear block diagram with three primary units (CPU, Memory, I/O) connected via a System Bus. Inside the CPU, show ALU, CU, and Registers as sub-blocks. Inside Memory, distinguish ROM and RAM. Label the bus as having Data, Address, and Control lines.

**[CPU Explanation — 1.5 marks]**
The CPU is the central unit that interprets and executes instructions. The **ALU** performs arithmetic (`+`, `-`, etc.) and logical (`AND`, `OR`, etc.) operations. The **CU** fetches instructions, decodes them, and generates control signals. **Registers** (PC, IR, MAR, MDR, ACC) provide high-speed internal storage for the CPU's working data.

**[Memory Explanation — 1.5 marks]**
Memory stores both instructions and data. **ROM** is non-volatile and holds the permanent program (boot code, firmware). **RAM** is volatile and holds temporary runtime data. Memory is organized as addressable locations, each with a unique address.

**[I/O Explanation — 1 mark]**
The I/O unit enables interaction with the external world. **Input devices** (keyboard, sensors) bring data in; **output devices** (display, actuators) deliver results. I/O controllers adapt signal levels and speeds to the CPU.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**[Bus Diagram — 2 marks]**
Show the three buses: Data Bus (16-bit, bidirectional), Address Bus (24-bit, unidirectional from CPU), and Control Bus (with Read, Write, Clock, Reset signals).

**[Bus Explanation — 1.5 marks]**
*(See Part A Q2 model answer above for the bus functions — write them out in 2-3 sentences each.)*

**[Calculation (i) — 1.5 marks]**
$$
n = 24 \text{ bits (address bus width)}
$$
$$
N = 2^{n} = 2^{24} = 2^{4} \times 2^{20} = 16 \times 1{,}048{,}576 = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ locations}
$$

**[Calculation (ii) — 1 mark]**
$$
C = 2^{n} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ bytes} = 16 \text{ MB}
$$

**[Calculation (iii) — 1 mark]**
$$
\text{Address range: } 0 \text{ to } (2^{24} - 1) = 16{,}777{,}215
$$
In hexadecimal: `0x000000` to `0xFFFFFF`.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the internal architecture of the CPU with a neat block diagram. Describe the functions of the ALU, Control Unit, and the major registers (PC, IR, MAR, MDR, ACC). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle step by step. Explain what happens during each phase, mentioning the role of the buses. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**[CPU Internal Block Diagram — 2 marks]**
Draw a CPU block subdivided into three sub-blocks: **ALU**, **Control Unit (CU)**, and **Register File**, all connected by an **Internal CPU Bus**. Show external connections: Data Bus, Address Bus, Control Bus to the rest of the system.

**[ALU Function — 1 mark]**
The ALU performs all arithmetic (`+`, `-`, `×`, `÷`), logical (`AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT`, shift, rotate), and comparison operations. It outputs result to a destination register and updates status flags (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow).

**[Control Unit Function — 1.5 marks]**
The CU orchestrates the entire system. It fetches instructions from memory, decodes the opcode, and generates the precise sequence of control signals that gate data movement between registers, ALU, and external buses. It is implemented using either *hardwired control* (combinational logic) or *microprogrammed control* (a microcode ROM).

**[Register Functions — 2.5 marks]**
- **PC (Program Counter):** Holds the address of the next instruction to fetch; auto-increments after each fetch.
- **IR (Instruction Register):** Holds the currently fetched instruction opcode while it is being decoded and executed.
- **MAR (Memory Address Register):** Holds the address of the memory location being accessed.
- **MDR (Memory Data Register):** Temporarily buffers data being transferred to/from memory.
- **ACC (Accumulator):** The primary register used for ALU operations; holds one operand and receives the result.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Phase 1: Fetch (3 marks)**
- Step 1: The contents of the PC are transferred to the MAR.
- Step 2: The address in MAR is placed on the **address bus**.
- Step 3: The Control Unit issues a **READ** signal on the **control bus**.
- Step 4: Memory reads the data at that address and places it on the **data bus**.
- Step 5: The CPU latches the data from the data bus into the MDR.
- Step 6: The MDR contents are transferred to the IR.
- Step 7: The PC is incremented (PC ← PC + 1) to point to the next instruction.

**Phase 2: Decode (1.5 marks)**
- The Control Unit examines the opcode in the IR.
- Using either hardwired logic or a microprogram, it determines which operation is required and which control signals to assert.
- If the instruction requires an operand, the CU initiates an operand fetch cycle.

**Phase 3: Execute (2.5 marks)**
- For an arithmetic instruction (e.g., ADD): the operand is fetched (similar sub-cycle to Fetch), the ALU is enabled with the correct inputs, the operation is performed, and the result is written back to the destination register (typically the ACC).
- The ALU updates the **Flag Register** with status bits (Z, C, N, V).
- The CPU returns to Phase 1 to fetch the next instruction, and the cycle repeats continuously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Subtlety:** Always mention that the **buses are shared resources** — only one device can drive the bus at a time, and the **Control Bus** is what arbitrates this. This is a common KTU follow-up question.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Block Diagram Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the three sub-buses:** Many students draw the System Bus as a single thick arrow. The KTU examiner expects **three separate labeled buses** (Data, Address, Control). Loss: **1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Not labeling bus directions:** The Data Bus is *bidirectional*; the Address Bus is *unidirectional* (CPU → outside). Writing this explicitly earns the full bus mark.
>
> 3. **Confusing PC and MAR:** The PC holds the *next* instruction's address (software concept). The MAR holds the *current* address being sent on the address bus (hardware concept). They are often the *same value* at fetch time, but conceptually distinct. Examiners *will* check this.
>
> 4. **Unit mistakes in calculations:** A 16-bit address bus does **not** mean 16 KB of memory. It means $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes = **64 KB**. Sloppy unit writing costs **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Missing the Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle entirely:** This is *the* core process. If your answer omits it, expect to lose **3–4 marks** out of 7 in any CPU question.
>
> 6. **Drawing registers as a single block:** The KTU syllabus specifically names PC, IR, MAR, MDR, and ACC. A generic "Registers" box will be marked down.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ☐ A computer has **3 primary blocks**: CPU, Memory, I/O — connected by the **System Bus**.
- ☐ The **CPU = ALU + Control Unit + Registers**, all linked by the **Internal CPU Bus**.
- ☐ The **ALU** does math + logic; the **CU** does NOT process data — it only *directs* data flow.
- ☐ The **System Bus** has three sub-buses:
  - **Data Bus** — bidirectional, carries *what*.
  - **Address Bus** — unidirectional (CPU → outside), carries *where*.
  - **Control Bus** — carries *when* and *how* (Read, Write, Interrupt, Clock, Reset).
- ☐ **Address bus width $n$ → $2^n$ addressable locations** (byte-addressable = $2^n$ bytes).
- ☐ **Data bus width $d$** → bits transferred per memory access.
- ☐ Key registers to remember by name: **PC, IR, MAR, MDR, ACC, SP**.
- ☐ Every instruction goes through **Fetch → Decode → Execute**, then repeats.
- ☐ In the **Fetch** phase, the PC is *incremented* — do not forget this step.
- ☐ Memory types: **RAM** (volatile, read/write) vs **ROM** (non-volatile, read-only).
- ☐ **Microcontrollers** are the on-chip *integration* of the same three blocks (CPU + Flash + SRAM + Peripherals) on a single silicon die.
- ☐ Common KTU values to memorize: $2^{10}=1\text{K}$, $2^{16}=64\text{K}$, $2^{20}=1\text{M}$, $2^{24}=16\text{M}$, $2^{32}=4\text{G}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Bus and Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** System Bus and Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a System Bus?

In the context of **microcontroller and embedded system design**, a **System Bus** is defined as a *shared communication pathway* — a set of parallel conductors, signal lines, and associated control logic — that interconnects the major functional blocks of a computer or microcontroller. These functional blocks are the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, the **Memory subsystem (RAM, ROM, Flash)**, and the **Input/Output (I/O) peripherals**.

A system bus is not a single wire; it is a *bundle of distinct, functionally classified sub-buses* that work cooperatively to transport three fundamental types of information between components: *where* (address), *what* (data), and *when/how* (control).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim)**
> *A bus is a set of common lines (wires) used for the interconnection of major components of a digital system. The system bus is divided into three functional groups: the data bus, the address bus, and the control bus.*

The system bus is the **structural backbone** of every microprocessor and microcontroller. Without a properly designed bus, the CPU cannot fetch instructions, read operands, write results, or communicate with sensors, actuators, or external memory.

---

## 1.2 The Three Functional Sub-Buses (Components of the System Bus)

The system bus is composed of **three orthogonal sub-buses**, each carrying a different category of information.

### 🔹 (a) Address Bus
The **Address Bus** is a *unidirectional* set of lines that carries the *memory address* or *I/O port address* from the CPU to memory and peripheral devices. It identifies **which** location the CPU intends to read from or write to.

- **Direction:** CPU → Memory / I/O (unidirectional, *output only from the CPU's perspective*).
- **Width:** Determines the maximum addressable memory space.
  - An *n*-bit address bus can address $2^n$ unique memory locations.
- **Example:** The classic **Intel 8085** has a **16-bit address bus** → $2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64 \text{ KB}$ addressable.
- **Example:** The **Intel 8086** has a **20-bit address bus** → $2^{20} = 1 \text{ MB}$ addressable.
- **Example:** The modern **ARM Cortex-M4** typically has a **32-bit address bus** → $2^{32} = 4 \text{ GB}$ addressable.

### 🔹 (b) Data Bus
The **Data Bus** is a *bidirectional* set of lines used to transport the *actual data* (operands, instructions, or I/O values) between the CPU, memory, and peripherals.

- **Direction:** Bidirectional — data can flow *to* or *from* the CPU.
- **Width:** Determines the *natural data word size* of the processor, often called the *processor's word length*.
- **Example:** **8085** has an **8-bit data bus** → can transfer 8 bits in parallel per bus cycle.
- **Example:** **8086** has a **16-bit data bus** → 16 bits per transfer.
- **Example:** **ARM Cortex-M4** has a **32-bit data bus** → 32 bits per transfer.

### 🔹 (c) Control Bus (Control / Status Bus)
The **Control Bus** is a *collection of heterogeneous control and timing signals* that coordinate and synchronize the activities of the CPU, memory, and I/O devices. Unlike the address and data buses, it is *not* uniform — it consists of many discrete signal lines, each with a specific purpose.

- **Direction:** Mostly CPU-driven (control outputs), but some are *status inputs* from peripherals.
- **Typical signals include:** $\overline{\text{RD}}$ (Read), $\overline{\text{WR}}$ (Write), $\overline{\text{MREQ}}$ (Memory Request), $\overline{\text{IORQ}}$ (I/O Request), $\overline{\text{M/IO}}$, $\overline{\text{INT}}$ (Interrupt), $\overline{\text{NMI}}$, $\overline{\text{BUSREQ}}$, $\overline{\text{BUSACK}}$, $\text{READY}$, $\text{CLK}$ (clock), and $\text{RESET}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations**
> A common mistake is to assume the control bus is also bidirectional and uniform. It is a *bundle of individual control/status signals* with mixed directions. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to be able to **list the signals** in the control bus and explain the function of each.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway System

Imagine a **bustling metropolitan city** as the microcontroller system:

- The **Address Bus** is like the **postal address system** or **house number**. When you want to deliver or collect something, you first specify *where*. The address bus is unidirectional because only the *post office* (CPU) can decide the address — the *recipient* (memory) cannot dictate it.

- The **Data Bus** is like the **trucks/couriers** that physically carry the *parcels* (actual data). Since parcels can flow in *both* directions (incoming and outgoing), the data highway must be **bidirectional**.

- The **Control Bus** is like the **traffic signals, road signs, and rules** — *green light* (RD), *red light with right turn* (WR), *merging lane signals* (BUSREQ), *emergency vehicle sirens* (interrupt), and the *master clock* that synchronizes every traffic light in the city.

Just as no city can function without a coordinated set of roads, addresses, and traffic rules, a microcontroller cannot function without a coordinated **address + data + control bus** triad.

---

## 1.4 Why the System Bus Matters in Embedded Systems

In embedded systems, the bus architecture directly determines:
- **Memory map layout** (where ROM, RAM, and peripherals sit in the address space).
- **I/O interfacing strategy** (memory-mapped I/O vs. port-mapped / isolated I/O).
- **System performance** (bus width × clock frequency = theoretical bandwidth).
- **Power consumption** (wider, faster buses consume more dynamic power).
- **Scalability** (ability to add external memory, displays, sensors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point**
> The 8085 processor uses **16-bit address** and **8-bit data** buses, with the address bus being **multiplexed** with the lower 8 bits of the data bus on lines $\text{AD}_0$–$\text{AD}_7$ using the **Address Latch Enable (ALE)** signal. This is a *very commonly tested* KTU concept.

---

## 1.5 Bus Architecture: Von Neumann vs. Harvard

A system bus is also defined by *how* it connects to memory. There are two principal architectural models:

| Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Program & Data Memory | Single shared memory | Physically separate memories |
| Bus | **Single** shared bus for instructions & data | **Two** separate buses |
| Performance Bottleneck | **Von Neumann bottleneck** — fetch & data compete for the same bus | Eliminated — simultaneous fetch + data access |
| Implementation | Simpler, cheaper | Faster, more silicon area |
| Example | 8085, 8051 (modified Harvard) | DSPs, ARM Cortex-M (modified Harvard) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **8051 microcontroller** uses a *modified Harvard* architecture: separate program and data memory spaces, but a common external bus for off-chip access. This is a frequently-asked KTU question.

---

## 1.6 Physical vs. Logical Bus

It is also important for KTU 2024 to distinguish between:

- **Physical Bus** — the *actual copper traces, wires, or PCB tracks* plus the associated *bus drivers/receivers* and *bus arbitration logic*.
- **Logical Bus** — the *protocol, timing, and signal semantics* that govern how information is exchanged over the physical bus. Examples: AMBA (ARM), PCI, I²C, SPI, CAN, USB.

For example, the **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** protocol used in ARM-based systems defines three logical buses: **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)**, **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)**, and **AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface)**.

---

## 1.7 Section Summary Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Recall for KTU Viva / Quiz**
> *"The system bus = Address Bus (unidirectional, identifies location) + Data Bus (bidirectional, carries data) + Control Bus (mixed-direction, manages timing & operation)."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the System Bus — Step-by-Step

When a CPU executes an instruction that accesses memory or I/O, the system bus executes a precisely orchestrated **Bus Cycle** (also called a *Machine Cycle* in 8085 terminology). The following is the generalized sequence:

1. **Step 1 — Address Placement (T1 state):** The CPU places the target *memory address* or *port number* on the **address bus**. Simultaneously, the CPU activates the appropriate *status signals* on the control bus to indicate whether the cycle is a *Memory Read*, *Memory Write*, *I/O Read*, or *I/O Write*.

2. **Step 2 — Address Decoding (T1–T2):** The external *address decoder* (typically built using 74LS138 / 74LS139 decoders) interprets the address and selects the *correct memory chip* or *I/O device* by activating its $\overline{\text{CS}}$ (Chip Select) or $\overline{\text{CE}}$ (Chip Enable) pin.

3. **Step 3 — Data Transfer (T2–T3):** Once the addressed device is enabled, the actual transfer occurs on the **data bus**:
   - For a *Read* operation ($\overline{\text{RD}} = 0$): The selected device *drives* the data bus with the contents of the addressed location.
   - For a *Write* operation ($\overline{\text{WR}} = 0$): The CPU *drives* the data bus with the value to be stored, and the selected device latches it.

4. **Step 4 — Cycle Termination (T3 end / T4):** The control signals are de-asserted, the data bus is released to high-impedance, and the CPU proceeds to the next machine cycle or instruction fetch.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The 8085 introduces *wait states* (Tw) inserted between T2 and T3 when slow memory devices cannot respond within the standard clock period. The CPU monitors the **READY** signal — if READY is LOW, wait states are inserted. This is critical for interfacing slow peripherals.

---

## 2.2 Bus Width vs. Addressable Memory — The Master Formula

The relationship between the **width of the address bus** and the **maximum addressable memory** is foundational.

$$
\boxed{\ M_{\max} = 2^{n} \times W\ }
$$

Where:
- $M_{\max}$ = total addressable memory in **bytes** (assuming byte-addressable memory).
- $n$ = number of address lines (bits).
- $W$ = data bus width in bytes (typically $W = 1$ byte for an 8-bit data bus).

**Derivation intuition:** Each of the $n$ address lines can be either HIGH (1) or LOW (0), giving $2^n$ unique binary combinations. Each combination corresponds to exactly **one** uniquely addressable memory location. Each location has a width of $W$ bytes (the data bus width).

### Examples for KTU Practice:
| Processor | Address Bus (n) | Data Bus | $M_{\max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8085 | 16 | 8-bit | $2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64 \text{ KB}$ |
| 8086 | 20 | 16-bit | $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 = 1 \text{ MB}$ |
| 80286 | 24 | 16-bit | $2^{24} = 16 \text{ MB}$ |
| 80386 | 32 | 32-bit | $2^{32} = 4 \text{ GB}$ |
| 8051 | 16 | 8-bit | $2^{16} = 64 \text{ KB}$ (external) |

---

## 2.3 Bus Bandwidth (Data Throughput) Formula

The **theoretical maximum data transfer rate** of a bus is called its *bandwidth*.

$$
\boxed{\ BW = \dfrac{n_{\text{data}} \times f_{\text{bus}}}{8} \ \text{bytes/sec}\ }
$$

Where:
- $BW$ = bus bandwidth in **bytes per second**.
- $n_{\text{data}}$ = data bus width in **bits**.
- $f_{\text{bus}}$ = bus clock frequency in **Hz**.

**Real-world utility:** A designer uses this to determine whether a bus can support a real-time video stream. For example, a 32-bit bus at 100 MHz gives:

$$
BW = \frac{32 \times 100 \times 10^{6}}{8} = 400 \times 10^{6} \ \text{bytes/sec} = 400 \text{ MB/s}
$$

---

## 2.4 Bus Loading and Driving Capability

A bus line is a *shared* electrical node. Every connected device places a *load* (capacitive and DC) on the bus. The **fan-out** of a bus driver is the maximum number of standard loads it can drive while maintaining valid logic levels.

$$
\boxed{\ \text{Fan-out} = \min \left( \frac{I_{OL}}{I_{IL}},\ \frac{I_{OH}}{I_{IH}} \right)\ }
$$

Where:
- $I_{OL}$ = output current when LOW (sink current).
- $I_{IL}$ = input current when LOW (load current).
- $I_{OH}$ = output current when HIGH (source current).
- $I_{IH}$ = input current when HIGH (load current).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Exceeding fan-out causes *logic-level degradation* — HIGH levels fall below $V_{IH(\min)}$ and LOW levels rise above $V_{IL(\max)}$, leading to bus errors. The solution is to insert **bus buffers/drivers** (e.g., 74LS245 for bidirectional data bus buffering, 74LS244 for unidirectional address bus buffering).

---

## 2.5 Bus Arbitration (Multi-Master Systems)

In a system with **multiple bus masters** (e.g., CPU + DMA controller), only one master can drive the bus at a time. **Bus arbitration** is the process of granting bus access to one master at a time.

Two principal schemes:

- **Daisy-Chain Arbitration:** Fixed priority; the master closest to the arbiter has the highest priority.
- **Independent Request / Grant (Parallel Arbitration):** Each master has dedicated $\overline{\text{BREQ}}$ and $\overline{\text{BGNT}}$ lines; the arbiter uses a *priority encoder* (e.g., 74LS148) to resolve concurrent requests.

The 8085 supports *basic* bus arbitration using $\overline{\text{HOLD}}$ (input) and **HLDA** (Hold Acknowledge, output) for DMA handshaking.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Key Constraint / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Max addressable memory | $M_{\max} = 2^{n} \times W$ | bytes | $n$ = address bus bits, $W$ = data bus bytes |
| 2 | Bus bandwidth | $BW = (n_{\text{data}} \times f_{\text{bus}}) / 8$ | bytes/sec | Assumes 1 transfer per bus cycle |
| 3 | Fan-out (DC) | $\text{Fan-out} = \min(I_{OL}/I_{IL},\ I_{OH}/I_{IH})$ | pure number | Both ratios must be $\geq 1$ |
| 4 | Propagation delay budget | $t_{pd(\text{total})} \leq T_{\text{cycle}}$ | seconds | $T_{\text{cycle}} = 1/f_{\text{clock}}$ for a state |
| 5 | Number of wait states | $N_W = \lceil (t_{\text{access}} - 2T)/T \rceil$ | integer | $t_{\text{access}}$ = memory access time, $T$ = clock period |
| 6 | Power dissipated by bus line | $P = C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | watts | $C$ = line capacitance, $f$ = switching frequency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Row 5 (Wait States) — Common KTU Derivation**
> If the memory access time $t_{\text{access}}$ exceeds the standard 2T (two clock periods) of a normal 8085 machine cycle, the number of *wait states* required is computed as $N_W = \lceil (t_{\text{access}} - 2T)/T \rceil$.

---

## 2.7 Address Decoding — The Heart of Bus Interfacing

For the CPU to *select* a specific memory chip out of many connected to the bus, an **address decoder** is required. Two common strategies:

- **Linear Decoding (Partial Decoding):** A subset of address lines is used; the remaining lines are *don't-cares*. Simple, but causes *address aliasing* (the same memory location can be accessed via multiple addresses).
- **Full Decoding (Absolute Decoding):** *All* address lines are used. Each memory location has a **unique** address. More hardware, but no aliasing.

A typical **3-to-8 decoder** like the **74LS138** uses 3 select inputs + 3 enable inputs to produce 8 mutually exclusive $\overline{Y_0}$–$\overline{Y_7}$ outputs. Each $\overline{Y_i}$ can be tied to the $\overline{\text{CS}}$ of one memory chip.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The system bus concept is foundational in *every* branch of modern engineering:

- **Automotive ECUs** use **CAN bus** for inter-ECU communication, defined by ISO 11898.
- **Aerospace** uses **MIL-STD-1553B** as a redundant, fault-tolerant bus.
- **Consumer electronics** use **AMBA (AHB/APB/AXI)** in ARM-based SoCs.
- **Industrial automation** uses **PROFIBUS / EtherCAT**.
- **Personal computers** use **PCIe, USB, SATA, I²C, SPI**.

Understanding the basic system bus triad (Address / Data / Control) is therefore the *gateway* to understanding every modern bus standard.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Addressable Memory from Address Bus Width

**Given:** An 8085 microprocessor with a 16-bit address bus and 8-bit data bus (byte-addressable memory).

**To find:** The maximum directly addressable memory $M_{\max}$.

### Step-by-Step Working

**Step 1 — Count the number of distinct address combinations.**

Each of the 16 address lines can independently be in one of two states (logic 0 or logic 1). By the multiplication principle of counting:

$$
N_{\text{addresses}} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \dots \times 2}_{16 \text{ factors}} = 2^{16}
$$

$$
N_{\text{addresses}} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ unique addresses}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to memory size.**

Since the 8085 data bus is 8 bits wide (1 byte), each address refers to exactly 1 byte of memory. Therefore the total addressable memory is:

$$
M_{\max} = N_{\text{addresses}} \times 1 \text{ byte} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in KB.**

$$
1 \text{ KB} = 1024 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
M_{\max} = \frac{65{,}536}{1024} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\ M_{\max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}\ }
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Wait States for a Slow Memory Device

**Given:**
- 8085 clock frequency $f_{\text{CLK}} = 3 \text{ MHz}$, so clock period $T = 1/f_{\text{CLK}}$.
- Memory access time $t_{\text{access}} = 1.0 \ \mu\text{s}$.
- Standard 8085 machine cycle = 4 clock states (T1, T2, T3, T4).

**To find:** Number of wait states (Tw) required.

### Step-by-Step Working

**Step 1 — Compute the clock period.**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_{\text{CLK}}} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{6}} = 0.333 \ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the memory access time in units of $T$.**

$$
\frac{t_{\text{access}}}{T} = \frac{1.0 \ \mu\text{s}}{0.333 \ \mu\text{s}} \approx 3.0
$$

**Step 3 — Determine available time in a standard machine cycle.**

A normal 8085 opcode fetch (or memory read/write) machine cycle uses **3 T-states for the actual data transfer** (T1 to T3, with T4 for internal operations). The CPU samples the data at the *end of T2 / start of T3* in a read operation.

The time budget between address placement (start of T1) and data sampling (end of T2) is:

$$
t_{\text{available}} = 2T = 2 \times 0.333 \ \mu\text{s} = 0.667 \ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the shortfall.**

$$
\Delta t = t_{\text{access}} - 2T = 1.0 \ \mu\text{s} - 0.667 \ \mu\text{s} = 0.333 \ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert shortfall to wait states.**

$$
N_W = \left\lceil \frac{\Delta t}{T} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{0.333 \ \mu\text{s}}{0.333 \ \mu\text{s}} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.0 \rceil = 1
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\ N_W = 1 \text{ wait state}\ }
$$

**Interpretation:** The memory device needs **1 additional wait state** to provide valid data. The READY line is held LOW for 1 clock cycle, then driven HIGH.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bus Bandwidth Calculation for a Hypothetical SoC

**Given:**
- 32-bit data bus.
- Bus clock frequency $f_{\text{bus}} = 50 \text{ MHz}$.
- One transfer per bus cycle (ideal case, no wait states, no protocol overhead).

**To find:** Theoretical peak bus bandwidth in MB/s.

### Step-by-Step Working

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

- $n_{\text{data}} = 32$ bits.
- $f_{\text{bus}} = 50 \times 10^{6}$ Hz.

**Step 2 — Apply the bandwidth formula.**

$$
BW = \frac{n_{\text{data}} \times f_{\text{bus}}}{8}
$$

$$
BW = \frac{32 \times 50 \times 10^{6}}{8} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{9}}{8} = 2 \times 10^{8} \ \text{bytes/sec}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to MB/s.**

$$
1 \text{ MB} = 10^{6} \ \text{bytes (in SI) or } 2^{20} \ \text{bytes (binary)}
$$

Using SI convention:

$$
BW = 2 \times 10^{8} \ \text{bytes/sec} = 200 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\ BW = 200 \text{ MB/s (theoretical peak)}\ }
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Actual sustained bandwidth is typically **50%–70%** of the theoretical peak due to bus turn-around cycles, address phases, idle cycles, and protocol overhead.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Address Decoding Range Using 74LS138

**Given:** A 74LS138 (3-to-8 line decoder) is used in an 8085 system. The decoder's three inputs $A$, $B$, $C$ are connected to address lines $A_{15}$, $A_{14}$, $A_{13}$. The enable inputs are tied such that the decoder is always enabled.

**To find:** The address range for which output $\overline{Y_2}$ is active (LOW).

### Step-by-Step Working

**Step 1 — Identify the binary input pattern for $\overline{Y_2}$.**

The 74LS138 produces a LOW on $\overline{Y_i}$ when the C-B-A input equals the binary representation of $i$. For $\overline{Y_2}$:

$$
C B A = 0\ 1\ 0 \quad \text{(binary 2)}
$$

**Step 2 — Map this to the address lines.**

- $A = A_{15} = 0$
- $B = A_{14} = 1$
- $C = A_{13} = 0$

**Step 3 — Determine the address pattern.**

The lower 13 address lines ($A_{12}$ down to $A_0$) are don't-cares — they can be anything from 000…0 to 111…1.

- **Minimum address:** $A_{15}A_{14}A_{13}A_{12}\dots A_0 = 010\,0000\,0000\,0000_2 = 2000\text{H}$
- **Maximum address:** $A_{15}A_{14}A_{13}A_{12}\dots A_0 = 010\,1111\,1111\,1111_2 = 3FFF\text{H}$

**Step 4 — Compute the size of the decoded block.**

$$
\text{Block size} = 2^{13} = 8192 \text{ bytes} = 8 \text{ KB}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\ \text{Address range for } \overline{Y_2} = 2000\text{H} \text{ to } 3FFF\text{H} \ (\text{8 KB block})\ }
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In *partial* (linear) decoding, the same memory chip may be mirrored (aliased) at multiple addresses. The above address range is *not* unique — it may also be selected at $6000\text{H}$–$7FFF\text{H}$ if the higher address lines are left as don't-cares. **Full decoding** eliminates aliasing.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Buses Used with Microcontrollers

| Bus Family | Width (bits) | Clock | Peak Bandwidth | Topology | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I²C** | 1 (SDA) + 1 (SCL) | 100 kHz / 400 kHz / 3.4 MHz | 0.4 MB/s (Fast mode) | Multi-master, 2-wire | Sensors, EEPROMs, RTCs |
| **SPI** | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS) | up to 50 MHz | ~6 MB/s | Master-slave, 4-wire | SD cards, displays, flash |
| **UART** | 1 (TX) + 1 (RX) | baud rate dependent | ~1 Mbaud | Point-to-point, 2-wire | Debug, GPS, BT modules |
| **CAN** | 1 (CANH) + 1 (CANL) | up to 1 Mbps | ~1 Mbps | Multi-master, 2-wire | Automotive, industrial |
| **PCI** | 32 / 64 | 33 / 66 MHz | 132–528 MB/s | Parallel, multi-drop | Legacy PC expansion |
| **AMBA AHB** | 32 / 64 / 128 | up to 200 MHz | ~3.2 GB/s | Shared bus, multi-master | ARM SoC high-speed |
| **AMBA AXI** | 32 / 64 / 128 | up to 1 GHz+ | >10 GB/s | Point-to-point, channels | ARM SoC high-perf |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — End-to-End Bus Sizing Problem

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A microcontroller-based data acquisition system samples 16 analog channels at 10 kHz each with 12-bit resolution. Data is stored in external RAM via an 8-bit data bus. Compute:
1. The minimum address bus width required to address 256 KB of external RAM.
2. The minimum bus bandwidth required.
3. The number of memory locations each analog sample occupies.

### Solution

**Part (1): Address bus width for 256 KB.**

$$
256 \text{ KB} = 256 \times 1024 \text{ bytes} = 262{,}144 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
2^n \geq 262{,}144 \implies n \geq \log_2(262{,}144) = 18
$$

$$
\boxed{\ n_{\text{address}} = 18 \text{ bits}\ }
$$

**Part (2): Minimum bus bandwidth.**

Total data rate = 16 channels × 10 kHz × 12 bits = 1,920,000 bits/s = 240,000 bytes/s ≈ **240 KB/s**.

Adding 25% protocol overhead:

$$
BW_{\min} = 1.25 \times 240 \text{ KB/s} = 300 \text{ KB/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\ BW_{\min} \approx 300 \text{ KB/s}\ }
$$

**Part (3): Memory locations per sample.**

Each 12-bit sample, when stored via an 8-bit bus, occupies $\lceil 12/8 \rceil = 2$ bytes (one full byte + a nibble, often stored as 2 bytes for alignment).

$$
\boxed{\ \text{Each sample occupies 2 memory locations (bytes)}\ }
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Three-Bus Architecture of a Microcontroller

The following **Mermaid functional block diagram** illustrates how the three sub-buses (Address, Data, Control) interconnect the major components of a typical microcontroller system.

```mermaid
graph TB
    CPU[Central Processing Unit]
    ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit]
    REG[Register File]
    MEM[Memory Subsystem]
    RAM[RAM]
    ROM[ROM]
    IO1[IO Peripheral 1]
    IO2[IO Peripheral 2]
    DEC[Address Decoder]
    CLK[Clock Generator]
    ABUS[Address Bus]
    DBUS[Data Bus]
    CBUS[Control Bus]
    BUF[Bus Buffers and Drivers]

    CPU --> ALU
    CPU --> REG
    CPU --> BUF
    CLK --> CPU
    CLK --> MEM
    CLK --> IO1
    CLK --> IO2
    BUF --> ABUS
    BUF --> DBUS
    BUF --> CBUS
    ABUS --> DEC
    ABUS --> MEM
    ABUS --> IO1
    ABUS --> IO2
    DBUS --> MEM
    DBUS --> IO1
    DBUS --> IO2
    CBUS --> MEM
    CBUS --> IO1
    CBUS --> IO2
    DEC --> MEM
    DEC --> IO1
    DEC --> IO2
    MEM --> RAM
    MEM --> ROM
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Clock Generator** drives the CPU and major peripherals to synchronize every bus operation.
- The **CPU** (containing ALU and Register File) drives the bus through **Buffers/Drivers** to provide adequate fan-out.
- The **Address Decoder** examines high-order address bits to activate the appropriate memory or I/O device via its $\overline{\text{CS}}$ pin.
- All three sub-buses reach every connected peripheral; only the addressed device responds.

---

## 4.2 Bus Cycle State Machine (8085-style Read Operation)

This diagram shows the timing flow of a typical *Memory Read* machine cycle.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> T1
    T1 --> T2 : Address placed on Address Bus; ALE pulse generated; RD asserted LOW
    T2 --> T2 : Wait States inserted if READY is LOW
    T2 --> T3 : READY goes HIGH; data latched from Data Bus
    T3 --> T4 : Internal operations; RD de-asserted
    T4 --> [*] : Cycle complete; CPU proceeds
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Tw (Wait State) is a *self-loop* on T2 — it is repeated as long as the **READY** signal remains LOW. Once READY goes HIGH, the state machine advances to T3.

---

## 4.3 Address Decoder Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A15[Address Line A15] --> DEC[74LS138 Decoder]
    A14[Address Line A14] --> DEC
    A13[Address Line A13] --> DEC
    DEC --> Y0[Output Y0 - RAM]
    DEC --> Y1[Output Y1 - ROM]
    DEC --> Y2[Output Y2 - IO Port]
    DEC --> Y3[Output Y3 - Peripheral]
    DEC --> Y4[Unused]
    DEC --> Y5[Unused]
    DEC --> Y6[Unused]
    DEC --> Y7[Unused]
    RD[Control Signal RD] --> RAM
    RD --> ROM
    WR[Control Signal WR] --> RAM
    WR --> IO2
    IO[IO Request IORQ] --> IO
```

**Explanation:**
- The 74LS138 accepts the three high-order address lines $A_{15}$, $A_{14}$, $A_{13}$ as binary input.
- It generates 8 mutually-exclusive active-LOW outputs $\overline{Y_0}$–$\overline{Y_7}$.
- Each $\overline{Y_i}$ is wired to the $\overline{\text{CS}}$ of one memory or I/O device.
- The control bus signals ($\overline{\text{RD}}$, $\overline{\text{WR}}$, $\overline{\text{IORQ}}$) further qualify whether the operation is a *read*, *write*, or *I/O* cycle.

---

## 4.4 Multiplexed Address/Data Bus (8085 AD0–AD7)

The 8085 uses a **multiplexed bus** to save pins. The same 8 pins carry both the lower address byte (during T1) and the data byte (during T2–T3). The **ALE** signal is used to latch the address.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU 8085
    participant LATCH as External Latch 74LS373
    participant MEM as Memory

    Note over CPU: T1 state begins
    CPU ->> LATCH: Place AD0-AD7 = A0-A7 (lower address)
    CPU ->> LATCH: ALE goes HIGH (latch enable)
    LATCH ->> LATCH: Captures A0-A7
    Note over LATCH: Address is now stable on A0-A7 pins
    CPU ->> LATCH: ALE goes LOW (latch disabled)
    Note over CPU: A8-A15 also placed on high address bus
    CPU ->> MEM: Full 16-bit address now stable
    CPU ->> MEM: RD goes LOW
    MEM ->> CPU: Data placed on AD0-AD7 (data bus)
    Note over CPU: CPU reads data at end of T2 / start of T3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This *multiplexing* technique is a hallmark of the 8085. In contrast, the 8086 uses a *demultiplexed* bus for the lower 16 lines (using the 8282 latch externally), but the address and data lines are physically separate on the chip.

---

## 4.5 System Bus and Components — Holistic Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph MASTER["BUS MASTER"]
        M1[CPU Core]
        M2[DMA Controller]
    end
    subgraph ARBITER["ARBITRATION LOGIC"]
        ARB[Bus Arbiter]
    end
    subgraph BUSES["SYSTEM BUS TRIAD"]
        A1[Address Bus - Unidirectional]
        A2[Data Bus - Bidirectional]
        A3[Control Bus - Mixed]
    end
    subgraph SLAVES["BUS SLAVES"]
        S1[ROM]
        S2[RAM]
        S3[IO Device 1]
        S4[IO Device 2]
        S5[External Peripheral]
    end
    M1 --> ARB
    M2 --> ARB
    ARB --> A1
    ARB --> A2
    ARB --> A3
    A1 --> S1
    A1 --> S2
    A1 --> S3
    A1 --> S4
    A1 --> S5
    A2 --> S1
    A2 --> S2
    A2 --> S3
    A2 --> S4
    A2 --> S5
    A3 --> S1
    A3 --> S2
    A3 --> S3
    A3 --> S4
    A3 --> S5
```

**Interpretation:** In multi-master systems (CPU + DMA), the **Bus Arbiter** ensures that only one master drives the address and data buses at any time. The control bus carries arbitration-related signals (HOLD, HLDA, BUSREQ, BUSACK) in addition to the standard read/write signals.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**"Differentiate between Address Bus, Data Bus, and Control Bus."**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Address Bus | Data Bus | Control Bus |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Carries the memory or I/O address of the operand | Carries the actual data (operand) to be transferred | Carries timing and control signals |
| **Direction** | Unidirectional (CPU → peripherals) | Bidirectional (CPU ↔ peripherals) | Mixed (CPU outputs and peripheral inputs) |
| **Width impact** | Determines addressable memory ($2^n$) | Determines word size / transfer size | No direct memory impact |
| **Typical lines** | A0–A15 (8085) | D0–D7 (8085) | $\overline{\text{RD}}, \overline{\text{WR}}, \text{IO/M}, \text{ALE}$ etc. |
| **Example (8085)** | 16 lines → 64 KB max | 8 lines → 8-bit transfer | ~10 control/status signals |

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**"What is bus arbitration? Why is it required in multi-master systems?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Bus arbitration** is the process of resolving *competing requests* for bus access from multiple bus masters (e.g., CPU and DMA controller) so that only **one master controls the bus at a time**.

**Why required:**
- In a multi-master system, two or more devices (CPU, DMA, co-processor) may request the bus simultaneously.
- If two masters drive the bus at the same time, the result is **bus contention**, causing logic-level conflicts, high current draw, and possible device damage.
- Arbitration ensures *mutual exclusion* and *deterministic* bus ownership, typically through a priority scheme (daisy-chain or parallel/independent-request).

**Common signals (8085 example):**
- $\overline{\text{HOLD}}$ — input to CPU from DMA requesting the bus.
- **HLDA (Hold Acknowledge)** — output from CPU releasing the bus.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a microprocessor-based system showing the three sub-buses. List at least 6 signals of the 8085 control bus and explain the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8085 system uses a 74LS138 decoder with inputs connected to $A_{15}, A_{14}, A_{13}$ (and appropriate enables). For the output $\overline{Y_4}$, determine: (i) the address range, (ii) the size of the addressable block, and (iii) the input pattern on the 74LS138. **(7 marks)**

#### 📘 Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Model Answer:**

The architecture consists of a **CPU**, **memory**, **I/O devices**, and an **address decoder** interconnected by the three sub-buses. *(Refer to Section 4.1 for the block diagram.)*

**Six signals of the 8085 control bus:**

1. **$\overline{\text{RD}}$ (Read, active LOW, output from CPU):** Asserted by the CPU to indicate a *read* operation. The addressed device places data on the data bus.

2. **$\overline{\text{WR}}$ (Write, active LOW, output from CPU):** Asserted by the CPU to indicate a *write* operation. The CPU places data on the bus, and the addressed device latches it.

3. **$\text{IO}/\overline{\text{M}}$ (I/O or Memory, output from CPU):** HIGH → I/O operation, LOW → memory operation. Distinguishes the address space being accessed.

4. **$\overline{\text{ALE}}$ (Address Latch Enable, output from CPU):** HIGH during T1 — used to latch the lower address byte (AD0–AD7) into an external latch (e.g., 74LS373) because AD0–AD7 is a *multiplexed* address/data bus.

5. **$\overline{\text{INTR}}$ (Interrupt Request, input to CPU):** A *maskable* interrupt request from an external peripheral. Acknowledged by the CPU during the instruction cycle using $\overline{\text{INTA}}$.

6. **$\overline{\text{HOLD}}$ (Hold, input to CPU):** Asserted by an external master (e.g., DMA) requesting the bus. The CPU completes the current machine cycle, places its address, data, and control lines in **high-impedance**, and asserts **HLDA**.

7. *(Bonus)* **$\overline{\text{RESET IN}}$ (Reset Input):** Resets the program counter to 0000H and clears internal registers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning:** Students often confuse $\text{IO}/\overline{\text{M}}$ with the *Intel 8086*'s **$\text{M}/\overline{\text{IO}}$** (which is logically inverted). On the 8085, when the signal is HIGH, the cycle refers to an **I/O** operation; on the 8086, when HIGH, it refers to a **Memory** operation. The KTU 2024 examiner will deduct marks for this inversion error.

**Part (b) — Model Answer:**

For $\overline{Y_4}$ of the 74LS138, the input pattern is:

$$
C\,B\,A = 1\,0\,0 \quad (\text{binary } 4)
$$

Therefore:
- $A = A_{15} = 0$
- $B = A_{14} = 0$
- $C = A_{13} = 1$

**(i) Address range:**
- Minimum: $A_{15}A_{14}A_{13}A_{12}\ldots A_0 = 100\,0000\,0000\,0000_2 = 4000\text{H}$
- Maximum: $A_{15}A_{14}A_{13}A_{12}\ldots A_0 = 100\,1111\,1111\,1111_2 = 5FFF\text{H}$

**(ii) Block size:**

$$
\text{Block size} = 2^{13} = 8192 \text{ bytes} = 8 \text{ KB}
$$

**(iii) Input pattern:** $CBA = 100_2$

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**(a)** Compare Von Neumann and Harvard architectures with diagrams. Explain why the 8051 is called a *modified Harvard* architecture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A memory device has an access time of 450 ns. It is interfaced to an 8085 system running at 4 MHz. Determine: (i) the clock period, (ii) whether wait states are required, and (iii) if yes, how many. Justify with the relevant formula. **(7 marks)**

#### 📘 Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory structure | Single shared memory for program and data | Separate program and data memories |
| Bus structure | Common bus for instructions and data | Separate buses for instructions and data |
| Simultaneous access | Not possible (shared bus) | Possible (parallel fetch and read) |
| Bottleneck | **Von Neumann bottleneck** (40%–50% performance loss) | Eliminated |
| Hardware cost | Lower | Higher (more bus lines) |
| Example | 8085, conventional desktop CPUs | DSPs, ARM Cortex-M cores |

**Modified Harvard in 8051:**
The 8051 has:
- **Separate address spaces** for program memory (ROM, up to 64 KB) and data memory (RAM, 128 bytes internal + 64 KB external).
- **Separate internal buses** for fetching instructions and accessing data.
- However, when accessing **external** memory, the buses are *combined* into a single external bus (multiplexed via $\overline{\text{PSEN}}$ for program and $\overline{\text{RD}}/\overline{\text{WR}}$ for data).

This *partial* separation justifies the term **modified Harvard**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often state the 8051 is "pure Harvard" — this is **incorrect**. KTU 2024 examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for this over-simplification. The correct answer must explicitly mention the *modification* — the merging of buses for external memory access.

**Part (b) — Model Answer:**

**(i) Clock period $T$:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^6} = 0.25 \ \mu\text{s} = 250 \text{ ns}
$$

**(ii) Compare with memory access time:**

The 8085 provides **2T = 500 ns** between address placement and data sampling in a memory read cycle.

$$
t_{\text{access}} = 450 \text{ ns} < 2T = 500 \text{ ns}
$$

Since 450 ns < 500 ns, the memory is **fast enough** and **no wait states are strictly required**.

**However**, accounting for *real-world* bus buffering delay, address decoder delay ($\sim$30 ns), and data setup time, the effective margin is only $500 - 450 - 30 - 20 = 0$ ns (tight!). In practice, **1 wait state is recommended** for design margin.

**(iii) Theoretical formula application:**

$$
N_W = \left\lceil \frac{t_{\text{access}} - 2T}{T} \right\rceil
$$

Plugging $t_{\text{access}} = 450$ ns and $T = 250$ ns:

$$
N_W = \left\lceil \frac{450 - 500}{250} \right\rceil = \lceil -0.2 \rceil = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\ N_W = 0 \text{ wait states (theoretical)}\ }
$$

**Conclusion:** Zero wait states theoretically suffice, but **one wait state is recommended** in practice for reliable operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students sometimes write $N_W = \lceil t_{\text{access}}/T \rceil$ without subtracting the standard 2T budget. This is wrong. The correct formula is $N_W = \lceil (t_{\text{access}} - 2T)/T \rceil$. A negative value is treated as 0 (no wait states needed).

---

## 5.3 Additional Practice Question (For Self-Assessment)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Practice]**
**(a)** Explain the role of bus buffers/drivers. With a neat diagram, show how a 74LS245 is used to buffer a bidirectional 8-bit data bus. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system has 32 KB ROM and 32 KB RAM. ROM is mapped at $0000\text{H}$–$7FFF\text{H}$. Design a full address decoding scheme using 74LS138, identifying the address range for RAM. **(7 marks)**

*(Solution sketch: ROM uses $\overline{Y_0}$, RAM uses $\overline{Y_1}$. With $A_{15}$ = 0 for both, the 74LS138 inputs are $A=A_{15}=0$, $B=A_{14}$, $C=A_{13}$. For RAM at $8000\text{H}$–$FFFF\text{H}$, the input pattern is $CBA=001$, so $\overline{Y_1}$ activates when $A_{15}A_{14}A_{13} = 001$. Address range: $8000\text{H}$ to $FFFF\text{H}$, size 32 KB. ✓)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Rapid-Fire Revision — System Bus and Components**

- ✅ A **system bus** = Address Bus + Data Bus + Control Bus.
- ✅ **Address bus is unidirectional**; data bus is bidirectional; control bus is mixed.
- ✅ Memory capacity formula: $M_{\max} = 2^n \times W$ bytes.
- ✅ **8085 specifics:** 16-bit address bus, 8-bit data bus, 64 KB max, **multiplexed** lower address/data on $\text{AD}_0$–$\text{AD}_7$, demultiplexed using **ALE + 74LS373**.
- ✅ **8086 specifics:** 20-bit address (1 MB) with *segment:offset*, 16-bit data bus, address demultiplexed using 8282 latches.
- ✅ **8051 specifics:** 16-bit address bus for external memory, 8-bit data bus, modified Harvard architecture.
- ✅ Wait states formula: $N_W = \lceil (t_{\text{access}} - 2T)/T \rceil$.
- ✅ Bandwidth formula: $BW = (n_{\text{data}} \times f_{\text{bus}})/8$ bytes/sec.
- ✅ Fan-out formula: $\min(I_{OL}/I_{IL},\ I_{OH}/I_{IH})$.
- ✅ **74LS138** is a 3-to-8 active-LOW decoder with 3 enable inputs (2 active LOW + 1 active HIGH).
- ✅ **Linear decoding** = partial; full address lines not used → *address aliasing*.
- ✅ **Absolute decoding** = full; every line used → *unique addresses*.
- ✅ **Bus arbitration** = granting bus to one master at a time; needed for DMA, multi-core.
- ✅ **Bus contention** = two devices driving the bus simultaneously → must be avoided.
- ✅ **Von Neumann bottleneck** = shared bus for instruction and data fetch.
- ✅ **Harvard** = separate instruction and data buses → higher throughput.
- ✅ **AMBA** is the standard ARM bus protocol family: APB (peripherals), AHB (high-speed), AXI (high-performance).
- ✅ **Bus buffer ICs:** 74LS245 (bidirectional, data), 74LS244 (unidirectional, address).
- ✅ **READY** signal is used to insert wait states; **HOLD/HLDA** is used for DMA handshaking.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RISC and CISC Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** RISC and CISC Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** is a CPU design philosophy based on the principle that a small, highly optimized set of instructions, combined with a deep pipeline and a large register file, can execute most operations in a single clock cycle. Each instruction performs a simple, atomic operation (e.g., load, store, add, branch).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** is a CPU design philosophy that favors a large, rich, and variable-length instruction set where a single instruction can perform multiple low-level operations (such as memory fetch, arithmetic operation, and memory store) in one go. Instructions may take multiple clock cycles and multiple operand formats.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Chef" Model

Imagine a **professional restaurant kitchen** versus a **fast-food kitchen**:

- **CISC is like a Gourmet Chef.** A single, highly experienced chef can take one complex order ("prepare a 5-course meal") and execute many sub-tasks (chopping, sautéing, plating) in a single, intricate motion. Each "instruction" is rich, takes variable time, and packs many operations. This is flexible but hard to parallelize, and the chef becomes a bottleneck.

- **RISC is like a Fast-Food Assembly Line.** The work is broken into the *smallest possible standardized steps* (one worker toasts the bun, another adds the patty, another wraps). Every "instruction" is a simple, uniform action that takes **exactly one fixed time slot**. Because every step is simple, you can run many lines (pipelining) in parallel and train new "workers" (compiler optimizations) easily.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** RISC trades richer instructions for *simplicity, speed per instruction, and parallel pipeline efficiency*. CISC trades simplicity for *compact code, fewer instructions per program, and reduced memory pressure* — historically valuable when memory was extremely expensive.

## 1.3 Standard Architectural Metrics (KTU Board-Favorite Numbers)

- **Instruction Length:** RISC = fixed (typically **32 bits**); CISC = variable (1 to **15 bytes** in x86).
- **Addressing Modes:** RISC uses **3–5** simple modes; CISC may use **12–24** complex modes.
- **Clock Cycles per Instruction (CPI):** RISC aims for **CPI = 1**; CISC historically ranges from **2 to 15**.
- **Register File Size:** RISC = **32 or more** general-purpose registers; CISC = **8 to 16** (with special-purpose registers).
- **Memory Access:** RISC uses dedicated **Load/Store** instructions only; CISC allows memory operands in *any* instruction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy of RISC — The "Five Golden Rules"

The foundational principles proposed by **Patterson and Ditzel (1980)** that define any true RISC architecture:

1. **Simplicity over complexity:** Every instruction should execute in a single cycle (CPI ≈ 1).
2. **Load/Store architecture:** Only `LOAD` and `STORE` instructions access memory; all data processing operates on registers. This decouples computation from memory access, enabling pipeline optimization.
3. **Large, uniform register file:** Minimizes memory traffic by giving the compiler many registers to keep operands resident.
4. **Fixed instruction length & format:** Simplifies instruction fetch, decode, and pipelining hardware.
5. **Hard-wired control (not microcoded):** Control logic uses combinatorial decoders, not microcode ROM, reducing cycle time.
6. **Optimize the common case:** Make the most frequently executed instructions the fastest; push complexity to the *compiler*, not the *hardware*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples of RISC architectures:** ARM (Cortex-M/A/R), RISC-V, MIPS, SPARC, PowerPC, AVR (used in Arduino).

## 2.2 Design Philosophy of CISC

CISC was developed in the **1970s** when memory was scarce, expensive, and slow. The driving motivations were:

- **Reduce the semantic gap** between high-level languages (HLL) and machine code.
- **Minimize program size** (memory footprint) by packing many operations into one instruction.
- **Backward compatibility** to preserve existing software investment.
- **Microcode-based control:** Complex instructions are broken down internally by a microcode engine into micro-operations (μops).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples of CISC architectures:** Intel x86, x86-64, Motorola 68k, VAX, IBM z/Architecture (mainframes).

## 2.3 The Iron Law of Processor Performance

The relationship between RISC and CISC performance is best understood through the **CPU Performance Equation**:

$$\text{CPU Time} = \text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI} \times \text{Clock Cycle Time}$$

Equivalently, in terms of program work:

$$\text{CPU Time} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI}}{\text{Clock Frequency}}$$

**Strategic comparison using the equation:**

| Strategy | Instruction Count | CPI | Clock Cycle Time | Net Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CISC Approach** | **Lower** (rich instructions do more) | **Higher** (variable, complex) | **Longer** (complex control unit) | Reduces program size; sacrifices cycle time |
| **RISC Approach** | **Higher** (simple instructions) | **Lower** (≈ 1, predictable) | **Shorter** (simple decoding) | More instructions but faster execution & pipelinable |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** This equation is the *master key* for any KTU numerical on processor performance. Any micro-architectural trade-off (RISC vs CISC, pipelining depth, cache size) is ultimately a fight to reduce one of the three multiplicative factors.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the single most important visual for KTU 2024 short-answer questions. Memorize the column headers and 3–4 distinguishing rows.

| Architectural Feature | RISC | CISC |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Set Size** | Small (typically **≤ 128** instructions) | Large (often **> 200** instructions) |
| **Instruction Length** | **Fixed** (usually 32 bits) | **Variable** (8 to 128 bits typical) |
| **Instruction Format** | Few, uniform formats | Many, irregular formats |
| **Addressing Modes** | Few (**3 to 5**) | Many (**12 to 24**) |
| **Memory Access** | Only via **Load/Store** instructions | Any instruction may access memory |
| **CPI (Clocks Per Instruction)** | **1** (designed target) | **2 to 15** (variable) |
| **Control Logic** | **Hardwired** (combinational) | **Microcoded** (firmware layer) |
| **Register File** | **Large** (≥ 32 GPRs) | **Small** (8 to 16 GPRs) |
| **Pipelining Efficiency** | **High** (uniform instructions) | **Difficult** (variable instructions) |
| **Code Density** | **Lower** (more instructions per task) | **Higher** (compact code) |
| **Compiler Dependency** | **Heavy** (compiler does optimization) | **Lighter** (hardware helps) |
| **Clock Frequency** | **High** (short cycle) | **Lower** (longer cycle) |
| **Power Consumption** | **Lower** per instruction | **Higher** per instruction |
| **Example Processors** | ARM, RISC-V, MIPS, AVR | Intel x86, AMD64, Motorola 68k |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Modern high-performance x86 CPUs (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen) are *internally RISC*. They decode incoming CISC x86 instructions into **micro-operations (μops)** — which are essentially RISC-like simple operations — and execute them on a RISC-style out-of-order pipeline. This is called **"CISC outside, RISC inside"**, a hybrid approach that gives compatibility with rich legacy code *and* modern pipeline performance.

## 2.5 Why RISC Won the Embedded World

For **microcontrollers and embedded systems** (the focus of KTU's PBRUT404 course), RISC dominates for four reasons:

- **Deterministic real-time behavior:** Fixed instruction length → predictable interrupt latency, crucial for RTOS-based designs.
- **Lower power per operation:** Critical for battery-powered IoT devices.
- **Smaller silicon area:** Leaves room for on-chip peripherals, Flash, ADC, etc.
- **Compiler-friendly:** Modern GCC/LLVM optimizations target RISC ISAs aggressively.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — CPU Time Comparison (RISC vs CISC)

> [!NOTE]
