> The **CPU** is the manager. The **ROM** is the *unchangeable policy book* the manager reads instructions from. The **RAM** is the *scratchpad* on his desk. The **ports** are the *doors* through which the factory talks to the outside world.

In short: **microcontroller = computer-on-a-chip**. Unlike a microprocessor which needs *external* memory and peripherals, the 8051 has *everything baked in* — this is the central "embedded" idea.

## 1.3 Key Architectural Metrics (Board Exam Favorites)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Numerical Facts You MUST Memorize:**
> - **Data bus width:** **8 bits**
> - **Address bus width (external):** **16 bits** → can address **2¹⁶ = 65,536 (64 KB)** locations
> - **On-chip program memory:** **4 KB ROM** (0000H to 0FFFH)
> - **On-chip data memory:** **128 bytes RAM** (00H to 7FH)
> - **Number of I/O pins:** **32** (organized as 4 ports × 8 pins = P0, P1, P2, P3)
> - **Number of registers:** **Register banks × 8 = 32** (4 banks of 8 registers R0–R7)
> - **Timers:** **2 × 16-bit** (Timer 0, Timer 1)
> - **Serial port:** **1 × full-duplex UART**
> - **Interrupt sources:** **5** (2 external, 2 timer, 1 serial)
> - **Operating frequency:** up to **12 MHz**
> - **Instruction cycle:** **12 oscillator periods = 1 machine cycle**

## 1.4 Why the 8051? (Syllabus Justification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8051 remains the **de-facto teaching vehicle** worldwide for embedded systems because it exposes students to *every* fundamental concept: registers, memory mapping, I/O, interrupts, timers, and serial communication — without the abstraction layers of modern ARM/Cortex chips.

The 8051 belongs to the **8-bit MCU family**, meaning all internal data movement and ALU operations occur on **8 bits at a time**, although the **PC (Program Counter)** and **DPTR** are 16-bit registers to enable 64 KB addressability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Block Architecture — Functional Decomposition

The 8051 chip is composed of **nine major functional blocks**. Understanding each is mandatory for KTU Module 1 questions.

### A. Central Processing Unit (CPU)
- Contains the **8-bit ALU** (Arithmetic Logic Unit).
- Performs arithmetic: ADD, ADDC, SUBB, MUL, DIV.
- Performs logic: ANL, ORL, XRL, CPL, CLR.
- Performs bit-level operations via the **Boolean processor** — a unique 8051 feature.

### B. Program Memory (ROM — 4 KB On-chip)
- Stores the **executable code** (machine instructions).
- 16-bit **Program Counter (PC)** addresses program memory.
- Address range: **0000H to 0FFFH** (on-chip); can extend to **FFFFH** (64 KB) externally.
- **Key Vector Addresses (MUST memorize):**
  - **0000H** → Reset vector
  - **0003H** → External Interrupt 0 (INT0)
  - **000BH** → Timer 0 Overflow
  - **0013H** → External Interrupt 1 (INT1)
  - **001BH** → Timer 1 Overflow
  - **0023H** → Serial Port Interrupt

### C. Data Memory (RAM — 128 Bytes)
- **Lower 128 bytes (00H–7FH):** General-purpose RAM
  - **00H–1FH:** 4 Register Banks × 8 bytes (R0–R7 each)
  - **20H–2FH:** 16 bytes bit-addressable (128 bits)
  - **30H–7FH:** General scratchpad (80 bytes)
- **Upper 128 bytes (80H–FFH):** Special Function Registers (SFRs)

### D. Special Function Registers (SFRs) — The "Control Panel"
> [!IMPORTANT]
> SFRs are the **address-mapped control registers** that govern every peripheral. They occupy **80H–FFH**, but only some addresses are used (others are reserved/holes).

**Key SFRs (board exam essentials):**

| SFR Name | Address | Function |
|----------|---------|----------|
| **ACC (A)** | **E0H** | Accumulator — primary ALU operand |
| **B** | **F0H** | Multiplication/division auxiliary |
| **PSW** | **D0H** | Program Status Word — flags |
| **SP** | **81H** | Stack Pointer |
| **DPL** | **82H** | DPTR low byte |
| **DPH** | **83H** | DPTR high byte |
| **P0–P3** | **80H, 90H, A0H, B0H** | Port latches |
| **TCON** | **88H** | Timer/Counter control |
| **TMOD** | **89H** | Timer/Counter mode |
| **SCON** | **98H** | Serial control |
| **SBUF** | **99H** | Serial data buffer |
| **IE** | **A8H** | Interrupt enable |
| **IP** | **B8H** | Interrupt priority |
| **PCON** | **87H** | Power control |

### E. I/O Ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)
- **P0:** 8 bits, open-drain — used as multiplexed **lower address/data bus (AD0–AD7)** when external memory is connected. Needs **external pull-up resistors (≈10 kΩ)**.
- **P1:** 8 bits, quasi-bidirectional, internal pull-up — **general-purpose I/O only**.
- **P2:** 8 bits, quasi-bidirectional — provides **higher address byte (A8–A15)** for external memory.
- **P3:** 8 bits, quasi-bidirectional — **alternate functions** (RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD).

### F. Timers/Counters
- **Timer 0 (TH0/TL0)** and **Timer 1 (TH1/TL1)** — both 16-bit.
- Used for **time delay generation**, **event counting**, or **baud-rate generation**.
- Controlled by **TMOD** (mode) and **TCON** (control).

### G. Serial Port (UART)
- **Full-duplex** — can transmit and receive simultaneously.
- Operates in **4 modes** (Mode 1 is most common: 8-bit UART, variable baud).
- **SBUF** is a single buffer address (99H) shared by TX and RX (write for TX, read for RX).

### H. Interrupt Controller
- **5 sources:** INT0, Timer 0, INT1, Timer 1, Serial port.
- **2 priority levels** (set by IP register).
- **Enable/Disable** controlled by IE register.

### I. Oscillator & Clock Circuit
- External **crystal** across **XTAL1 (pin 19) and XTAL2 (pin 18)**.
- Internal oscillator generates clock; **machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **must-know equations** for the 8051 — frequently tested in 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{osc} = \dfrac{1}{f_{osc}}$ | One oscillator period | seconds |
| 2 | $T_{mc} = 12 \times T_{osc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | One machine cycle duration | seconds |
| 3 | $f_{mc} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | Machine cycle frequency | Hz |
| 4 | $N_{addr} = 2^n$ | Number of addressable locations for n-bit bus | locations |
| 5 | $N_{ext} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64 \text{ KB}$ | Max external memory (16-bit address) | bytes |
| 6 | $N_{RAM} = 128 \text{ bytes}$ | On-chip data memory | bytes |
| 7 | $N_{ROM} = 4 \text{ KB} = 4096 \text{ bytes}$ | On-chip program memory | bytes |
| 8 | $\text{Delay} = \text{Count} \times T_{mc} \times 12$ | Timer-based delay (Mode 1) | seconds |
| 9 | $\text{TH-TL} = 65{,}536 - (\text{Delay} \times f_{osc}/12)$ | Timer reload value | decimal |
| 10 | $\text{Baud Rate (Mode 1)} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | UART baud rate | bps |
| 11 | $\text{Vector spacing} = 8 \text{ bytes}$ | Between consecutive ISRs | bytes |
| 12 | $N_{bit} = 128 \text{ bits}$ | Bit-addressable RAM (20H–2FH) | bits |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU boards, **failing to multiply by 12** (forgetting the 12-oscillator-cycle per machine-cycle rule) is the **#1 reason students lose marks** in timer/baud-rate numericals.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 8051's architecture is the **conceptual template** behind **billions of embedded devices**: washing machines, microwave ovens, automotive dashboards, industrial PLCs, smart meters, and consumer electronics. The **Boolean processor** (single-bit operations) makes it especially suitable for **industrial control** (relay driving, switch sensing, real-time control logic), and the **Harvard architecture** (separate code/data buses) gives it deterministic, real-time response — a property modern ARM Cortex-M chips deliberately inherit.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Addressable Memory Using 16-bit Bus

**Problem:** Show that the 8051 can address exactly **65,536 bytes** of external memory.

**Step 1 — Identify the address bus width.**
The Program Counter (PC) and Data Pointer (DPTR) are both **16-bit** registers in the 8051.

$$n = 16 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the binary addressing formula.**

$$N = 2^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$N = 2^{16}$$

**Step 4 — Expand using $2^{10} = 1024$.**

$$N = 2^{6} \times 2^{10} = 64 \times 1024$$

**Step 5 — Convert to KB.**

$$N = 64 \text{ KB} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes}$$

**Conclusion:** The 8051 can address a total of **64 KB of program memory** and **64 KB of data memory** (separate buses — Harvard), giving a combined addressable space of **128 KB**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Machine Cycle Time at 12 MHz Crystal

**Problem:** Compute the duration of one machine cycle for an 8051 operating with a 12 MHz crystal.

**Step 1 — Recall the machine cycle rule.**
In the 8051, **one machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**.

$$T_{mc} = 12 \times T_{osc}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the oscillator period.**

$$T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{1}{12 \times 10^{6}} \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{osc} = 83.33 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by 12.**

$$T_{mc} = 12 \times 83.33 \text{ ns} = 1000 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{mc} = 1 \text{ µs}$$

**Conclusion:** At **12 MHz**, every machine cycle takes exactly **1 µs** — a beautifully clean number that simplifies all delay calculations. This is why **12 MHz is the most-used crystal** in 8051 academic labs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Timer Reload Value for a 1 ms Delay (Mode 1, 12 MHz)

**Problem:** Compute the value to be loaded into TH0/TL0 to generate a 1 ms delay using Timer 0 in Mode 1 (16-bit) at $f_{osc} = 12 \text{ MHz}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the count needed.**
Since each machine cycle is **1 µs** at 12 MHz, for 1 ms delay:

$$\text{Count} = \frac{\text{Delay}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{1 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ µs}} = 1000 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the timer initial value.**

$$\text{Timer Value} = 2^{16} - \text{Count} = 65{,}536 - 1000$$

$$\text{Timer Value} = 64{,}536$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hex.**

$$64{,}536 \div 16 = 4033 \text{ remainder } 8$$

$$64{,}536 = \text{FC18H}$$

**Step 4 — Split into TH0 and TL0.**

$$\text{TH0} = \text{FCH} = 252$$

$$\text{TL0} = \text{18H} = 24$$

**Verification:**

$$\text{Reload} = 65{,}536 - 64{,}536 = 1000 \text{ counts}$$

$$1000 \times 1 \text{ µs} = 1000 \text{ µs} = 1 \text{ ms} \;\; \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Baud Rate for 9600 bps, 11.0592 MHz Crystal, Timer 1 Mode 2

**Problem:** Find TH1 to generate 9600 bps UART using Timer 1 in Mode 2 (auto-reload), $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$, SMOD = 0.

**Step 1 — Use the baud-rate formula (Mode 1 UART with Timer 1 overflow):**

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:** SMOD = 0 → $2^{0} = 1$.

$$9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $(256 - \text{TH1})$:**

$$9600 \times 32 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

$$307{,}200 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$12 \times (256 - \text{TH1}) = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{307{,}200} = 36$$

**Step 5 — Solve for TH1:**

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{36}{12} = 3$$

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FDH}$$

**Conclusion:** With **TH1 = FDH** and crystal **11.0592 MHz**, the UART generates exactly **9600 bps** with **0% error** — this is the standard lab configuration.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Memory Map Summary

| Address Range | Type | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0000H–0FFFH | On-chip ROM | 4 KB | User program (internal) |
| 1000H–FFFFH | External ROM | 60 KB | External program memory |
| 00H–1FH | Internal RAM | 32 B | 4 Register Banks (R0–R7) |
| 20H–2FH | Internal RAM | 16 B | Bit-addressable (128 bits) |
| 30H–7FH | Internal RAM | 80 B | General-purpose scratchpad |
| 80H–FFH | SFR Space | 128 B | Special Function Registers |
| 0000H–00FFH | External RAM (via MOVX) | 256 B | External data memory page |
| 0000H–FFFFH | External RAM (max) | 64 KB | Full external data memory |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Internal Block Diagram (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU ALU 8-BIT WITH BOOLEAN PROCESSOR"]
    PC["PROGRAM COUNTER 16-BIT"]
    ROM["ON CHIP ROM 4 KB PROGRAM MEMORY"]
    RAM["ON CHIP RAM 128 BYTES DATA MEMORY"]
    SFR["SFR BLOCK 80H TO FFH"]
    P0["PORT 0 AD0 AD7 OPEN DRAIN"]
    P1["PORT 1 GENERAL PURPOSE IO"]
    P2["PORT 2 A8 A15 ADDRESS HIGH"]
    P3["PORT 3 ALTERNATE FUNCTIONS RXD TXD INT T0 T1 WR RD"]
    TMR["TIMER 0 AND TIMER 1 16 BIT"]
    SIO["SERIAL PORT UART FULL DUPLEX"]
    INT["INTERRUPT CONTROLLER 5 SOURCES"]
    OSC["ON CHIP OSCILLATOR XTAL1 XTAL2"]
    BUS_INTERNAL["INTERNAL DATA BUS 8 BIT"]
    BUS_ADDR["INTERNAL ADDRESS BUS 16 BIT"]

    OSC --> CPU
    CPU --> BUS_INTERNAL
    CPU --> BUS_ADDR
    BUS_ADDR --> ROM
    BUS_ADDR --> RAM
    BUS_ADDR --> SFR
    BUS_INTERNAL --> P0
    BUS_INTERNAL --> P1
    BUS_INTERNAL --> P2
    BUS_INTERNAL --> P3
    BUS_INTERNAL --> TMR
    BUS_INTERNAL --> SIO
    BUS_INTERNAL --> INT
    PC --> BUS_ADDR
    INT --> CPU
    TMR --> INT
    SIO --> INT
```

## 4.2 8051 Memory Organization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MEM["8051 MEMORY SPACE"] --> PM["PROGRAM MEMORY 64 KB"]
    MEM --> DM["DATA MEMORY"]

    PM --> IROM["INTERNAL ROM 0000H 0FFFH 4 KB"]
    PM --> XROM["EXTERNAL ROM 1000H FFFFH 60 KB"]
    IROM --> V0["0000H RESET VECTOR"]
    IROM --> V1["0003H INT0 VECTOR"]
    IROM --> V2["000BH TIMER 0 VECTOR"]
    IROM --> V3["0013H INT1 VECTOR"]
    IROM --> V4["001BH TIMER 1 VECTOR"]
    IROM --> V5["0023H SERIAL VECTOR"]

    DM --> IRAM["INTERNAL RAM 00H 7FH 128 B"]
    DM --> XRAM["EXTERNAL RAM 0000H FFFFH 64 KB"]
    DM --> SFRS["SFR 80H FFH 128 B"]

    IRAM --> RB["REGISTER BANKS 00H 1FH 4 X 8 BYTES"]
    IRAM --> BA["BIT ADDRESSABLE 20H 2FH 16 B 128 BITS"]
    IRAM --> GP["GENERAL PURPOSE 30H 7FH 80 B"]
```

## 4.3 Pin Diagram Summary Table (40-Pin DIP)

| Pin Group | Pin Numbers | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **P1.0–P1.7** | 1–8 | Port 1 (8-bit quasi-bidirectional I/O) |
| **RST** | 9 | Reset input (active HIGH, min 2 machine cycles) |
| **P3.0–P3.7** | 10–17 | Port 3 (with alt functions: RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD) |
| **XTAL2, XTAL1** | 18, 19 | Crystal oscillator connections |
| **GND** | 20 | Ground (0 V) |
| **P2.0–P2.7** | 21–28 | Port 2 (high address byte A8–A15) |
| **PSEN** | 29 | Program Store Enable (external ROM read strobe) |
| **ALE/PROG** | 30 | Address Latch Enable / EPROM programming pulse |
| **EA/VPP** | 31 | External Access (LOW = use external ROM) / EPROM VPP |
| **P0.0–P0.7** | 32–39 | Port 0 (multiplexed AD0–AD7) |
| **VCC** | 40 | +5 V supply |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**List the features of 8051 microcontroller. Mention the size of on-chip data and program memory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1.5 marks per sub-part):**

**Features of 8051:**
1. **8-bit CPU** with **Boolean processor** for single-bit operations.
2. **On-chip oscillator** and **clock circuit**.
3. **4 KB on-chip ROM** (program memory) — addresses **0000H to 0FFFH**.
4. **128 bytes on-chip RAM** (data memory) — addresses **00H to 7FH**.
5. **Four 8-bit I/O ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3) — total **32 I/O lines**.
6. **Two 16-bit timers/counters** (Timer 0, Timer 1).
7. **Full-duplex UART** for serial communication.
8. **5 interrupt sources** with 2 priority levels.
9. **64 KB external program** and **64 KB external data** memory addressability.

> **Memory Sizes (1 mark for both):**
> - **On-chip data memory (RAM) = 128 bytes** (00H–7FH)
> - **On-chip program memory (ROM) = 4 KB** (0000H–0FFFH)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Explain the role of the Program Status Word (PSW) register in 8051.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **PSW (Program Status Word)** is an **8-bit SFR** at address **D0H** that contains the **CPU flag bits** reflecting the result of the most recent arithmetic/logic operation. It is **bit-addressable**.

| Bit | Name | Address | Function |
|-----|------|---------|----------|
| PSW.7 | **CY (Carry Flag)** | D7H | Set on carry-out from bit 7 |
| PSW.6 | **AC (Auxiliary Carry)** | D6H | Set on carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (BCD) |
| PSW.5 | **F0 (User Flag 0)** | D5H | General-purpose user flag |
| PSW.4 & PSW.3 | **RS1, RS0** | D4H, D3H | **Register bank select** (00=Bank 0, 01=Bank 1, 10=Bank 2, 11=Bank 3) |
| PSW.2 | **OV (Overflow)** | D2H | Set on signed arithmetic overflow |
| PSW.1 | **— (Reserved)** | D1H | Not used |
| PSW.0 | **P (Parity)** | D0H | Set if ACC has odd number of 1s (even parity) |

**Significance:** The **CY, AC, OV, and P** flags are *hardware-updated*; the **RS0/RS1** bits are *software-controlled* and decide which register bank is currently active. (1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for table, 1 mark for significance)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Module 1 — Internal Choice Pattern:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from this module.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the **internal block diagram of 8051** and explain the function of each block in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **memory organization of 8051** with a neat diagram. Differentiate between Harvard and Von-Neumann architectures.

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Internal Block Diagram [7 marks]:**

Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes. The 8051 consists of:

1. **CPU with ALU (8-bit):** Performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷), logic (AND, OR, XOR), and bit operations via the Boolean processor. (1.5 marks)
2. **Program Counter (PC, 16-bit):** Holds the address of the next instruction to fetch. Auto-increments after each fetch. (0.5 mark)
3. **On-chip ROM (4 KB):** Stores executable code from **0000H to 0FFFH**. Can be masked-ROM (8051), EPROM (8751), or Flash (8951). (1 mark)
4. **On-chip RAM (128 B):** Lower 128 bytes (00H–7FH) for general use — register banks, bit-addressable area, scratchpad. (1 mark)
5. **SFRs (80H–FFH):** Control registers for ports, timers, serial port, interrupts, and CPU flags. (0.5 mark)
6. **I/O Ports (P0–P3):** Four 8-bit ports provide 32 I/O lines. P0 is open-drain (needs pull-ups); P1, P2, P3 have internal pull-ups. P3 has alternate functions. (1 mark)
7. **Timers/Counters:** Two 16-bit timers for delays, event counting, baud-rate generation. (0.5 mark)
8. **Serial Port (UART):** Full-duplex, four modes. (0.5 mark)
9. **Interrupt Controller:** 5 sources, 2 priority levels. (0.5 mark)
10. **Oscillator Circuit:** External crystal + on-chip inverter generates system clock. (0.5 mark)

**(b) Memory Organization [7 marks]:**

| Region | Address | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Internal ROM | 0000H–0FFFH | 4 KB | On-chip program memory |
| External ROM | 1000H–FFFFH | 60 KB | External program memory |
| Internal RAM | 00H–7FH | 128 B | 4 register banks (00H–1FH), bit area (20H–2FH), scratch (30H–7FH) |
| SFRs | 80H–FFH | 128 B | Control/status registers |
| External RAM | 0000H–FFFFH | 64 KB | Accessed via MOVX instruction |

**Harvard vs Von-Neumann [2 marks]:**

| Feature | Harvard | Von-Neumann |
|---|---|---|
| **Bus structure** | **Separate** code and data buses | **Common** bus for code and data |
| **Speed** | **Faster** (parallel fetch + access) | **Slower** (bottleneck) |
| **Architecture used** | **8051, PIC, ARM Cortex** | **8085, 8086, general PC CPUs** |
| **Memory access** | Simultaneous code fetch + data read/write | Sequential |
| **Pin count** | Higher (more bus pins) | Lower |

The 8051 uses **Harvard architecture** — this allows the CPU to fetch the next instruction from ROM while simultaneously reading/writing data in RAM, doubling throughput at the cost of more complex internal routing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **register set of 8051** with their functions. List the **Special Function Registers** and their addresses.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **pin configuration of 8051 (40-pin DIP)**. Mention the alternate functions of Port 3.

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Register Set and SFRs [7 marks]:**

**1. General Purpose Registers (4 banks × 8 = 32 registers):**
The 8051 has **4 register banks** of 8 registers each (R0–R7), located at **00H–1FH**. Only **one bank is active** at a time, selected by **RS0–RS1 bits in PSW**. The default bank (after reset) is **Bank 0 (00H–07H)**.

**2. CPU Registers:**
- **Accumulator (A, ACC) — E0H:** Used as the primary operand in most ALU operations. **(1 mark)**
- **B Register — F0H:** Used in MUL AB and DIV AB instructions. Holds the high byte after multiplication, remainder after division. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Program Counter (PC) — 16-bit:** Holds address of next instruction. **Not part of SFR space** (internal only). After reset, PC = 0000H. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Data Pointer (DPTR = DPH + DPL) — 83H + 82H:** 16-bit register used to access external data memory (via MOVX) and look-up tables (via MOVC). **(0.5 mark)**
- **Stack Pointer (SP) — 81H:** 8-bit pointer to RAM stack. **Initialized to 07H after reset**; stack grows upward. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Program Status Word (PSW) — D0H:** Flags (CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, P). **(0.5 mark)**

**3. Key SFRs (1.5 marks):**
| SFR | Address | SFR | Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| P0 | 80H | TCON | 88H |
| SP | 81H | TMOD | 89H |
| DPL | 82H | SCON | 98H |
| DPH | 83H | SBUF | 99H |
| PCON | 87H | IE | A8H |
| P1 | 90H | P3 | B0H |
| P2 | A0H | IP | B8H |
| ACC | E0H | B | F0H |
| PSW | D0H | | |

**(b) Pin Configuration [7 marks]:**

Refer to the **Pin Diagram Table in Section 4.3** of these notes for the full mapping.

**Detailed Pin Groups [5 marks]:**

1. **Pins 1–8: Port 1 (P1.0–P1.7)** — Quasi-bidirectional I/O with internal pull-ups. No alternate functions.

2. **Pin 9: RST** — Reset input. Must receive a HIGH for at least **2 machine cycles** to reset. Resets PC = 0000H, SP = 07H, clears all SFRs (P0–P3 = FFH), sets ports to input mode.

3. **Pins 10–17: Port 3 (P3.0–P3.7)** — Quasi-bidirectional I/O with alternate functions. **(Listed below)**

4. **Pins 18, 19: XTAL2, XTAL1** — Connect external crystal (typically 11.0592 MHz or 12 MHz). Two 30 pF capacitors to ground complete the oscillator.

5. **Pin 20: GND** — Ground reference (0 V).

6. **Pins 21–28: Port 2 (P2.0–P2.7)** — Quasi-bidirectional. Provides **upper address byte A8–A15** when external memory (>4 KB) is used.

7. **Pin 29: PSEN (Program Store Enable)** — Active LOW output strobe to read external ROM.

8. **Pin 30: ALE/PROG** — Address Latch Enable pulse to demultiplex P0's AD0–AD7 into 74HC373 latch. Also used as programming pulse for EPROM variants.

9. **Pin 31: EA/VPP** — External Access. **Tied LOW** to use external ROM; **tied HIGH** to use internal ROM (when ≤ 4 KB needed). Also receives VPP (12.75 V) during EPROM programming.

10. **Pins 32–39: Port 0 (P0.0–P0.7)** — Open-drain (no internal pull-up). Used as multiplexed **AD0–AD7 (lower address + data bus)**. Requires **10 kΩ external pull-ups** when used as I/O.

11. **Pin 40: VCC** — +5 V power supply.

**Alternate Functions of Port 3 [2 marks]:**

| Pin | Port 3 Bit | Alternate Function | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | P3.0 | **RXD** | Serial input (UART receive) |
| 11 | P3.1 | **TXD** | Serial output (UART transmit) |
| 12 | P3.2 | **INT0** | External Interrupt 0 (active LOW) |
| 13 | P3.3 | **INT1** | External Interrupt 1 (active LOW) |
| 14 | P3.4 | **T0** | Timer 0 external input |
| 15 | P3.5 | **T1** | Timer 1 external input |
| 16 | P3.6 | **WR** | External RAM write strobe (active LOW) |
| 17 | P3.7 | **RD** | External RAM read strobe (active LOW) |

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on 8051 Architecture Questions (Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 12-oscillator-per-machine-cycle rule** — Always state $T_{mc} = 12/f_{osc}$ explicitly. Marks are awarded for this factor.
> 2. **Confusing internal and external memory** — Internal RAM is **128 bytes**; internal ROM is **4 KB**; **external** memory can be **64 KB each** for code and data. Don't mix them up.
> 3. **Wrong SP initial value** — After reset, **SP = 07H** (NOT 00H). The first PUSH writes to address 08H.
> 4. **SFR addresses in upper 128 bytes of RAM** — SFRs are **NOT** in the 00H–7FH range. They occupy **80H–FFH** and must be addressed with direct addressing.
> 5. **P0 vs P1/P2/P3 confusion** — P0 is **open-drain** (needs pull-ups); P1, P2, P3 are **quasi-bidirectional** (internal pull-up). Saying "all four ports are the same" will cost 1 mark minimum.

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — 8051 Architecture):**
>
> - ✅ 8051 is an **8-bit Harvard-architecture CISC microcontroller** with on-chip **CPU + ROM + RAM + I/O + Timers + UART + Interrupts**.
> - ✅ **4 KB on-chip ROM** (0000H–0FFFH); **128 B on-chip RAM** (00H–7FH).
> - ✅ **16-bit PC and DPTR** enable **64 KB external memory** each (program + data).
> - ✅ **4 I/O ports** = **32 pins**; P0 is open-drain, P1/P2/P3 are quasi-bidirectional.
> - ✅ **P3 has alternate functions** — RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD.
> - ✅ **SFRs occupy 80H–FFH** (upper 128 B of internal RAM address space) and are bit-addressable for select registers.
> - ✅ **5 interrupt sources**, **2 priority levels**, controlled by **IE** and **IP**.
> - ✅ **2 timers**, 16-bit, **4 operating modes** (controlled by TMOD).
> - ✅ **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods** → $T_{mc} = 12/f_{osc}$.
> - ✅ **Reset vector = 0000H**; interrupt vectors at 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H (8 bytes apart).
> - ✅ **Default register bank** after reset = **Bank 0** (R0–R7 at 00H–07H).
> - ✅ **SP initializes to 07H** after reset.
> - ✅ **Harvard architecture** = separate code/data buses → faster, real-time friendly.
> - ✅ **Boolean processor** enables single-bit operations directly on port pins (SETB, CLR, ANL C, ORL C).
> - ✅ **EA pin** = 0 → use external ROM; **EA pin** = 1 → use internal ROM (≤ 4 KB).
> - ✅ **ALE** demultiplexes address from data on P0 using an external latch (74HC373).
> - ✅ **PSEN** strobes external ROM; **RD/WR** strobe external RAM (via MOVX).
> - ✅ **Standard crystal for UART = 11.0592 MHz** (gives 0% baud error); **standard crystal for delay calculations = 12 MHz** (gives 1 µs/machine cycle).
> - ✅ **MUL AB** → result in B:A (16-bit); **DIV AB** → quotient in A, remainder in B.

### 🎯 Memory Aid — The "4-2-5-2" Mnemonic for 8051

> **4 KB ROM · 2 Timers · 5 Interrupts · 2 Priority Levels · 2 Serial Modes (common)**

Master these and Module 1 is yours. Good luck on the KTU board exam! 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Register Organization - Special Function Registers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Register Organization - Special Function Registers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** are dedicated, addressable 8-bit (and 16-bit composite) hardware registers physically embedded inside the **8051 Microcontroller's CPU core and integrated peripherals**. They occupy the **upper 128 bytes of the internal RAM (addresses 0x80 to 0xFF)** and are accessed only through **direct addressing mode**. SFRs control the behaviour of on-chip hardware such as I/O ports, timers, serial port, interrupts, and the arithmetic logic unit. The 8051 microcontroller has **128 bytes of on-chip RAM** (lower RAM: 0x00–0x7F) and a **separate Special Function Register space of 128 bytes** (0x80–0xFF). The total addressable memory map appears as a unified 256-byte space in direct addressing, but these two regions are physically distinct and are accessed through different internal pathways. > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Definition to memorize:** > *"SFRs are the control and status interface between the CPU and the on-chip peripherals of the 8051 microcontroller. They are mapped into the internal data memory space from addresses 80H to FFH and must be accessed using direct addressing."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Control Panel of a Factory Machine" Imagine a large industrial CNC machine. The machine has many internal parts — motors, sensors, coolant valves, conveyor belts, and emergency stops. As the floor operator, you **cannot physically open the machine** to turn a gear or press a micro-switch every time. Instead, you are given a **dedicated control panel** with labelled buttons, switches, dials, and indicator lights. - Each **button on the panel** = an SFR (e.g., `TCON` for timer control). - Each **indicator light** = a flag bit inside an SFR (e.g., `TF0` — Timer 0 Overflow flag). - The **panel itself** = the SFR memory space (0x80 – 0xFF). - The **operator** = your C/Assembly program writing to those registers. Just like an operator must know *which switch does what* and *when to press it*, a 8051 programmer must know the **address, name, bit-layout, and function** of every SFR. The 8051 datasheet is essentially the "user manual" of this control panel. ### 1.3 The 8051 Register Organization — Broad Classification The 8051 register file is divided into two major categories: | Category | Address Range | Count | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **General Purpose Registers (GPRs)** | 0x00 – 0x1F | 32 bytes (4 banks × 8) | Scratchpad data storage, R0–R7 | | **Bit-Addressable Area** | 0x20 – 0x2F | 16 bytes = 128 bits | Single-bit boolean flags | | **General Purpose RAM (Scratchpad)** | 0x30 – 0x7F | 80 bytes | User data, stack, variables | | **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** | 0x80 – 0xFF | Up to 128 addresses (21 active) | CPU + peripheral control/status | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Fact:** Out of the **128 possible SFR addresses**, only **21 are physically implemented** in the standard 8051. The remaining 107 addresses (0x80–0xFF excluding implemented ones) are **reserved** and accessing them yields undefined behaviour. ### 1.4 Why SFRs Matter — The "Why" Behind Learning This Without SFRs, the 8051 would be a useless silicon die. They are the **only way** the CPU can: 1. Read from or write to physical I/O pins. 2. Configure and start/stop timers. 3. Transmit/receive serial data. 4. Enable/disable hardware interrupts and set their priorities. 5. Switch between register banks. 6. Manage power-saving modes. 7. Perform arithmetic via the Accumulator and B register. Every single line of meaningful 8051 code eventually manipulates one or more SFRs. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Complete SFR Address Map (Direct Addressing — KTU 2024 High-Yield) The 21 implemented SFRs of the standard 8051, listed by their canonical addresses, are shown below. Bit-addressable SFRs (those whose internal address ends in `0` or `8`) can be manipulated using single-bit instructions like `SETB` and `CLR`. | SFR Name | Address (Hex) | Bit-Addressable? | Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **P0** | 0x80 | YES | Port 0 latch (lower 8 bits of Address/Data bus in external mode) | | **SP** | 0x81 | NO | Stack Pointer (resets to 0x07) | | **DPL** | 0x82 | NO | Data Pointer Low byte | | **DPH** | 0x83 | NO | Data Pointer High byte | | **PCON** | 0x87 | NO | Power Control Register | | **TCON** | 0x88 | YES | Timer/Counter Control | | **TMOD** | 0x89 | NO | Timer/Counter Mode | | **TL0** | 0x8A | NO | Timer 0 Low byte | | **TL1** | 0x8B | NO | Timer 1 Low byte | | **TH0** | 0x8C | NO | Timer 0 High byte | | **TH1** | 0x8D | NO | Timer 1 High byte | | **P1** | 0x90 | YES | Port 1 latch (general purpose quasi-bidirectional I/O) | | **SCON** | 0x98 | YES | Serial Port Control | | **SBUF** | 0x99 | NO | Serial Data Buffer (TX and RX share one address) | | **P2** | 0xA0 | YES | Port 2 latch (upper 8 bits of external address bus) | | **IE** | 0xA8 | YES | Interrupt Enable register | | **P3** | 0xB0 | YES | Port 3 latch (alternate functions: INT, T, R, WR, RD) | | **IP** | 0xB8 | YES | Interrupt Priority register | | **PSW** | 0xD0 | YES | Program Status Word (flags + register bank select) | | **A / ACC** | 0xE0 | YES | Accumulator (primary ALU operand) | | **B** | 0xF0 | YES | B register (used in MUL/DIV and as scratch) | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** The SFRs end at **0xF0 (B register)**. The total implemented SFR count is **21** for the classic 8051. Modern variants (8052) add a few more (e.g., T2CON at 0xC8). ### 2.2 Detailed Bit-Level Breakdown of Critical SFRs #### 2.2.1 Program Status Word (PSW) — Address 0xD0 (Bit-Addressable) The PSW is the CPU's "dashboard" — it shows the result of the last arithmetic/logic operation and selects the active register bank. | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Name** | **CY** | **AC** | **F0** | **RS1** | **RS0** | **OV** | **—** | **P** | | **Function** | Carry Flag | Aux. Carry | User Flag 0 | Register Bank Select 1 | Register Bank Select 0 | Overflow | Reserved | Parity (auto) | **Register Bank Selection Logic:** | RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | Address Range (R0–R7) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** | 0x00 – 0x07 | | 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | 0x08 – 0x0F | | 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | 0x10 – 0x17 | | 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | 0x18 – 0x1F | #### 2.2.2 Timer/Counter Control Register (TCON) — 0x88 (Bit-Addressable) | Bit | Name | Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 7 | **TF1** | Timer 1 Overflow Flag (set by HW, cleared by SW) | | 6 | **TR1** | Timer 1 Run Control (1 = start) | | 5 | **TF0** | Timer 0 Overflow Flag | | 4 | **TR0** | Timer 0 Run Control | | 3 | **IE1** | External Interrupt 1 Edge Flag | | 2 | **IT1** | External Interrupt 1 Type (1 = edge, 0 = level) | | 1 | **IE0** | External Interrupt 0 Edge Flag | | 0 | **IT0** | External Interrupt 0 Type | #### 2.2.3 Timer/Counter Mode Register (TMOD) — 0x89 (NOT bit-addressable) | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Group** | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 1} | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 0} | | **Name** | **GATE** | **C/T** | **M1** | **M0** | **GATE** | **C/T** | **M1** | **M0** | **Mode Selection Truth Table:** | M1 | M0 | Mode | Operating Mode | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 0 | 0 | Mode 0 | 13-bit Timer (8048 compatibility) | | 0 | 1 | Mode 1 | 16-bit Timer | | 1 | 0 | Mode 2 | 8-bit Auto-Reload Timer | | 1 | 1 | Mode 3 | Split Timer (T0 → two 8-bit, T1 → stopped) | #### 2.2.4 Serial Port Control Register (SCON) — 0x98 (Bit-Addressable) | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Name** | **SM0** | **SM1** | **SM2** | **REN** | **TB8** | **RB8** | **TI** | **RI** | **Serial Mode Selection:** | SM0 | SM1 | Mode | Description | Baud Rate Source | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 0 | 0 | Mode 0 | 8-bit Shift Register | Fosc / 12 (fixed) | | 0 | 1 | Mode 1 | 8-bit UART | Timer 1 Overflow | | 1 | 0 | Mode 2 | 9-bit UART | Fosc / 32 or Fosc / 64 | | 1 | 1 | Mode 3 | 9-bit UART | Timer 1 Overflow | #### 2.2.5 Interrupt Enable Register (IE) — 0xA8 (Bit-Addressable) | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Name** | **EA** | **—** | **—** | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** | | **Function** | Global Enable | Reserved | Reserved | Serial Port | Timer 1 | Ext. Int 1 | Timer 0 | Ext. Int 0 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Golden Rule of 8051 Interrupts:** *No interrupt is acknowledged unless `EA = 1` (global enable).* This is a frequent 3-mark KTU question. #### 2.2.6 Port 3 Alternate Functions (P3 — 0xB0) | Port Pin | Alternate Function | SFR Bit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **P3.0** | RxD (Serial Input) | RXD | | **P3.1** | TxD (Serial Output) | TXD | | **P3.2** | External Interrupt 0 | INT0 | | **P3.3** | External Interrupt 1 | INT1 | | **P3.4** | Timer 0 External Input | T0 | | **P3.5** | Timer 1 External Input | T1 | | **P3.6** | External Data Memory Write | WR | | **P3.7** | External Data Memory Read | RD | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Engineering Quantities) > [!NOTE] > This topic is mostly architectural, but the **timer reload and baud rate formulas** are extremely high-yield in 8051 numerical questions. | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Timer count for delay $t$ (Mode 1) | $\text{Count} = 2^{16} - \dfrac{t \times f_{osc}}{12}$ | $f_{osc}$ in Hz, $t$ in seconds | | Auto-reload value (Mode 2) | $\text{TH} = 256 - \dfrac{t \times f_{osc}}{12}$ | 8-bit timer | | Timer tick frequency | $f_{tick} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | 1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods | | **Baud Rate (Mode 1, Timer 1 Auto-reload)** | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | SMOD is PCON.7 (0 or 1) | | Timer overflow rate | $f_{ov} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (2^{16} - N)}$ | For 16-bit mode | | Address of an SFR bit | $\text{bit\_addr} = 0x20 + \text{byte\_offset} \times 8 + \text{bit\_position}$ | For bits 0x20–0x2F only | | SFR bit address for 0x80–0xF8 range | $\text{bit\_addr} = \text{SFR\_addr} + \text{bit\_position}$ | Direct bit addressing | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Embedded Firmware Development:** Every peripheral driver (UART, ADC via timer, GPIO, PWM) writes to SFRs directly using `` or `<8051.h>` header in Keil/SDCC. - **Automotive ECUs:** 8051 variants (e.g., in older instrument clusters) use SFRs to drive stepper motors for gauge needles. - **Industrial Control:** SFR-based timers generate precise delays for relay sequencing, debouncing, and watchdogs. - **Consumer Electronics:** Microwave ovens, washing machines, and remote controls use SFRs for keypad scanning and seven-segment multiplexing. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — SFR Bit Address Calculation A KTU-favourite question asks: *"Find the bit address of a flag bit inside an SFR."* For SFRs with addresses ending in `0` or `8` (e.g., `0x88`, `0xD0`), each bit gets a unique address in the range `0x80` to `0xFF`, computable as: $$\text{Bit Address} = \text{Byte Address} + \text{Bit Position}$$ **Worked Example 1:** Find the bit address of `TF1` (Timer 1 Overflow Flag). **Given:** `TF1` is bit 7 of `TCON`. `TCON` address = 0x88. **Step 1 — Identify the bit position.** `TF1` is the MSB of `TCON`, so bit position = **7**. **Step 2 — Apply the formula.** $$\text{Bit Address} = 0x88 + 7 = 0x8F$$ **Step 3 — Verify against the standard bit-address map.** The 8051 hardware manual lists `TF1` at bit address `0x8F`. ✓ --- **Worked Example 2:** Find the bit address of `RS0` (Program Status Word). **Given:** `RS0` is bit 3 of `PSW`. `PSW` address = 0xD0. **Step 1 — Identify the bit position.** `RS0` = bit 3. **Step 2 — Apply the formula.** $$\text{Bit Address} = 0xD0 + 3 = 0xD3$$ **Step 3 — Verify.** The hardware reference lists `RS0` at `0xD3`. ✓ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common Mistake (KTU valuation tip):** Students often mistakenly use the bit-address map of the lower 16 bytes (0x20–0x2F) formula, which is **0x20 + byte_offset × 8 + bit_position**. That formula applies **only** to the bit-addressable general-purpose RAM, not to SFRs. ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis — Timer Delay Calculation (Mode 1, 16-bit) This is a numerical classic worth 7–14 marks in KTU ESE. **Problem:** Using an 8051 with $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz, generate a delay of **50 ms** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**. Compute the value to be loaded into `TH0:TL0`. **Step 1 — Compute the timer tick frequency.** The 8051 divides the oscillator by 12 to get the machine cycle, and increments the timer every machine cycle. $$f_{tick} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12} = 921.6 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the time period of one tick.** $$T_{tick} = \frac{1}{f_{tick}} = \frac{1}{921.6 \times 10^3} \approx 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \text{ µs}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the total number of timer counts needed for 50 ms.** $$N = \frac{\text{Required Delay}}{T_{tick}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46080 \text{ counts}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the reload value (16-bit timer counts up from the loaded value to 0xFFFF, then overflows).** The total range of a 16-bit timer is $2^{16} = 65536$ states (from 0x0000 to 0xFFFF, with overflow at the 65536th count). $$\text{Initial Count} = 2^{16} - N = 65536 - 46080 = 19456$$ **Step 5 — Convert 19456 to hexadecimal.** $$19456 \div 16 = 1216 \quad \text{remainder } 0$$ $$1216 \div 16 = 76 \quad \text{remainder } 0$$ $$76 \div 16 = 4 \quad \text{remainder } 12 \quad (C)$$ $$4 \div 16 = 0 \quad \text{remainder } 4$$ Reading remainders bottom-up: $19456 = 0x4C00$. **Step 6 — Split into TH0 and TL0.** $$\text{TH0} = 0x4C = 76_{10}$$ $$\text{TL0} = 0x00 = 0_{10}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The value $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz is **not arbitrary** — it is the exact frequency that yields clean integer baud rates (9600, 19200, etc.) with a 16-bit auto-reload Timer 1. This is why 11.0592 MHz crystals dominate 8051 boards. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SFR Reset Values Many KTU questions ask the SFR value after reset. The table below lists the reset state of the key SFRs. | SFR | Reset Value (Hex) | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **ACC** | 0x00 | Accumulator cleared | | **B** | 0x00 | B register cleared | | **PSW** | 0x00 | Bank 0 selected (RS1=RS0=0) | | **SP** | 0x07 | Stack starts at RAM address 0x07 (just above Bank 0) | | **DPTR** | 0x0000 | Data pointer reset to 0 | | **P0 – P3** | 0xFF | All port latches HIGH (quasi-bidirectional) | | **IP** | 0x00 | All interrupts at **low** priority | | **IE** | 0x00 | All interrupts **disabled** | | **TMOD** | 0x00 | All timers in Mode 0 (13-bit) and stopped | | **TCON** | 0x00 | Timer run bits cleared, edge-triggered | | **SCON** | 0x00 | Serial port Mode 0, receive disabled | | **PCON** | 0x00 | Power-on, no SMOD bit set | > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU Fact:** After reset, **P0, P1, P2, P3 are all set to 1 (0xFF)**. This is a frequently asked 3-mark conceptual question because the value `0xFF` corresponds to a logic HIGH on the port latches, not necessarily on the physical pins (which are open-drain). ### 3.4 Step-by-Step — Activating an External Interrupt **Problem:** Configure the 8051 to accept an **edge-triggered** external interrupt on **INT1 (P3.3)** and enable it. **Step 1 — Set the global enable bit (`EA`) of IE register (0xA8).** $$\text{IE} = 0x80 \quad \text{(EA = 1, all others = 0)}$$ **Step 2 — Enable the specific interrupt `EX1` (bit 2 of IE).** $$\text{IE} = \text{IE} \mid 0x04 = 0x84$$ **Step 3 — Configure INT1 for edge-triggered mode (set `IT1` = bit 2 of TCON).** $$\text{TCON} = \text{TCON} \mid 0x04 = 0x04$$ **Step 4 — Verify with assembly (Keil syntax).** ``` SETB EA ; Global enable SETB EX1 ; Enable External Interrupt 1 SETB IT1 ; Edge-triggered ``` The interrupt vector for INT1 is at program address **0x0013**. The ISR must end with `RETI`. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Internal RAM and SFR Memory Map of the 8051 The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the full 256-byte internal data memory space, showing how the lower 128 bytes of general-purpose RAM coexist with the 128-byte SFR space at the same logical address range. ```mermaid graph TD A0[Address 0x00 to 0x1F
Register Banks 0-3
32 bytes R0-R7] A1[Address 0x20 to 0x2F
Bit Addressable Area
16 bytes 128 bits] A2[Address 0x30 to 0x7F
General Purpose RAM
80 bytes scratchpad] A3[Address 0x80 to 0xFF
SFR Space
128 bytes 21 implemented] A4[Address 0x80
P0 Port Latch] A5[Address 0x81
SP Stack Pointer] A6[Address 0x82 to 0x83
DPL DPH Data Pointer] A7[Address 0x88
TCON Timer Control] A8[Address 0x89
TMOD Timer Mode] A9[Address 0x98
SCON Serial Control] A10[Address 0x99
SBUF Serial Buffer] A11[Address 0xA8
IE Interrupt Enable] A12[Address 0xB0
P3 Port 3] A13[Address 0xB8
IP Interrupt Priority] A14[Address 0xD0
PSW Program Status] A15[Address 0xE0
ACC Accumulator] A16[Address 0xF0
B Register] A3 --> A4 A3 --> A5 A3 --> A6 A3 --> A7 A3 --> A8 A3 --> A9 A3 --> A10 A3 --> A11 A3 --> A12 A3 --> A13 A3 --> A14 A3 --> A15 A3 --> A16 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SFR Interaction The diagram below shows how the CPU, ALU, and peripherals exchange data through the SFR bus. ```mermaid graph LR CPU[CPU Core
Instruction Decoder] ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit] RAM[Internal RAM 128 bytes
0x00 to 0x7F] SFRBUS[SFR Bus 0x80 to 0xFF] ACC_SFR[ACC 0xE0
Accumulator] B_SFR[B 0xF0
B Register] PSW_SFR[PSW 0xD0
Flags and Bank Select] PORT0[P0 0x80
I/O Latch] PORT1[P1 0x90
I/O Latch] PORT2[P2 0xA0
I/O Latch] PORT3[P3 0xB0
I/O with Alt Func] TIMER[Timer SFRs
TMOD TCON TH TL] SERIAL[Serial SFRs
SCON SBUF] INT[Interrupt SFRs
IE IP] PWR[Power Control
PCON 0x87] CPU --> ALU ALU --> ACC_SFR ACC_SFR --> ALU ALU --> B_SFR ALU --> PSW_SFR CPU --> SFRBUS SFRBUS --> PORT0 SFRBUS --> PORT1 SFRBUS --> PORT2 SFRBUS --> PORT3 SFRBUS --> TIMER SFRBUS --> SERIAL SFRBUS --> INT SFRBUS --> PWR SFRBUS --> ACC_SFR SFRBUS --> B_SFR SFRBUS --> PSW_SFR RAM <--> CPU ``` ### 4.3 SFR Categorization — Functional Groups ```mermaid graph TD ROOT[8051 Special Function Registers
Total Implemented 21] CPU[Cpu Core Group] IO[Parallel I/O Group] TMR[Timer Counter Group] SRL[Serial Communication Group] IRQ[Interrupt Control Group] PWR[Power Management Group] ROOT --> CPU ROOT --> IO ROOT --> TMR ROOT --> SRL ROOT --> IRQ ROOT --> PWR CPU --> CPU_A[ACC 0xE0] CPU --> CPU_B[B 0xF0] CPU --> CPU_PSW[PSW 0xD0] CPU --> CPU_SP[SP 0x81] CPU --> CPU_DPTR[DPL 0x82 DPH 0x83] IO --> IO_P0[P0 0x80] IO --> IO_P1[P1 0x90] IO --> IO_P2[P2 0xA0] IO --> IO_P3[P3 0xB0] TMR --> TMR_TMOD[TMOD 0x89] TMR --> TMR_TCON[TCON 0x88] TMR --> TMR_TH0[TH0 0x8C] TMR --> TMR_TL0[TL0 0x8A] TMR --> TMR_TH1[TH1 0x8D] TMR --> TMR_TL1[TL1 0x8B] SRL --> SRL_SCON[SCON 0x98] SRL --> SRL_SBUF[SBUF 0x99] IRQ --> IRQ_IE[IE 0xA8] IRQ --> IRQ_IP[IP 0xB8] PWR --> PWR_PCON[PCON 0x87] ``` ### 4.4 PSW Bit Interaction Flow ```mermaid graph TD ALU_OP[ALU Operation Result] CARRY[Carry Out from Bit 7] AUX[Carry from Bit 3 to Bit 4] OVF[Overflow Detection] PARITY[Parity of Accumulator] PSWREG[PSW Register 0xD0] ALU_OP --> CARRY ALU_OP --> AUX ALU_OP --> OVF ALU_OP --> PARITY CARRY --> CY[Bit 7 CY Carry Flag] AUX --> AC[Bit 6 AC Aux Carry] OVF --> OV[Bit 2 OV Overflow] PARITY --> P[Bit 0 P Parity] CY --> PSWREG AC --> PSWREG OV --> PSWREG P --> PSWREG SEL[Bank Select Logic] PSWREG --> SEL SEL --> BANK0[Bank 0 0x00 to 0x07] SEL --> BANK1[Bank 1 0x08 to 0x0F] SEL --> BANK2[Bank 2 0x10 to 0x17] SEL --> BANK3[Bank 3 0x18 to 0x1F] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip:** The PSW block in the Mermaid diagram is the most-tested SFR. Examiners can frame a question around *"which flag is set when adding 0xFF + 0x01?"* — the answer involves CY=1, AC=1, P=0, OV=0. Trace through the diagram to understand the path. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *What are Special Function Registers (SFRs) in the 8051 microcontroller? List any four examples with their addresses.* **Model Answer:** SFRs are dedicated 8-bit registers inside the 8051 that control and monitor the on-chip hardware resources. They occupy the upper 128 bytes of the internal data memory (addresses 0x80 to 0xFF) and are accessed only through direct addressing mode. They are not part of the general-purpose RAM. Four examples: 1. **ACC (Accumulator)** — Address **0xE0** 2. **B (B register)** — Address **0xF0** 3. **PSW (Program Status Word)** — Address **0xD0** 4. **SP (Stack Pointer)** — Address **0x81** Other valid answers: P0 (0x80), P1 (0x90), P2 (0xA0), P3 (0xB0), TCON (0x88), TMOD (0x89), SCON (0x98), SBUF (0x99), IE (0xA8), IP (0xB8), PCON (0x87), DPL (0x82), DPH (0x83), TH0 (0x8C), TL0 (0x8A), TH1 (0x8D), TL1 (0x8B). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between bit-addressable and non-bit-addressable SFRs. Give two examples of each.* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Bit-Addressable SFRs | Non-Bit-Addressable SFRs | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Address requirement** | Internal address must end in 0 or 8 hex | Any address | | **Bit operations** | Allowed (`SETB P1.0`, `CLR TF0`) | Not allowed | | **Granularity** | Can manipulate individual bits | Must operate on the entire byte | | **Examples** | P0 (0x80), TCON (0x88), P1 (0x90), SCON (0x98), P2 (0xA0), IE (0xA8), P3 (0xB0), IP (0xB8), PSW (0xD0), ACC (0xE0), B (0xF0) | SP (0x81), DPL (0x82), DPH (0x83), PCON (0x87), TMOD (0x89), TL0 (0x8A), TL1 (0x8B), TH0 (0x8C), TH1 (0x8D), SBUF (0x99) | **Two examples each:** - Bit-addressable: **P3 (0xB0)** and **PSW (0xD0)**. - Non-bit-addressable: **TMOD (0x89)** and **SP (0x81)**. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Always draw the SFR address map** as part of your answer — examiners award 1–2 marks for a well-labelled memory map. > - **Distinguish between the SFR space and bit-addressable RAM space.** Students often confuse the bit-address formula for the 0x20–0x2F range with the SFR bit address formula. KTU markers **deduct 1 full mark** for this. > - **Mention both the SFR name and its address** for every register you list. Naming without an address is treated as incomplete. > - In numerical timer/baud-rate questions, **show the units conversion (Hz to MHz, seconds to µs)** explicitly. Marks are reserved for unit clarity. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024]* **(a)** Explain the **PSW register** of the 8051 in detail, with the help of a neat bit-format diagram. Mention the function of each flag bit. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the function of the **TMOD** and **TCON** registers with their bit formats. Explain the four timer modes. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — PSW Register:** The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is an 8-bit, **bit-addressable** SFR located at address **0xD0**. It reflects the result of the most recent ALU operation and also selects the active register bank. **Bit-format diagram (bit 7 → bit 0):** | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Symbol | **CY** | **AC** | **F0** | **RS1** | **RS0** | **OV** | **—** | **P** | **Function of each bit:** 1. **CY (Carry Flag, bit 7):** Set to 1 if there is a carry-out from bit 7 (addition) or a borrow-in to bit 7 (subtraction). Also used as the carry input/output for rotation instructions. 2. **AC (Auxiliary Carry, bit 6):** Set if there is a carry from bit 3 to bit 4. Used in BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal) arithmetic. 3. **F0 (User Flag 0, bit 5):** A general-purpose flag available to the programmer for any custom use. 4. **RS1, RS0 (Register Bank Select, bits 4 and 3):** Selects which of the four register banks is active. RS1=RS0=0 selects Bank 0, RS1=0/RS0=1 selects Bank 1, and so on. 5. **OV (Overflow Flag, bit 2):** Set when a signed arithmetic operation produces a result that overflows the 8-bit signed range (−128 to +127). Computed as the XOR of the carry into bit 7 and the carry out of bit 7. 6. **Bit 1:** Reserved (undefined value). 7. **P (Parity Flag, bit 0):** Automatically set to 1 if the accumulator contains an **odd number of 1-bits** (even parity), and cleared if the number of 1-bits is even. The hardware sets this bit every accumulator write cycle. --- **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — TMOD and TCON Registers:** **TMOD (Timer Mode Register) — Address 0x89, NOT bit-addressable:** TMOD configures the **operating mode** of Timer 0 and Timer 1. The upper 4 bits control Timer 1; the lower 4 bits control Timer 0. | Bit | 7 (T1) | 6 (T1) | 5 (T1) | 4 (T1) | 3 (T0) | 2 (T0) | 1 (T0) | 0 (T0) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Symbol | GATE | C/T | M1 | M0 | GATE | C/T | M1 | M0 | - **GATE:** When set, timer runs only if `TRx` is high AND the corresponding `INTx` pin is high (hardware control). When cleared, timer runs whenever `TRx` is set (software control). - **C/T:** Cleared for **timer** operation (counts internal machine cycles); set for **counter** operation (counts external pulses on the `Tx` pin). - **M1, M0:** Select the mode (see below). **TCON (Timer Control Register) — Address 0x88, bit-addressable:** TCON holds the **run control bits** and **overflow flags** for the timers, plus the edge/level control bits for external interrupts. | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Symbol | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 | - **TR0, TR1:** Software run control — `1` to start, `0` to stop. - **TF0, TF1:** Overflow flags — set by hardware when the timer rolls over; cleared by software. - **IT0, IT1:** Interrupt type — `1` for edge-triggered, `0` for level-triggered on `INT0/INT1`. - **IE0, IE1:** External interrupt edge flags — set by hardware on detection. **The Four Timer Modes:** | M1 | M0 | Mode | Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit timer (compatible with 8048). Uses 8 bits of THx and lower 5 bits of TLx. Rarely used in modern code. | | 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit timer. THx and TLx form a full 16-bit counter. Most flexible but requires manual reload after every overflow. | | 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit auto-reload timer. TLx counts from the reload value (in THx) to 0xFF, then overflows and THx is automatically copied into TLx. Ideal for baud-rate generation. | | 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split timer. Timer 0 becomes two independent 8-bit timers (TL0 and TH0). Timer 1 stops (can still be used for baud-rate generation in modes 0, 1, or 2). | --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **internal RAM organization** of the 8051 microcontroller. Distinguish between the lower 128 bytes and the upper 128 bytes (SFR space). *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the function of the **SCON**, **SBUF**, and **PCON** registers. How is the baud rate computed in serial Mode 1? *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Internal RAM Organization:** The 8051 has a **256-byte** internal data memory address space (addresses 0x00 to 0xFF), divided into two distinct physical regions: **Region 1: Lower 128 bytes (0x00 – 0x7F) — General Purpose RAM:** | Sub-region | Address | Size | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Register Banks | 0x00 – 0x1F | 32 bytes | 4 banks of R0–R7; active bank chosen by PSW | | Bit-Addressable | 0x20 – 0x2F | 16 bytes | 128 individually addressable bits (0x00–0x7F in bit space) | | Scratchpad | 0x30 – 0x7F | 80 bytes | User data, stack, variables | **Region 2: Upper 128 bytes (0x80 – 0xFF) — SFR Space:** This region is **physically separate** from the lower RAM, although the address range overlaps in the logical address space. It contains the 21 implemented Special Function Registers that control the CPU and peripherals. **Key Distinctions:** | Feature | Lower 128 bytes | Upper 128 bytes (SFR) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Access mode | Direct + Indirect | Direct only | | Purpose | Data storage | Hardware control/status | | Stack usage | Yes (SP points here) | Never used for stack | | Initial SP value | 0x07 | — | ```mermaid graph LR L0[0x00 to 0x1F Banks 0-3 R0-R7] L1[0x20 to 0x2F Bit Addressable 128 bits] L2[0x30 to 0x7F Scratchpad 80 bytes] U0[0x80 to 0xFF SFRs 21 implemented] L0 --> L1 L1 --> L2 L2 --> U0 ``` --- **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — SCON, SBUF, PCON, and Baud Rate:** **SCON (Serial Port Control Register) — 0x98, bit-addressable:** | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Symbol | SM0 | SM1 | SM2 | REN | TB8 | RB8 | TI | RI | - **SM0, SM1:** Mode selection (0=8-bit shift register, 1=8-bit UART, 2=9-bit UART fixed baud, 3=9-bit UART variable baud). - **SM2:** Multiprocessor communication enable. - **REN:** Receive enable. - **TB8, RB8:** 9th transmitted/received bit (used in modes 2 and 3). - **TI:** Transmit interrupt flag (set by hardware at end of byte transmission; must be cleared by software). - **RI:** Receive interrupt flag (set by hardware when a byte is received; must be cleared by software). **SBUF (Serial Data Buffer) — 0x99:** A single 8-bit address that physically maps to **two separate registers**: - **Writing** to SBUF loads the transmit register. - **Reading** from SBUF reads the receive register. This dual-naming trick saves SFR address space. **PCON (Power Control Register) — 0x87, NOT bit-addressable:** | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Symbol | SMOD | — | — | — | GF1 | GF0 | PD | IDL | - **SMOD:** Serial baud-rate doubler. When 1, baud rate is doubled in modes 1, 2, and 3. - **GF0, GF1:** General-purpose user flags. - **PD (Power Down):** Setting this bit activates the power-down mode (oscillator stopped). - **IDL (Idle Mode):** Setting this bit activates idle mode (CPU stopped, peripherals running). **Baud Rate Computation in Mode 1:** In Mode 1 (8-bit UART, variable baud), the baud rate is generated by **Timer 1 overflow** in its auto-reload configuration (Mode 2). The formula is: $$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$ **Example:** With $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz, SMOD = 0, and TH1 = 0xFD = 253: $$\text{Baud} = \frac{2^0}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - 253)} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{36}$$ $$\text{Baud} = \frac{1}{32} \times 307200 = 9600 \text{ bps}$$ This is exactly why the crystal 11.0592 MHz is preferred — it yields clean standard baud rates. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Special Function Registers** - [x] **SFRs live in 0x80 – 0xFF** of the internal data memory space. - [x] **Only 21 are implemented** in the standard 8051; 107 addresses are reserved. - [x] SFRs are accessed using **direct addressing mode** only. - [x] **Bit-addressable** SFRs have addresses ending in `0` or `8` (e.g., 0x80, 0x88, 0x90, 0x98, 0xA0, 0xA8, 0xB0, 0xB8, 0xD0, 0xE0, 0xF0). - [x] **PSW bits:** CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, —, P. - [x] **SP resets to 0x07**, **all port latches reset to 0xFF**, **all interrupts disabled (IE=0x00)**, **all timers in Mode 0 stopped (TMOD=TCON=0x00)**. - [x] **Register bank selection** is via RS1, RS0 bits of PSW. - [x] **P3 alternate functions:** RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD. - [x] **TMOD GATE bit:** enables hardware control of the timer via the `INTx` pin. - [x] **TMOD C/T bit:** 0 = timer (internal), 1 = counter (external on Tx pin). - [x] **Timer modes:** 0=13-bit, 1=16-bit, 2=8-bit auto-reload, 3=split timer. - [x] **EA must be 1** in IE for any interrupt to be recognized. - [x] **SCON mode selection bits SM0, SM1** — 00, 01, 10, 11 for modes 0, 1, 2, 3. - [x] **SBUF** is dual-mapped: write→TX, read→RX at same address 0x99. - [x] **Baud rate formula** for Mode 1: $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$. - [x] **Bit address for SFR flag:** $\text{Bit Address} = \text{Byte Address} + \text{Bit Position}$. - [x] **Parity bit (P)** is auto-set by hardware — it is **even parity** of the accumulator. - [x] **11.0592 MHz** crystal gives exact 9600 baud with TH1 = 0xFD. - [x] **PCON.SMOD** doubles the baud rate when set. - [x] **Power-Down mode** stops the oscillator; **Idle mode** stops only the CPU. - [x] **DPL (0x82) and DPH (0x83)** together form the 16-bit **DPTR** used for external data memory access. - [x] The 8051 has **no dedicated `STATUS` or `FLAGS` register** — flag info is consolidated in PSW. - [x] SFRs are **not initialized to zero by C startup code in Keil** — they retain their reset values from hardware. - [x] The SFR space and the lower 128 bytes of RAM are **physically separate** despite sharing the 0x80–0xFF logical addresses with general-purpose bit space — always clarify which you mean.

topics.title: Memory Organization of 8051 Microcontrollers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory Organization of 8051 Microcontrollers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Memory Organization of the 8051 Microcontroller** refers to its logically and physically separated **Harvard Memory Architecture**, in which the **Program (Code) Memory** and the **Data Memory** occupy independent address spaces, each with its own bus, control signals, and addressing instructions. The 8051 can address up to **64 KB (65,536 bytes)** of code memory, up to **64 KB of external data memory**, and contains **256 bytes of on-chip internal RAM** (split into 128 bytes of general-purpose user RAM and 128 bytes of Special Function Register space).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** *"The 8051 employs a Harvard architecture with separate program and data address spaces. The program memory is read-only (accessed via MOVC and PSEN), while the data memory is read/write (accessed via MOV, MOVX). Total addressable space is 64 KB code + 64 KB external data + 256 bytes internal RAM."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Library System

Imagine a small but extremely busy office that has **two completely separate libraries** inside the building:

- **Library A — "The Instruction Library" (Program Memory):** The clerk (CPU) can only **READ** instruction manuals here. New instructions cannot be physically added to the bookshelf while working. This is **Read-Only Memory (ROM/Flash)**. The clerk pulls out an instruction using one staircase (the **Program Bus** controlled by the $\overline{\text{PSEN}}$ signal).
- **Library B — "The Data Library" (Data Memory):** The clerk can **READ and WRITE** working notes, scratchpads, and register values here. This is **Read/Write RAM**. The clerk uses a different staircase (the **Data Bus** controlled by $\overline{\text{RD}}$ and $\overline{\text{WR}}$).

The genius of the design is that the clerk can **simultaneously fetch an instruction from Library A** *and* **read/write data from Library B** in the same machine cycle — doubling throughput. This is the defining advantage of **Harvard Architecture** over Von Neumann's single-library design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Remember (Bold Constants for KTU Board):**
> - **Code Memory Size = 64 KB (0x0000 to 0xFFFF)**
> - **External Data Memory Size = 64 KB**
> - **Internal RAM Size = 256 bytes (0x00 to 0xFF)**
> - **Register Banks = 4 (each with 8 registers R0–R7)**
> - **Bit-Addressable Area = 16 bytes (128 individually addressable bits)**
> - **SFR Area Size = 128 bytes (0x80 to 0xFF)**

## 1.3 Why Harvard Architecture? (KTU Frequently Asked Concept)

The Von Neumann architecture (used in general-purpose PCs) uses a **single memory and single bus** for both instructions and data. This creates a bottleneck called the **Von Neumann Bottleneck**. The 8051, being a real-time embedded controller, must execute instructions **deterministically and quickly**, so Intel designed it with the **Harvard architecture** where the program fetch and data access occur in parallel — critical for interrupt-driven and time-sensitive embedded applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051's Three Distinct Memory Spaces

The 8051 is unique because it logically exposes **three separate memory spaces** to the programmer, even though physically there may be fewer chips:

### Space 1 — Program Memory (Code Space)
- **Size: 64 KB (65,536 bytes)**
- **Address Range: 0000H to FFFFH**
- **Type: Read-Only** during normal execution
- **Accessed via: MOVC A,@A+DPTR** or **MOVC A,@A+PC**
- **Control Signal: $\overline{\text{PSEN}}$** (Program Store Enable)
- **Initial Address: 0000H** (Reset vector — CPU starts fetching from here)

### Space 2 — Internal Data Memory (On-Chip RAM)
- **Size: 256 bytes (0x00 to 0xFF)**
- **Sub-divided into:**
  - **Lower 128 bytes (0x00–0x7F):** User-accessible RAM
  - **Upper 128 bytes (0x80–0xFF):** Special Function Registers (SFRs)
- **Accessed via: MOV** instruction
- **Speed: Fastest** (single machine cycle)

### Space 3 — External Data Memory
- **Size: Up to 64 KB**
- **Address Range: 0000H to FFFFH** (same range as code, but separate bus)
- **Accessed via: MOVX** instruction
- **Control Signals: $\overline{\text{RD}}$ and $\overline{\text{WR}}$**

## 2.2 Internal RAM Detailed Layout (Lower 128 Bytes)

| Region (Hex) | Size | Function | Addressing Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| 00H – 07H | 8 bytes | **Register Bank 0** (R0–R7) | Direct / Register |
| 08H – 0FH | 8 bytes | **Register Bank 1** (R0–R7) | Direct / Register |
| 10H – 17H | 8 bytes | **Register Bank 2** (R0–R7) | Direct / Register |
| 18H – 1FH | 8 bytes | **Register Bank 3** (R0–R7) | Direct / Register |
| 20H – 2FH | 16 bytes | **Bit-Addressable Area** (128 bits: 00H–7FH) | Direct / Bit |
| 30H – 7FH | 80 bytes | **General-Purpose Scratchpad RAM** | Direct / Indirect |

> [!NOTE]
> Only **one Register Bank is active** at any time. Selection is done via bits **RS0 and RS1** of the **Program Status Word (PSW)** register.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination Ready)

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Code Memory Size | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes = **64 KB** | 16-bit PC |
| External Data Size | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes = **64 KB** | 16-bit DPTR |
| Total Internal RAM | $2^8 = 256$ bytes | 8-bit addressing |
| User RAM (Lower) | 128 bytes = $2^7$ | Address 0x00–0x7F |
| SFR Space (Upper) | 128 bytes = $2^7$ | Address 0x80–0xFF |
| Register Banks | 4 banks $\times$ 8 registers = **32 bytes** | Banks selectable via PSW |
| Bit-Addressable Area | 16 bytes $\times$ 8 bits = **128 bits** | Bit addresses 0x00–0x7F |
| General Purpose RAM | $128 - 32 - 16 = 80$ bytes | Addresses 0x30–0x7F |
| Stack Size | Maximum **128 bytes** (grows upward) | SP is 8-bit; default = 0x07 |
| Maximum code address | $\text{FFFFH} = 65{,}535$ | 0-indexed |
| Total addressable bits in bit area | $16 \times 8 = 128$ | Bits 00H–7FH |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The bit-addressable area's bit addresses (00H–7FH) are **DIFFERENT** from byte addresses. Bit address 00H corresponds to byte address 20H, bit 0. Bit address 7FH corresponds to byte address 2FH, bit 7. This dual-addressing is a frequently tested concept.

## 2.4 Special Function Registers (SFRs) — Quick Map

| SFR | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|
| ACC (Accumulator) | E0H | Primary arithmetic register |
| B Register | F0H | Used in MUL/DIV |
| PSW | D0H | Program Status Word (flags + bank select) |
| SP (Stack Pointer) | 81H | Points to top of stack |
| DPTR (DPL+DPH) | 82H, 83H | 16-bit data pointer |
| P0, P1, P2, P3 | 80H, 90H, A0H, B0H | Port latches |
| TCON, TMOD | 88H, 89H | Timer control/mode |
| TL0, TH0, TL1, TH1 | 8AH–8DH | Timer low/high bytes |
| SCON, SBUF | 98H, 99H | Serial control/buffer |
| IE, IP | A8H, B8H | Interrupt enable/priority |
| PCON | 87H | Power control |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding memory organization is critical for:
1. **Writing efficient embedded C/assembly code** — knowing which variables go to bit-addressable, which to register banks, and which require MOVX.
2. **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) design** — selecting alternate register banks to avoid context-save overhead.
3. **Memory-mapped I/O interfacing** — reading SFR bit-fields for port manipulation.
4. **External memory expansion** — connecting EPROM, RAM, or peripheral chips using the external memory interface pins.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why 64 KB Maximum Memory?

The 8051 uses a **16-bit Program Counter (PC)** to address code memory.

$$
\text{Maximum Code Memory} = 2^{(\text{PC width in bits})}
$$

$$
\text{Maximum Code Memory} = 2^{16} \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Maximum Code Memory} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

Similarly, the 16-bit **DPTR (Data Pointer)** register is used to address external data memory:

$$
\text{Maximum External Data Memory} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

For internal RAM, an 8-bit address is used:

$$
\text{Maximum Internal RAM} = 2^8 = 256 \text{ bytes}
$$

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Mapping of Register Banks

Let us derive which physical RAM address each register R0–R7 of each bank occupies.

**Step 1:** Identify the base address of Register Bank 0.

$$
\text{Bank 0 Base} = 00\text{H}
$$

**Step 2:** Each register bank is **8 bytes wide**, so the next bank starts 8 bytes higher.

$$
\text{Bank 1 Base} = 00\text{H} + 08\text{H} = 08\text{H}
$$

$$
\text{Bank 2 Base} = 08\text{H} + 08\text{H} = 10\text{H}
$$

$$
\text{Bank 3 Base} = 10\text{H} + 08\text{H} = 18\text{H}
$$

**Step 3:** General formula for Bank $n$:

$$
\text{Bank}_n \text{ Base} = n \times 08\text{H}, \quad n \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}
$$

**Step 4:** For any specific register R$k$ in Bank $n$:

$$
\text{Address of } R_k \text{ in Bank}_n = (n \times 08\text{H}) + k, \quad k \in \{0, 1, \dots, 7\}
$$

**Example:** Find the RAM address of R5 in Bank 2.

$$
\text{Address} = (2 \times 08\text{H}) + 5 = 10\text{H} + 05\text{H} = 15\text{H}
$$

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Bit-Addressable Memory Mapping

The bit-addressable area spans 16 bytes from **20H to 2FH**.

**Step 1:** Total number of individually addressable bits.

$$
N_{bits} = 16 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} = 128 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2:** The bits are numbered from **00H to 7FH** (in bit-address space).

**Step 3:** Map relationship between byte address $B$, bit position $b$ (0–7), and bit address $D$.

$$
D = (B - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + b
$$

**Step 4:** Verify with extremes.

For byte 20H, bit 0:

$$
D = (20\text{H} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + 0 = 00\text{H} \quad \checkmark
$$

For byte 2FH, bit 7:

$$
D = (2\text{FH} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + 7 = (0\text{FH}) \times 8 + 7 = 78 + 7 = 7\text{FH} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Example:** What is the bit address of bit 3 of byte 25H?

$$
D = (25\text{H} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + 3 = 5 \times 8 + 3 = 40 + 3 = 43\text{H}
$$

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Stack Pointer (SP) Behavior

**Step 1:** After reset, the Stack Pointer is initialized to:

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{reset}} = 07\text{H}
$$

This means the first PUSH will store data at address 08H (the start of Register Bank 1).

**Step 2:** On every PUSH, SP **increments before** storing:

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 1
$$

**Step 3:** On every POP, SP **decrements after** reading:

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 1
$$

**Step 4:** Maximum stack depth within internal RAM:

$$
\text{SP}_{\max} = \text{FFH} \rightarrow \text{Depth} = \text{FFH} - 07\text{H} = \text{F8H} = 248 \text{ bytes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Stack is limited to **128 bytes** of internal RAM in practice (0x80 to 0xFF are SFRs and cannot hold stack data reliably). Practical stack space is **30H–7FH = 80 bytes** or **08H–7FH = 120 bytes** if banks are unused.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: PSW Register Bank Selection

The PSW (Program Status Word) at address D0H has bits RS0 and RS1 (bits 3 and 4) for bank selection.

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 00H–07H |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 08H–0FH |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 10H–17H |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 18H–1FH |

The relationship is:

$$
\text{Bank Number} = (RS1 \times 2) + (RS0 \times 1)
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Memory Types

| Parameter | Code Memory | Internal RAM | External RAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Size | 64 KB | 256 bytes | 64 KB |
| Address Width | 16-bit (PC) | 8-bit | 16-bit (DPTR) |
| Access Instruction | MOVC | MOV | MOVX |
| Control Signal | $\overline{\text{PSEN}}$ | Internal | $\overline{\text{RD}}$, $\overline{\text{WR}}$ |
| Volatility | Non-volatile (ROM) | Volatile | Volatile |
| Speed | Slow (external) / Fast (internal) | Fastest | Slowest |
| Read/Write | Read-only | Read/Write | Read/Write |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Explain the Harvard memory architecture of 8051 with a neat diagram.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 microcontroller uses the **Harvard architecture**, in which the **program memory** and the **data memory** are physically and logically separated with distinct buses and control signals.

**(i) Program Memory (Code Space):**
- Size: **64 KB (0000H to FFFFH)**
- Read-only during normal operation
- Accessed using the **MOVC** instruction
- Read control signal: **$\overline{\text{PSEN}}$** (Program Store Enable)
- Addressed by the **16-bit Program Counter (PC)**

**(ii) Data Memory Space:**
- Internal RAM: **256 bytes** (00H to FFH)
- External RAM: up to **64 KB**
- Accessed via **MOV** (internal) and **MOVX** (external)
- Read/Write control signals: **$\overline{\text{RD}}$ and $\overline{\text{WR}}$**

**Key Advantage:** The CPU can fetch an instruction from program memory **simultaneously** with a data read/write operation, doubling throughput compared to Von Neumann architecture.

```
        +---------+         +-----------+
        |   CPU   |--PC--->|  CODE MEM |
        |  8051   |         |   64 KB   |
        |         |         +-----------+
        |         |            ^ PSEN
        |         |--DPTR->+-----+
        |         |         | DATA MEM |
        +---------+         | 64 KB+RAM|
                            +-----+
                              ^ RD, WR
```

---

### Question 2: List the three distinct memory spaces of 8051 and state the instructions used to access each.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 microcontroller has **three separate memory spaces**:

| Memory Space | Size | Address Range | Instruction Used | Control Signal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Program (Code) Memory** | 64 KB | 0000H – FFFFH | MOVC A,@A+DPTR / MOVC A,@A+PC | $\overline{\text{PSEN}}$ |
| **Internal Data Memory (RAM)** | 256 bytes | 00H – FFH | MOV direct, MOV @Ri | Internal |
| **External Data Memory** | 64 KB | 0000H – FFFFH | MOVX @DPTR / MOVX @Ri | $\overline{\text{RD}}$, $\overline{\text{WR}}$ |

**Key Point:** The code and external data memory share the same address range (0000H to FFFFH) but are differentiated by the **control signals** and the **instruction mnemonics** used to access them.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the internal RAM organization of 8051 and explain the function of each region. How are the register banks selected?

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 1 | Part B | 7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The internal RAM of the 8051 is **256 bytes** wide, divided into a **lower 128-byte user area** and an **upper 128-byte SFR area**.

```
Address Range     Region                    Size
+--------------+----------------+----------+
00H - 07H      | Register Bank 0| 8 bytes  | R0 - R7
08H - 0FH      | Register Bank 1| 8 bytes  | R0 - R7
10H - 17H      | Register Bank 2| 8 bytes  | R0 - R7
18H - 1FH      | Register Bank 3| 8 bytes  | R0 - R7
20H - 2FH      | Bit-Addressable| 16 bytes | 128 bits
30H - 7FH      | General Purpose| 80 bytes | Scratchpad
80H - FFH      | SFR Space      | 128 bytes| Control regs
+--------------+----------------+----------+
```

**Function of Each Region:**

**(i) Register Banks (00H–1FH):** Four banks of 8 registers each (R0–R7). The active bank is selected using **RS0 and RS1 bits of the PSW register**. This allows fast context switching during interrupts.

**(ii) Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH):** 16 bytes = **128 individually addressable bits** (bit addresses 00H to 7FH). Useful for boolean operations using SETB, CLR, ANL, ORL.

**(iii) General Purpose RAM (30H–7FH):** 80 bytes used for variable storage, stack, and scratchpad operations.

**(iv) SFR Area (80H–FFH):** 128 bytes of special function registers controlling ports, timers, serial port, interrupts, and other peripherals.

**Register Bank Selection:** Bits **RS0 (PSW.3)** and **RS1 (PSW.4)** of the PSW register select the active bank. Example: `SETB RS0` selects Bank 1.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of the Stack Pointer (SP) in 8051. What is its default value after reset, and what is the maximum stack depth possible in internal RAM?

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 1 | Part B | 7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of the Stack Pointer (SP):**

The **Stack Pointer (SP)** is an 8-bit Special Function Register located at internal RAM address **81H**. It always points to the **topmost (last used) location of the stack** in internal RAM. The stack grows **upward** (toward higher addresses) in the 8051.

**Default Value After Reset:**

After a hardware reset, the SP is initialized to:

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{reset}} = 07\text{H}
$$

This means the first **PUSH** instruction will store data at location **08H**, which is the start of **Register Bank 1**. To avoid stack-bank conflict, it is standard practice to initialize SP in the program:

```assembly
MOV SP, #0x5F   ; Reserve top of internal RAM for stack
```

**Maximum Stack Depth in Internal RAM:**

Since SP is 8-bit, it can hold values from 00H to FFH. The maximum usable stack depth within internal RAM (excluding SFRs) is from address 30H to 7FH (or even up to FFH if SFR area is carefully managed).

$$
\text{Maximum theoretical depth} = \text{FFH} - 07\text{H} = \text{F8H} = 248 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Practical safe depth} = 7\text{FH} - 30\text{H} = 4\text{FH} = 79 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Stack Operation Examples:**

```assembly
PUSH ACC   ; (1) SP = SP+1, (2) ACC -> [SP]
POP B      ; (1) B <- [SP], (2) SP = SP-1
CALL SUB   ; (1) SP = SP+1, (2) PC_low -> [SP], (3) SP = SP+1, (4) PC_high -> [SP]
RET        ; (1) PC_high <- [SP], SP=SP-1, (2) PC_low <- [SP], SP=SP-1
```

**Importance:** The stack is essential for **subroutine calls (LCALL, ACALL)**, **interrupts**, and **temporary data storage** during program execution.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between MOV, MOVX, and MOVC instructions in 8051. State clearly which memory space each accesses and give one example for each.

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 1 | Part B | 7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Instruction | Memory Accessed | Size | Control Signal | Addressing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MOV** | Internal RAM (00H–FFH) and SFRs | 8-bit | Internal | Direct, Indirect, Register |
| **MOVX** | External Data Memory (up to 64 KB) | 8-bit | $\overline{\text{RD}}$, $\overline{\text{WR}}$ | Indirect via @DPTR or @Ri |
| **MOVC** | Program Memory (Code ROM, 64 KB) | 8-bit | $\overline{\text{PSEN}}$ | Indexed via @A+DPTR or @A+PC |

**MOV Example (Internal RAM):**
