```assembly
MOV A, 30H       ; Load accumulator from internal RAM address 30H
MOV R0, #0x55    ; Load immediate value into R0
```

**MOVX Example (External RAM):**
```assembly
MOV DPTR, #0x2000 ; DPTR = 2000H (external address)
MOVX A, @DPTR     ; Read external RAM at 2000H into ACC
MOVX @DPTR, A     ; Write ACC to external RAM at 2000H
```

**MOVC Example (Code ROM):**
```assembly
MOV A, #0x05      ; ACC = 05H (offset)
MOV DPTR, #0x1000 ; DPTR = 1000H (table base in code ROM)
MOVC A, @A+DPTR   ; Read code byte at (1005H) into ACC
```

**Key Distinction:** Although code and external data memory share the same 16-bit address range (0000H–FFFFH), they are **never confused** because:
1. The **CPU instruction** is different (MOVX vs MOVC).
2. The **hardware control signals** are different ($\overline{\text{RD}}/\overline{\text{WR}}$ vs $\overline{\text{PSEN}}$).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe with a block diagram how external program memory and external data memory are interfaced to 8051. Explain the role of $\overline{\text{EA}}$, $\overline{\text{PSEN}}$, and **ALE** pins.

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 1 | Part B | 7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**External Memory Interfacing Block Diagram:**

```
        +---------------+          +--------------+
 P0  -->| 8051 P0 AD0-7|--->|Latch 74373|<--- A0-A7 (Latched)
        |               |    |              | 
 P2  -->| 8051 P2 A8-A15|-->| ROM         | (Code Memory)
        |               |    | (EPROM)     |
        |          PSEN--->|              |
        |          ALE---->|Latch Enable |
        |          EA=VCC or GND
 P0,P2->| 8051          |-->| RAM         | (Data Memory)
        |          RD  --->| (SRAM)      |
        |          WR  --->|              |
        +---------------+    +--------------+
                              A0-A15
```

**Role of Control Pins:**

**(i) $\overline{\text{EA}}$ (External Access, Pin 31):**
- When $\overline{\text{EA}} = 1$ (VCC): The 8051 fetches code from **internal ROM** for addresses 0000H–0FFFH (4 KB) and from **external ROM** for 1000H–FFFFH.
- When $\overline{\text{EA}} = 0$ (GND): The 8051 fetches **all** code from external ROM (used for 8031 which has no internal ROM).

**(ii) $\overline{\text{PSEN}}$ (Program Store Enable, Pin 29):**
- This is an **output** signal that goes LOW whenever the 8051 reads from **external program memory**.
- It is connected to the **Output Enable (OE)** pin of the external EPROM/ROM chip.
- It is **never** activated for external data memory access.

**(iii) ALE (Address Latch Enable, Pin 30):**
- The 8051's Port 0 is **multiplexed** — it carries both the lower address byte (A0–A7) and the data byte (D0–D7) at different times.
- ALE is used to **latch** the address byte into an external latch (74HC573 or 74LS373) so the lower address remains valid for the entire memory cycle.
- ALE goes HIGH during the address phase and LOW during the data phase.

**Interfacing Summary Table:**

| Memory Type | Latch Used? | $\overline{\text{PSEN}}$ Used? | $\overline{\text{RD}}/\overline{\text{WR}}$ Used? |
|---|---|---|---|
| External Code ROM | Yes (for low byte) | Yes | No |
| External Data RAM | Yes (for low byte) | No | Yes |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing MOVC and MOVX:** Many students interchange these instructions. Remember: **MOVC = Code (read-only ROM)**, **MOVX = eXternal data RAM**.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention control signals:** A full-mark answer must always specify the control signal ($\overline{\text{PSEN}}$, $\overline{\text{RD}}$, $\overline{\text{WR}}$, $\overline{\text{EA}}$, ALE) associated with each memory operation.
> 3. **Ignoring bit-addressability:** When asked about 20H–2FH, students often forget to mention that 128 individual bits are addressable, with bit addresses 00H–7FH.
> 4. **Incorrect stack pointer default value:** Many students write SP = 08H after reset. The **correct** value is **07H**, and the first PUSH stores at 08H.
> 5. **Mixing up Register Bank addresses:** Bank 0 = 00H–07H, Bank 1 = 08H–0FH, Bank 2 = 10H–17H, Bank 3 = 18H–1FH. Memorize this pattern: each bank is 8 bytes apart.
> 6. **ALE pin confusion:** ALE is for **latching the address** from the multiplexed P0 bus, not for general-purpose latching.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Memory Organization of 8051:**

- ⭐ **Architecture:** Harvard — separate program and data memory spaces with independent buses.
- ⭐ **Code Memory:** 64 KB, ROM, accessed via **MOVC**, controlled by **$\overline{\text{PSEN}}$**, addressed by 16-bit **PC**.
- ⭐ **External Data Memory:** Up to 64 KB, RAM, accessed via **MOVX**, controlled by **$\overline{\text{RD}}$** and **$\overline{\text{WR}}$**, addressed by 16-bit **DPTR**.
- ⭐ **Internal RAM Total:** 256 bytes split as **128 bytes user + 128 bytes SFR**.
- ⭐ **Register Banks:** 4 banks × 8 registers = 32 bytes at 00H–1FH. Active bank selected by **PSW bits RS0, RS1**.
- ⭐ **Bit-Addressable Area:** 16 bytes (20H–2FH) → 128 bits numbered **00H to 7FH** in bit-address space.
- ⭐ **General Purpose RAM:** 80 bytes from 30H to 7FH.
- ⭐ **SFR Area:** 80H to FFH (128 bytes). Key SFRs: ACC (E0H), B (F0H), PSW (D0H), SP (81H), DPTR (82H/83H), P0–P3 (80H, 90H, A0H, B0H).
- ⭐ **Stack Pointer (SP):** 8-bit register at 81H. **Default after reset = 07H**. Grows **upward**. Max depth in internal RAM = **248 bytes** (theoretical).
- ⭐ **$\overline{\text{EA}}$ Pin:** Logic 1 → internal code first (4 KB), then external; Logic 0 → all code from external ROM.
- ⭐ **ALE Pin:** Latches the lower address byte (A0–A7) from multiplexed P0 bus.
- ⭐ **$\overline{\text{PSEN}}$ Pin:** Activated ONLY for external code memory reads.
- ⭐ **Three instructions for three memories:** **MOV** → internal RAM; **MOVX** → external RAM; **MOVC** → code ROM.
- ⭐ **Reset Vector:** 0000H (CPU starts here after power-on or reset).
- ⭐ **Interrupt Vectors:** Located at 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H (5 vector locations for 5 interrupts).
- ⭐ **Von Neumann vs Harvard:** Von Neumann has one shared bus (slower); Harvard has separate buses (faster, deterministic — used by 8051).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Set: Arithmetic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Set: Arithmetic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Arithmetic Instruction Set** of the **MCS-51 (8051) family** of microcontrollers is a collection of 8-bit binary operations performed by the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** on the **Accumulator (A)** and selected operands. These instructions govern addition, addition-with-carry, subtraction-with-borrow, increment, decrement, multiplication, division, and **Binary Coded Decimal (BCD)** correction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Arithmetic instructions operate on 8-bit data stored in the Accumulator (A), general-purpose registers (R0–R7), internal RAM (data memory), external data memory (via @Ri), or the **Program Counter pointer (DPTR)**. The PSW (Program Status Word, address **D0H**) flags — **CY (PSW.7)**, **AC (PSW.6)**, **OV (PSW.2)**, and **P (PSW.0)** — are conditionally updated to reflect the result.

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is the central flag register for every arithmetic operation:

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Symbol | **CY** | **AC** | **F0** | **RS1** | **RS0** | **OV** | — | **P** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Shopkeeper's Calculator

Imagine a small shopkeeper using an old **8-digit pocket calculator**:

- The **Accumulator (A)** is the calculator's **display screen** (it always shows the current answer).
- The **general-purpose registers (R0–R7)** are the **8 memory buttons (M+, MR)** where he stores recent numbers.
- The **CY (Carry Flag)** is the **overflow indicator** that blinks when the result is too big for the 8 digits.
- The **AC (Auxiliary Carry)** is a hidden, internal half-digit transfer that the shopkeeper uses for **currency-paisa conversion** (i.e., BCD arithmetic).
- The **OV (Overflow Flag)** is the **"result sign is wrong"** alarm — it beeps when the *signed* answer is mathematically incorrect.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a shopkeeper cannot directly multiply two memory slots (he must recall them onto the screen first), the 8051 ALU **requires the Accumulator (A) to be one of the two operands in every arithmetic operation except MUL/DIV/INC/DEC**. This is the most critical rule to internalize.

## 1.3 The Three Addressing Modes Used in Arithmetic

The 8051 arithmetic group supports four distinct operand-fetching strategies:

1. **Register addressing** → `ADD A, Rn` (n = 0 to 7)
2. **Direct addressing** → `ADD A, 30H` (any internal RAM or SFR)
3. **Register-indirect addressing** → `ADD A, @Ri` (i = 0 or 1, points to internal/external RAM)
4. **Immediate addressing** → `ADD A, #45H` (constant embedded in code)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Fact:** Out of the **24 arithmetic mnemonics** in the 8051 ISA, **MUL AB**, **DIV AB**, **DA A**, and **INC A** are the *only* four that operate **without** an external operand — the Accumulator acts as the implicit (and only) source/destination.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8 Functional Categories of Arithmetic Instructions

The 8051 arithmetic set is logically divided into **eight sub-groups**. Each sub-group has a distinct purpose, byte-size, machine-cycle count, and flag-affection profile.

### Category 1 — Unsigned Addition: `ADD`
- **Syntax:** `ADD A, <src>`
- **Operation:** $\text{A} \leftarrow \text{A} + \text{src}$
- **Bytes:** 1 (Reg/Indirect) or 2 (Direct/Immediate)
- **Machine Cycles:** 1
- **Flags Affected:** **CY, AC, OV, P**

### Category 2 — Addition with Carry: `ADDC`
- **Syntax:** `ADDC A, <src>`
- **Operation:** $\text{A} \leftarrow \text{A} + \text{src} + \text{CY}$
- **Bytes/Cycles:** Same as ADD
- **Flags Affected:** **CY, AC, OV, P**
- **Use-Case:** Multi-precision (16-bit, 24-bit) arithmetic, where the lower byte's carry must be added to the higher byte.

### Category 3 — Subtraction with Borrow: `SUBB`
- **Syntax:** `SUBB A, <src>`
- **Operation:** $\text{A} \leftarrow \text{A} - \text{src} - \text{CY}$
- **Bytes/Cycles:** Same as ADD
- **Flags Affected:** **CY, AC, OV, P**
- **Note:** The 8051 has **no** plain `SUB` — subtraction *always* includes the borrow (CY). To perform `A = A - src` with CY=0, you must explicitly `CLR C` beforehand.

### Category 4 — Increment (Add 1): `INC`
- **Syntax:** `INC A` / `INC Rn` / `INC direct` / `INC @Ri` / `INC DPTR`
- **Operation:** $\text{operand} \leftarrow \text{operand} + 1$
- **Bytes:** 1 (A, Rn, @Ri, DPTR) or 2 (direct)
- **Cycles:** 1 (2 for INC DPTR)
- **Flags Affected:** **NONE** (only INC DPTR internally uses an 11-bit increment)
- **Crucial Note:** Unlike ADD, **INC never sets the CY flag** — even on a wrap from FFH to 00H, CY remains untouched.

### Category 5 — Decrement (Subtract 1): `DEC`
- **Syntax:** `DEC A` / `DEC Rn` / `DEC direct` / `DEC @Ri`
- **Operation:** $\text{operand} \leftarrow \text{operand} - 1$
- **Bytes:** 1 or 2 (direct)
- **Cycles:** 1
- **Flags Affected:** **NONE**
- **Note:** `DEC DPTR` is **not a valid instruction** in the standard 8051 ISA.

### Category 6 — Multiplication: `MUL AB`
- **Operation:** $\text{B (high byte)} \cdot \text{A (low byte)} \leftarrow \text{A} \times \text{B}$
- **Bytes/Cycles:** 1 byte / 4 machine cycles
- **Flags Affected:** **CY = 0, OV = 1** if B ≠ 0 after multiplication (i.e., result > 255), else **OV = 0**

### Category 7 — Division: `DIV AB`
- **Operation:** $\text{A (quotient)}, \text{B (remainder)} \leftarrow \text{A} \div \text{B}$
- **Bytes/Cycles:** 1 byte / 4 machine cycles
- **Flags Affected:** **CY = 0, OV = 1** if B = 0 (division by zero trap), else **OV = 0**

### Category 8 — Decimal Adjust Accumulator: `DA A`
- **Operation:** Corrects the Accumulator's binary addition result into valid **BCD (Binary Coded Decimal)** form.
- **Bytes/Cycles:** 1 byte / 1 machine cycle
- **Flags Affected:** **CY**
- **Use-Case:** Displaying decimal digits on 7-segment displays without converting to ASCII.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important table** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module-1 viva and written exams. Memorize the **Flag columns** and **Byte/Cycle columns**.

| Instruction | Operation | Bytes | Cycles | CY | AC | OV | P |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ADD A, Rn | A = A + Rn | 1 | 1 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| ADD A, direct | A = A + (direct) | 2 | 1 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| ADD A, @Ri | A = A + (Ri) | 1 | 1 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| ADD A, #data | A = A + data | 2 | 1 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| ADDC A, src | A = A + src + CY | 1/2 | 1 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| SUBB A, src | A = A − src − CY | 1/2 | 1 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| INC A | A = A + 1 | 1 | 1 | — | — | — | — |
| INC Rn | Rn = Rn + 1 | 1 | 1 | — | — | — | — |
| INC direct | (direct) = (direct)+1 | 2 | 1 | — | — | — | — |
| INC @Ri | (Ri) = (Ri) + 1 | 1 | 1 | — | — | — | — |
| INC DPTR | DPTR = DPTR + 1 | 1 | 2 | — | — | — | — |
| DEC A | A = A − 1 | 1 | 1 | — | — | — | — |
| DEC Rn | Rn = Rn − 1 | 1 | 1 | — | — | — | — |
| DEC direct | (direct) = (direct)−1 | 2 | 1 | — | — | — | — |
| DEC @Ri | (Ri) = (Ri) − 1 | 1 | 1 | — | — | — | — |
| MUL AB | B·A = A × B | 1 | 4 | 0 | — | 1/0 | ✓ |
| DIV AB | A÷B → A,B | 1 | 4 | 0 | — | 1/0 | ✓ |
| DA A | Decimal Adjust A | 1 | 1 | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓ |

**The Five Flag-Setting Formulas (derivable by heart):**

$$
\text{CY} = C_8
$$

$$
\text{AC} = C_4
$$

$$
\text{OV} = C_7 \oplus C_6
$$

$$
\text{P} = \text{Parity bit set if A has an odd number of 1's (8051 uses EVEN parity convention)}
$$

$$
\text{DA A Adjustment Rule: } \text{If } (A_{low\,nibble} > 9) \text{ OR } (AC = 1) \text{ then } A \leftarrow A + 06H
$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Arithmetic Instruction Used | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Digital Weighing Scales** | ADD, DA A | Decimal weights must be displayed exactly (e.g., 1.25 kg, not 1.249...) |
| **Industrial Temperature Controllers** | SUBB, JC | Comparing ADC output against a threshold using signed math |
| **Motor RPM Measurement** | MUL AB, DIV AB | Converting pulse-count × constant to RPM via 16-bit arithmetic |
| **Multi-Byte Counters (RTCs)** | ADDC A, @R0 | 32-bit up-counter chained across 4 internal RAM bytes |
| **DTMF / Telephone Dialing** | DA A | Generating exact BCD tone pairs for the dual-tone matrix |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **fully worked numerical problems** — the exact question type KTU 2024 examiners set for the 14-mark Part B. No steps are skipped.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Flag Calculation for ADD

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
> If A = **0xC3** and R3 = **0x6E**, determine the result of `ADD A, R3` and the state of the CY, AC, and OV flags.

**Step 1 — Decimal & Binary Representation**

$$
A = 0xC3 = 1100\,0011_2
$$

$$
R3 = 0x6E = 0110\,1110_2
$$

**Step 2 — Bit-by-Bit Binary Addition (with carry propagation)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 1100\,0011 \\
+\;& 0110\,1110 \\
\hline
& \text{Bit 0: } 1+0 = 1,\ \text{C}_1 = 0 \\
& \text{Bit 1: } 1+1+0 = 10,\ \text{sum}=0,\ \text{C}_2 = 1 \\
& \text{Bit 2: } 0+1+1 = 10,\ \text{sum}=0,\ \text{C}_3 = 1 \\
& \text{Bit 3: } 0+1+1 = 10,\ \text{sum}=0,\ \text{C}_4 = 1\ \Rightarrow\ \text{AC} = 1 \\
& \text{Bit 4: } 1+1+1 = 11,\ \text{sum}=1,\ \text{C}_5 = 1 \\
& \text{Bit 5: } 0+1+1 = 10,\ \text{sum}=0,\ \text{C}_6 = 1 \\
& \text{Bit 6: } 0+1+1 = 10,\ \text{sum}=0,\ \text{C}_7 = 1\ \Rightarrow\ \text{C}_6 \text{(into bit 7)} = 1 \\
& \text{Bit 7: } 1+0+1 = 10,\ \text{sum}=0,\ \text{C}_8 = 1\ \Rightarrow\ \text{CY} = 1,\ \text{C}_7 \text{(out of bit 7)} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final Result Byte**

$$
A_{\text{new}} = 0001\,0001_2 = 0x11
$$

**Step 4 — Flag Assignment from the Carries**

$$
\text{CY} = C_8 = 1
$$

$$
\text{AC} = C_4 = 1
$$

$$
\text{OV} = C_7 \oplus C_6 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Verdict:** Result A = **0x11**, **CY = 1, AC = 1, OV = 0**. Since CY = 1, the unsigned 8-bit result has overflowed (true sum 0x131). However, OV = 0 indicates the *signed* 8-bit result is mathematically valid.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Detecting Signed Overflow (OV = 1)

**Problem Statement:**
> Compute the result and PSW flags when A = **0x7F** and R4 = **0x6E** after `ADD A, R4`.

**Step 1 — Identify Signed Interpretation**

$$
A = 0x7F = 0111\,1111_2 = +127_{\text{signed}}
$$

$$
R4 = 0x6E = 0110\,1110_2 = +110_{\text{signed}}
$$

$$
\text{Expected mathematical sum} = +237 \quad \text{(outside } [-128,\,+127] \text{ range)}
$$

**Step 2 — Bit-Wise Addition with Carry Tracking**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 0111\,1111 \\
+\;& 0110\,1110 \\
\hline
& \text{Bit 0: } 1+0 = 1,\ \text{carry} = 0 \\
& \text{Bit 1: } 1+1+0 = 10,\ \text{sum}=0,\ \text{carry} = 1 \\
& \text{Bit 2: } 1+1+1 = 11,\ \text{sum}=1,\ \text{carry} = 1 \\
& \text{Bit 3: } 1+1+1 = 11,\ \text{sum}=1,\ \text{carry} = 1\ \Rightarrow\ \text{AC} = 1 \\
& \text{Bit 4: } 1+0+1 = 10,\ \text{sum}=0,\ \text{carry} = 1 \\
& \text{Bit 5: } 1+1+1 = 11,\ \text{sum}=1,\ \text{carry} = 1 \\
& \text{Bit 6: } 1+1+1 = 11,\ \text{sum}=1,\ \text{carry} = 1\ \Rightarrow\ \text{C}_6 = 1 \\
& \text{Bit 7: } 0+0+1 = 1,\ \text{sum}=1,\ \text{carry} = 0\ \Rightarrow\ \text{C}_7 = 0,\ \text{CY} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Result and Flag Determination**

$$
A_{\text{new}} = 1110\,1101_2 = 0xED = -19_{\text{signed}}
$$

$$
\text{OV} = C_7 \oplus C_6 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Verdict:** Two positive numbers (+127 and +110) summed to a **negative** result (−19). This is the textbook signed-overflow scenario. **OV = 1** alerts the programmer that the signed arithmetic is **invalid** and the result must be re-evaluated using wider precision (16-bit).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Multi-Precision (16-Bit) Addition using ADDC

**Problem Statement:**
> Add the two 16-bit numbers stored in internal RAM as **MSB at 30H, LSB at 31H** and **MSB at 40H, LSB at 41H**. Store the 16-bit sum at **50H (MSB) and 51H (LSB)**.

**Assembly Program (KTU Model Answer):**

```assembly
        MOV  A, 31H        ; Load LSB of first number
        ADD  A, 41H        ; A = LSB1 + LSB2
        MOV  51H, A        ; Store result LSB
        MOV  A, 30H        ; Load MSB of first number
        ADDC A, 40H        ; A = MSB1 + MSB2 + Carry
        MOV  50H, A        ; Store result MSB
HERE:   SJMP HERE          ; Stop (infinite loop)
```

**Trace Example:**
- Number 1: 30H = 0x1A, 31H = 0xFF  →  0x1AFF
- Number 2: 40H = 0xE5, 41H = 0x02  →  0xE502
- Expected sum: 0x1AFF + 0xE502 = 0x10001 (overflows 16-bit → true sum 0x0001 with CY = 1)

**Execution Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: } A = 0xFF,\ (0xFF + 0x02) = 0x01,\ \text{CY} = 1,\ 51H = 0x01 \\
&\text{Step 2: } A = 0x1A,\ (0x1A + 0xE5 + 1) = 0x00,\ \text{CY} = 1,\ 50H = 0x00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:** 50H = 0x00, 51H = 0x01, **CY = 1** (signaling 17-bit overflow).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — BCD Arithmetic with DA A

**Problem Statement:**
> Add 0x38 (decimal 38) and 0x49 (decimal 49) using 8051 instructions such that the Accumulator ends up with the BCD result.

**Code:**

```assembly
        MOV  A, #38H        ; A = 38H
        ADD  A, #49H        ; A = 38H + 49H = 81H
        DA   A              ; Adjust: lower nibble 1+9=10>9, so add 06H
                            ; 81H + 06H = 87H ✓
```

**Detailed DA A Logic (Lower Nibble):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& A_{\text{before DA}} = 0x81 \\
& \text{Lower nibble} = 0x1 + 0x9 = 0xA = 10 \ \Rightarrow\ 10 > 9 \\
& \text{Adjustment: } A \leftarrow A + 0x06 \\
& A_{\text{after DA}} = 0x87
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** 0x87 in BCD = **87 decimal** ✓ (38 + 49 = 87).

> [!NOTE]
> Without `DA A`, the result A = 0x81 would be displayed as "81" by a 7-segment driver — which is *still* correct here, but for sums crossing 0x9A boundary (e.g., 58+49=0xA1 raw), the BCD interpretation breaks down. `DA A` adds 0x66 (0x06 + 0x60) to produce 0x07 with CY = 1, correctly displaying 107.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — MUL AB and DIV AB

**Multiplication:**
- A = 0x10 (16), B = 0x20 (32)
- $A \times B = 512 = 0x0200$
- After `MUL AB`: B = 0x02 (high), A = 0x00 (low)
- Since B ≠ 0, **OV = 1, CY = 0**

**Division:**
- A = 0xFB (251), B = 0x12 (18)
- $251 \div 18 = 13$ remainder $17$
- After `DIV AB`: A = 0x0D (quotient = 13), B = 0x11 (remainder = 17)
- **OV = 0, CY = 0**
- If B had been 0, **OV = 1** (division-by-zero flag).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Arithmetic Instruction Family — Logical Topology

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **functional classification** of every 8051 arithmetic instruction, mapped against the operand-types and flag-update channels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Arithmetic Instruction Set of 8051] --> B1[Addition Group]
    A0 --> B2[Subtraction Group]
    A0 --> B3[Step Group INC DEC]
    A0 --> B4[Multiply Divide]
    A0 --> B5[BCD Helper DA A]

    B1 --> C1[ADD A src]
    B1 --> C2[ADDC A src]
    C1 --> D1[Op1 Operand Types]
    D1 --> E1[Rn Register]
    D1 --> E2[Direct Address]
    D1 --> E3[Indirect at Ri]
    D1 --> E4[Immediate data]
    C1 --> F1[Flags CY AC OV P]
    C2 --> F1

    B2 --> C3[SUBB A src]
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> F1

    B3 --> C4[INC A Rn direct Ri DPTR]
    B3 --> C5[DEC A Rn direct Ri]
    C4 --> G1[Flags NONE except DPTR internal 11 bit]
    C5 --> G1

    B4 --> C6[MUL AB]
    B4 --> C7[DIV AB]
    C6 --> H1[Result B high A low]
    C7 --> H2[Result A quotient B remainder]
    C6 --> I1[OV 1 if B nonzero]
    C7 --> I2[OV 1 if B zero]

    B5 --> C8[DA A]
    C8 --> J1[Adds 06 or 60 or 66]
    C8 --> J2[Only flag affected CY]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — ALU, Accumulator, and PSW Interaction

The schematic below illustrates how data flows from registers, through the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, and how the ALU signals back to the **Program Status Word (PSW)**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REG[Internal Register File R0 to R7] --> MUX1
    DRAM[Internal Data RAM 00 to 7FH] --> MUX2
    XRAM[External Data RAM via MOVX] --> MUX3
    IDATA[Immediate Data from Code ROM] --> MUX4

    MUX1 --> ALU
    MUX2 --> ALU
    MUX3 --> ALU
    MUX4 --> ALU
    ACC[Accumulator A E0H] --> ALU

    ALU --> ACC2[Updated Accumulator A]
    ALU --> PSW[Program Status Word D0H]

    PSW --> F1[Bit 7 CY Carry]
    PSW --> F2[Bit 6 AC Aux Carry]
    PSW --> F3[Bit 2 OV Overflow]
    PSW --> F4[Bit 0 P Parity]

    ALU --> BREG[B Register F0H used only by MUL DIV]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **B register (SFR address F0H)** is exclusively used by `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`. For all other arithmetic instructions, B is a general-purpose register in the upper half of **Register Bank 3** (addresses 0x0F, 0x1F, 0x2F, 0x3F). Misusing B as a multiplier register during an MUL/DIV operation is a common bug source.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

### Question 1: Differentiate between `ADD` and `ADDC` instructions of 8051. When is `ADDC` preferred?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **ADD A, src** performs binary addition of the Accumulator and source operand, storing the result in A. It updates CY, AC, OV, and P flags.
- **ADDC A, src** performs the same addition **plus the current Carry flag**: $A \leftarrow A + src + C$.
- **ADDC** is preferred during **multi-byte (multi-precision) addition** of numbers wider than 8 bits (e.g., 16-bit, 24-bit, or 32-bit operands). After the lower byte is added with plain `ADD`, the resulting CY (carry-out) is automatically chained into the higher byte's addition via `ADDC`, ensuring mathematical correctness without manual carry-tracking.

---

### Question 2: What is the purpose of the `DA A` instruction? Why is it placed immediately after `ADD`?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `DA A` (Decimal Adjust Accumulator) converts the **binary sum in the Accumulator** into valid **BCD (Binary Coded Decimal)** form by adding 0x06, 0x60, or 0x66 to the Accumulator depending on the values of the lower nibble, upper nibble, and the CY flag.
- It is placed **immediately after `ADD`** (and never after `SUBB`/`MUL`/`DIV`) because its correction logic is **only valid for a *just-completed* binary addition** of two BCD operands. Inserting any other instruction between `ADD` and `DA A` corrupts the flag states (AC, CY) on which the adjustment depends, producing incorrect BCD output.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, ESE Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain the PSW register of 8051. List all flags affected by the `ADD`, `SUBB`, and `MUL` instructions with appropriate examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is a Special Function Register located at internal RAM address **D0H**. It contains the four arithmetic flags plus two register-bank-select bits and a user-definable flag.

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | CY | Carry flag — set on unsigned overflow out of bit 7 |
| 6 | AC | Auxiliary carry — set on carry out of bit 3 (used by DA A) |
| 5 | F0 | User-defined general-purpose flag |
| 4 | RS1 | Register bank select bit 1 |
| 3 | RS0 | Register bank select bit 0 |
| 2 | OV | Overflow flag — set on signed arithmetic error (C7 ⊕ C6) |
| 1 | — | Reserved (undefined) |
| 0 | P | Parity flag — set if A has odd number of 1's |

**Flag Behaviour of ADD:**
For A = 0x3F, R1 = 0x2D: sum = 0x6C. CY = 0, AC = 1, OV = 0, P = 0 (0x6C = 0110 1100 has four 1's, even).

**Flag Behaviour of SUBB:**
For A = 0x3F, R1 = 0x2D: A − R1 = 0x12. CY = 0 (no borrow needed), AC = 1, OV = 0.

**Flag Behaviour of MUL AB:**
For A = 0x10, B = 0x20: result B = 0x02, A = 0x00. **OV = 1** (B ≠ 0), **CY = 0**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Parity flag (P)** is automatically set/cleared by hardware after *every* instruction that writes to the Accumulator, including ADD, ADDC, SUBB, MUL, DIV, and DA A. It reflects the *new* A, not the operands.

#### (b) Write an 8051 assembly program to subtract two **16-bit unsigned numbers** stored in internal RAM at **20H (LSB), 21H (MSB)** and **30H (LSB), 31H (MSB)**. Store the difference in **40H (LSB), 41H (MSB)**. Use comments. **(7 marks)**

**Model Program:**

```assembly
        ; 16-bit subtraction: (21H,20H) - (31H,30H) -> (41H,40H)
        MOV  A, 20H        ; A = LSB of minuend
        CLR  C             ; CY = 0 (no borrow for first byte)
        SUBB A, 30H        ; A = LSB1 - LSB2 - 0
        MOV  40H, A        ; Store difference LSB
        MOV  A, 21H        ; A = MSB of minuend
        SUBB A, 31H        ; A = MSB1 - MSB2 - CY
        MOV  41H, A        ; Store difference MSB
        ; After execution, CY = 1 indicates the minuend < subtrahend
HERE:   SJMP HERE          ; Halt (trap loop)
```

**Why `CLR C` is mandatory:**
The 8051 instruction set contains *no* plain `SUB` — every subtraction uses `SUBB` (subtract with borrow). If CY is not explicitly cleared before the first byte, the result will be off by 1, and the multi-byte subtraction will produce a wrong answer. This is the **#1 student mistake** in 8051 arithmetic exams.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) Explain the `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` instructions in detail. Show the contents of A and B registers after executing the following code: A = 0x55, B = 0x03 initially, then `MUL AB` followed by `DIV AB`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**`MUL AB`:**
- **Opcode:** 0xA4 (1 byte)
- **Operation:** The 8-bit unsigned value in A is multiplied by the 8-bit unsigned value in B. The 16-bit result is stored with the **high byte in B** and the **low byte in A**.
- **Cycles:** 4
- **Flags:** CY is always cleared; OV is set if the high byte (B) is non-zero, else cleared.

**`DIV AB`:**
- **Opcode:** 0x84 (1 byte)
- **Operation:** A is divided by B. The integer **quotient** goes to A and the **remainder** goes to B. If B = 0 (division by zero), the values of A and B are left undefined and OV = 1.
- **Cycles:** 4
- **Flags:** CY is always cleared; OV is set only if B was 0.

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Initial: } A = 0x55 = 85_{10},\ B = 0x03 = 3_{10} \\
&\text{After MUL AB: } A \times B = 85 \times 3 = 255 = 0xFF \\
&\text{High byte in B: } 0x00,\ \text{Low byte in A: } 0xFF \\
&\text{Flags after MUL: } \text{CY} = 0,\ \text{OV} = 0\ (\text{since B} = 0) \\
&\text{After DIV AB: } 0xFF \div 0x03 = 255 \div 3 = 85\ \text{remainder}\ 0 \\
&\text{Quotient in A: } 0x55,\ \text{Remainder in B: } 0x00 \\
&\text{Flags after DIV: } \text{CY} = 0,\ \text{OV} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final State:** A = **0x55**, B = **0x00**, OV = 0, CY = 0.

#### (b) With a neat program, demonstrate BCD addition of two 3-digit decimal numbers (e.g., 123 + 456) using 8051 arithmetic instructions. Explain the role of the AC flag. **(7 marks)**

**Model Program:**

```assembly
        ; BCD addition: 123 + 456 = 579
        ; Storage: 21H,20H = 01,23 (MSB packed) and 31H,30H = 04,56
        ; Result stored at 41H,40H
        MOV  A, 20H        ; A = 23H (units and tens)
        ADD  A, 30H        ; A = 23H + 56H = 79H
        DA   A             ; Adjust: low nibble 3+6=9, ok; high nibble 2+5+0=7, ok
        MOV  40H, A        ; 40H = 79H (lower 2 BCD digits of result)
        MOV  A, 21H        ; A = 01H (hundreds)
        ADDC A, 31H        ; A = 01H + 04H + 0 (no carry from lower byte)
        DA   A             ; Adjust: 01+04=05H, no correction needed
        MOV  41H, A        ; 41H = 05H (hundreds digit of result)
        ; Final: (41H,40H) = 05H,79H = decimal 579 ✓
HERE:   SJMP HERE
```

**Role of AC (Auxiliary Carry):**
- AC is the **bit-3-to-bit-4 carry** indicator. It is set whenever there is a carry from the lower 4-bit nibble (bits 0–3) into the upper 4-bit nibble (bits 4–7).
- The `DA A` instruction uses AC to decide whether to add **0x06** to the Accumulator. If AC = 1, the lower nibble's BCD digit has overflowed past 9, and an adjustment of 0x06 must be applied to wrap the digit back into valid BCD (0–9) range.
- **Without AC, BCD arithmetic via binary ALU would be impossible**, since the hardware has no other way to detect decimal overflow in the low nibble.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. Forgetting `CLR C` before multi-byte SUBB → **2 marks deducted**.
> 2. Writing `INC DPTR` or `DEC DPTR` as a `DPTR = DPTR ± 1` operation without noting that **DEC DPTR is INVALID** in 8051 → **1 mark deducted**.
> 3. Stating that `ADD` sets only CY and AC (forgetting OV and P) → **1 mark deducted**.
> 4. Treating `DA A` as a general-purpose decimal converter (it works *only* after `ADD`, never after `SUBB` or `MUL`) → **2 marks deducted**.
> 5. In MUL/DIV trace problems, writing "A = 85 × 3 = 255" without explicitly noting that **B becomes 0x00 and A becomes 0xFF** → **1 mark deducted**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10 Golden Rules of 8051 Arithmetic Instructions — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam:**

- [x] **Rule 1:** The Accumulator (A) is **mandatory** in every ADD/ADDC/SUBB instruction. The only exceptions are MUL, DIV, DA A, INC, and DEC.
- [x] **Rule 2:** `ADD` and `ADDC` differ only in the extra `+ CY` — use ADDC for multi-byte addition.
- [x] **Rule 3:** There is **no plain SUB** — `SUBB` always subtracts the carry flag. Always `CLR C` before the *least significant byte* of any multi-byte subtraction.
- [x] **Rule 4:** `INC` and `DEC` **do not modify any flag** — not even on wraparound from 0xFF to 0x00.
- [x] **Rule 5:** `INC DPTR` is a **valid 1-byte, 2-cycle** instruction. `DEC DPTR` **does not exist**.
- [x] **Rule 6:** `MUL AB` stores the **high byte in B** and the **low byte in A**. OV = 1 means the result exceeded 8 bits.
- [x] **Rule 7:** `DIV AB` stores the **quotient in A** and the **remainder in B**. OV = 1 only on **division by zero**.
- [x] **Rule 8:** `DA A` **must immediately follow** an `ADD` (or `ADDC`) on BCD operands. It is the only arithmetic instruction whose flag effect is *input-dependent* (reads CY and AC).
- [x] **Rule 9:** The **Parity flag (P)** reflects the *new* Accumulator after any instruction that writes to A.
- [x] **Rule 10:** The **Overflow flag (OV)** is computed as **C7 ⊕ C6** (carry-out XOR carry-in of bit 7). It signals **signed arithmetic** errors, not unsigned ones.

> **Final Mnemonic for the OV Formula:**
> **"OV = C out XOR C in of MSB"** — if these two carries disagree, your signed result has flipped sign, and the 8-bit signed answer is wrong.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller architecture**, **Data Transfer** refers to the set of instructions that copy the contents of a source operand (the *source*) into a destination operand (the *destination*) **without altering the source value**. Data transfer operations form the most fundamental class of instructions in any processor instruction set architecture (ISA), and the 8051 implements a rich variety of such instructions, often referred to collectively as the **MOV-family**, **stack operations**, and **exchange operations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *Data transfer instructions in the 8051 are those instructions that move (copy) a byte of data between the accumulator, general-purpose registers, internal RAM (data memory), external data memory, program memory (code ROM), and the stack. Except for the **XCH**, **XCHD**, and **SWAP** operations, the source is **never modified**.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workshop Storage Room"

Imagine an **electronics workshop** with the following storage locations:

- **Workbench (Accumulator, A)** – The place where the technician is currently working. It is the *fastest accessible* workspace.
- **Personal toolbox (Registers R0–R7)** – A small set of 8 labelled drawers at the workbench. The active bank gives you 8 drawers; switching banks swaps the *labelling* of these drawers.
- **Cupboard shelves (Internal RAM, 00H–7FH)** – 128 labelled cubbyholes on the workshop wall.
- **External warehouse (External RAM, 0000H–FFFFH)** – A separate building that can be accessed only with a special request slip.
- **Manufacturer's manual (Program/Code ROM)** – A read-only reference book, used to fetch constants or lookup tables.
- **In/Out tray (Stack)** – A vertical pile of trays where items can only be added to the top or removed from the top.

**Data transfer** is simply the act of *moving a part from one of these locations to another*. The **format of the addressing** tells the microcontroller *where the part is located* and *how to read the location label*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constants/metrics in 8051 Data Transfer:**
> - **Internal RAM size = 128 bytes** (address range **00H to 7FH**)
> - **SFR (Special Function Register) space = 128 bytes** (address range **80H to FFH**)
> - **External data memory = up to 64 KB** (address range **0000H to FFFFH**)
> - **Program memory (code) = up to 64 KB** (address range **0000H to FFFFH**)
> - **Register file size = 8 bytes per bank × 4 banks = 32 bytes** (00H–1FH)

## 1.3 Why Data Transfer is the "First Family" of Instructions

In any C/C++/Assembly program running on a microcontroller, **~30–40% of total executed instructions** are data-movement instructions. The reason is simple — to perform arithmetic on a value, you must first *bring it to the accumulator*; to output it, you must *copy it to a port*. **No useful computation can occur without data transfer.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of 8051 Data Transfer Instructions

The 8051 data transfer group is formally divided into **five sub-categories** as per the official Intel MCS-51 architecture manual and adopted in the **KTU PBRUT404 syllabus**:

| **S.No.** | **Sub-Category** | **Mnemonic(s)** | **Function** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Move constant / register / memory | **MOV** | Copy data between registers, RAM, SFRs |
| 2 | Move code byte (lookup) | **MOVC** | Read byte from program ROM (LPM) |
| 3 | Move external data | **MOVX** | Read/Write external data memory |
| 4 | Stack operations | **PUSH**, **POP** | Save/restore data on the stack |
| 5 | Exchange & Swap | **XCH**, **XCHD**, **SWAP** | Swap bytes/lower nibbles between A and RAM |

## 2.2 The Five Addressing Modes for Data Transfer

The 8051 supports **five addressing modes**, and *every* data transfer instruction uses exactly one of them. The mode is determined by the **opcode format and the operand syntax**.

### (a) Immediate Addressing — `MOV A, #25H`

The data is *embedded inside the instruction*. The "#" sign denotes "this is the actual value, not an address."

- **Syntax:** `MOV destination, #data8`
- **Encoding:** 2 bytes (opcode + immediate byte)
- **Example:** `MOV A, #0FH`  →  A ← **0FH** (15 decimal)
- **KTU Highlight:** *Frequently used to initialize counters, load constants, and set up lookup pointers.*

### (b) Register Addressing — `MOV A, R0`

The data resides in one of the **8 general-purpose registers (R0–R7)** of the currently active register bank. **No RAM access is needed**, making this the *fastest* mode (1 machine cycle).

- **Syntax:** `MOV A, Rn`  (where n = 0..7)
- **Encoding:** 1 byte (opcode embeds the register number in 3 bits)
- **Active bank selected by PSW bits RS1, RS0**

| **RS1** | **RS0** | **Active Bank** | **RAM Addresses** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 00H – 07H |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 08H – 0FH |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 10H – 17H |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 18H – 1FH |

### (c) Direct Addressing — `MOV A, 30H`

The instruction explicitly provides the **8-bit internal RAM address** (00H–FFH). The upper 128 bytes (80H–FFH) are the **SFR space**.

- **Syntax:** `MOV A, direct`
- **Encoding:** 2 bytes (opcode + direct address)
- **Example:** `MOV A, 30H` → A ← RAM[30H]
- **Example (SFR):** `MOV P1, A` → Port 1 latch ← A

### (d) Register Indirect Addressing — `MOV A, @R0`

The address of the data is *held inside* the register R0 or R1. The "@" sign denotes "the contents of R0 is the *address* of the data."

- **Syntax:** `MOV A, @Ri`  (i = 0 or 1 only)
- **Encoding:** 1 byte
- **Example:** `MOV A, @R0` → A ← RAM[[R0]]
- **Pointer wraps to 16 bits** when used with `MOVX` (for external memory).

### (e) Indexed Addressing — `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`

Used *exclusively* with `MOVC` to read program memory. The address = **(A) + (DPTR)** or **(A) + (PC)**.

- **Syntax:** `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`  or  `MOVC A, @A+PC`
- **Encoding:** 1 byte
- **Used in:** Lookup tables (LUT) for sine waves, 7-segment codes, ASCII conversion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are essential for KTU exam problems involving pointer arithmetic and address computations in data transfer.

| **Formula / Rule** | **LaTeX Form** | **Description** |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| 16-bit external data address | $A_{16} = (P2 \ll 8) \;\vert\; P1$ | High byte from P2, low byte from P1 |
| 16-bit code address (MOVC) | $A_{16} = A + DPTR$ | Lookup table address with DPTR base |
| 16-bit code address (MOVC, PC) | $A_{16} = (PC) + 1 + A$ | PC auto-increments past opcode |
| Stack pointer after PUSH | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 1$ | SP increments *before* write |
| Stack pointer after POP | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 1$ | SP decrements *after* read |
| Internal RAM size | $N_{IRAM} = 128 \text{ bytes}$ | Address 00H – 7FH |
| External data memory | $N_{XRAM} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes}$ | Address 0000H – FFFFH |
| Program memory (code) | $N_{ROM} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes}$ | Address 0000H – FFFFH |
| Program counter increment | $PC_{new} = PC_{old} + \text{opcode bytes}$ | Auto-advance for next fetch |
| Cycle-time (XTAL = 11.0592 MHz) | $T_{cyc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}} = 1.085\,\mu s$ | 1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical memory boundary rule (KCU/KTU exam favorite):**
> - `MOVC` accesses **code ROM only** (always read).
> - `MOVX` accesses **external data RAM** (read/write).
> - `MOV` (without C or X) **never** touches external memory.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application** | **Data Transfer Instruction Used** | **Why?** |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Reading keypad scan codes | `MOV A, P1`  (direct) | Captures port snapshot |
| Stepper motor sequencing | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | Lookup of next phase code |
| LCD character display | `MOV P0, A` | Sends ASCII byte to LCD bus |
| Stack-based local variables | `PUSH/POP` | C-compiler (Keil) uses for call frames |
| Multiplexed 7-segment display | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | Reads segment LUT from external EEPROM |
| Interrupt context save | `PUSH ACC; PUSH PSW` | Standard ISR prologue |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Stack Operation Mechanics

**Problem:** Show the *exact* state of the stack pointer (SP) and internal RAM when the following 8051 code executes, assuming **SP = 07H** initially.

```assembly
MOV 30H, #0ABH
MOV 31H, #0CDH
PUSH 30H
PUSH 31H
POP  30H
POP  31H
```

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Initial state (before any instruction):**

$$
SP = 07H \quad ; \quad RAM[30H] = xx \quad ; \quad RAM[31H] = xx
$$

**Step 1:** `MOV 30H, #0ABH` — 2 bytes, 1 cycle.

$$
RAM[30H] \leftarrow 0ABH \quad ; \quad SP = 07H \text{ (unchanged)}
$$

**Step 2:** `MOV 31H, #0CDH` — 2 bytes, 1 cycle.

$$
RAM[31H] \leftarrow 0CDH \quad ; \quad SP = 07H \text{ (unchanged)}
$$

**Step 3:** `PUSH 30H` — 2 bytes, 2 cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the 8051, **PUSH first increments SP, then writes**. This is the *opposite* order from the 8085/Z80 microprocessors.

$$
SP \leftarrow SP + 1 = 08H
$$
$$
RAM[08H] \leftarrow RAM[30H] = 0ABH
$$

**Step 4:** `PUSH 31H` — 2 bytes, 2 cycles.

$$
SP \leftarrow SP + 1 = 09H
$$
$$
RAM[09H] \leftarrow RAM[31H] = 0CDH
$$

**Step 5:** `POP 30H` — 2 bytes, 2 cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> In the 8051, **POP first reads from RAM, then decrements SP** (opposite of PUSH increment order).

$$
RAM[30H] \leftarrow RAM[SP] = RAM[09H] = 0CDH
$$
$$
SP \leftarrow SP - 1 = 08H
$$

**Step 6:** `POP 31H` — 2 bytes, 2 cycles.

$$
RAM[31H] \leftarrow RAM[SP] = RAM[08H] = 0ABH
$$
$$
SP \leftarrow SP - 1 = 07H
$$

### Final State Verification

$$
RAM[30H] = 0CDH \quad ; \quad RAM[31H] = 0ABH \quad ; \quad SP = 07H
$$

The two bytes have been **swapped** using only PUSH and POP — a classic interview/exam trick question. **SP always returns to its original value 07H.**

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — MOVC Lookup Table Address Computation

**Problem:** Given the 8051 program fragment below, compute the **exact program memory address** that the `MOVC` instruction reads from when **A = 04H** initially.

```assembly
ORG  0200H        ; Program starts at 0200H
MOV  DPTR, #0300H ; DPTR = 0300H
MOV  A, #04H      ; A = 04H
MOVC A, @A+DPTR   ; Read from code ROM
...               ; (assume next instruction is at 0208H)
```

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Initialize base pointer.

$$
DPTR = 0300H
$$

**Step 2:** Initialize accumulator offset.

$$
A = 04H
$$

**Step 3:** Compute effective code memory address.

$$
A_{eff} = (A) + (DPTR) = 04H + 0300H
$$

$$
A_{eff} = 0304H
$$

**Step 4:** Byte fetched into A.

$$
A \leftarrow ROM[0304H]
$$

### Verification using the alternate `@A+PC` form

If the same instruction were `MOVC A, @A+PC` (with PC pointing to the byte *after* the MOVC opcode):

$$
A_{eff} = (PC) + (A) = (0205H)_{approx} + 04H
$$

The PC-relative form is **position-dependent** (bad for relocatable code), while the `@A+DPTR` form is **position-independent** (good for relocatable LUT).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Addressing Mode Quick Comparison

| **Parameter** | **Immediate** | **Register** | **Direct** | **Register Indirect** | **Indexed** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Symbol | `#data` | `Rn` | `addr8` | `@Ri` | `@A+DPTR` |
| Opcode bytes | 2–3 | 1 | 2–3 | 1 | 1 |
| Machine cycles | 1 | 1 | 1–2 | 1–2 | 2 |
| Memory accessed | None (constant) | Register file | Internal RAM/SFR | Internal RAM | Program ROM |
| Source modifiable? | No | No | No | No | No |
| Used with MOVC? | No | No | No | No | **Yes (only)** |
| Used with MOVX? | No | No | No | **Yes (R0/R1 = 16-bit)** | No |

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation 3 — External Data Memory Address via MOVX

**Problem:** A C program running on the 8051 executes the following assembly:

```assembly
MOV  P2, #0FH
MOV  P1, #0A0H
MOV  R0, #55H
MOVX A, @R0
```

Compute the **16-bit external RAM address** that is accessed.

### Solution

The 8051 uses a **multiplexed address/data bus** on Port 0, with the high byte latched using the **ALE** signal. When `MOVX` executes:

- **P2** holds the **upper 8 bits** of the 16-bit address (A15–A8).
- **R0 (or DPTR low byte)** holds the **lower 8 bits** (A7–A0).

$$
A_{15\text{-}8} = (P2) = 0FH
$$
$$
A_{7\text{-}0} = (R0) = 55H
$$

$$
A_{16} = (0FH \ll 8) \;\vert\; 55H = 0F55H
$$

The byte at external address `0F55H` is copied into A. **P1 is irrelevant** in this calculation — it serves as a general-purpose I/O port, *not* an address bus pin, unless the designer specifically chooses to demultiplex external memory using latches like **74HC573** with P1 as the high address byte (a common but non-standard configuration).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Data Transfer Instruction Family in 8051

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[8051 Data Transfer Instruction Family] --> nodeA[MOV Instructions]
    nodeStart --> nodeB[Stack Operations]
    nodeStart --> nodeC[Exchange and Swap]
    nodeStart --> nodeD[Code Memory Access]
    nodeStart --> nodeE[External Memory Access]

    nodeA --> nodeA1[Immediate: MOV A, #data]
    nodeA --> nodeA2[Register: MOV A, Rn]
    nodeA --> nodeA3[Direct: MOV A, addr8]
    nodeA --> nodeA4[Register Indirect: MOV A, @Ri]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[PUSH direct]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[POP direct]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[XCH A, Rn]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[XCH A, direct]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[XCHD A, @Ri]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[SWAP A]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[MOVC A, @A+DPTR]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[MOVC A, @A+PC]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[MOVX A, @Ri 8 bit ptr]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[MOVX A, @DPTR 16 bit ptr]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[MOVX @Ri, A]
    nodeE --> nodeE4[MOVX @DPTR, A]

    style nodeStart fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style nodeA fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    style nodeB fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    style nodeC fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    style nodeD fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    style nodeE fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Data Flow Between 8051 Memory Spaces

```mermaid
graph LR
    ACC[Accumulator A] -->|MOV direct| IRAM[Internal RAM 00H to 7FH]
    ACC -->|MOV direct| SFR[SFR Space 80H to FFH]
    ACC -->|MOVX @DPTR| XRAM[External Data RAM 0000H to FFFFH]
    ACC -->|MOVC @A+DPTR| ROM[Program Code ROM 0000H to FFFFH]
    IRAM -->|MOV A direct| ACC
    SFR -->|MOV A direct| ACC
    XRAM -->|MOVX A @DPTR| ACC
    ROM -->|MOVC A @A+DPTR| ACC
    SP[Stack Pointer SP] -->|PUSH direct| IRAM2[Internal RAM Stack Area]
    IRAM2 -->|POP direct| SP

    style ACC fill:#c00000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style IRAM fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
    style SFR fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
    style XRAM fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff
    style ROM fill:#7030a0,color:#fff
    style SP fill:#ffc000,color:#000
    style IRAM2 fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Selecting the Correct MOV Variant

```mermaid
graph TD
    q1{What is the source of the data?}
    q1 -->|Constant inside instruction| q2{Which destination?}
    q1 -->|A general register Rn| q3[MOV dest, Rn]
    q1 -->|A specific RAM address| q4[MOV dest, direct]
    q1 -->|An address held in R0 or R1| q5[MOV dest, @Ri]
    q1 -->|A byte in program ROM| q6[MOVC A, @A+DPTR or @A+PC]
    q1 -->|A byte in external data RAM| q7[MOVX A, @DPTR or @Ri]

    q2 -->|To accumulator| q2a[MOV A, #data]
    q2 -->|To a register| q2b[MOV Rn, #data]
    q2 -->|To direct RAM| q2c[MOV direct, #data]
    q2 -->|To DPTR| q2d[MOV DPTR, #data16]

    style q1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    style q2 fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    style q2a fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
    style q2b fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
    style q2c fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
    style q2d fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
    style q3 fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
    style q4 fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
    style q5 fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
    style q6 fill:#7030a0,color:#fff
    style q7 fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between `MOV`, `MOVC`, and `MOVX` instructions in the 8051 microcontroller.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

- **`MOV` (Move):** Used to transfer data *within* the **internal data memory space** (RAM 00H–7FH and SFRs 80H–FFH). It does **not** access any external or program memory. Operands can use any of the five addressing modes. *(1 mark)*
- **`MOVC` (Move Code):** Used to read a byte from the **program code memory (ROM)**. The accumulator is *always* the destination. The source address is computed as `A + DPTR` or `A + PC`. *(1 mark)*
- **`MOVX` (Move External):** Used to read from or write to the **external data memory** (up to 64 KB). When R0/R1 is used as pointer, it forms an 8-bit address; when DPTR is used, it forms a 16-bit address. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the function of the `SWAP` and `XCHD` instructions with one example each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

- **`SWAP A`:** Interchanges the **upper nibble (bits 4–7)** with the **lower nibble (bits 0–3)** of the accumulator, without using any other register.
  Example: If A = 7AH (0111 1010), then after `SWAP A`, A = A7H (1010 0111). *(1.5 marks)*
- **`XCHD A, @R0`:** Exchanges only the **lower nibble** of the accumulator with the **lower nibble** of the internal RAM byte pointed to by R0. The upper nibbles remain unchanged.
  Example: If A = ABH and RAM[20H] = 7CH, then after `XCHD A, @R0`, A = ACH and RAM[20H] = 7BH. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **five addressing modes** of the 8051 microcontroller with at least **one example instruction** for each. State which addressing mode is used to access the **SFRs**.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat **memory map**, describe the **internal RAM organization** of the 8051, including the four register banks, the bit-addressable area, and the general-purpose RAM area. Indicate which areas are accessed using **direct** and which using **register-indirect** addressing.

---

#### Model Answer for Q-A(a) — 7 marks

The 8051 supports **five addressing modes** for data transfer and arithmetic operations.

**1. Immediate Addressing** *(1 mark)*
The operand is a constant encoded inside the instruction. Used to load known values.
```assembly
MOV A, #25H     ; A ← 25H
MOV DPTR, #2000H ; DPTR ← 2000H
```

**2. Register Addressing** *(1 mark)*
The operand is one of the 8 working registers R0–R7 of the currently selected bank. The fastest mode (1 byte, 1 cycle).
```assembly
MOV A, R3        ; A ← R3
ADD A, R0        ; A ← A + R0
```

**3. Direct Addressing** *(1.5 marks)*
The operand is the **8-bit address** of an internal RAM or SFR byte.
```assembly
MOV A, 30H       ; A ← RAM[30H]
MOV P1, A        ; Port 1 ← A
```
**SFRs are accessed using direct addressing only.** Examples: `MOV A, P0` (P0 = 80H), `MOV TMOD, #01H` (TMOD = 89H).

**4. Register Indirect Addressing** *(1.5 marks)*
The address of the operand is *contained in* R0 or R1 (use `@` symbol). R0/R1 are the only allowed pointer registers for internal RAM.
```assembly
MOV A, @R0       ; A ← RAM[(R0)]
MOV @R1, A       ; RAM[(R1)] ← A
```

**5. Indexed Addressing** *(2 marks)*
Used exclusively with `MOVC`. The effective code address is the sum of the accumulator and a 16-bit base register (DPTR or PC).
```assembly
MOVC A, @A+DPTR  ; A ← ROM[A + DPTR]
```

---

#### Model Answer for Q-A(b) — 7 marks

The 8051 internal data memory (lower 128 bytes) is organized as follows:

| **Address Range** | **Region** | **Size** | **Access Method** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 00H – 07H | Register Bank 0 (R0–R7) | 8 B | Direct / Register |
| 08H – 0FH | Register Bank 1 | 8 B | Direct / Register |
| 10H – 17H | Register Bank 2 | 8 B | Direct / Register |
| 18H – 1FH | Register Bank 3 | 8 B | Direct / Register |
| 20H – 2FH | Bit-Addressable Area | 16 B | Direct / Bit |
| 30H – 7FH | General-Purpose RAM | 80 B | Direct / Indirect |

**Key points:** *(2 marks)*
- Only **one** of the four register banks is *active* at a time, chosen by PSW bits RS1, RS0.
- Bit-addressable region 20H–2FH allows `SETB 20H`, `CLR 7FH`, etc., giving **128 individually addressable bits** (16 bytes × 8 bits).
- The upper 128 bytes (80H–FFH) overlap in address with the SFR space but are physically distinct — they are accessed **only by direct addressing**.

**Memory map diagram (text representation):** *(2 marks)*

```
  7FH  ┌────────────────┐
       │  General RAM   │   ← 80 bytes
  30H  ├────────────────┤
       │ Bit-Addr 20-2F │   ← 16 bytes
  20H  ├────────────────┤
       │  Register Bk 3 │
  18H  ├────────────────┤
       │  Register Bk 2 │
  10H  ├────────────────┤
       │  Register Bk 1 │
  08H  ├────────────────┤
       │  Register Bk 0 │  ← Reset default
  00H  └────────────────┘
```

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the working of **PUSH** and **POP** instructions in the 8051 with a suitable example. Show the changes in the **Stack Pointer (SP)** at each step, assuming SP is initially 07H. Mention why SP is initialized to 07H after reset.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write an 8051 assembly program to **add two 8-bit numbers** stored in internal RAM locations `30H` and `31H`, and store the result in `32H`. Use appropriate **MOV** instructions and explain every step. If the result exceeds FFH, indicate how the **carry flag** can be tested using a conditional jump.

---

#### Model Answer for Q-B(a) — 7 marks

**Theory (3 marks):** PUSH and POP are data transfer instructions that use the **stack** — a LIFO (Last In, First Out) memory structure pointed to by the **SP (Stack Pointer) register** (SFR address 81H).

- **`PUSH direct`**: Increments SP by 1, then copies the byte from `direct` into RAM[SP]. *(2 bytes, 2 cycles)*
- **`POP direct`**: Copies RAM[SP] into `direct`, then decrements SP by 1. *(2 bytes, 2 cycles)*

**Example** (Initial SP = 07H) *(3 marks)*:

```assembly
MOV 30H, #0ABH    ; RAM[30H] = ABH
MOV 31H, #0CDH    ; RAM[31H] = CDH
PUSH 30H          ; SP=08H, RAM[08H] = ABH
PUSH 31H          ; SP=09H, RAM[09H] = CDH
POP  30H          ; RAM[30H] = CDH, SP=08H
POP  31H          ; RAM[31H] = ABH, SP=07H
```

**After reset, SP = 07H** because the **reset value of the SFR SP is 07H** in the 8051 design. This deliberately points SP just *above* Register Bank 1, so stack growth (SP incrementing upward) does not corrupt the working registers. Programmers usually move SP to 30H or higher to use general-purpose RAM as stack space.

---

#### Model Answer for Q-B(b) — 7 marks

**Program (5 marks):**
```assembly
; Program: Add RAM[30H] and RAM[31H], store sum in RAM[32H]
        MOV  A, 30H       ; A ← RAM[30H]  (1)
        ADD  A, 31H       ; A ← A + RAM[31H], CY updated (2)
        MOV  32H, A       ; RAM[32H] ← A   (3)
        JNC  NO_CARRY     ; If CY = 0, jump to NO_CARRY (4)
        SETB P1.0         ; If CY = 1, set P1.0 (overflow indicator) (5)
        SJMP END_PROG
NO_CARRY:
        CLR  P1.0         ; Clear P1.0
END_PROG:
        NOP               ; End
```

**Step-by-step explanation** (2 marks):
1. **Line (1):** Loads the first operand from RAM[30H] into the accumulator using **direct addressing** — necessary because arithmetic can only be performed on A.
2. **Line (2):** The `ADD` instruction adds the contents of RAM[31H] to A. If the result exceeds FFH, the **Carry Flag (CY)** in the PSW register is set to 1; otherwise it is cleared.
3. **Line (3):** The result in A is stored back into RAM[32H] using direct addressing.
4. **Line (4):** `JNC` (Jump if No Carry) tests CY. If CY = 0, the program branches past the overflow flag-setting code.
5. **Line (5):** If the addition overflowed, the program sets **P1.0** (an LED or buzzer line connected to the user) to indicate the overflow condition. `SJMP` is used to skip the clear instruction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing PUSH/POP order:** Students often write "PUSH first writes, then increments SP." This is **WRONG** for the 8051. Correct order is: **PUSH → increment → write**, **POP → read → decrement.** *(Loses ~1 mark in stack questions.)*
> 2. **Treating `MOVC` as a generic MOV:** Many students think `MOVC` accesses data RAM. It accesses **Code ROM only** — the "C" stands for Code. *(Loses 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions.)*
> 3. **Forgetting that R2–R7 cannot be used for register indirect addressing:** Only **R0 and R1** can hold an address. Using `@R2` or `@R5` is illegal in 8051. *(Loses ~1 mark.)*
> 4. **Initial SP confusion:** SP resets to **07H** in the 8051 (not 00H as in 8085). *(Loses 1 mark if quoted as 00H.)*
> 5. **SFR access via indirect addressing:** SFRs (80H–FFH) **cannot** be accessed using `@R0`/`@R1` — only `MOV direct` works. *(Loses 1–2 marks.)*

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Data transfer instructions** copy data — the source is **not modified** (except `XCH`, `XCHD`, `SWAP`).
- The 8051 has **five addressing modes**: Immediate (`#`), Register (`Rn`), Direct (`addr8`), Register Indirect (`@Ri`), Indexed (`@A+DPTR`/`@A+PC`).
- **`MOV`** → internal RAM + SFRs only.
- **`MOVC`** → Code ROM only; A is always destination.
- **`MOVX`** → External data RAM only; uses `@R0/@R1` (8-bit) or `@DPTR` (16-bit).
- **`PUSH` first increments SP, then writes**; **`POP` first reads, then decrements SP** — this is the reverse of 8085.
- **SP resets to 07H**; the upper nibble-cleared value `07H` is the reset default.
- **Register Bank** is selected by PSW bits **RS1 (PSW.4)** and **RS0 (PSW.3)**.
- **Bit-addressable area** is `20H–2FH` → 128 individual bits (`00H–7FH` of bit space).
- The upper 128 bytes of internal data space (80H–FFH) are **SFRs**, accessible *only by direct addressing*.
- Only **R0 and R1** can be used as pointer registers (`@R0`, `@R1`).
- `SWAP A` exchanges upper and lower nibbles of A; `XCHD A, @Ri` exchanges only the lower nibble.
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` is **position-independent** (recommended); `MOVC A, @A+PC` is **position-dependent**.
- **One machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**; for 11.0592 MHz crystal, T_cyc = **1.085 μs**.
- **External data memory** is accessed via the **multiplexed bus** on P0 (low byte + data) and P2 (high byte), latched using **ALE** and an external 74-series latch (e.g., 74HC573).
- For **16-bit MOVX address**: $A_{16} = (P2 \ll 8) \;\vert\; (\text{low-byte from DPTR or P0})$.
- Stack grows **upward** in 8051 (unlike 8085/8086 which can be configured to grow downward).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Control Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Control Transfer Instructions** in a microcontroller are a class of machine-level instructions that explicitly alter the sequential flow of program execution by modifying the **Program Counter (PC)**. They enable the processor to deviate from the default "fetch–decode–execute" linear progression, thereby supporting **conditional branching**, **unconditional jumps**, **subroutine invocations**, and **hardware interrupt servicing**.

In the **8051 architecture** (the canonical reference microcontroller for KTU PBRUT404), the Program Counter is a **16-bit register** capable of addressing up to **64 KB** of program memory (address range `0x0000` to `0xFFFF`). Every control transfer instruction manipulates this 16-bit PC in a defined way.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the 8051 microcontroller, control transfer is classified into four major categories:
> 1. **Unconditional Jump Instructions** (`LJMP`, `AJMP`, `SJMP`, `JMP @A+DPTR`)
> 2. **Conditional Jump Instructions** (`JZ`, `JNZ`, `JC`, `JNC`, `DJNZ`, `CJNE`)
> 3. **Subroutine Call and Return** (`LCALL`, `ACALL`, `RET`)
> 4. **Interrupt-based Control Transfer** (Hardware-triggered PC redirection + `RETI`)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Train Conductor"

Imagine a train running on a single, straight railway track. By default, the train moves from **Station 1 → Station 2 → Station 3 → ...** in strict sequence. The **Program Counter** is like the conductor's clipboard, always pointing to the *next* station (memory address) the train must visit.

- **Normal execution** = Train follows the straight track (incrementing PC by 1, 2, or 3 bytes depending on instruction).
- **Unconditional jump (`LJMP`)** = The conductor rips up the schedule and says, "We are going directly to **Station 50**, no matter what."
- **Conditional jump (`JZ`)** = The conductor checks a signal flag. "If the red flag is up, go to **Station 20**; otherwise, continue to the next station."
- **Subroutine call (`LCALL`)** = The conductor parks the train, takes a memo of the *current station* in his pocket (pushes PC onto the **Stack**), and runs a quick errand to a sub-station. When finished, he looks at the memo (`RET`) and resumes the original journey.
- **Interrupt** = An emergency phone call from the station master. The conductor immediately writes down where he is, handles the emergency (Interrupt Service Routine), and then returns to the original journey.

This analogy directly maps to the microcontroller's **PC, flags register (PSW), and stack pointer (SP)** — the three core hardware elements involved in every control transfer.

## 1.3 Physical & Architectural Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical 8051 Hardware Parameters** (must be memorized for KTU exams):
> - **PC width:** 16 bits → Address space = $2^{16} = \mathbf{65{,}536}$ bytes = **64 KB**
> - **Stack Pointer (SP):** 8-bit, points to top of internal RAM (default: `0x07` on reset)
> - **Stack growth direction:** **Upward** (RAM addresses increase on each PUSH)
> - **Return address stack size:** Each call/return uses **2 bytes** on stack (low byte pushed first, then high byte)
> - **Maximum subroutine nesting depth:** Limited only by available stack RAM (default 128 bytes → up to 64 nested calls theoretically)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Underlying Mechanisms of Control Transfer

Every control transfer instruction in the 8051 ultimately works through **one or more** of these three low-level mechanisms:

### Mechanism A — Direct PC Reload (for Jumps)
1. The instruction opcode is fetched from program memory.
2. The instruction's operand(s) are decoded as the **target address**.
3. The PC is **overwritten** with this new target address in the same machine cycle.
4. The next fetch occurs from the target address.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key difference from CALL:** A *jump* does **not** save the return address. A *call* saves the return address on the stack before modifying the PC.

### Mechanism B — Conditional Evaluation against PSW Flags
1. The ALU evaluates a condition (e.g., "Is Accumulator zero?" or "Is Carry flag set?").
2. If the condition is **TRUE**, the PC is modified by the relative offset.
3. If the condition is **FALSE**, the PC simply advances to the next sequential instruction.

The 8051 uses the **Program Status Word (PSW)** register, which contains the critical flags:

$$ \text{PSW} = \underbrace{CY}_{Bit\ 7} \;\vert\; \underbrace{AC}_{Bit\ 6} \;\vert\; \underbrace{F0}_{Bit\ 5} \;\vert\; \underbrace{RS1}_{Bit\ 4} \;\vert\; \underbrace{RS0}_{Bit\ 3} \;\vert\; \underbrace{OV}_{Bit\ 2} \;\vert\; \underbrace{-}_{Bit\ 1} \;\vert\; \underbrace{P}_{Bit\ 0} $$

### Mechanism C — Stack-Based Return Address Storage (for CALL/Interrupt)
1. SP is incremented; the low byte of the current PC is pushed onto the stack.
2. SP is incremented again; the high byte of the current PC is pushed.
3. The PC is loaded with the subroutine's start address.
4. On `RET` / `RETI`, the bytes are popped in reverse order, restoring the original PC.

## 2.2 The Instruction Categories — Detailed Logic Steps

### A. Unconditional Jump Instructions
| Instruction | Operand Size | Jump Range | Bytes | Cycles |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `LJMP addr16` | 16-bit | 64 KB (full) | 3 | 2 |
| `AJMP addr11` | 11-bit | 2 KB (within page) | 2 | 2 |
| `SJMP rel` | 8-bit signed | $-128$ to $+127$ | 2 | 2 |
| `JMP @A+DPTR` | Indexed | 64 KB (computed) | 1 | 2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule — AJMP Page Boundary:**
> `AJMP` uses **11 bits** for the target. Since each program memory page is 2 KB $= 2^{11}$, the upper 5 bits of the PC remain unchanged. This means `AJMP` can only reach addresses within the **same 2 KB page** as the instruction immediately following it.

### B. Conditional Jump Instructions
| Mnemonic | Condition Tested | Action if TRUE |
|---|---|---|
| `JZ rel` | A = 0 | PC $\leftarrow$ PC + rel |
| `JNZ rel` | A $\neq$ 0 | PC $\leftarrow$ PC + rel |
| `JC rel` | CY = 1 | PC $\leftarrow$ PC + rel |
| `JNC rel` | CY = 0 | PC $\leftarrow$ PC + rel |
| `JB bit,rel` | Direct bit = 1 | PC $\leftarrow$ PC + rel |
| `JNB bit,rel` | Direct bit = 0 | PC $\leftarrow$ PC + rel |
| `JBC bit,rel` | Direct bit = 1, then **clear bit** | PC $\leftarrow$ PC + rel, bit $\leftarrow$ 0 |
| `DJNZ Rn,rel` | Decrement Rn, jump if not zero | Rn $\leftarrow$ Rn $-$ 1; if Rn $\neq$ 0 then PC $\leftarrow$ PC + rel |
| `CJNE A,direct,rel` | Compare A with direct byte | If A $\neq$ direct, PC $\leftarrow$ PC + rel; CY set if A $<$ direct |
| `CJNE A,#data,rel` | Compare A with immediate | If A $\neq$ data, PC $\leftarrow$ PC + rel; CY set if A $<$ data |
| `CJNE Rn,#data,rel` | Compare Rn with immediate | If Rn $\neq$ data, PC $\leftarrow$ PC + rel; CY set if Rn $<$ data |
| `CJNE @Ri,#data,rel` | Compare memory with immediate | If (Ri) $\neq$ data, PC $\leftarrow$ PC + rel; CY set if (Ri) $<$ data |

### C. Subroutine Call & Return
| Mnemonic | Function | Stack Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `LCALL addr16` | Long call (full 64 KB range) | Pushes 2-byte return address, increments SP twice |
| `ACALL addr11` | Absolute call (within 2 KB page) | Pushes 2-byte return address, increments SP twice |
| `RET` | Return from subroutine | Pops 2-byte return address into PC, SP decremented twice |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table for Part A & Part B numericals in KTU exams.**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| PC addressable range | $0 \leq \text{PC} \leq 2^{16} - 1$ | $0$ to $65{,}535$ bytes |
| Relative jump offset | $-128 \leq \text{rel} \leq +127$ | Signed 8-bit, two's complement |
| Forward jump max | $+127$ bytes from PC of next instruction | $127 \times 1$ address |
| Backward jump max | $-128$ bytes from PC of next instruction | $-128 \times 1$ address |
| AJMP / ACALL page size | $2^{11} = 2048$ bytes | $2$ KB page |
| Stack bytes per CALL | $2$ bytes (PCH first, then PCL) | $1$ byte per PUSH |
| Stack growth | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 2$ after CALL | Upward only |
| Maximum nested CALLs | $\lfloor \text{Stack RAM available} / 2 \rfloor$ | Default: $128/2 = 64$ |
| Target address (rel jump) | $\text{PC}_{\text{target}} = \text{PC}_{\text{next}} + \text{rel}$ | Where PC$_{\text{next}}$ is PC of instruction after the jump |
| Number of cycles for `CALL` | $2$ machine cycles ($24$ oscillator periods) | At 12 MHz: $2\ \mu s$ |
| Number of cycles for `RET` | $2$ machine cycles ($24$ oscillator periods) | At 12 MHz: $2\ \mu s$ |

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

Control transfer is the **backbone of all real-world embedded firmware**:

- **Industrial PLC controllers** use conditional jumps for sensor threshold checks (e.g., "If temperature > 90°C, trip alarm").
- **Automotive ECUs** employ subroutine calls for reusable diagnostic routines (reading OBD-II codes).
- **Interrupt-driven I/O** in washing machines, microwave ovens, and medical infusion pumps allows the CPU to remain idle until an event occurs — conserving power and enabling **real-time responsiveness**.
- **Finite State Machines (FSMs)** for protocol stacks (UART, SPI, I²C) rely entirely on `CJNE` and `DJNZ` to implement state transitions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relative Offset Calculation for `SJMP`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** KTU frequently asks students to compute the relative offset byte for a forward/backward jump. Many students lose marks because they compute the offset from the *wrong base address*.

### Problem Setup
Given the following 8051 code segment stored starting at address `0x0100`:

```
0x0100:   75 90 10     MOV   P1, #10H
0x0103:   80 FE        SJMP  $
0x0105:   78 55        MOV   R0, #55H
0x0107:   00           NOP
0x0108:   00           NOP
0x0109:   00           NOP
0x010A:   00           NOP
0x010B:   00           NOP
0x010C:   00           NOP
0x010D:   00           NOP
0x010E:   00           NOP
0x010F:   00           NOP
0x0110:   00           NOP
...
0x0120:   00           NOP      <-- Target of jump
```

We need to compute the offset byte for `SJMP` to land on address `0x0120`.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the PC value *after* the SJMP instruction.**

The `SJMP` instruction is at address `0x0103` and is **2 bytes** long. Therefore:

$$ \text{PC}_{\text{next}} = \text{Address of SJMP} + \text{Length of SJMP} $$

$$ \text{PC}_{\text{next}} = 0x0103 + 2 = 0x0105 $$

**Step 2: Identify the target address.**

The target address is given as `0x0120`.

$$ \text{PC}_{\text{target}} = 0x0120 $$

**Step 3: Apply the relative offset formula.**

The 8051 defines the offset as:

$$ \text{rel} = \text{PC}_{\text{target}} - \text{PC}_{\text{next}} $$

**Step 4: Substitute values.**

$$ \text{rel} = 0x0120 - 0x0105 $$

Converting to decimal for clarity:

$$ \text{rel} = 288_{\text{dec}} - 261_{\text{dec}} = 27_{\text{dec}} $$

**Step 5: Express as 2-digit hex (since the assembler stores the offset byte).**

$$ \text{rel} = 0x1B $$

**Step 6: Verify the range.**

$$ -128 \leq 0x1B = 27 \leq +127 \quad \checkmark $$

Therefore, the correct instruction encoding is `80 1B` (opcode `0x80` for `SJMP`, followed by offset `0x1B`).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stack Behavior During `LCALL` and `RET`

### Problem Setup
Suppose the 8051 executes `LCALL 0x2050` when:
- Current PC = `0x1234`
- SP (before call) = `0x0F`

Determine the stack memory contents and SP value during the call and after the return.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the return address that must be saved.**

The 8051 pushes the address of the instruction *following* the `LCALL`. Since `LCALL` is 3 bytes long:

$$ \text{Return Address} = \text{PC}_{\text{current}} + 3 = 0x1234 + 3 = 0x1237 $$

**Step 2: Decompose into high and low bytes.**

$$ \text{PCL} = 0x37 \quad \text{(low byte, pushed first)} $$

$$ \text{PCH} = 0x12 \quad \text{(high byte, pushed second)} $$

**Step 3: Increment SP and push PCL.**

$$ \text{SP} = 0x0F + 1 = 0x10 $$

$$ \text{RAM}[0x10] = 0x37 $$

**Step 4: Increment SP and push PCH.**

$$ \text{SP} = 0x10 + 1 = 0x11 $$

$$ \text{RAM}[0x11] = 0x12 $$

**Step 5: Load the new PC with the subroutine address.**

$$ \text{PC} = 0x2050 $$

**Step 6: After subroutine execution, `RET` is encountered. The reverse happens:**

$$ \text{PCH} = \text{RAM}[0x11] = 0x12 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SP decrements to } 0x10 $$

$$ \text{PCL} = \text{RAM}[0x10] = 0x37 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SP decrements to } 0x0F $$

**Step 7: Reconstruct the PC.**

$$ \text{PC} = (0x12 \ll 8) \;\vert\; 0x37 = 0x1237 $$

Execution resumes correctly at the instruction after `LCALL`.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Correct Jump Instruction

| Scenario | Best Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Need to jump more than 2 KB ahead | `LJMP` or `SJMP` | `AJMP` cannot cross page boundary |
| Need to jump within the same 2 KB page | `AJMP` | Saves 1 byte vs `LJMP` (2 vs 3 bytes) |
| Need a tight infinite loop | `SJMP $` ($ means PC of next instr) | Offset byte = `0xFE` = $-2$ |
| Need a loop with a counter | `DJNZ Rn, label` | Built-in decrement + branch in 1 instruction |
| Need a multi-way branch (switch-case) | `JMP @A+DPTR` | Indexed jump via jump table |
| Need to call a function anywhere in 64 KB | `LCALL` | Full 16-bit address |
| Need a function only used within a page | `ACALL` | Saves 1 byte |
| Need to return from interrupt safely | `RETI` | Re-enables interrupts of equal/lower priority |
| Need to return from ordinary subroutine | `RET` | Simpler, no interrupt re-enable logic |

## 3.4 Derivation: Time Delay Calculation Using Nested `DJNZ`

A common KTU numerical asks: *What is the total time delay generated by the following 8051 code, with crystal frequency 11.0592 MHz?*

```
DELAY:  MOV  R0, #0FFH      ; 1 machine cycle
        MOV  R1, #0FFH      ; 1 machine cycle
LOOP1:  DJNZ R1, LOOP1      ; 2 machine cycles per iteration
        DJNZ R0, LOOP1      ; 2 machine cycles per iteration (outer)
        RET
```

**Step 1: Compute one machine cycle duration.**

$$ T_{\text{machine}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \text{ seconds} $$

$$ T_{\text{machine}} = 1.085\ \mu s $$

**Step 2: Compute total cycles.**

$$ N_{\text{cycles}} = 1 + 1 + (2 \times 255) + (2 \times 255 \times 255) $$

$$ N_{\text{cycles}} = 2 + 510 + 130{,}050 = 130{,}562 \text{ machine cycles} $$

**Step 3: Compute total time.**

$$ T_{\text{delay}} = 130{,}562 \times 1.085\ \mu s $$

$$ T_{\text{delay}} \approx 141{,}660\ \mu s \approx 141.66\ \text{ms} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget the 2 setup cycles (`MOV R0` and `MOV R1`) and end up with $130{,}050$ cycles only. **Always include all instruction cycles in the path before `RET`.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Classification Tree of 8051 Control Transfer

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Control Transfer Instructions]
    nodeB[Unconditional Jumps]
    nodeC[Conditional Jumps]
    nodeD[Subroutine Calls]
    nodeE[Interrupt Driven Transfer]
    nodeF[LJMP 16 bit address]
    nodeG[AJMP 11 bit within 2KB page]
    nodeH[SJMP 8 bit relative]
    nodeI[JMP at A plus DPTR indexed]
    nodeJ[Tests Zero flag in A]
    nodeK[Tests Carry flag CY]
    nodeL[Tests Direct Bit]
    nodeM[Loop Control DJNZ and CJNE]
    nodeN[LCALL 16 bit address]
    nodeO[ACALL 11 bit within 2KB page]
    nodeP[RET pops PC from stack]
    nodeQ[RETI pops PC and restores interrupt logic]
    nodeR[Hardware triggered vectored PC load]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeC --> nodeK
    nodeC --> nodeL
    nodeC --> nodeM
    nodeD --> nodeN
    nodeD --> nodeO
    nodeD --> nodeP
    nodeE --> nodeQ
    nodeE --> nodeR
```

## 4.2 Stack Memory Activity Diagram During LCALL and RET

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[CPU executes LCALL 2050H at PC 1234H]
    nodeB[Step 1 SP increments from 0F to 10]
    nodeC[Step 2 PCL value 37H pushed to RAM 10]
    nodeD[Step 3 SP increments from 10 to 11]
    nodeE[Step 4 PCH value 12H pushed to RAM 11]
    nodeF[Step 5 PC loaded with subroutine target 2050H]
    nodeG[Subroutine executes its body]
    nodeH[Step 6 RET encountered]
    nodeI[Step 7 PCH value 12H popped from RAM 11 to PC high]
    nodeJ[Step 8 SP decrements from 11 to 10]
    nodeK[Step 9 PCL value 37H popped from RAM 10 to PC low]
    nodeL[Step 10 SP decrements from 10 to 0F]
    nodeM[PC fully restored to 1237H ready for next fetch]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: How an Interrupt Hijacks Control Transfer

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Main Program executing linearly]
    nodeB[Hardware event occurs eg Timer overflow or External INT0]
    nodeC[CPU finishes current instruction and detects interrupt flag]
    nodeD[CPU pushes current PC and PSW onto stack automatically in hardware]
    nodeE[CPU loads PC from fixed vector address from interrupt source]
    nodeF[Interrupt Service Routine ISR executes at new PC]
    nodeG[ISR body performs required action]
    nodeH[ISR ends with RETI instruction]
    nodeI[RETI pops saved PC and PSW from stack automatically]
    nodeJ[Interrupt priority logic re-enabled for lower priority sources]
    nodeK[Main Program resumes exactly from interrupted PC plus 1]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeA
```

## 4.4 Comparative Reach Diagram: Jump Instruction Ranges

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Complete 64 KB program memory]
    nodeB[LJMP range full 64 KB using 16 bit address]
    nodeC[AJMP range limited to current 2 KB page using 11 bit address]
    nodeD[SJMP range backward 128 bytes and forward 127 bytes using 8 bit signed offset]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
```

> [!NOTE]
