# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define the term "Addressing Mode". List any FIVE addressing modes supported by the 8051 microcontroller with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
> An **addressing mode** is the method by which the 8051 CPU specifies the operand (data) for an instruction. It describes the *location* of the data — whether in a register, in memory, or embedded in the instruction itself.

**Five Addressing Modes (2 Marks — 0.4 each):**

| # | Mode | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Immediate | `MOV A, #25H` |
| 2 | Register | `MOV A, R1` |
| 3 | Direct | `MOV A, 30H` |
| 4 | Register Indirect | `MOV A, @R0` |
| 5 | Indexed | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` |

---

> ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between Direct Addressing and Register Indirect Addressing in 8051 with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Direct Addressing | Register Indirect |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbol** | No special symbol | `@` prefix on register |
| **Address Source** | 8-bit address embedded in instruction | Held in a pointer register (R0/R1/DPTR) |
| **Regions Accessible** | Internal RAM 00H–7FH, SFR 80H–FFH | Internal RAM (via R0/R1), External XRAM (via DPTR) |
| **SFR Access** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Example** | `MOV A, 40H` | `MOV A, @R0` |
| **Flexibility** | Fixed at compile-time | Dynamic — pointer can change at runtime |

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> ### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **six different addressing modes of 8051** with suitable examples. Highlight which mode is exclusively used for accessing **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** and which mode is used for **Code ROM lookup tables**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the following 8051 assembly program. Identify the **addressing mode** of each instruction, and compute the **final value of the Accumulator (A)** at the end of the program. Assume internal RAM location `20H` contains `35H` and `21H` contains `67H`. Also, determine the **total number of machine cycles** consumed. **(7 Marks)**
> ```assembly
>         MOV  R0,  #20H
>         MOV  A,   @R0
>         ADD  A,   #10H
>         MOV  20H, A
>         MOV  R1,  #21H
>         MOV  A,   @R1
>         SUBB A,   #05H
> ```

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Mode 1: Immediate Addressing (1 Mark)**
The operand is a constant stored as part of the instruction.
- Example: `MOV A, #0F0H` → A = `0xF0`
- Marked by `#` prefix.

**Mode 2: Register Addressing (1 Mark)**
Operand is inside a CPU register.
- Example: `MOV A, R0` → A ← R0 (no memory access).
- 0 extra machine cycles.

**Mode 3: Direct Addressing (1 Mark)**
The instruction contains an 8-bit address.
- Example: `MOV A, 30H` → A ← RAM[30H].
- **Sole mode to access SFRs (e.g., `MOV P1, A`).**

**Mode 4: Register Indirect Addressing (1 Mark)**
A pointer register holds the effective address.
- Example: `MOV A, @R0` → A ← RAM[R0].
- Cannot access SFRs.

**Mode 5: Indexed Addressing (1 Mark)**
Effective address = Base + Accumulator.
- Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → A ← CODE[DPTR + A].
- **Used exclusively for Code ROM lookup tables.**

**Mode 6: Relative Addressing (1 Mark)**
Target = PC + signed offset.
- Example: `SJMP LOOP`.
- Offset range = **−128 to +127**.

**Highlighted Region Summary (1 Mark):**
- **SFRs** → **Direct Addressing** (only mode).
- **Code ROM** → **Indexed Addressing** (only mode, via `MOVC`).

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

| # | Instruction | Mode | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV R0, #20H` | Immediate | R0 ← `0x20` |
| 2 | `MOV A, @R0` | Reg Indirect | A ← RAM[20H] = `0x35` |
| 3 | `ADD A, #10H` | Immediate | A ← `0x35 + 0x10 = 0x45` |
| 4 | `MOV 20H, A` | Direct | RAM[20H] ← `0x45` |
| 5 | `MOV R1, #21H` | Immediate | R1 ← `0x21` |
| 6 | `MOV A, @R1` | Reg Indirect | A ← RAM[21H] = `0x67` |
| 7 | `SUBB A, #05H` | Immediate (with borrow) | A ← `0x67 − 0x05 = 0x62` (CY = 0) |

**Final Accumulator Value:**
$$\boxed{A = 0x62 \text{ (Hex)} = 98 \text{ (Decimal)}}$$

**Total Machine Cycles Calculation (2 Marks):**
- All 7 instructions are 1-cycle each, except `SUBB` is also 1-cycle.
- Total = **7 machine cycles**.

**Execution Time** (at 12 MHz oscillator, 1 MC = 1 µs):
$$T_{\text{exec}} = 7 \times 1.0 \ \mu s = 7 \ \mu s$$

---

> ### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the concept of **Indexed Addressing Mode** in 8051 with two examples. Explain how it is used to implement a **lookup table** for 7-segment display codes. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to **add two 8-bit numbers** stored in internal RAM locations `30H` and `31H` and **store the result in `32H`** (assume no carry). Identify the addressing mode used in each instruction. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Concept of Indexed Addressing (2 Marks):**
In indexed addressing, the **effective address** of the operand is computed as the **sum of a 16-bit base register and the 8-bit accumulator content**.

$$\text{Effective Address} = \text{Base Register} + A$$

In the 8051, this mode is **exclusively** used with the `MOVC` (Move Code) instruction to read from **program memory (ROM)**.

**Two Examples (2 Marks):**
1. `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — uses DPTR as the 16-bit base.
2. `MOVC A, @A+PC` — uses PC as the 16-bit base (offset is calculated from the *next* instruction's PC).

**Lookup Table Implementation (3 Marks):**
A 7-segment lookup table stores hex-to-segment codes in Code ROM.

```assembly
        ORG  0000H
        MOV  DPTR, #TABLE       ; DPTR = base address of TABLE
        MOV  A,   #03H          ; A = digit to display (say 3)
        MOVC A,   @A+DPTR       ; A = segment code for digit 3
        MOV  P1,  A             ; Output to Port 1 (7-seg display)

        SJMP $                  ; Halt

; --- Lookup Table stored in Code ROM ---
TABLE:  DB    0C0H, 0F9H, 0A4H, 0B0H  ; 0, 1, 2, 3
        DB    99H,  92H, 82H,  0F8H    ; 4, 5, 6, 7
        DB    80H,  90H, 88H,  83H     ; 8, 9, A, B
        DB    0C6H, 0A1H, 86H,  8EH    ; C, D, E, F
        END
```

**Working:** If A = 3, then `A + DPTR` = DPTR + 3 = address of `0B0H`. Thus A = `0B0H`, which is the segment code for "3".

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Program (4 Marks):**
```assembly
        ORG  0000H
        MOV  A,   30H      ; Direct — A ← RAM[30H]
        ADD  A,   31H      ; Direct — A ← A + RAM[31H]
        MOV  32H,  A       ; Direct — RAM[32H] ← A
        END
```

**Addressing Mode Table (3 Marks):**

| # | Instruction | Mode |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV A, 30H` | **Direct Addressing** |
| 2 | `ADD A, 31H` | **Direct Addressing** (both operands — A is Register, 31H is Direct) |
| 3 | `MOV 32H, A` | **Direct Addressing** (destination address) |

**Result:** RAM[32H] = RAM[30H] + RAM[31H].

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout
>
> **Most Common Mark-Deduction Points on Addressing Mode Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `#` symbol** in immediate mode — `MOV A, 25H` (wrong) vs. `MOV A, #25H` (correct). The `#` is *the* differentiator — losing this means 0 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing Register Indirect with Direct** — students write `MOV A, R0` thinking it's "indirect" because R0 holds a value. It is NOT. Indirect requires the `@` symbol (`MOV A, @R0`).
>
> 3. **SFR access via `@R0`** — this is **invalid** in 8051. If a question says "access P1 via R0," it cannot be done. Use `MOV A, 80H` or `MOV A, P1` (assembler-direct).
>
> 4. **PC offset calculation in relative addressing** — students forget that the offset is computed **after** PC auto-increments past the branch instruction. Add the offset to the address of the **NEXT** instruction, not the branch itself.
>
> 5. **Confusing `MOV` vs `MOVX` vs `MOVC`** — `MOVC` = Code ROM (indexed), `MOVX` = External XRAM (register indirect), `MOV` = Internal RAM/SFR (direct/indirect).
>
> 6. **Machine cycle calculation** — the 8051's 1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods (NOT 1). This is a high-frequency 1–2 mark question.

---

## 5.3 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Addressing Modes**

### 🔹 The Big Picture
- **8051 supports 6 addressing modes** (Mazidi's classification): Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed, Relative.
- The mode determines **where the operand is located** and **how the EA is computed**.

### 🔹 Mode-by-Mode Quick Hooks
- **Immediate** → `#` symbol → constant baked in opcode (2/3 bytes).
- **Register** → A, B, R0–R7, DPTR → fastest (0 extra cycles).
- **Direct** → 8-bit address in instruction → **ONLY** way to access SFRs.
- **Register Indirect** → `@R0`, `@R1`, `@DPTR` → **NOT** for SFRs; used for arrays and `MOVX`.
- **Indexed** → `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC` (with `MOVC` only) → Code ROM lookup.
- **Relative** → `PC + signed offset` → branch instructions (SJMP, CJNE, DJNZ).

### 🔹 Critical Numerical Constants
- 1 machine cycle = **12 oscillator periods**.
- 1 byte of SFR address space = **128 bytes (80H–FFH)**.
- Indexed addressing range = **64 KB (0000H–FFFFH)**.
- Relative offset range = **−128 to +127** (signed 2's complement).

### 🔹 High-Frequency KTU Question Hooks
- ✅ "Which mode accesses SFRs?" → **Direct**
- ✅ "Which mode reads Code ROM?" → **Indexed (with MOVC)**
- ✅ "Fastest mode?" → **Register**
- ✅ "Mode used in PUSH/POP?" → **Register Indirect**
- ✅ "Mode with `SJMP`?" → **Relative**
- ✅ "Difference between `MOVX` and `MOVC`?" → MOVX = external data, MOVC = code memory

### 🔹 Memory Cycle & Speed Summary
- Register = **0 extra** cycles.
- Immediate / Direct / Indirect = **1 cycle**.
- Indexed / `MOVX` = **2 cycles**.

### 🔹 Common Exam Traps
- 🚫 `#` missing → 0 marks.
- 🚫 `@R0` to access SFR → invalid.
- 🚫 `MOVC` to access RAM → invalid (only Code ROM).
- 🚫 `MOVX @R0` → only `@DPTR` works for `MOVX` (not `@R0` or `@R1`).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 8051 Microcontroller Programming and Peripherals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 8051 Microcontroller Programming and Peripherals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **8051 Microcontroller** is an **8-bit Harvard architecture** single-chip microcontroller originally developed by Intel in **1980**. It integrates a CPU, **4 KB** of on-chip ROM/Program Memory, **128 bytes** of on-chip data RAM, four bidirectional 8-bit I/O ports (P0, P1, P2, P3), two 16-bit Timer/Counters, a full-duplex UART (serial port), **5 interrupt sources**, and an on-chip oscillator driver on a single silicon die.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** *Module 2 explicitly maps to "8051 Programming Model, Instruction Set, Addressing Modes, Assembly Directives, I/O & Timer Programming, Serial Port Programming, and Interfacing peripherals like LED, 7-segment, Keypad, LCD, Stepper Motor, ADC & DAC."*

## 1.2 8051 Programming Model — The CPU's "Workspace"

| Component | Size | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Accumulator (A)** | 8-bit | Primary register for all ALU operations |
| **B Register** | 8-bit | Used in MUL/AB and DIV/AB |
| **PSW** | 8-bit | Program Status Word (flags) |
| **SP** | 8-bit | Stack Pointer (default 07H) |
| **DPTR (DPH+DPL)** | 16-bit | Data Pointer for external memory access |
| **PC** | 16-bit | Program Counter (max 65535 = FFFFH) |
| **R0–R7** | 8-bit × 4 banks | Four register banks (Bank 0–3) |
| **SFRs** | 8-bit × 21 | Special Function Registers |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Workshop" 🏭

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of the 8051 as a **tiny automated workshop**.
> - **CPU** → the **foreman** who follows written instructions.
> - **ROM/Program Memory** → the **instruction manual** permanently stored in the workshop.
> - **RAM** → the **workbench** where temporary tools (variables) are placed.
> - **Accumulator** → the **foreman's hand** — almost every job passes through it.
> - **I/O Ports** → the **loading docks** where raw materials (sensors) come in and finished products (LEDs, motors) go out.
> - **Timers** → the **stopwatches** counting machine cycles.
> - **Serial Port** → the **walkie-talkie** that lets the workshop talk to the outside world.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- Crystal Frequency: **11.0592 MHz** (chosen because 11.0592 MHz ÷ 12 = 921.6 kHz, which divides cleanly to standard baud rates like 9600).
- One Machine Cycle = **12 oscillator periods**.
- Internal RAM = **128 bytes** (00H–7FH).
- SFR space = **128 bytes** (80H–FFH), bit-addressable.
- Code memory (internal) = **4 KB** (0000H–0FFFH) for 8051; 8 KB for 8052.
- External code memory space = **64 KB** (0000H–FFFFH).
- I/O Ports = **4** (P0, P1, P2, P3) — each **8-bit**, bidirectional.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Instruction Set — Categorical Breakdown

The 8051 supports **111 base instructions** (≈ 255 opcodes when addressing modes are considered). The instructions fall into **5 functional groups**:

### Group 1: Data Transfer Instructions
- **MOV** — Move data between registers, memory, and accumulator.
- **MOVX** — Move to/from **external** data memory (uses DPTR or R0/R1).
- **MOVC** — Move **code** memory to A (used for lookup tables, e.g., `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`).
- **PUSH / POP** — Stack operations.
- **XCH / XCHD** — Exchange bytes/digits.

### Group 2: Arithmetic Instructions
- **ADD / ADDC** — Addition with/without carry.
- **SUBB** — Subtract with borrow (note: no plain SUB exists).
- **INC / DEC** — Increment/Decrement (1 machine cycle).
- **MUL AB** — Multiply A × B → result in B:A (16-bit).
- **DIV AB** — Divide A ÷ B → quotient in A, remainder in B.

### Group 3: Logical Instructions
- **ANL / ORL / XRL** — Bitwise AND, OR, XOR.
- **CLR / CPL** — Clear or complement a bit/byte.
- **RL / RLC / RR / RRC** — Rotate (with/without carry).

### Group 4: Boolean (Bit) Instructions
- The 8051 has a **bit-addressable** area in RAM (20H–2FH) and in SFRs.
- **SETB, CLR, ANL C, ORL C, MOV C,bit** — operate on single bits.
- Example: `SETB P1.0` sets pin P1.0 HIGH.

### Group 5: Program Branching Instructions
- **LJMP / SJMP / AJMP** — Jump (Long 16-bit / Short relative 8-bit / Absolute 11-bit).
- **LCALL / ACALL** — Subroutine calls.
- **JZ / JNZ** — Jump if A is zero / not zero.
- **JC / JNC / JB / JNB / JBC** — Conditional jumps on bits.
- **CJNE** — Compare and Jump if Not Equal.
- **DJNZ** — Decrement and Jump if Not Zero (used for loops).
- **RET / RETI** — Return from subroutine / interrupt.

## 2.2 The 8051 Addressing Modes (High-Yield Topic ⭐)

| # | Mode | Syntax Example | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Immediate** | `MOV A, #25H` | Loads literal constant `#25H` into A |
| 2 | **Register** | `MOV A, R0` | Copies content of R0 to A |
| 3 | **Direct** | `MOV A, 30H` | Copies content of internal RAM/SFR at address 30H |
| 4 | **Register Indirect** | `MOV A, @R0` | A ← contents of RAM address held in R0 |
| 5 | **Indexed** | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | A ← code memory at (A + DPTR) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Exam Pearl:** `@R0` and `@R1` are the **only** registers that may be used for **register indirect** addressing in the standard 8051. `@R2`–`@R7` is **not allowed** in 8-bit indirect mode for MOV.

## 2.3 PSW (Program Status Word) — Flag Register

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| PSW.7 | **CY** | D7H | Carry Flag |
| PSW.6 | **AC** | D6H | Auxiliary Carry (for BCD) |
| PSW.5 | **F0** | D5H | User-definable flag |
| PSW.4 & PSW.3 | **RS1, RS0** | D4H, D3H | Register Bank Select (00=B0, 01=B1, 10=B2, 11=B3) |
| PSW.2 | **OV** | D2H | Overflow Flag |
| PSW.1 | **—** | D1H | Reserved |
| PSW.0 | **P** | D0H | Parity Flag (1 = odd number of 1s in A) |

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **exam-critical formulas** for 8051 timing, baud rates, and timer reloads.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | seconds | **12 oscillator periods** per machine cycle |
| 2 | Instruction Cycles | 1, 2, or 4 MC | — | Most are 1 MC; MUL/DIV = 4 MC |
| 3 | Timer Clock Frequency | $f_{T} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12}$ | Hz | Internal mode, $f_{OSC} = 11.0592$ MHz → $921.6$ kHz |
| 4 | Timer Delay | $T_{delay} = (65536 - X) \times T_{MC}$ | seconds | For **Mode 1 (16-bit)**: $X$ = THx TLx initial value |
| 5 | Auto-Reload (Mode 2) Delay | $T_{delay} = (256 - X) \times T_{MC}$ | seconds | 8-bit timer with auto-reload of $X$ into THx |
| 6 | Timer Reload Value (Mode 1) | $X = 65536 - \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}}$ | integer | Convert $X$ → THx (high byte), TLx (low byte) |
| 7 | Timer Reload Value (Mode 2) | $X = 256 - \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}}$ | integer | Place $X$ in **both** THx and TLx |
| 8 | UART Baud Rate (Mode 1, Timer 1) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | bps | SMOD = 0 or 1 (PCON.7) |
| 9 | TH1 for given baud | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{OSC}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$ | integer | Standard answer at **11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0: TH1 = FDh = 253** for 9600 baud |
| 10 | Effective Baud (11.0592 MHz) | $9600$ bps with TH1 = **FDH** | bps | **0% error** at 11.0592 MHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Table-Break Rule:** All absolute-value notations inside formulas are rendered using `\vert` so the markdown table pipes are not broken. For example, the parity flag can be written as $P = \vert 1\text{'s in A}\vert \bmod 2$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 8051's instruction set and programming model are not academic relics — they form the **backbone of industrial embedded design**:
- **Automotive ECUs** (early generation), washing machine controllers, and microwave oven controllers.
- **Sensor data acquisition** using ADC0804/DAC0808 with port handshaking.
- **Stepper motor control** in CNC and robotics using unipolar/bipolar driving circuits.
- **LCD/Keypad human-machine interfaces (HMI)** in vending machines and instrument panels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Machine Cycle Period from Crystal Frequency

**Problem:** Compute the period of one machine cycle for an 8051 system clocked with an **11.0592 MHz** quartz crystal.

### Step 1 — Define the relationship
The 8051's internal architecture divides the external oscillator by a fixed prescaler of 12. Therefore, the frequency fed to the instruction-timing logic is:

$$
f_{MC} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the crystal frequency

$$
f_{MC} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}}{12} = 921.6 \times 10^{3} \text{ Hz}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the machine cycle **period** (the reciprocal)

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{1}{f_{MC}} = \frac{1}{921.6 \times 10^{3} \text{ Hz}} = 1.085\,\mu s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verdict:** $T_{MC} = 1.085\,\mu s$ — this is the **fundamental unit of time** for every instruction's execution budget. Every "1 µs" delay loop must be expressed as a multiple of $1.085\,\mu s$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Timer Reload Value for a Desired Delay (Mode 1)

**Problem:** Generate a **50 ms** delay using **Timer 0 in Mode 1** with $f_{OSC} = 11.0592$ MHz.

### Step 1 — Compute machine cycle period

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\,\mu s
$$

### Step 2 — Count the number of timer ticks required
Each timer increment consumes exactly $1\,T_{MC}$.

$$
N = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46080 \text{ ticks}
$$

### Step 3 — Invert the count to obtain the initial value
The timer counts UP from $X$ to **65536**, then overflows and sets **TF0 = 1**. So the number of ticks it must count is $65536 - X$.

$$
65536 - X = 46080
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $X$

$$
X = 65536 - 46080 = 19456
$$

### Step 5 — Split $X$ into TH0 and TL0 (High/Low bytes)

$$
X = 19456_{10} = 4C00_{H}
$$

$$
TH0 = 4CH, \quad TL0 = 00H
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick-Check:** If the result is **negative**, the requested delay **exceeds** the 16-bit timer's range. The maximum single-shot delay in Mode 1 is $65536 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx 71.1$ ms. For longer delays, use nested loops or repeat the timer inside a software counter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: TH1 for 9600 Baud at 11.0592 MHz (SMOD = 0)

**Problem:** Configure the 8051 serial port to **9600 bps** using Timer 1 in **Mode 2** (auto-reload) with SMOD = 0.

### Step 1 — Apply the baud-rate equation

$$
\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values

$$
9600 = \frac{2^{0}}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify and isolate $TH1$

$$
9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times 9600}
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the right-hand side

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{3\,686\,400} = 3
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $TH1$

$$
TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = FDH
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the canonical exam answer.** TH1 = **FDH** with $f_{OSC} = 11.0592$ MHz, SMOD = 0 gives **exactly 9600 baud with 0% error**. This is precisely why 11.0592 MHz is the de-facto standard crystal for 8051-based serial communication projects.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Time-Delays & Reload Values

| Delay Wanted | $f_{OSC}$ | Mode | Reload Value (Hex) | THx | TLx |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 100 µs | 11.0592 MHz | 2 (8-bit) | $256 - 92 = 164 = $ A4H | A4H | A4H |
| 1 ms | 11.0592 MHz | 1 (16-bit) | $65536 - 921 = 64615 = $ FC67H | FCH | 67H |
| 10 ms | 11.0592 MHz | 1 (16-bit) | $65536 - 9216 = 56320 = $ DC00H | DCH | 00H |
| 50 ms | 11.0592 MHz | 1 (16-bit) | $65536 - 46080 = 19456 = $ 4C00H | 4CH | 00H |
| 500 µs | 12 MHz | 1 (16-bit) | $65536 - 500 = 65036 = $ FE0CH | FEH | 0CH |

> [!NOTE]
> At $f_{OSC} = 12$ MHz, $T_{MC} = 1\,\mu s$ exactly — making the arithmetic a clean multiple of 1 µs. This is why 12 MHz is preferred in **teaching kits** even though it is suboptimal for serial comms.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: 8051 I/O Port Pull-Up & Drive Requirements

| Port | Internal Pull-ups | Sink Current (per pin) | Source Current | Role in External Memory Access |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P0** | **No pull-ups** | Up to ~26 mA | Weak (needs ext. 10 kΩ pull-ups) | Multiplexed AD0–AD7 + A0–A7 |
| **P1** | Weak internal pull-ups | ~10 mA | Limited | General-purpose I/O only |
| **P2** | Weak internal pull-ups | ~10 mA | Limited | A8–A15 (high address byte) |
| **P3** | Weak internal pull-ups | ~10 mA | Limited | Has **alternate functions** (RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing to P0 without external pull-up resistors when used as **general-purpose output** results in the port failing to drive HIGH reliably. Always use a 10 kΩ pull-up array on P0 for non-multiplexed I/O.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid Block-Level)

## 4.1 8051 Internal Architecture — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OSC["Crystal Oscillator\n11.0592 MHz"] --> CLK["Clock & Timing\n12-Divide Prescaler"]
    CLK --> MC["Machine Cycle Generator\nTMC = 12/fOSC"]
    PC["Program Counter\n16-bit"] --> ROM["Internal Code ROM\n4 KB (0000H-0FFFH)"]
    ROM --> DEC["Instruction Decoder\n& Control Unit"]
    DEC --> ALU["8-bit ALU\n+ MUL / DIV"]
    RAM["Internal RAM\n128 Bytes"] --> ALU
    SFR["SFR Space\n80H-FFH"] --> ALU
    ALU --> A["Accumulator A"]
    P0["Port 0\nAD0-AD7"] --> EXT
    P1["Port 1\nGeneral I/O"] --> EXT
    P2["Port 2\nA8-A15"] --> EXT
    P3["Port 3\nAlt Functions"] --> EXT
    EXT["External Peripherals\nLED / LCD / Motor / ADC"]
    T0["Timer 0\n16-bit"] --> INT["Interrupt Controller\n5 Sources"]
    T1["Timer 1\n16-bit"] --> INT
    SIO["Serial Port\nUART"] --> INT
    EXT1["INT0, INT1"] --> INT
    INT --> DEC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The 8051 is a strict **single-bus, single-ALU** Harvard design. Code and data memories have **separate address spaces** and **separate buses**, allowing simultaneous fetch + access.

## 4.2 Timer/Counter Operational Flow (Mode 1)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START([Start]) --> INIT["Load TH0, TL0\nwith reload value X"]
    INIT --> SETTR["Set TR0 = 1\nvia SETB TR0"]
    SETTR --> TICK{Counter\nIncremented\nevery TMC}
    TICK -- "Count < 65536" --> TICK
    TICK -- "Overflow" --> SETFLAG["Set TF0 = 1\nRequest Interrupt"]
    SETFLAG --> HANDLER["ISR Vector\n000BH for T0"]
    HANDLER --> RELOAD["Re-load TH0, TL0"]
    RELOAD --> SETTR
```

## 4.3 8051 Serial Communication — Mode 1 Frame

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SBUF["SBUF Register\n(99H)"] --> TXD["TXD Pin\nP3.1"]
    RXD["RXD Pin\nP3.0"] --> RECV["Receive Shift Register"]
    RECV --> SBUFR["SBUF\n(read)"]
    SCON["SCON Register\n98H"] --> CTRL["Mode 1 Controller\n10-bit UART"]
    CTRL --> SBUF
    CTRL --> RECV
    BAUD["Timer 1\nAuto-Reload\nTH1 = FDH"] --> CTRL
```

## 4.4 I/O Port Configuration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DECIDE{Is P0 used\nfor external\nmemory?}
    DECIDE -- "Yes" --> AUTO["P0 = AD0-AD7\nP2 = A8-A15\nAuto-controlled by\nEA, ALE, PSEN signals"]
    DECIDE -- "No" --> NORMAL["Use as GPIO\nAdd 10K pull-ups on P0"]
    NORMAL --> DIR{Direction?}
    DIR -- "Output" --> OUT["Write to latch:\nMOV P1, #0FFH"]
    DIR -- "Input" --> INP["Write 1s to latch first\n(Quasi-bidirectional)\nThen read pin:\nMOV A, P1"]
```

## 4.5 Interrupt Priority & Vector Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RESET["Reset\n0000H"] --> MAIN["Main Program"]
    MAIN -.-> EXT0["Ext Interrupt 0\n0003H"]
    MAIN -.-> T0["Timer 0 Overflow\n000BH"]
    MAIN -.-> EXT1["Ext Interrupt 1\n0013H"]
    MAIN -.-> T1["Timer 1 Overflow\n001BH"]
    MAIN -.-> SIO["Serial Port\n0023H"]
    EXT0 --> PRIO{"Priority?\nIP Register"}
    T0 --> PRIO
    EXT1 --> PRIO
    T1 --> PRIO
    SIO --> PRIO
    PRIO -- "Higher" --> SRV["Service Immediately"]
    PRIO -- "Lower" --> WAIT["Wait for RETI\nfrom current ISR"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"List the five interrupt sources of the 8051 along with their vector addresses. What is the role of the IP register?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 8051 supports **5 interrupt sources**:

| # | Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address | Default Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | IE0 | **0003H** | Highest (1) |
| 2 | Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 | **000BH** | 2 |
| 3 | External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | IE1 | **0013H** | 3 |
| 4 | Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 | **001BH** | 4 |
| 5 | Serial Port (TI/RI) | TI/RI | **0023H** | Lowest (5) |

The **IP (Interrupt Priority) register** at address **B8H** sets each interrupt to either **high priority (1)** or **low priority (0)**. A high-priority interrupt can preempt a low-priority ISR, but two interrupts of the same priority are resolved by the natural polling order above.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse the **vector address** (jump target of the CPU) with the **flag bit** (set by hardware). The IE0 flag belongs to TCON.1 — not a separate register. Mixing these up costs 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Explain the difference between MOVX and MOVC instructions in 8051 with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **MOVX** | **MOVC** |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Type Accessed** | **External** data memory (XRAM, max 64 KB) | **Code/Program** memory (ROM, internal + external) |
| **Mnemonic Origin** | "X" = **eXternal** | "C" = **Code** |
| **Addressing** | Uses **@DPTR** or **@R0/@R1** | Always uses **@A+DPTR** or **@A+PC** |
| **Read/Write** | Both read and write (e.g., to external ADC) | **Read-only** (since code is ROM) |
| **Example** | `MOVX A, @DPTR` (read XRAM into A) | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` (lookup table) |

**Example Use Cases:**
- `MOVC` → implementing a 7-segment lookup table.
- `MOVX` → reading ADC0804 connected to external memory-mapped port.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **8051 Timer/Counter in Mode 1** and **derive the TH0 and TL0 values** to generate a **delay of exactly 30 ms** using an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Write an **8051 ALP (Assembly Language Program)** to toggle all bits of **Port P1 every 1 second** using Timer 0 in Mode 1, with the same crystal. Explain each instruction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q. (a) — 7 Marks

**Diagram & Pin-Out Reference:** The Timer 0 consists of two 8-bit registers **TH0 (8CH)** and **TL0 (8AH)**, controlled by **TMOD (89H)** and **TCON (88H)**.

**Mode 1 Configuration:**
- TMOD byte for Timer 0 = `00000001B = 01H` (Mode 1, timer operation, software control via TR0).
- TCON bit TR0 (TCON.4) is set by `SETB TR0` to start; cleared by `CLR TR0` to stop.

**Step 1 — Compute machine cycle period:**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Number of timer ticks required:**

$$
N = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 27648
$$

**Step 3 — Compute reload value $X$:**

$$
X = 65536 - 27648 = 37888
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hex:**

$$
X = 37888_{10} = 9400_{H}
$$

**Step 5 — Split into TH0 and TL0:**

$$
\boxed{TH0 = 94H, \quad TL0 = 00H}
$$

**Initialization Sequence (Model):**
```
MOV TMOD, #01H    ; Timer 0, Mode 1
MOV TH0, #94H     ; Load high byte
MOV TL0, #00H     ; Load low byte
SETB TR0          ; Start timer
```

**Polling Method (Model):**
```
WAIT:  JNB TF0, WAIT   ; Loop until TF0 = 1
       CLR TR0         ; Stop timer
       CLR TF0         ; Clear flag
```

---

#### Solution to Q. (b) — 7 Marks

**Design Logic:** Since one 16-bit timer gives a **maximum of 71.1 ms** delay, we cannot toggle every 1 second directly. We use **timer-overflow counting** in a software register.

**Reload value for 50 ms:** From §3.2 above, $X = 4C00H$, so **TH0 = 4CH, TL0 = 00H**.

**Number of 50 ms intervals in 1 second = 1000/50 = 20.** So a software counter (R7) must count to **20**.

**Program (Using SFR, Register, and Immediate Addressing):**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 000BH        ; Timer 0 ISR vector
        LJMP ISR_T0

        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #01H   ; T0 in Mode 1 (timer)
        MOV TH0, #4CH    ; Reload high byte
        MOV TL0, #00H    ; Reload low byte
        MOV R7, #20      ; 20 × 50 ms = 1 second
        SETB ET0         ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB EA          ; Enable global interrupt
        SETB TR0         ; Start timer
HERE:   SJMP HERE        ; Idle loop (CPU does nothing)

ISR_T0: MOV TH0, #4CH    ; Re-load high byte
        MOV TL0, #00H    ; Re-load low byte
        DJNZ R7, EXIT    ; If not 20, exit
        CPL P1           ; Else toggle P1
        MOV R7, #20      ; Reset counter
EXIT:   RETI
        END
```

**Instruction Explanations (for 1–2 marks):**
- `ORG` → assembler directive, sets program origin.
- `LJMP` → 16-bit long jump.
- `SETB ET0` → enables Timer 0 interrupt in IE register.
- `CPL P1` → **complements** (toggles) all bits of P1.
- `RETI` → returns from interrupt and clears the in-progress flag.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to **re-load TH0/TL0 inside the ISR** — the timer would only overflow once, then keep counting from 0000H, giving wrong delay.
> 2. Using `CPL A` instead of `CPL P1` — `CPL` works on a single byte and complements the entire P1 SFR (bit-addressable port). Students often wrongly write `CPL P1.0` alone.
> 3. Forgetting `SETB EA` — global interrupt enable. Without it, the CPU masks ALL interrupts.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **four addressing modes of the 8051** with an example of each. Show how **`MOVC A,@A+DPTR`** is used to fetch a value from a lookup table. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to **transmit the byte 0x55 serially at 9600 baud, 8N1**, with $f_{OSC} = 11.0592$ MHz, using **polling** method. Show the TH1 calculation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q. (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Immediate Addressing:** Operand is a constant prefixed with `#`.
- `MOV A, #25H` → loads `25H` (37 decimal) into A.

**2. Register Addressing:** Operand is one of R0–R7.
- `MOV A, R0` → copies R0 into A.

**3. Direct Addressing:** Operand is the actual address of an SFR or RAM location.
- `MOV A, 30H` → copies the content of RAM address `30H` into A.

**4. Register Indirect Addressing:** Operand's address is held in R0 or R1 (signified by `@`).
- `MOV A, @R0` → A ← MEM[R0].

**5. Indexed Addressing (Bonus):** Address = A + DPTR (or A + PC).
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → A ← CODE[A + DPTR].

**Lookup Table Example — Square of a Number (0–9):**

```assembly
        MOV DPTR, #SQUARE_TABLE   ; DPTR points to table
        MOV A, #05H               ; We want 5^2
        MOVC A, @A+DPTR           ; A = table[5] = 19H = 25
        SJMP $

SQUARE_TABLE:
        DB  00H, 01H, 04H, 09H, 10H
        DB  19H, 24H, 31H, 40H, 51H
        END
```

The assembler directive **`DB`** (Define Byte) places each constant in program memory. When `A = 05H`, the effective address = `DPTR + 5`, and `MOVC` fetches `19H` (= 25 decimal) into A.

---

#### Solution to Q. (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute TH1 for 9600 baud:**
From the derivation in §3.3:
$$
\boxed{TH1 = FDH, \text{ with SMOD} = 0}
$$

**Step 2 — Initialize the UART for Mode 1 (8-bit UART, variable baud):**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H

MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #20H   ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload)
        MOV TH1, #0FDH   ; 9600 baud
        MOV SCON, #50H   ; Mode 1, REN enabled
        SETB TR1         ; Start Timer 1

TRANSMIT:
        MOV SBUF, #55H   ; Load byte into transmit buffer
WAIT_TI:JNB TI, WAIT_TI  ; Wait for TI flag
        CLR TI           ; Clear TI for next byte
        SJMP TRANSMIT    ; Loop
        END
```

**Instruction-Level Explanation:**
- `MOV SCON, #50H` → SCON = `0101 0000B`. Bits: SM0=0, SM1=1 (Mode 1 = 8-bit UART), REN=1 (receive enable).
- `MOV SBUF, #55H` → writing to **SBUF (99H)** loads the transmit shift register. Hardware begins transmission.
- `JNB TI, WAIT_TI` → poll the **TI (Transmit Interrupt) flag at SCON.1** until hardware sets it after the last stop bit is sent.
- `CLR TI` → software must clear TI; the 8051 does **not** clear it automatically on write to SBUF.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to clear **TI** after transmission — the program will not transmit the next byte.
> 2. Loading **TH1** with the wrong value for a non-11.0592 MHz crystal — students must use the formula from §2.4, not memorize.
> 3. Forgetting **SETB TR1** — Timer 1 won't run, no baud clock, no transmission.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2**

- ✅ The 8051 has **4 KB** internal ROM, **128 bytes** RAM, **4 I/O ports**, **2 timers**, **1 UART**, **5 interrupts**.
- ✅ One **machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods** (NOT 1/fOSC).
- ✅ The 8051 is a **Harvard architecture** — code and data memories are **separate**.
- ✅ **Default Stack Pointer** is **07H** after reset; can be re-initialized via `MOV SP, #xxH`.
- ✅ **Register banks** are selected by **RS1:RS0** in PSW. Default = Bank 0 (R0–R7 at 00H–07H).
- ✅ **P0** has **no internal pull-ups** — always add external 10 kΩ resistors.
- ✅ **P3** has **alternate functions** — RXD(P3.0), TXD(P3.1), INT0(P3.2), INT1(P3.3), T0(P3.4), T1(P3.5), WR(P3.6), RD(P3.7).
- ✅ **MOVX** = external data memory; **MOVC** = code memory (read-only).
- ✅ **PSW flags**: CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, P (P = 1 if odd parity in A).
- ✅ **Timer Modes**: Mode 0 (13-bit, legacy 8048), Mode 1 (16-bit), Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload), Mode 3 (split timer).
- ✅ **Mode 2** is the **only** mode with hardware auto-reload.
- ✅ **Serial Port Modes**: Mode 0 (8-bit shift register), Mode 1 (8-bit UART, var. baud), Mode 2 (9-bit UART, fixed), Mode 3 (9-bit UART, var. baud).
- ✅ **9600 baud + 11.0592 MHz + SMOD=0 + TH1=FDH = 0% error** — exam gold.
- ✅ **Timer delay formula**: $X = 65536 - T_{delay} / T_{MC}$ (Mode 1); $X = 256 - T_{delay} / T_{MC}$ (Mode 2).
- ✅ **Interrupt vector addresses** (in ascending priority of polling): **0023H, 001BH, 0013H, 000BH, 0003H**.
- ✅ `CJNE` and `DJNZ` are the two **loop-building** workhorses of 8051 ALP.
- ✅ **EA pin = 0** forces the CPU to fetch all code from **external ROM**.
- ✅ **PSEN** (Program Store Enable) is the read strobe for **external code memory**.
- ✅ **ALE** (Address Latch Enable) demultiplexes the low-byte address from P0 using an external **74HC373 latch**.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of KTU Module 2 Notes — 8051 Microcontroller Programming and Peripherals.**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme — PBRUT404 — Outcome-Based Education & Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Cognitive Levels: L1–L5).*
