topics.title: Timers/Counters - TMOD and TCON Registers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Timers/Counters - TMOD and TCON Registers # Timers/Counters in 8051 — TMOD and TCON Registers ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Timers/Counters** are dedicated on-chip hardware peripheral subsystems in the **8051 Microcontroller** used to generate precise time delays, count external events, or trigger periodic interrupts — *without continuous CPU intervention*. They are configured by two Special Function Registers (SFRs): the **TMOD (Timer Mode) Register** and the **TCON (Timer Control) Register**. The 8051 contains **two 16-bit Timers/Counters** named **Timer 0** and **Timer 1**. Each is built from two 8-bit SFRs: | Timer | High Byte | Low Byte | |---|---|---| | Timer 0 | TH0 | TL0 | | Timer 1 | TH1 | TL1 | Together (e.g., TH0:TL0) they form a 16-bit register that increments on every timer tick or external pulse. A *tick* corresponds to one **machine cycle**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Stopwatch vs. The Door Counter** > - **Timer Mode (Internal Clock):** Imagine a stopwatch. Every time the crystal oscillator "ticks," the stopwatch advances. When it overflows (reaches its maximum value, e.g., 65535 → 0), it sets a flag — like a buzzer telling the CPU "time's up!" > - **Counter Mode (External Pulses):** Imagine a turnstile at a stadium door. Every person passing through (each pulse on the T0/T1 pin) increments the counter. When a preset number is reached, the same buzzer (overflow flag) rings. > > The **TMOD register** is the *control panel* where you choose: "stopwatch or turnstile?", "use 8-bit or 16-bit?", and "manual or auto-start?". The **TCON register** is the *start button* and the *buzzer indicator*. The fundamental timing relationship is: $$\text{One Machine Cycle} = \frac{12}{\text{Oscillator Frequency } (f_{osc})}$$ For the standard **11.0592 MHz** crystal used in KTU labs, this gives a machine cycle of approximately **1.085 µs**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** TMOD configures *mode* and *function*; TCON controls *run/stop* flags and *overflow/interrupt* flags. KTU board questions frequently test bit-level register configuration. ## 1.3 Why Timers Matter in Embedded Systems - **Precision timing** for serial baud-rate generation (Timer 1 in Mode 2 for UART). - **Event scheduling** (debouncing switches, periodic sampling). - **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** for motor control. - **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) tick generation.** - Eliminating software `delay()` loops that waste CPU cycles. --- # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The TMOD Register (Address 89H, Not Bit-Addressable) TMOD is an **8-bit register** divided into two identical 4-bit nibbles — the upper nibble controls **Timer 1** and the lower nibble controls **Timer 0**. ``` Bit: 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 +-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+ TMOD = | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 | +-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+ |-------Timer 1------| |-------Timer 0------| ``` ### 2.1.1 Bit-by-Bit Meaning | Bit | Symbol | Function | |-----|--------|----------| | **GATE** | Software/Hardware Control | When **1**, timer is enabled only if `INTx` pin is HIGH **and** `TRx` bit is set. When **0**, timer runs whenever `TRx = 1` (purely software control). | | **C/T̄** | Counter / Timer Select | **0** = Timer mode (uses internal oscillator ÷ 12). **1** = Counter mode (uses external pulse on T0/T1 pin). | | **M1, M0** | Mode Selector Bits | Select one of four operating modes (see table below). | ### 2.1.2 The Four Timer Modes > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Hot Topic:** Mode 1 and Mode 2 appear in nearly every board paper. Master these two. | M1 | M0 | Mode | Description | Max Count | Register Width | |----|----|------|-------------|-----------|----------------| | 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit Timer (legacy 8048 compatibility) | 8192 | 13 bits (THx 8-bit + TLx 5-bit) | | 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit Timer | 65536 | 16 bits (THx:TLx) | | 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit Auto-Reload Timer | 256 | 8 bits (TLx counts, THx reloads) | | 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split Timer (only for Timer 0) | 256 / 256 | TL0 + TH0 as two independent 8-bit timers | ### 2.1.3 Mode 2 — The Auto-Reload Mechanism (Most Used) In **Mode 2**, only `TLx` counts up from the value loaded into it. When `TLx` overflows from `FFH → 00H`, the value previously stored in `THx` is **automatically reloaded** into `TLx`, and the `TFx` flag is set. This is widely used for **baud-rate generation** in serial communication. ## 2.2 The TCON Register (Address 88H, Bit-Addressable) ``` Bit: 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 +-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+ TCON = | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 | +-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+-------+ ``` | Bit | Symbol | Function | Type | |-----|--------|----------|------| | **TF1** | Timer 1 Overflow Flag | Set by hardware when Timer 1 overflows; cleared by software. | Overflow | | **TR1** | Timer 1 Run Control | **1** = start Timer 1; **0** = stop Timer 1 (software controlled). | Run Bit | | **TF0** | Timer 0 Overflow Flag | Set by hardware when Timer 0 overflows. | Overflow | | **TR0** | Timer 0 Run Control | **1** = start Timer 0; **0** = stop Timer 0. | Run Bit | | **IE1** | External Interrupt 1 Edge Flag | Set on edge detect of INT1. | Interrupt | | **IT1** | Interrupt 1 Type Select | **1** = falling edge; **0** = low-level triggered. | Interrupt | | **IE0** | External Interrupt 0 Edge Flag | Set on edge detect of INT0. | Interrupt | | **IT0** | Interrupt 0 Type Select | **1** = falling edge; **0** = low-level triggered. | Interrupt | > [!NOTE] > **Why TCON is bit-addressable:** This means you can directly set/clear individual bits using instructions like `SETB TR0` or `CLR TF1`, making timer control extremely efficient in assembly. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formula values must be in SI units. Machine cycle is in seconds; delay in seconds.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------| | 1 | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | $f_{osc}$ in Hz, $T_{mc}$ in seconds | | 2 | Timer Delay (Mode 1) | $\Delta t = (2^{16} - N_{initial}) \times T_{mc}$ | $N_{initial}$ = value loaded into THx:TLx | | 3 | Timer Delay (Mode 0) | $\Delta t = (2^{13} - N_{initial}) \times T_{mc}$ | 13-bit, rarely used | | 4 | Timer Delay (Mode 2) | $\Delta t = (2^{8} - N_{initial}) \times T_{mc}$ | Auto-reload, $N_{initial}$ = THx = TLx | | 5 | Required Count Value | $N = 2^{n} - \dfrac{\Delta t_{desired}}{T_{mc}}$ | $n$ = 13, 16, or 8 | | 6 | Frequency from Timer Overflow | $f_{overflow} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta t}$ | Useful for square-wave generation | | 7 | Auto-Reload THx Value | $THx = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times \text{baud} \times 32}$ | For serial UART with Timer 1 Mode 2 (×32 mode, SMOD=0) | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Real-World Applications 1. **Serial Communication (UART):** Timer 1 in Mode 2 generates the baud rate. For 9600 baud with 11.0592 MHz: `TH1 = 253` (FDH). 2. **Traffic Light Controllers:** Periodic 1-second delays for sequencing red/yellow/green phases. 3. **Industrial Counters:** Counting products on a conveyor belt using Counter Mode with a photoelectric sensor. 4. **Servo Motor Control:** Precise 20 ms PWM pulse generation. 5. **Watchdog Timers:** Resetting the system if software hangs. --- # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Timer Count and Delay ### Step 1 — Define the Oscillator The 8051 uses an external crystal oscillator of frequency $f_{osc}$ (typically 11.0592 MHz or 12 MHz). Each oscillator period is: $$T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}}$$ ### Step 2 — Derive Machine Cycle The 8051 divides the oscillator by **12** to produce one machine cycle. Therefore, the duration of a single machine cycle is: $$T_{mc} = 12 \times T_{osc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$ **Reasoning:** Dividing by 12 ensures timing stability and gives the CPU enough time to fetch and execute instructions reliably (each instruction takes 1–4 machine cycles). ### Step 3 — Timer Increment In Timer Mode (`C/T̄ = 0`), the timer register (THx:TLx) increments by **1 on every machine cycle**. $$N_{counts} = \frac{\Delta t_{delay}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{\Delta t_{delay} \times f_{osc}}{12}$$ ### Step 4 — Overflow Condition The timer overflows when its value wraps from its maximum to 0. In **Mode 1 (16-bit)**, the maximum is $2^{16} - 1 = 65535$, and the next count becomes 0. **Number of counts needed from initial value $N_{initial}$ to overflow:** $$N_{counts} = 2^{16} - N_{initial}$$ ### Step 5 — Equate and Solve Setting the two expressions for $N_{counts}$ equal: $$2^{16} - N_{initial} = \frac{\Delta t_{delay} \times f_{osc}}{12}$$ Rearranging for the required initial value: $$N_{initial} = 2^{16} - \frac{\Delta t_{delay} \times f_{osc}}{12}$$ The corresponding delay formula: $$\Delta t_{delay} = (2^{16} - N_{initial}) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The same logic applies to Mode 0 (replace $2^{16}$ with $2^{13} = 8192$) and Mode 2 (replace with $2^{8} = 256$). ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Generating 50 ms Delay Using Timer 0, Mode 1 **Given:** - Crystal frequency: $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz - Required delay: $\Delta t = 50$ ms - Mode: Mode 1 (16-bit) ### Step 1 — Machine Cycle $$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \,\mu s$$ ### Step 2 — Required Number of Timer Counts $$N_{counts} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46{,}082.95 \approx 46{,}083 \text{ counts}$$ ### Step 3 — Initial Timer Value $$N_{initial} = 65{,}536 - 46{,}083 = 19{,}453$$ In hexadecimal: $19{,}453_{10} = 4BFD_{H}$ ### Step 4 — Split into TH0 and TL0 $$TL0 = 19{,}453 \,\bmod\, 256 = FD_{H} = 253_{10}$$ $$TH0 = \left\lfloor \frac{19{,}453}{256} \right\rfloor = 4B_{H} = 75_{10}$$ ### Step 5 — TMOD Configuration For Mode 1, Timer 0, software-controlled, timer mode: - `GATE = 0`, `C/T̄ = 0`, `M1 = 0`, `M0 = 1` → Lower nibble = `0001` = `01H` - Upper nibble (Timer 1) typically left as `0000` → `00H` $$\text{TMOD} = 0{\times}01$$ ### Step 6 — TCON Steps - Set `TR0 = 1` to start Timer 0. - Poll `TF0` until it becomes 1 (overflow). - Clear `TR0` and `TF0` for next cycle. ### Step 7 — C Code Equivalent ```c void timer0_delay_50ms(void) { TMOD = 0x01; // Timer 0, Mode 1 TH0 = 0x4B; // High byte TL0 = 0xFD; // Low byte TR0 = 1; // Start timer while (TF0 == 0); // Wait for overflow TR0 = 0; // Stop timer TF0 = 0; // Clear overflow flag } ``` ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Maximum Delay Achievable in Mode 1 **Given:** $f_{osc} = 12$ MHz ### Step 1 — Machine Cycle $$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \,\mu s$$ ### Step 2 — Maximum Count (Initial Value = 0000H) $$N_{max} = 2^{16} - 0 = 65{,}536 \text{ counts}$$ ### Step 3 — Maximum Delay $$\Delta t_{max} = 65{,}536 \times 1 \,\mu s = 65.536 \,\text{ms}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** The maximum delay in **Mode 1** with a 12 MHz crystal is **65.536 ms**, NOT 65,536 µs. Students commonly confuse units. To generate longer delays, use a software loop that calls the timer routine multiple times. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mode Selection Decision Table | Requirement | Recommended Mode | Reason | |-------------|------------------|--------| | Delay > 65 ms (12 MHz) | Mode 1 + software loop counter | Single Mode-1 timer is insufficient | | Baud rate generation (UART) | Mode 2 | Auto-reload gives glitch-free repetitive timing | | 13-bit legacy compatibility | Mode 0 | Matches 8048 family | | Two independent 8-bit timers from Timer 0 | Mode 3 | Splits Timer 0 into TL0 + TH0 | | Counting external events > 65535 | Counter Mode + software overflow counter | Hardware can only count to 65536 directly | ## 3.5 Mode 3 Split-Timer Architecture (For Completeness) In **Mode 3**, Timer 0 is split into two independent 8-bit timers: - **TL0** acts as an 8-bit timer controlled by Timer 0's `TR0` and `TF0` flags. - **TH0** acts as a separate 8-bit timer, but it **borrows Timer 1's control bits** (`TR1` becomes its run bit, `TF1` becomes its overflow flag). - Timer 1 in Mode 3 simply halts (can be used as a baud-rate generator since it still has TH1/TL1, but cannot be started). > [!NOTE] > Mode 3 is rarely used in practice but is a favorite KTU question for testing depth of understanding. --- # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 TMOD Register Bit Structure ```mermaid graph LR A["TMOD Register
Address 89H
Not Bit-Addressable"] A --> B["Upper Nibble
Timer 1"] A --> C["Lower Nibble
Timer 0"] B --> B1["Bit 7: GATE1"] B --> B2["Bit 6: C-T-bar 1"] B --> B3["Bit 5: M1 of T1"] B --> B4["Bit 4: M0 of T1"] C --> C1["Bit 3: GATE0"] C --> C2["Bit 2: C-T-bar 0"] C --> C3["Bit 1: M1 of T0"] C --> C4["Bit 0: M0 of T0"] B1 --> D1["1 = HW Enable
via INT1"] B2 --> D2["0 = Timer
1 = Counter"] B3 --> D3["Mode Select
M1 M0"] B4 --> D4["00 Mode 0
01 Mode 1
10 Mode 2
11 Mode 3"] C1 --> E1["1 = HW Enable
via INT0"] C2 --> E2["0 = Timer
1 = Counter"] C3 --> E3["Mode Select
M1 M0"] C4 --> E4["00 Mode 0
01 Mode 1
10 Mode 2
11 Mode 3"] ``` ## 4.2 TCON Register Bit Structure ```mermaid graph TB P["TCON Register
Address 88H
Bit-Addressable"] P --> Q["Timer Control Bits"] P --> R["Interrupt Control Bits"] Q --> Q1["Bit 7: TF1
Timer 1 Overflow Flag"] Q --> Q2["Bit 6: TR1
Timer 1 Run Bit"] Q --> Q3["Bit 5: TF0
Timer 0 Overflow Flag"] Q --> Q4["Bit 4: TR0
Timer 0 Run Bit"] R --> R1["Bit 3: IE1
INT1 Edge Flag"] R --> R2["Bit 2: IT1
INT1 Type Select"] R --> R3["Bit 1: IE0
INT0 Edge Flag"] R --> R4["Bit 0: IT0
INT0 Type Select"] Q1 --> F1["Set by HW on overflow
Cleared by SW"] Q2 --> F2["1 = Start
0 = Stop"] Q4 --> F3["Used with
SETB TR0"] ``` ## 4.3 Timer 0 in Mode 1 — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid graph LR OSC["Crystal Oscillator
f_osc"] --> DIV["Divide by 12
Counter"] DIV --> MC["Machine Cycle
Pulse T_mc"] MC --> MUX["MUX
Select Internal or External"] T0PIN["External Pin T0
P3.4"] --> MUX MUX --> CT["C-T-bar bit
TMOD.2"] CT --> INC["Increment Logic
+1 per pulse"] INC --> REG["16-bit Register
TH0:TL0"] REG --> OVF["Overflow Detector
FFFFH to 0000H"] OVF --> TFLG["Set TF0
in TCON.5"] TRBIT["TR0
TCON.4"] --> AND1["AND Gate"] GATE["GATE
TMOD.3"] --> AND2["AND Gate"] INT0["INT0 Pin
P3.2"] --> AND2 AND2 --> AND1 AND1 --> INC REG --> APPL["Application Layer
Delay Generation
Event Counting"] ``` ## 4.4 Auto-Reload Mechanism — Mode 2 Flow ```mermaid graph TD S1["Start: Load THx value
into TLx"] --> S2["Increment TLx on
every machine cycle"] S2 --> S3{"TLx overflow?
FFH to 00H"} S3 -- No --> S2 S3 -- Yes --> S4["Set TFX flag
in TCON"] S4 --> S5["Auto-reload:
TLx gets THx value"] S5 --> S6["Continue counting
without SW intervention"] S6 --> S3 S6 --> APP["Use for:
Baud Rate Generation
Periodic Interrupts"] ``` ## 4.5 Complete Timer Programming Sequence ```mermaid graph TD A["Step 1: Calculate
Timer Count Value N"] --> B["Step 2: Load TMOD
with Mode and Options"] B --> C["Step 3: Load THx
with High Byte"] C --> D["Step 4: Load TLx
with Low Byte"] D --> E["Step 5: Set TRx = 1
Start Timer"] E --> F{"Step 6: Wait for
TFX = 1?"} F -- No --> F F -- Yes --> G["Step 7: Clear TRx = 0
Stop Timer"] G --> H["Step 8: Clear TFX = 0
Reset flag"] H --> I["Delay Achieved!
Repeat if needed"] ``` --- # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Differentiate between Timer mode and Counter mode of the 8051 with respect to the `C/T̄` bit.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Timer Mode (`C/T̄ = 0`) | Counter Mode (`C/T̄ = 1`) | |---|---|---| | Clock Source | Internal oscillator divided by 12 | External pulse on T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) pin | | Use Case | Generating time delays | Counting external events | | Trigger | Automatic (every machine cycle) | External edge/level on pin | | Typical Application | Baud rate generation, PWM | Product counting, frequency measurement | *(3 marks — 1 mark per row + 1 mark for clarity)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is the function of the GATE bit in the TMOD register? Explain hardware-controlled timer operation.** **Model Answer:** The **GATE bit** in the TMOD register provides **hardware control** over the timer's start/stop operation. - When `GATE = 0` (software control): The timer runs whenever the corresponding `TRx` bit is set to 1. - When `GATE = 1` (hardware control): The timer runs **only when both** `TRx = 1` **and** the corresponding `INTx` pin is HIGH. **Application:** Measuring pulse width — the timer starts when an external pulse begins (INTx goes HIGH with TRx already set) and stops when the pulse ends (INTx goes LOW), giving a direct digital measurement of the pulse width. *(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for truth table, 1 mark for application)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **TMOD register** of the 8051 microcontroller. List all four modes of operation. **(7 marks)** **b)** An 8051 system uses a **12 MHz crystal**. Write an 8051 C program to generate a delay of **exactly 25 ms** using **Timer 1 in Mode 1**. Show all calculations and TMOD configuration. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) The **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** is an 8-bit special function register located at address **89H**. It is **not bit-addressable** and is configured only by writing a full byte. **Bit Structure:** | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 | |-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------| | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 | | \multicolumn{4}{c}{Timer 1} | \multicolumn{4}{c}{Timer 0} | - **GATE:** Hardware enable control. - **C/T̄:** Counter (1) / Timer (0) select. - **M1, M0:** Mode select bits. **Four Modes:** | M1 | M0 | Mode | Description | |----|----|------|-------------| | 0 | 0 | Mode 0 | 13-bit timer (compatible with 8048) | | 0 | 1 | Mode 1 | 16-bit timer (most flexible) | | 1 | 0 | Mode 2 | 8-bit auto-reload timer | | 1 | 1 | Mode 3 | Split timer (Timer 0 only) | *(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 2 for bit description, 3 for modes table)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $f_{osc} = 12$ MHz, $\Delta t = 25$ ms, Timer 1, Mode 1. **Step 1 — Machine Cycle:** $$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \,\mu s$$ **Step 2 — Required Count:** $$N_{counts} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 25{,}000$$ **Step 3 — Initial Timer Value:** $$N_{initial} = 65{,}536 - 25{,}000 = 40{,}536$$ In hexadecimal: $40{,}536_{10} = 9E58_{H}$ **Step 4 — Split into TH1 and TL1:** $$TL1 = 40{,}536 \bmod 256 = 58_{H} = 88_{10}$$ $$TH1 = \left\lfloor \frac{40{,}536}{256} \right\rfloor = 9E_{H} = 158_{10}$$ **Step 5 — TMOD Value (Timer 1, Mode 1, Software Control, Timer Mode):** - Upper nibble: GATE=0, C/T̄=0, M1=0, M0=1 → `0001` = `0x1` - Lower nibble (Timer 0 unused): `0000` = `0x0` - `TMOD = 0x10` **Step 6 — C Program:** ```c #include void timer1_delay_25ms(void) { TMOD = 0x10; // Timer 1, Mode 1 TH1 = 0x9E; // High byte TL1 = 0x58; // Low byte TR1 = 1; // Start Timer 1 while (TF1 == 0); // Wait for overflow TR1 = 0; // Stop Timer 1 TF1 = 0; // Clear overflow flag } void main(void) { while (1) { P1 = 0xFF; // LED ON timer1_delay_25ms(); P1 = 0x00; // LED OFF timer1_delay_25ms(); } } ``` *(7 marks — 1 for $T_{mc}$, 2 for count, 1 for TH/TL split, 1 for TMOD, 2 for program)* --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **a)** Explain the **TCON register** in detail. Discuss the role of `TR0`, `TF0`, `TR1`, and `TF1` bits. **(7 marks)** **b)** Explain the **Auto-Reload mechanism** of Timer 0 in **Mode 2**. Why is this mode preferred for **baud-rate generation** in serial communication? Show how to calculate the TH0 value for **9600 baud** with an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) The **TCON (Timer Control) register** is at address **88H** and is **bit-addressable**, allowing direct bit manipulation (e.g., `SETB TR0`). **Bit Description:** | Bit | Name | Function | |-----|------|----------| | TF1 (bit 7) | Timer 1 Overflow Flag | Hardware sets on overflow; software must clear | | TR1 (bit 6) | Timer 1 Run Bit | `1` = start, `0` = stop (software controlled) | | TF0 (bit 5) | Timer 0 Overflow Flag | Hardware sets on overflow; software must clear | | TR0 (bit 4) | Timer 0 Run Bit | `1` = start, `0` = stop (software controlled) | | IE1, IT1, IE0, IT0 | External Interrupt Control | Manage INT0 and INT1 pins | **Role of Key Bits:** - **TR0/TR1 (Run Bits):** Act as the master switch. The timer is enabled only when the corresponding TRx bit is set (and GATE conditions are satisfied). This is the **start/stop control** for counting. - **TF0/TF1 (Overflow Flags):** Act as the **completion indicator**. They are set by hardware when the timer register overflows from its maximum value to zero. The CPU either polls these flags or uses them to trigger an interrupt (if enabled in the IE register). **Programming Pattern:** ```c TR0 = 1; // Start while (TF0 == 0); // Wait TR0 = 0; // Stop TF0 = 0; // Clear ``` *(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 2 for bit description, 3 for role explanation)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Auto-Reload Mechanism (Mode 2):** In Mode 2, the timer operates as an **8-bit counter** using only `TLx`. The user preloads an 8-bit value into `THx`. The counting process is: 1. `TLx` is loaded with the value in `THx` initially. 2. `TLx` increments on every machine cycle. 3. When `TLx` overflows from `FFH → 00H`, the hardware **automatically reloads** `THx` value into `TLx`. 4. Simultaneously, the `TFx` flag is set. 5. The process repeats **without any software intervention**, making it ideal for continuous periodic events. **Why Mode 2 for Baud-Rate Generation:** - **Glitch-free timing:** The auto-reload happens in a single machine cycle, so the timing is precise. - **No software reload overhead:** Once initialized, the timer runs continuously. - **Compact:** Uses only one 8-bit register pair, leaving Timer 0 free for other uses. **Calculation for 9600 Baud:** The UART in Mode 1 uses Timer 1 overflow at **32 times the baud rate** (when SMOD=0). $$\text{Timer Overflow Frequency} = \text{Baud Rate} \times 32 = 9600 \times 32 = 307{,}200 \,\text{Hz}$$ $$\text{Overflow Period} = \frac{1}{307{,}200} = 3.255 \,\mu s$$ $$\text{Number of counts} = \frac{3.255 \,\mu s}{1.085 \,\mu s} = 3 \text{ counts}$$ $$\text{TH1 (Reload Value)} = 256 - 3 = 253 = FD_{H}$$ **Verification:** The standard value for 9600 baud with 11.0592 MHz crystal is **`TH1 = 0xFD`**, confirming our calculation. *(7 marks — 2 for mechanism, 2 for justification, 3 for calculation)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing µs with ms in delay calculations. Always convert to seconds before applying formulas. > 2. **Forgetting to Clear TFx:** The overflow flag is NOT auto-cleared. Forgetting `TFx = 0` causes the next loop to exit immediately. > 3. **Wrong TMOD Nibble:** Timer 1 bits occupy the **upper** nibble (bits 4–7), not the lower. Easy to swap. > 4. **Integer Truncation:** Using `int` for delay variables in C may overflow for delays > 32 seconds. > 5. **GATE Bit Misuse:** Setting `GATE = 1` without driving `INTx` pin HIGH will prevent the timer from running at all — a frequent lab debugging issue. > 6. **Mode 3 Limitation:** Timer 1 in Mode 3 simply halts. Do not use Timer 1 for baud rate if Timer 0 is in Mode 3. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — TMOD & TCON Registers** - ✅ The 8051 has **2 timers** (Timer 0, Timer 1), each **16-bit** in Mode 1. - ✅ **TMOD (89H)** — configures mode, function (timer/counter), and gating. **Not bit-addressable.** - ✅ **TCON (88H)** — controls run bits (TR0, TR1) and overflow flags (TF0, TF1). **Bit-addressable.** - ✅ **Mode 1** = 16-bit (max delay 65.536 ms at 12 MHz). - ✅ **Mode 2** = 8-bit auto-reload (used for **baud rate**). - ✅ **Mode 0** = 13-bit (legacy). - ✅ **Mode 3** = split timer (Timer 0 only). - ✅ **Machine Cycle** = $12 / f_{osc}$. - ✅ **Delay Formula (Mode 1):** $\Delta t = (65536 - N_{initial}) \times T_{mc}$. - ✅ **Baud rate for 9600 with 11.0592 MHz:** `TH1 = 0xFD`. - ✅ **GATE = 1** enables hardware control via `INTx` pin. - ✅ **C/T̄ = 0** → Timer (internal); **C/T̄ = 1** → Counter (external on T0/T1). - ✅ Always **clear TFx** after detecting overflow, or it will persist. - ✅ Maximum external count = **65,536** in 16-bit mode. ---

topics.title: Serial Communication - SBUF, SCON, PCON Registers and Their Functions (Details of Different Modes of Operation Not Included)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Serial Communication - SBUF, SFRs 8051, SCON, PCON, Registers Functions, UART, Asynchronous, SBUF, SCON, SM0, SM1, SM2, REN, TB8, RB8, TI, RI, SMOD, PCON Register Functions, KTU 2024, Excluding Modes of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Serial Communication in 8051** is a method of transferring data **one bit at a time** (sequentially) over a single communication line, as opposed to parallel communication where multiple bits are transmitted simultaneously across multiple lines. The 8051 microcontroller's on-chip **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)** peripheral handles this bit-serial data transfer, and it is governed by **three Special Function Registers (SFRs)** — namely **SBUF, SCON, and PCON**.

The 8051 contains a **full-duplex** serial port, meaning it can transmit and receive data simultaneously using **two physically separate 8-bit registers** that share the same SFR address (99H). These registers are:

| SFR | Name | Address | Primary Role |
| :- | :- | :- | :- |
| **SBUF** | Serial Buffer | 99H | Holds data byte for Tx/Rx |
| **SCON** | Serial Control | 98H | Configures modes & flags |
| **PCON** | Power Control | 87H | Controls baud rate doubler (SMOD) |

## 1.2 The Three Pillars — Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Post Office Counter:**
> Imagine a small post office counter with a single customer window:
> - **SBUF** is the **envelope tray**. When you *drop* a letter in, it goes out (transmit). When you *pick up* a letter, it came in (receive). Same physical tray, but two separate compartments inside.
> - **SCON** is the **bulletin board** displaying operational rules: "Is the line open?", "Should we accept incoming mail?", "A new letter just arrived!", "The last letter was sent!"
> - **PCON (specifically bit 7, SMOD)** is the **manager's switch** that says "Run the conveyor belt at normal speed or **double speed**."

## 1.3 Why These Registers Matter (Engineering Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the **function of each bit** of SCON and PCON, the **physical separation of SBUF**, and the **baud rate relationship** to Timer 1. Mastering these registers is foundational for **UART programming, GSM module interfacing, GPS modules, Bluetooth (HC-05) communication, and embedded IoT sensor networks**.

## 1.4 Overview of the Three Registers

1. **SBUF (Serial Data Buffer) — Address 99H**
   - Writing to SBUF loads the **transmit register** → starts transmission.
   - Reading from SBUF fetches data from the **receive register**.
   - Physically two separate 8-bit registers sharing one address (hardware-managed).

2. **SCON (Serial Port Control) — Address 98H, Bit-Addressable**
   - 8-bit control + status register.
   - Controls mode selection, receiver enable, and interrupt flags.

3. **PCON (Power Control) — Address 87H, NOT Bit-Addressable**
   - 8-bit register primarily for power-saving modes (Idle, Power-Down).
   - **Bit 7 (SMOD)** doubles the baud rate when set to 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SBUF — The Serial Buffer Register (Deep Dive)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Dual-Nature Trick:** The 8051 has only **one SFR address (99H)** for SBUF, but internally the hardware contains **two physically separate 8-bit latches** — a *Transmit Buffer* and a *Receive Buffer*.

### Operational Logic of SBUF

1. **To Transmit (Write Operation):**
   - CPU executes `MOV SBUF, A`
   - The byte is **latched into the Transmit Buffer**.
   - Serial port hardware immediately begins shifting it out bit-by-bit on **TxD pin (P3.1)**.
   - When the last bit is shifted out, the **TI flag** is set.

2. **To Receive (Read Operation):**
   - When a complete byte arrives on **RxD pin (P3.0)**, hardware latches it into the Receive Buffer.
   - The **RI flag** is set to alert the CPU.
   - CPU executes `MOV A, SBUF` to read the received byte.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Exams:** Both buffers exist *independently*. You can write to SBUF (start a new transmission) **without disturbing the previously received byte** still sitting in the receive buffer. This is what enables **full-duplex** operation.

### SBUF Register — Visual Snapshot

| Operation | Action | Result |
| :- | :- | :- | :- |
| `MOV SBUF, A` (Write) | Loads transmit latch | Starts sending frame on TxD |
| `MOV A, SBUF` (Read) | Reads receive latch | Retrieves received byte from RxD |
| Reset State | **00H** (both buffers) | No data in transit |

---

## 2.2 SCON — The Serial Control Register (Bit-by-Bit Masterclass)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SCON is the **most tested register** in KTU Module 2. It is **bit-addressable** at SFR address **98H**. Every single bit has a specific function.

### SCON Bit Layout

| Bit | Symbol | Function | Reset Value |
| :- | :- | :- | :- |
| **D7** | **SM0** | Serial port mode selector bit 0 (with SM1) | 0 |
| **D6** | **SM1** | Serial port mode selector bit 1 (with SM0) | 0 |
| **D5** | **SM2** | Multiprocessor communication enable | 0 |
| **D4** | **REN** | Receiver Enable (must be 1 to receive) | 0 |
| **D3** | **TB8** | 9th transmitted bit (Modes 2 & 3) | 0 |
| **D2** | **RB8** | 9th received bit (Modes 2 & 3) | 0 |
| **D1** | **TI** | Transmit Interrupt flag (set by hardware) | 0 |
| **D0** | **RI** | Receive Interrupt flag (set by hardware) | 0 |

### Functional Breakdown of Each Bit

**1. SM0 and SM1 — Mode Select Bits (D7, D6)**
> [!NOTE]
> Although the KTU 2024 syllabus states *"Details of different modes of operation not included,"* you **must still know** what the bit combinations *represent at a high level*, since questions may ask "What mode is selected when SM0=0, SM1=1?"

| SM0 | SM1 | Mode | Description | Baud Rate Source |
| :- | :- | :- | :- | :- |
| 0 | 0 | Mode 0 | 8-bit Shift Register | Fixed (f_osc/12) |
| 0 | 1 | Mode 1 | 8-bit UART | Timer 1 Overflow |
| 1 | 0 | Mode 2 | 9-bit UART | Fixed (f_osc/32 or /64) |
| 1 | 1 | Mode 3 | 9-bit UART | Timer 1 Overflow |

**2. SM2 — Multiprocessor Communication Enable (D5)**
- In **Modes 2 and 3**: If SM2 = 1, **RI is set only when the 9th received bit (RB8) = 1**. This enables multi-8051 networks where slaves ignore frames meant for others.
- In **Mode 1**: If SM2 = 1, RI is activated only if a valid stop bit is received.
- In **Mode 0**: SM2 has no effect.

**3. REN — Receiver Enable (D4)**
- Set to **1 by software** to allow serial reception.
- Cleared to **0 by software** to disable the receiver.
- Must be set **before** and **during** reception; no effect on transmission.

**4. TB8 — 9th Transmitted Bit (D3)**
- Used **only in Modes 2 and 3** (9-bit data modes).
- Software loads TB8 with the 9th bit (often used for parity or slave address).
- TB8 is the 9th bit shifted out **before** the 8 SBUF bits.

**5. RB8 — 9th Received Bit (D2)**
- Used **only in Modes 2 and 3**.
- Hardware stores the 9th received bit here.
- Software reads RB8 to retrieve the 9th bit.

**6. TI — Transmit Interrupt Flag (D1)**
- Set to **1 by hardware** at the **end of the 8th bit** in Mode 0, or at the **beginning of the stop bit** in Modes 1, 2, 3.
- **Must be cleared by software** (this is a classic KTU pitfall).
- Often used to trigger the next transmission in polling mode.

**7. RI — Receive Interrupt Flag (D0)**
- Set to **1 by hardware** at the **end of the 8th received bit** (Mode 0), or at the **middle of the stop bit** (Modes 1, 2, 3).
- **Must be cleared by software**.
- Indicates that a complete byte is sitting in the receive buffer, ready to be read.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget to clear TI and RI after servicing them. If not cleared, the program will treat every transmission/reception as an infinite loop event.

### SCON Reset Value: 00H
On power-on or reset, all SCON bits are 0 → Mode 0 with reception disabled, no flags set.

---

## 2.3 PCON — The Power Control Register (Focused on SMOD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PCON is **NOT bit-addressable** (unlike SCON). This means you cannot use instructions like `SETB SMOD`. You must read the entire byte, modify it, and write it back.

### PCON Bit Layout

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
| :- | :- | :- |
| **D7** | **SMOD** | **Serial Baud Rate Doubler** (1 = double the baud rate) |
| D6 | — | Reserved |
| D5 | — | Reserved |
| D4 | — | Reserved |
| D3 | GF1 | General Purpose Flag 1 |
| D2 | GF0 | General Purpose Flag 0 |
| D1 | **PD** | Power-Down mode bit |
| D0 | **IDL** | Idle mode bit |

### The Critical Bit for Serial Communication: SMOD (D7)

> [!NOTE]
> When **SMOD = 1**, the baud rate generated by the serial port is **doubled**. This is the only PCON bit directly relevant to UART. The GF0/GF1 are user-defined flags, and PD/IDL control power-saving modes (not part of KTU Module 2 syllabus).

### Engineering Utility of SMOD

- **Practical scenario:** You need 19200 baud, but Timer 1 cannot generate exactly 19200 (only 19200 with a certain TH1 reload and 2× doubling). Setting SMOD=1 lets you use a **lower Timer 1 overflow rate** and effectively double it.
- The **standard 11.0592 MHz crystal** is popular because, combined with SMOD=0, it yields **error-free** standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 2400, 1200).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all baud rate formulas** that the 8051 serial port uses, classified by mode. The syllabus states "details of modes not included," but the **formula relationships** are mandatory for KTU numerical problems.

| Mode | Frame Size | Baud Rate Formula | Variables |
| :- | :- | :- | :- |
| Mode 0 | 8-bit | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12}$ | $f_{\text{osc}}$ = crystal frequency |
| Mode 2 | 9-bit | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{64} \times f_{\text{osc}}$ | $f_{\text{osc}}$, SMOD |
| **Mode 1** | 8-bit | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | $f_{\text{osc}}$, TH1, SMOD |
| **Mode 3** | 9-bit | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | $f_{\text{osc}}$, TH1, SMOD |

### Inverted Form — Calculating TH1 for a Desired Baud Rate

For Modes 1 and 3, solving for TH1:

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Almost every KTU Module 2 numerical problem gives you $f_{\text{osc}}$, SMOD, and target baud rate, and asks you to compute TH1. Memorize the inverted formula above.

### Standard 8051 Crystal Frequencies and Their Purpose

| Crystal ($f_{\text{osc}}$) | Common Use | Reason |
| :- | :- | :- |
| **11.0592 MHz** | Standard serial communication | Gives **exact** baud rates (9600, 4800, etc.) with SMOD=0 and integer TH1 values |
| **12 MHz** | Common general-purpose | Suitable for Mode 0 timing |
| **24 MHz** | Higher-speed designs | Used when faster operations are needed |

### SCON Bit Configuration Snippets (Ready for KTU)

| Application | SCON Value | Setting |
| :- | :- | :- |
| Mode 1, Receiver enabled, TI/RI cleared | 50H | `MOV SCON, #50H` |
| Mode 3, Receiver enabled | D0H | `MOV SCON, #0D0H` |
| Mode 0, no reception | 00H | `MOV SCON, #00H` |
| Mode 2, Receiver enabled | 90H | `MOV SCON, #90H` |

> [!NOTE]
> **PCON Doubling Snippet (C equivalent in assembly):**
> To set SMOD bit, we use:
> $$\text{PCON} = \text{PCON} \;\vert\; 0\text{x}80$$
> This is the **OR-mask method** since PCON is not bit-addressable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Baud Rate Formula Has $2^{\text{SMOD}} / 32$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation explains the *origin* of the $2^{\text{SMOD}} / 32$ factor in Modes 1 and 3 — a frequent KTU theory question.

### The Reasoning Chain

1. **Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** produces an overflow every:
   $$T_{\text{overflow}} = 12 \times (256 - \text{TH1}) \times T_{\text{machine}}$$
   where $T_{\text{machine}} = 1 / f_{\text{osc}}$ is the oscillator period.

2. The **Timer 1 overflow rate** (frequency of overflows per second) is:
   $$f_{\text{overflow}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{overflow}}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

3. In **Mode 1 (8-bit UART)**, the 8051 **divides Timer 1's overflow rate by 32** to generate the baud rate. This is a hardware-fixed divider inside the serial port block.

4. Therefore, the **base baud rate** (with SMOD=0) is:
   $$\text{Baud}_{\text{base}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{overflow}}}{32} = \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

5. When **SMOD = 1**, the hardware divider effectively changes from /32 to /16, **doubling** the baud rate. This is mathematically equivalent to multiplying by $2^{\text{SMOD}}$:
   $$\text{Baud}_{\text{final}} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

6. Since $32 = 2^5$, the formula is sometimes rewritten as:
   $$\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor **384 = 32 × 12** appears frequently in textbooks. It is the product of the hardware divider (32) and the machine-cycle multiplier (12 clocks per machine cycle).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — TH1 Calculation for 9600 Baud

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. The serial port is configured in **Mode 1 (8-bit UART)** with **SMOD = 0**. Calculate the value to be loaded into **TH1** to achieve a baud rate of **9600 bps**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11{,}059{,}200 \text{ Hz}$
- $\text{SMOD} = 0 \Rightarrow 2^{\text{SMOD}} = 1$
- $\text{Baud Rate} = 9600 \text{ bps}$

**Step 1 — Apply the Inverted Formula**

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values**

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{1 \times 11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 9600}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Denominator**

$$384 \times 9600 = 3{,}686{,}400$$

**Step 4 — Divide**

$$\dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400} = 3$$

**Step 5 — Subtract from 256**

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Hexadecimal**

$$253 \div 16 = 15 \text{ remainder } 13 \Rightarrow 253 = 0\text{xFD}$$

**Result:** $\text{TH1} = 253 = 0\text{xFD} = 1111\,1101_2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reason **11.0592 MHz** is the most popular crystal is that it gives an **integer value of 3** in the division step, producing TH1 = 0xFD — a clean, error-free setting for 9600 baud.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — TH1 Calculation for 2400 Baud (SMOD=1)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Same 11.0592 MHz crystal. Target baud rate = **2400**, SMOD = **1**. Find TH1.

**Given:**
- $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11{,}059{,}200 \text{ Hz}$
- $\text{SMOD} = 1 \Rightarrow 2^{\text{SMOD}} = 2$
- $\text{Baud Rate} = 2400 \text{ bps}$

**Step 1 — Apply the Inverted Formula**

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{2 \times 11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 2400}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Denominator**

$$384 \times 2400 = 921{,}600$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Numerator**

$$2 \times 11{,}059{,}200 = 22{,}118{,}400$$

**Step 4 — Divide**

$$\dfrac{22{,}118{,}400}{921{,}600} = 24$$

**Step 5 — Subtract from 256**

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 24 = 232$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Hexadecimal**

$$232 = 14 \times 16 + 8 = 0\text{xE8}$$

**Result:** $\text{TH1} = 232 = 0\text{xE8} = 1110\,1000_2$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Mode 2 Baud Rate Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 12 MHz crystal is used in **Mode 2** with **SMOD = 1**. Calculate the baud rate.

**Given:**
- $f_{\text{osc}} = 12 \text{ MHz} = 12{,}000{,}000 \text{ Hz}$
- $\text{SMOD} = 1 \Rightarrow 2^{\text{SMOD}} = 2$

**Step 1 — Mode 2 Formula**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{64} \times f_{\text{osc}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2}{64} \times 12{,}000{,}000 = \dfrac{1}{32} \times 12{,}000{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compute**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = 375{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mode 2 produces **very high fixed baud rates** (375 kbps at 12 MHz, 345.6 kbps at 11.0592 MHz). It is rarely used in practice and is most often referenced theoretically in KTU exams.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Function of Each Register

| Aspect | SBUF (99H) | SCON (98H) | PCON (87H) |
| :- | :- | :- | :- |
| **Primary Purpose** | Data buffer (Tx/Rx) | Configuration + flags | Power + baud rate doubler |
| **Bit-Addressable?** | No | **Yes** | No |
| **Size** | 8 bits | 8 bits | 8 bits |
| **Reset Value** | 00H | 00H | 0xxx0000b |
| **Hardware-Set Bits** | None (data-driven) | **TI, RI** | None |
| **Software-Set Bits** | None (CPU writes byte) | **SM0, SM1, SM2, REN, TB8** | **SMOD, PD, IDL, GF0, GF1** |
| **Read Operation** | Fetches received byte | Reads flag status (TI, RI, RB8) | Reads current power mode |
| **Write Operation** | Loads byte to transmit | Configures mode & enables | Changes power mode |
| **KTU Weightage** | Moderate (concept) | **Highest** (frequently tested) | High (SMOD specifically) |
| **Affected Pins** | P3.0 (RxD), P3.1 (TxD) | Controls all serial pins | None directly (modifies baud) |

---

## 3.6 Comprehensive SBUF vs. Two-Register Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following schematic-style breakdown clarifies why one address (99H) serves two registers:

| SFR Address | Hardware Behind It | Read Effect | Write Effect |
| :- | :- | :- | :- |
| **99H (SBUF)** | **Transmit Latch** | — | Loads byte, starts transmission |
| **99H (SBUF)** | **Receive Latch** | Returns received byte | — |

The CPU cannot access the *Tx latch* by reading or the *Rx latch* by writing. The hardware steering logic in the 8051's serial port block automatically directs the operation:

$$\text{CPU Action: Write to 99H} \rightarrow \text{Serial Port routes byte to Transmit Shift Register}$$

$$\text{CPU Action: Read from 99H} \rightarrow \text{Serial Port routes byte from Receive Shift Register}$$

This means the instruction **MOV A, SBUF** is fundamentally different from **MOV SBUF, A** at the hardware level, even though both reference the same SFR address 99H.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level 8051 Serial Port Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of the 8051 serial communication system, showing how SBUF, SCON, and PCON interact with Timer 1 and the I/O pins.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["8051 CPU Core"]
    SBUFTX["SBUF Transmit Latch"]
    SBUFRX["SBUF Receive Latch"]
    SHIFTTX["Transmit Shift Register"]
    SHIFTRX["Receive Shift Register"]
    SCON["SCON Register 98H"]
    PCON["PCON Register 87H SMOD bit"]
    TIMER1["Timer 1 Overflow Rate"]
    BAUDGEN["Baud Rate Generator Div by 32 or 16"]
    TXD["TxD Pin P3.1"]
    RXD["RxD Pin P3.0"]
    TIFLAG["TI Flag Set"]
    RIFLAG["RI Flag Set"]

    CPU -- MOV SBUF A --> SBUFTX
    SBUFTX --> SHIFTTX
    SHIFTTX -- Bit serial --> TXD
    SHIFTTX -- End of frame --> TIFLAG
    TIFLAG --> SCON

    RXD -- Bit serial --> SHIFTRX
    SHIFTRX --> SBUFRX
    SBUFRX -- MOV A SBUF --> CPU
    SHIFTRX -- End of frame --> RIFLAG
    RIFLAG --> SCON

    TIMER1 --> BAUDGEN
    PCON -- Doubles baud when SMOD 1 --> BAUDGEN
    BAUDGEN -- Clock --> SHIFTTX
    BAUDGEN -- Clock --> SHIFTRX

    CPU -- Configures --> SCON
    SCON -- Mode select REN --> SHIFTTX
    SCON -- Mode select REN --> SHIFTRX
```

## 4.2 SCON Register — Bit-by-Bit Layout (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCON["SCON Register Address 98H Bit Addressable"]
        SM0["D7 SM0 Mode Bit 0"]
        SM1["D6 SM1 Mode Bit 1"]

        REN["D4 REN Receiver Enable"]
        TB8["D3 TB8 9th Tx Bit"]
        RB8["D2 RB8 9th Rx Bit"]
        TI["D1 TI Tx Interrupt"]
        RI["D0 RI Rx Interrupt"]
    end
```

| Bit Position | Bit Name | Type | Trigger / Function |
| :- | :- | :- | :- |
| D7 | SM0 | Software Control | Mode select MSB |
| D6 | SM1 | Software Control | Mode select LSB |
| D5 | SM2 | Software Control | Multiprocessor filter |
| D4 | REN | Software Control | Enable receiver |
| D3 | TB8 | Software Load | 9th bit to transmit |
| D2 | RB8 | Hardware Latch | 9th bit received |
| D1 | TI | Hardware Set, Software Clear | Tx done flag |
| D0 | RI | Hardware Set, Software Clear | Rx done flag |

## 4.3 PCON Register — Bit-by-Bit Layout (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PCONREG["PCON Register Address 87H Not Bit Addressable"]
        SMODBIT["D7 SMOD Baud Doubler"]

        RES4["D4 Reserved"]
        GF1["D3 GF1 General Flag"]
        GF0["D2 GF0 General Flag"]

        IDLBIT["D0 IDL Idle Mode"]
    end
```

| Bit Position | Bit Name | Relevance to Serial Communication |
| :- | :- | :- |
| D7 | SMOD | **CRITICAL** — Doubles baud rate when set |
| D6–D4 | Reserved | None for serial |
| D3 | GF1 | General purpose user flag |
| D2 | GF0 | General purpose user flag |
| D1 | PD | Power-down (not serial-related) |
| D0 | IDL | Idle mode (not serial-related) |

## 4.4 SBUF — Dual-Latch Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPUWRITE["CPU Instruction MOV SBUF A"]
    CPUREAD["CPU Instruction MOV A SBUF"]
    ADDRESS99["SFR Address 99H"]
    STEER["Hardware Steering Logic"]
    TXLATCH["Transmit Data Latch 8 bits"]
    RXLATCH["Receive Data Latch 8 bits"]
    TXSHIFT["Transmit Shift Register"]
    RXSHIFT["Receive Shift Register"]
    TXDPIN["TxD Pin P3.1"]
    RXDPIN["RxD Pin P3.0"]

    CPUWRITE --> ADDRESS99
    CPUREAD --> ADDRESS99
    ADDRESS99 --> STEER
    STEER -- Write to SBUF --> TXLATCH
    STEER -- Read from SBUF --> RXLATCH
    TXLATCH --> TXSHIFT
    TXSHIFT -- Serial bits --> TXDPIN
    RXDPIN -- Serial bits --> RXSHIFT
    RXSHIFT --> RXLATCH
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The arrows in the diagram above reveal the **key conceptual point**: the same SFR address (99H) feeds a steering block that routes the operation to either the transmit or receive latch based on whether the CPU is reading or writing.

## 4.5 Flowchart — Transmission Process (Conceptual Steps)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    STEP1["Step 1 Load SBUF with data byte to transmit"]
    STEP2["Step 2 Byte latched into Transmit Shift Register"]
    STEP3["Step 3 Hardware shifts out 8 bits LSB first on TxD pin"]
    STEP4["Step 4 Each bit timed by baud rate generator"]
    STEP5["Step 5 After final bit hardware sets TI flag in SCON"]
    STEP6["Step 6 CPU polls or is interrupted by TI"]
    STEP7["Step 7 CPU clears TI flag by software"]
    STEP8["Step 8 CPU loads next byte into SBUF if any"]

    STEP1 --> STEP2
    STEP2 --> STEP3
    STEP3 --> STEP4
    STEP4 --> STEP5
    STEP5 --> STEP6
    STEP6 --> STEP7
    STEP7 --> STEP8
    STEP8 --> STEP1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These mirror KTU's Part A format — concise, definition-based, and focused on register functions.

### Question 1: Explain the function of SBUF register in 8051. Why does it have only one SFR address but serves two purposes? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **SBUF (Serial Buffer) register** at SFR address **99H** is an 8-bit register used for serial data transfer in the 8051. It serves two purposes:

1. **Write Operation (`MOV SBUF, A`):** The CPU writes a byte to address 99H, which is latched into the **Transmit Buffer**. The serial port hardware then shifts the byte out bit-by-bit on the **TxD pin (P3.1)**. Once transmission is complete, the **TI flag** in SCON is set.

2. **Read Operation (`MOV A, SBUF`):** The CPU reads from address 99H, which fetches data from the **Receive Buffer**, where a complete byte received on the **RxD pin (P3.0)** is stored. After successful reception, the **RI flag** in SCON is set.

**Why one address, two registers?** The 8051 internally has **two physically separate 8-bit registers** (a transmit latch and a receive latch) that share the SFR address 99H. **Hardware steering logic** automatically directs a CPU write to the transmit latch and a CPU read to the receive latch. This design enables **full-duplex** communication, allowing simultaneous transmission and reception without address conflicts.

---

### Question 2: What is the role of the SCON register in 8051? List its bits and state the function of SM0, SM1, REN, TI, and RI. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **SCON (Serial Control) register** at SFR address **98H** is an 8-bit, **bit-addressable** register that controls the operation of the 8051's on-chip serial port. It configures the serial mode, enables the receiver, and stores interrupt flags.

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
| :- | :- | :- |
| D7 | **SM0** | Serial port mode selector bit 0 (used with SM1) |
| D6 | **SM1** | Serial port mode selector bit 1 (used with SM0) |
| D5 | SM2 | Multiprocessor communication enable |
| D4 | **REN** | Receiver Enable — must be set to 1 by software to allow reception |
| D3 | TB8 | 9th transmitted bit (Modes 2 and 3) |
| D2 | RB8 | 9th received bit (Modes 2 and 3) |
| D1 | **TI** | Transmit Interrupt flag — set by hardware at end of transmission; must be cleared by software |
| D0 | **RI** | Receive Interrupt flag — set by hardware when a complete byte is received; must be cleared by software |

On reset, SCON = **00H** (Mode 0, receiver disabled, flags cleared).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. You are given **Question A** and **Question B**. Pick **ONE** to answer. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A: Serial Communication Registers in 8051 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the function of **SBUF** and **SCON** registers in 8051. Discuss the role of **TI** and **RI** flags. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**SBUF (Serial Buffer) Register — Address 99H:**

The SBUF register is an 8-bit register used for both **transmitting and receiving** serial data in the 8051. It is a **dual-function register**:

- **For Transmission:** When the CPU executes `MOV SBUF, A`, the byte in accumulator A is loaded into the internal **Transmit Shift Register**. The serial port then begins shifting the byte out on the **TxD pin (P3.1)**, LSB first, at the configured baud rate. After the last bit (and stop bit) is sent, the **TI flag** is set to 1.

- **For Reception:** When a complete byte is received on **RxD pin (P3.0)**, it is automatically stored in the internal **Receive Buffer**. The **RI flag** is set to alert the CPU. The CPU then executes `MOV A, SBUF` to read the received byte.

**SCON (Serial Control) Register — Address 98H, Bit-Addressable:**

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
| :- | :- | :- |
| SM0 | D7 | Mode select bit 0 |
| SM1 | D6 | Mode select bit 1 |
| SM2 | D5 | Multiprocessor enable |
| REN | D4 | Receiver Enable |
| TB8 | D3 | 9th Tx bit |
| RB8 | D2 | 9th Rx bit |
| TI | D1 | Transmit Interrupt flag |
| RI | D0 | Receive Interrupt flag |

**Role of TI and RI Flags:**

- **TI (Transmit Interrupt Flag, D1 of SCON):**
  - Set to 1 by **hardware** at the end of the last data bit in Mode 0, or at the start of the stop bit in Modes 1, 2, 3.
  - **Must be cleared by software** before exiting the interrupt service routine or polling loop.
  - Signals that SBUF is ready to accept the next byte for transmission.

- **RI (Receive Interrupt Flag, D0 of SCON):**
  - Set to 1 by **hardware** at the end of the 8th received bit (Mode 0) or at the middle of the stop bit (Modes 1, 2, 3).
  - **Must be cleared by software**.
  - Signals that a complete byte is available in the receive buffer of SBUF.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students often write "TI and RI are cleared by hardware" — **this is WRONG**. They are set by hardware but **must be cleared by software**. Losing 1 mark on this is extremely common.

---

**A (b)** Explain the **PCON** register. Discuss the significance of the **SMOD** bit and derive the **baud rate equation** for Mode 1 of the 8051 serial port. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**PCON (Power Control) Register — Address 87H, NOT Bit-Addressable:**

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
| :- | :- | :- |
| D7 | **SMOD** | **Baud rate doubler** (1 = double the baud rate) |
| D6–D4 | — | Reserved |
| D3 | GF1 | General-purpose user flag 1 |
| D2 | GF0 | General-purpose user flag 0 |
| D1 | **PD** | Power-Down mode activation |
| D0 | **IDL** | Idle mode activation |

Since PCON is **not bit-addressable**, to set SMOD we use:

$$\text{PCON} = \text{PCON} \;\vert\; 0\text{x}80$$

**Significance of SMOD:**

The **SMOD bit** is the most important PCON bit for serial communication. When **SMOD = 1**, the baud rate generated by the serial port is **doubled**. This allows designers to:
- Use a lower Timer 1 overflow rate and still achieve a higher baud rate.
- Match standard baud rates that would otherwise be difficult to generate exactly with integer TH1 values.

**Baud Rate Derivation for Mode 1:**

In Mode 1, the baud rate is derived from **Timer 1's overflow rate** (assuming Timer 1 is in auto-reload Mode 2). The derivation steps are:

1. **Timer 1 overflow frequency:**
   $$f_{\text{overflow}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

2. **Serial port hardware divides this overflow rate by 32** (with SMOD=0) or by 16 (with SMOD=1), i.e., divides by $32 / 2^{\text{SMOD}}$.

3. **Final baud rate equation:**
   $$\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

4. Combining $32 \times 12 = 384$:
   $$\boxed{\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}}$$

5. **Inverted form for TH1 calculation:**
   $$\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Many students confuse the divisor in the baud rate formula. The "32" in the denominator comes from the serial port's hardware divider, and the "12" comes from the 8051's 12-clock machine cycle. **Do not swap them.**

---

### Question B: Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**B (a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **SBUF register architecture** in 8051. How does the same address serve both transmission and reception? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**SBUF Register Architecture:**

The SBUF register resides at SFR address **99H** in the 8051's Special Function Register map. However, it is **not a single register** — it is a **dual-buffer structure** with two physically separate 8-bit latches:

1. **Transmit Data Buffer (Write Latch):** Receives data from the CPU when `MOV SBUF, A` is executed. Once loaded, the byte is transferred to the **Transmit Shift Register**, which serially shifts out the bits on the **TxD pin (P3.1)**, LSB first.

2. **Receive Data Buffer (Read Latch):** Receives incoming serial bits from the **RxD pin (P3.0)** via the **Receive Shift Register**. Once 8 bits are assembled, the byte is held in the receive latch until the CPU reads it via `MOV A, SBUF`.

**How One Address Serves Both Purposes:**

The 8051's **internal steering logic** examines the type of CPU operation:

| CPU Action | Hardware Effect |
| :- | :- |
| **Write** to address 99H | Routes data to the **Transmit Latch** |
| **Read** from address 99H | Fetches data from the **Receive Latch** |

This transparent hardware mechanism means the programmer only needs to remember address **99H**, and the read/write nature of the instruction automatically selects the correct physical register.

**Engineering Advantages:**

- **Full-duplex operation:** Transmission and reception can occur simultaneously.
- **No address conflict:** Both buffers share address 99H without overwriting each other.
- **Baud-rate independence:** The transmit and receive sections can operate at the same baud rate as configured in SCON and PCON.

---

**B (b)** Explain the **SCON register** bit-by-bit. What is the function of the **REN bit**? Describe the significance of **TI and RI flags** and explain why they must be cleared by software. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**SCON Register (Address 98H, Bit-Addressable):**

| Bit | Symbol | Reset Value | Function |
| :- | :- | :- | :- |
| D7 | SM0 | 0 | Mode bit 0 |
| D6 | SM1 | 0 | Mode bit 1 |
| D5 | SM2 | 0 | Multiprocessor enable |
| D4 | REN | 0 | Receiver enable |
| D3 | TB8 | 0 | 9th Tx bit |
| D2 | RB8 | 0 | 9th Rx bit |
| D1 | TI | 0 | Transmit interrupt flag |
| D0 | RI | 0 | Receive interrupt flag |

**Function of REN Bit:**

**REN (Receiver Enable, D4)** is a software-controlled bit:
- When **REN = 1**, the 8051's serial receiver is **enabled**, and incoming data on RxD pin (P3.0) is accepted.
- When **REN = 0**, the receiver is **disabled**, and any data appearing on RxD is ignored.
- REN has **no effect on transmission**; it controls reception only.
- REN must be set to 1 by software **before** the start of reception.

**Significance of TI and RI Flags:**

Both TI (D1) and RI (D0) serve as **status indicators** for the serial port:

- **TI (Transmit Interrupt Flag):** Set by hardware when the last data bit has been transmitted. It signals that **SBUF is empty** and ready to accept the next byte. In polling-based transmission, the CPU loops on TI until it becomes 1.

- **RI (Receive Interrupt Flag):** Set by hardware when a complete byte has been received and is sitting in the receive buffer. The CPU reads SBUF and processes the data.

**Why TI and RI Must Be Cleared by Software:**

1. **They are NOT auto-cleared:** Unlike many other status flags, TI and RI remain set at 1 once activated by hardware.

2. **Polling-mode requirement:** If TI is not cleared, the next `JNB TI, $` loop will think a transmission just completed (even if it didn't) and reload SBUF prematurely.

3. **Interrupt-mode requirement:** If RI is not cleared at the end of the interrupt service routine (ISR), the 8051 will continuously re-enter the ISR, creating an **infinite interrupt loop** and hanging the system.

4. **Standard clearing code:**
   $$\text{Inside ISR or polling loop: } \text{CLR TI} \;\; \text{and} \;\; \text{CLR RI}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:** A common KTU answer omits the statement that "TI and RI are set by hardware and must be cleared by software." Examiners specifically look for this — failure to mention it loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Serial Communication Registers**

### 🔹 SBUF Register
- **Address:** 99H, 8-bit, NOT bit-addressable.
- **Dual nature:** Two physical latches share one address.
- **Write to SBUF** → Transmit buffer → TxD pin (P3.1).
- **Read from SBUF** → Receive buffer ← RxD pin (P3.0).
- **Full-duplex:** Tx and Rx can occur simultaneously.

### 🔹 SCON Register
- **Address:** 98H, 8-bit, **bit-addressable** (rare and important!).
- **Reset value:** 00H (Mode 0, no reception, no flags).
- **SM0, SM1 (D7, D6):** Mode selection (4 modes: 0, 1, 2, 3).
- **SM2 (D5):** Multiprocessor communication enable.
- **REN (D4):** Software-controlled receiver enable.
- **TB8 (D3):** 9th transmitted bit (Modes 2, 3).
- **RB8 (D2):** 9th received bit (Modes 2, 3).
- **TI (D1):** Hardware-set, software-cleared transmit flag.
- **RI (D0):** Hardware-set, software-cleared receive flag.

### 🔹 PCON Register
- **Address:** 87H, 8-bit, **NOT bit-addressable**.
- **SMOD (D7):** **Baud rate doubler** — most important for serial.
- **GF1, GF0 (D3, D2):** General-purpose user flags.
- **PD (D1):** Power-down mode (out of syllabus scope).
- **IDL (D0):** Idle mode (out of syllabus scope).

### 🔹 Key Formula (Modes 1 and 3)
$$\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

### 🔹 Key Formula (Mode 2)
$$\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{64}$$

### 🔹 Key Formula (Mode 0)
$$\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12}$$

### 🔹 Most Common Numerical Problem
- Given: $f_{\text{osc}}$, SMOD, target baud rate.
- Find: TH1 value (use inverted formula).
- **Standard crystal:** **11.0592 MHz** (gives error-free baud rates).

### 🔹 SCON Configuration Snippet
- **Mode 1 with reception enabled and TI/RI cleared:** `MOV SCON, #50H`
- **Mode 3 with reception enabled:** `MOV SCON, #0D0H`

### 🔹 PCON SMOD Snippet
- **Set SMOD = 1:** `ORL PCON, #80H`
- **Clear SMOD = 0:** `ANL PCON, #7FH`

### 🔹 Top 5 KTU Pitfalls to Avoid
1. ❌ Saying "TI/RI are cleared by hardware" — **they must be cleared by software**.
2. ❌ Forgetting to set REN = 1 before expecting reception.
3. ❌ Using baud rate formula for Mode 1 in Mode 2 numerical problems.
4. ❌ Confusing $f_{\text{osc}}$ with machine cycle frequency (divide by 12).
5. ❌ Forgetting to set SMOD when using PCON for baud rate doubling (PCON is not bit-addressable).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** For maximum marks, **always draw the SCON bit-layout table** in your answer sheet. Examiners reward visual clarity, and the bit-table is a high-yield, quick-to-write component that demonstrates mastery.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Timer Flag Interrupt in 8051** is a hardware-generated asynchronous event triggered automatically by the microcontroller's on-chip **Timer/Counter subsystem** when an internal 16-bit (or 8-bit) timer register rolls over from its maximum count (`FFFFH` for Mode 1 or `FFH` for Mode 2) back to `0000H`. This rollover sets the corresponding **Timer Flag bit (TF0 for Timer 0, TF1 for Timer 1)** in the **TCON (Timer/Counter Control) register**, which in turn requests an interrupt service routine (ISR) to the CPU provided the corresponding interrupt is enabled in the **IE (Interrupt Enable) register**.

In the 8051 architecture, the Timer Flag Interrupt is one of the **five interrupt sources** natively supported by the standard 8051 core. The two hardware timer flags are:

- **TF0 (Timer 0 Overflow Flag)** — Bit 5 of TCON (SFR address `88H`)
- **TF1 (Timer 1 Overflow Flag)** — Bit 7 of TCON (SFR address `88H`)

Each flag is set by hardware when its respective timer overflows, and is **automatically cleared by hardware** when the CPU vectors to the corresponding interrupt service routine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The 8051 supports a **fixed, non-vectored priority structure** for its interrupts. Timer 0 and Timer 1 interrupts share a high-priority level by default but can be re-prioritized using the **IP (Interrupt Priority) register**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "Kitchen Timer" Metaphor)

Imagine you are **boiling milk on a stove** while simultaneously doing your homework.

- **Without an Interrupt (Polling Method):** You would have to *repeatedly* stop your homework, walk to the kitchen, and check whether the milk has boiled. Most of these checks are wasteful — the milk is not yet ready. This is CPU time wasted on **busy-waiting (polling)** the `TF` flag.

- **With a Timer Flag Interrupt (Event-Driven Method):** You set a **kitchen timer** for the boiling duration and go back to your homework. The moment the timer *rings* (analogous to the `TF` flag going HIGH), it *interrupts* you, and you go attend to the milk (the **ISR** runs). After finishing, you return to the homework (the **main program resumes from where it was paused**).

This is precisely the elegance of the Timer Flag Interrupt — the CPU is **free to execute other tasks** (or enter idle mode) and is notified *only* when a precisely timed event has occurred, ensuring **deterministic real-time response** without wasting clock cycles.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **8051 Machine Cycle Frequency** = Oscillator Frequency ÷ **12**
- **One Timer Tick Duration** = **12 / f<sub>OSC</sub>** seconds
- **Maximum Count (Mode 1, 16-bit):** **65,536 counts** (`0000H` to `FFFFH`)
- **Maximum Count (Mode 2, 8-bit Auto-Reload):** **256 counts** (`00H` to `FFH`)
- **Default Interrupt Vector Addresses:** 
  - Timer 0 ISR → **`000BH`**
  - Timer 1 ISR → **`001BH`**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All timing calculations in the 8051 are based on the **machine cycle**, NOT the oscillator cycle. This is a frequent source of calculation errors in KTU exams. **Always divide the crystal frequency by 12 first.**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Timer Flag Interrupt Lifecycle

The Timer Flag Interrupt follows a strict, deterministic 5-stage hardware sequence:

1. **Timer Initialization (Software Phase)**
   - The programmer loads the **TMOD register** to select the timer mode (Mode 0, 1, 2, or 3) and operation (Timer vs. Counter).
   - The **initial count value** is loaded into the high byte (**THx**) and low byte (**TLx**) registers.
   - The **IE register** is configured to enable the specific timer interrupt (e.g., `ET0 = 1` for Timer 0).
   - The **EA (Enable All) bit** in IE must be set to `1` to globally permit interrupts.

2. **Timer Start (Software Phase)**
   - The **TRx (Timer Run) bit** in the TCON register is set to `1` via software (e.g., `TR0 = 1;`).
   - From this moment, the timer begins incrementing on every machine cycle.

3. **Overflow Event (Hardware Phase)**
   - The 16-bit (or 8-bit) timer rolls over from its maximum value to zero.
   - **Hardware automatically sets the TFx flag** in the TCON register.
   - Hardware simultaneously raises an **internal interrupt request** to the interrupt controller.

4. **Interrupt Acceptance (Hardware Phase)**
   - If no higher-priority interrupt is in service and the corresponding IEx bit is set, the CPU completes the current instruction.
   - The CPU **automatically saves the PC (Program Counter)** onto the stack.
   - The PC is loaded with the **predefined vector address** (`000BH` for TF0, `001BH` for TF1).
   - **Hardware clears the TFx flag** automatically.

5. **ISR Execution & Return (Software Phase)**
   - The user's ISR executes (e.g., toggling a pin, reloading timer, etc.).
   - A **`RETI`** instruction at the end of the ISR pops the saved PC from the stack, restoring execution to the main program.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `RETI` and not `RET`?** The `RETI` instruction additionally signals the interrupt controller that the ISR is complete, allowing further interrupts of the same or lower priority to be accepted. Using `RET` will cause subsequent interrupts to be ignored — a classic KTU exam pitfall.

---

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why use a Timer Flag Interrupt instead of software delays?** Software delay loops tie up the CPU completely, blocking all other operations. A timer interrupt allows the CPU to do useful work between timer overflows — this is the foundation of any **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** or multi-tasking embedded system.
- **Why is the TF flag cleared by hardware, but TR is cleared by software?** This asymmetry is by design — TF is a *transient event indicator* (one-shot), whereas TR is a *persistent state control* (must remain ON as long as the timer is to run).

---

### 2.3 Key Registers Involved (SFR Map for KTU Exams)

| SFR Name | Address | Function in Timer Flag Interrupt |
|---|---|---|
| **TMOD** | `89H` | Selects timer mode (Mode 1 / Mode 2 most common) and timer/counter operation |
| **TCON** | `88H` | Contains `TR0`, `TR1` (run control) and `TF0`, `TF1` (overflow flags) |
| **IE** | `A8H` | Interrupt Enable register: `EA`, `ET0`, `ET1` bits |
| **IP** | `B8H` | Interrupt Priority register: `PT0`, `PT1` bits |
| **TH0 / TL0** | `8CH` / `8AH` | Timer 0 high/low byte registers |
| **TH1 / TL1** | `8DH` / `8BH` | Timer 1 high/low byte registers |

> **TCON Register Bit Layout (for quick reference):**
>
> | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 |
> | T1 Flag | T1 Run | T0 Flag | T0 Run | Ext Int | | | |

> **IE Register Bit Layout:**
>
> | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | EA | — | — | ES | ET1 | EX1 | ET0 | EX0 |
> | Global | | | Serial | **Timer 1** | Ext 1 | **Timer 0** | Ext 0 |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every KTU university exam question on timers.**

| Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Machine Cycle Period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | Seconds per tick |
| Maximum Counts (Mode 1) | $N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | 16-bit timer |
| Maximum Counts (Mode 2) | $N_{max} = 2^{8} = 256$ | 8-bit auto-reload |
| Timer Delay (Mode 1) | $T_{delay} = (65{,}536 - TH:TL_{initial}) \times T_{MC}$ | Seconds |
| Timer Delay (Mode 2) | $T_{delay} = (256 - TH_{reload}) \times T_{MC}$ | Seconds |
| Initial Value (Mode 1) | $TH:TL_{initial} = 65{,}536 - \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}}$ | Decimal value |
| Initial Value (Mode 2) | $TH_{reload} = 256 - \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}}$ | Decimal value |
| Hex Conversion | Divide decimal value by 16; quotient and remainder map to hex digits | — |
| Timer Frequency | $f_{timer} = \dfrac{1}{T_{delay}}$ | Hz (events per second) |
| Interrupt Vector Timer 0 | `000BH` | Code memory address |
| Interrupt Vector Timer 1 | `001BH` | Code memory address |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to "find the value to be loaded into TH and TL," students often forget that the *initial value must be converted to HEXADECIMAL* before splitting into TH (high byte) and TL (low byte). This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** if skipped.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Timer Flag Interrupts are the **backbone of all real-time embedded systems**:

- **Automotive ECUs:** Sampling crank-shaft position every 1 ms via Timer 0 overflow.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Generating precise PWM waveforms for motor speed control using Mode 2 auto-reload.
- **Medical Devices:** Scheduling periodic sensor reads in patient monitors.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Generating accurate 1 ms system tick in RTOS kernels (e.g., FreeRTOS port for 8051).
- **Communication Protocols:** Implementing precise baud-rate generation for the 8051's built-in UART (Timer 1 is conventionally used for this).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Derivation: Timer Delay Equation

**Goal:** Derive the general formula for the time delay generated by an 8051 timer in **Mode 1 (16-bit)**.

**Step 1 — Define the Machine Cycle**

The 8051 divides the external oscillator frequency by 12 to produce the internal machine cycle clock. This is because each 8051 instruction takes a multiple of one machine cycle (typically 1 to 4 machine cycles).

$$
f_{MC} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12} \quad \text{(Hz)}
$$

The time period of one machine cycle tick is therefore:

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{1}{f_{MC}} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} \quad \text{(seconds)}
$$

**Step 2 — Count the Number of Ticks Before Overflow**

In Mode 1, the timer uses a 16-bit register pair (THx:TLx). It counts up from the loaded initial value until it reaches the maximum 16-bit value `FFFFH` and then **rolls over** to `0000H`, setting the TF flag.

The number of increment operations (ticks) required to roll over is:

$$
N_{ticks} = (FFFFH - TH:TL_{initial}) + 1 = 2^{16} - TH:TL_{initial} = 65{,}536 - TH:TL_{initial}
$$

The `+ 1` is critical — it accounts for the fact that the count `0000H` is also a valid state, requiring one more increment to reach it.

**Step 3 — Multiply Ticks by Tick Duration**

The total time delay between enabling the timer and the overflow event is:

$$
T_{delay} = N_{ticks} \times T_{MC} = (65{,}536 - TH:TL_{initial}) \times \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Required Initial Value (Inverse)**

Rearranging for `TH:TL_initial` (the value the programmer must load):

$$
TH:TL_{initial} = 65{,}536 - \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} = 65{,}536 - \frac{T_{delay} \times f_{OSC}}{12}
$$

**Step 5 — Mode 2 Specialization (8-bit Auto-Reload)**

In Mode 2, TLx is the counter (8-bit) and THx holds the auto-reload value. The same derivation applies with $2^{16} \to 2^{8}$:

$$
T_{delay} = (256 - TH_{reload}) \times \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}
$$

$$
TH_{reload} = 256 - \frac{T_{delay} \times f_{OSC}}{12}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the asymmetry between Mode 1 and Mode 2?** Mode 1 offers a **wide delay range** but requires **software reload** every overflow (16-bit value, two SFR writes). Mode 2 offers a **short delay range (max 256 ticks)** but achieves **hardware auto-reload** — perfect for high-frequency periodic events like baud-rate generation.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Classic KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Configure **Timer 0 in Mode 1** to generate a delay of exactly **50 ms**. Calculate the value to be loaded into `TH0` and `TL0`.

**Step 1 — Calculate the Machine Cycle Period**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\,\mu s
$$

> The choice of 11.0592 MHz is intentional — it is **divisible by standard baud-rate factors (300, 1200, 9600, 19200)** with zero remainder, eliminating UART timing error.

**Step 2 — Calculate the Number of Ticks Required**

$$
N_{ticks} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46{,}080 \text{ ticks}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Initial Decimal Value**

$$
TH:TL_{initial} = 65{,}536 - 46{,}080 = 19{,}456
$$

**Step 4 — Convert Decimal to Hexadecimal**

Performing successive division by 16:

$$
19{,}456 \div 16 = 1{,}216 \quad \text{remainder } 0
$$
$$
1{,}216 \div 16 = 76 \quad \text{remainder } 0
$$
$$
76 \div 16 = 4 \quad \text{remainder } 12 \rightarrow C
$$
$$
4 \div 16 = 0 \quad \text{remainder } 4
$$

Reading remainders bottom-up: $19{,}456_{10} = 4C00_{H}$

**Step 5 — Split into High and Low Bytes**

$$
TH0 = 4C_{H} \quad \text{(high byte)}
$$
$$
TL0 = 00_{H} \quad \text{(low byte)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** $65{,}536 - 19{,}456 = 46{,}080$ ticks × $1.085\,\mu s$ = $50.0\,ms$ ✓

---

### 3.3 Complete Interrupt Setup Procedure (C and Assembly)

**C Language (Keil µVision syntax — KTU standard):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;       // LED connected to P1.0

void timer0_ISR(void) interrupt 1   // Vector address 000BH
{
    TH0 = 0x4C;        // Reload high byte
    TL0 = 0x00;        // Reload low byte
    LED = ~LED;        // Toggle LED
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x01;       // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), Timer operation
    TH0  = 0x4C;
    TL0  = 0x00;
    ET0  = 1;          // Enable Timer 0 interrupt
    EA   = 1;          // Enable global interrupt
    TR0  = 1;          // Start Timer 0
    while(1);          // Main loop does nothing — CPU is free
}
```

**Assembly Language (8051 syntax):**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN         ; Jump over ISR vector area

        ORG 000BH         ; Timer 0 interrupt vector
TO_ISR: MOV TH0, #4CH     ; Reload high byte
        MOV TL0, #00H     ; Reload low byte
        CPL P1.0          ; Toggle P1.0
        RETI              ; Return from interrupt

        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #01H    ; Timer 0, Mode 1
        MOV TH0, #4CH
        MOV TL0, #00H
        SETB ET0          ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB EA           ; Enable global interrupt
        SETB TR0          ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   SJMP HERE         ; Idle loop
        END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to reload `TH0` and `TL0` *inside* the ISR for Mode 1 operation. Without reload, the timer will count from `0000H` (its overflow value) on the next cycle, producing a much longer delay (~71 ms instead of 50 ms with an 11.0592 MHz crystal).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Timer Flag Interrupt — Hardware-Software Interaction Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU["CPU CORE"]
        PC["Program Counter PC"]
        SP["Stack Pointer SP"]
        ALU["ALU and Control Unit"]
    end

    subgraph TMOD_REG["TMOD Register 89H"]
        T0M1["T0M1 Bit 1"]
        T0M0["T0M0 Bit 0"]
    end

    subgraph T0["TIMER 0 HARDWARE"]
        TH0REG["TH0 Register 8CH"]
        TL0REG["TL0 Register 8AH"]
        INC["Increment Logic per Machine Cycle"]
        OVFL["Overflow Detector"]
    end

    subgraph TCON_REG["TCON Register 88H"]
        TF0FL["TF0 Flag Bit 5"]
        TR0BT["TR0 Run Bit 4"]
    end

    subgraph IE_REG["IE Register A8H"]
        EABT["EA Global Bit 7"]
        ET0BT["ET0 Timer 0 Bit 1"]
    end

    subgraph INT_CTRL["INTERRUPT CONTROLLER"]
        PRIO["Priority Resolver"]
        VECT["Vector Generator 000BH"]
    end

    subgraph ISR["INTERRUPT SERVICE ROUTINE"]
        ISRCOD["User ISR Code at 000BH"]
        RELOAD["Reload TH0 and TL0"]
        ACTION["Perform Timed Action"]
        RETI["RETI Instruction"]
    end

    subgraph MAIN["MAIN PROGRAM"]
        MAINLOOP["Main While Loop"]
    end

    TMOD_REG --> T0
    TCON_REG --> TR0BT
    TR0BT -->|"Enable Counting"| INC
    INC --> TH0REG
    INC --> TL0REG
    TH0REG --> OVFL
    TL0REG --> OVFL
    OVFL -->|"Roll over to 0000H"| TF0FL
    TF0FL --> INT_CTRL
    ET0BT --> INT_CTRL
    EABT --> INT_CTRL
    PRIO --> VECT
    VECT -->|"Push PC to stack"| SP
    VECT -->|"Load 000BH to PC"| PC
    PC --> ISR
    ISR --> ISRCOD
    ISRCOD --> RELOAD
    RELOAD --> ACTION
    ACTION --> RETI
    RETI -->|"Pop PC from stack"| PC
    PC --> MAINLOOP
    MAINLOOP -->|"Concurrent execution"| CPU
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **software path** (left side): TMOD configures the timer mode, TCON's TR0 starts the counting.
- The **hardware path** (centre): The increment logic ticks every machine cycle; the overflow detector sets TF0.
- The **interrupt path** (right side): TF0 + ET0 + EA together trigger the vector generator, which saves the PC and jumps to `000BH`.
- The **return path** (bottom): `RETI` restores the PC, and the main loop resumes seamlessly.
