### 4.2 Timer Mode Selection Decision Tree (TMOD Configuration Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Need to configure Timer 0"]
    Q1{"Desired delay range?"}
    M1["Mode 1: 16 bit, range 1 to 65536 ticks"]
    M2["Mode 2: 8 bit auto reload, range 1 to 256 ticks"]

    Q3{"Need frequent periodic interrupts?"}
    USE2["Use Mode 2: no reload code needed in ISR"]
    USE1["Use Mode 1: must reload TH0:TL0 in ISR"]
    END["Configure IE register: ET0 = 1, EA = 1"]
    TRSTART["Set TR0 = 1 to start timer"]

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|"Wide range > 256 ticks"| M1
    Q1 -->|"Short, frequent, periodic"| M2
    M1 --> USE1
    M2 --> USE2
    USE1 --> Q2
    USE2 --> Q2
    Q2 --> CALC
    CALC --> Q3
    Q3 --> END
    END --> TRSTART
```

### 4.3 8051 Interrupt Vector Table (Block Memory Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CODE_MEM["8051 CODE MEMORY MAP"]
        RST["0000H: RESET Vector"]
        EXT0["0003H: External Interrupt 0"]
        T0["000BH: TIMER 0 OVERFLOW TF0"]
        EXT1["0013H: External Interrupt 1"]
        T1["001BH: TIMER 1 OVERFLOW TF1"]
        SINT["0023H: Serial Port Interrupt"]
    end

    TF0_HL["TF0 Flag Set by Hardware"]
    CPU_FETCH["CPU Fetches ISR from 000BH"]
    ISR_RUN["User Code Executes"]
    TF0_CLR["TF0 Auto Cleared by Hardware"]

    TF0_HL -->|"Vector Address Generated"| CPU_FETCH
    CPU_FETCH --> T0
    T0 --> ISR_RUN
    ISR_RUN --> TF0_CLR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Spacing Note:** The 8051 leaves **8 bytes** between consecutive vector addresses (e.g., `000BH` to `0013H`). If your ISR exceeds 8 bytes (very common), it will overlap the next vector. **Always use `LJMP` or `AJMP` to redirect the ISR to a free memory region** — a key KTU exam point.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**What is a Timer Flag Interrupt in the 8051 microcontroller? Name the SFRs and flag bits involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Timer Flag Interrupt is a hardware-triggered interrupt generated when an internal 16-bit (or 8-bit) timer/counter register **overflows from its maximum count value** to `0000H`, automatically setting the corresponding **Timer Flag bit (TF0 for Timer 0 in TCON.5, or TF1 for Timer 1 in TCON.7)** in the **TCON register** (SFR address `88H`).

The key SFRs involved are:

1. **TMOD (`89H`)** — selects the timer mode and operation type.
2. **TCON (`88H`)** — contains the `TF0`/`TF1` overflow flags and `TR0`/`TR1` run control bits.
3. **IE (`A8H`)** — Interrupt Enable register with `ET0`/`ET1` and global `EA` bits.

When the flag is set and the corresponding interrupt is enabled, the CPU vectors to the predefined ISR address: **`000BH` for Timer 0** and **`001BH` for Timer 1**, automatically clearing the flag.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Differentiate between software delay loops and Timer Flag Interrupts for generating time delays in the 8051.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Software Delay Loop | Timer Flag Interrupt |
|---|---|---|
| CPU Utilization | **Tied up 100%** in the delay loop (busy-wait) | **CPU is free** to execute other tasks |
| Accuracy | Dependent on instruction cycle count | **Highly accurate** (hardware-driven) |
| Power Efficiency | Poor — CPU always active | Excellent — CPU can idle between events |
| Implementation | Simple nested loops in C/ASM | Requires SFR configuration and ISR |
| Use Case | Short, non-critical delays | **Real-time, periodic, multitasking** systems |

The Timer Flag Interrupt is therefore the **preferred mechanism** in real-time embedded systems where deterministic response and CPU efficiency are critical.

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**(a)** With a neat **block diagram**, explain the architecture of the 8051 Timer/Counter subsystem and the role of the **TMOD** and **TCON** registers. List all four timer modes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses a **12 MHz crystal**. **Timer 0** is configured in **Mode 1** to generate a delay of **exactly 25 ms**. Calculate the value to be loaded into **TH0 and TL0**, and write a complete **C program** to toggle **P1.0** every 25 ms using the Timer 0 interrupt. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

The 8051 contains **two 16-bit Timer/Counters (Timer 0 and Timer 1)**, each composed of two 8-bit registers (THx high byte, TLx low byte).

**Architecture Components:**

- **Oscillator Divider:** Divides the external crystal frequency by 12 to produce the machine cycle clock.
- **Increment Logic:** Increments the TLx register on every machine cycle (in Timer mode) or on every external pulse (in Counter mode, via pin T0/P3.4 or T1/P3.5).
- **Overflow Detector:** Compares the 16-bit count to `FFFFH`. On rollover, sets the TFx flag.
- **TMOD Register (`89H`):** Configures the mode of operation. Bit assignments:
  - **Lower nibble (T0):** T0_GATE, T0_CT, T0_M1, T0_M0
  - **Upper nibble (T1):** T1_GATE, T1_CT, T1_M1, T1_M0
- **TCON Register (`88H`):** Contains run control (`TR0`/`TR1`) and overflow flags (`TF0`/`TF1`), plus external interrupt control bits.

**Four Timer Modes:**

| Mode | M1 | M0 | Description | Max Counts |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode 0** | 0 | 0 | 13-bit timer (legacy 8048 mode) | 8,192 |
| **Mode 1** | 0 | 1 | 16-bit timer | 65,536 |
| **Mode 2** | 1 | 0 | 8-bit auto-reload | 256 |
| **Mode 3** | 1 | 1 | Split timer (Timer 0 only) | Variable |

**Block Diagram (textual representation for 7-mark answer):**

```
+----------+      +-----------+      +-----------+      +--------+
| f_OSC    | ---> | Divide by | ---> | Increment | ---> |  TLx   |
| Crystal  |      |    12     |      |  Logic    |      | 8-bit  |
+----------+      +-----------+      +-----------+      +---+----+
                                                                 |
                                                          Carry Propagates
                                                                 v
                                                            +---+----+
                                                            |  THx   |
                                                            | 8-bit  |
                                                            +---+----+
                                                                 |
                                                          Overflow at FFFFH
                                                                 v
                                                            +---+----+
                                                            |  TFx   |
                                                            | (Set)  |
                                                            +---+----+
                                                                 |
                                                                 v
                                                          Interrupt Request
                                                            to CPU
```

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute Machine Cycle Period:**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1.0\,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Required Ticks:**

$$
N_{ticks} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 25{,}000 \text{ ticks}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Initial Decimal Value:**

$$
TH:TL_{initial} = 65{,}536 - 25{,}000 = 40{,}536
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hexadecimal:**

$$
40{,}536 \div 16 = 2{,}533 \text{ rem } 8
$$
$$
2{,}533 \div 16 = 158 \text{ rem } 5
$$
$$
158 \div 16 = 9 \text{ rem } 14 \rightarrow E
$$
$$
9 \div 16 = 0 \text{ rem } 9
$$

Therefore: $40{,}536_{10} = 9E58_{H}$

**Step 5 — Split into TH0 and TL0:**

$$
TH0 = 9E_{H} \quad , \quad TL0 = 58_{H}
$$

**Step 6 — Complete C Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;

void timer0_ISR(void) interrupt 1   // Vector 000BH for Timer 0
{
    TH0 = 0x9E;        // Reload high byte
    TL0 = 0x58;        // Reload low byte
    LED = ~LED;        // Toggle P1.0
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x01;       // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), software-controlled
    TH0  = 0x9E;       // Load initial high byte
    TL0  = 0x58;       // Load initial low byte
    ET0  = 1;          // Enable Timer 0 interrupt
    EA   = 1;          // Enable global interrupts
    TR0  = 1;          // Start Timer 0
    while(1)           // Main loop idle — CPU is free
    {
        // CPU can perform other tasks here
    }
}
```

---

#### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Explain the **Interrupt Enable (IE) register** of the 8051 in detail. Describe the step-by-step procedure to enable the **Timer 0 Flag Interrupt**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 with a **11.0592 MHz crystal** must generate a **square wave of 1 kHz frequency** on pin **P1.7** using **Timer 1 in Mode 2 (auto-reload)**. Calculate the value to be loaded into **TH1** and write the complete **8051 Assembly Language program**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**IE Register (SFR Address `A8H`) — Bit-by-Bit Description:**

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **7** | **EA** | **Enable All** — Global master switch for all interrupts. Must be `1` to enable any interrupt. |
| 6 | — | Reserved (undefined) |
| 5 | — | Reserved (undefined) |
| 4 | **ES** | Enable Serial Port interrupt |
| 3 | **ET1** | Enable Timer 1 overflow interrupt |
| 2 | **EX1** | Enable External Interrupt 1 |
| 1 | **ET0** | **Enable Timer 0 overflow interrupt** |
| 0 | **EX0** | Enable External Interrupt 0 |

**Step-by-Step Procedure to Enable Timer 0 Flag Interrupt:**

1. **Set the mode in TMOD** to configure Timer 0 in the desired mode (e.g., `TMOD = 0x01;` for Mode 1).
2. **Load initial value** into TH0 and TL0 for the required delay.
3. **Set the ET0 bit** in the IE register: `ET0 = 1;` (or `IE = 0x82;` to set EA + ET0).
4. **Set the global EA bit** in the IE register: `EA = 1;`.
5. **Start the timer** by setting TR0: `TR0 = 1;`.
6. **Write the ISR** at vector address `000BH` and end it with `RETI`.
7. **Enable interrupts globally** with the C keyword `interrupt 1` if using C, or place the ISR address at `000BH` in assembly.

**In Assembly:**

```assembly
        SETB ET0       ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB EA        ; Enable all interrupts
        SETB TR0       ; Start Timer 0
```

**In C:**

```c
IE = 0x82;   // EA = 1, ET0 = 1, all others = 0
```

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Timer Period from Frequency:**

For a 1 kHz square wave, the period is:

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1.0\,ms = 1000\,\mu s
$$

Since one full period of the square wave requires **two timer overflows** (one to set HIGH, one to set LOW), each half-period delay must be:

$$
T_{half} = \frac{T}{2} = 500\,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Machine Cycle Period:**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\,\mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Number of Ticks Required:**

$$
N_{ticks} = \frac{T_{half}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{500}{1.085} = 460.8 \approx 461 \text{ ticks}
$$

> **Note:** The non-integer result is rounded **down** to 461 because we cannot have fractional ticks. The actual frequency will be **slightly off** from 1 kHz — this is a real-world constraint.

**Step 4 — Calculate TH1 Reload Value (Mode 2):**

$$
TH_{reload} = 256 - 461 = -205
$$

A negative result indicates that **the desired delay is not achievable in Mode 2** with this crystal. We must either:
- (a) Use Mode 1 (16-bit), or
- (b) Divide the 1 kHz generation differently.

**Revised Approach (using prescaling in software — acceptable in KTU exams):**

Let the timer overflow every $T_{overflow} = 256 \times 1.085 = 277.76\,\mu s$. To achieve 500 µs, we need approximately $\lceil 500 / 277.76 \rceil = 2$ overflows per half-period.

So: $TH1 = 00H$ (timer counts full 256 ticks), and we maintain a software counter that triggers P1.7 toggle on every 2nd overflow.

**Alternative: Use Mode 1 for 1 ms half-period directly:**

$$
N_{ticks} = \frac{500}{1.085} \approx 461 \text{ (still problematic)}
$$

**Most practical KTU answer:** Use **Mode 1** with $T_{delay} = 500\,\mu s$ approximately:

$$
TH:TL_{initial} = 65{,}536 - 461 = 65{,}075 = FE35_{H}
$$

So $TH1 = FEH$ and $TL1 = 35H$.

**Step 5 — Complete Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN

        ORG 001BH        ; Timer 1 interrupt vector
T1_ISR: MOV TH1, #0FEH  ; Reload high byte
        MOV TL1, #035H  ; Reload low byte
        CPL P1.7        ; Toggle P1.7 (complements each interrupt)
        RETI

        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #10H  ; Timer 1, Mode 1 (16-bit)
        MOV TH1, #0FEH
        MOV TL1, #035H
        SETB ET1        ; Enable Timer 1 interrupt
        SETB EA         ; Enable global interrupt
        SETB TR1        ; Start Timer 1
HERE:   SJMP HERE       ; Idle loop
        END
```

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Timer Flag Interrupt Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to HEX** before splitting TH and TL. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Using `RET` instead of `RETI`** at the end of the ISR — disables future interrupts. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Not setting the `EA` (global enable) bit** — interrupt will never fire. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Forgetting to reload TH/TL in Mode 1 ISR** — timer counts from `0000H` next time, delay doubles. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 5. **Confusing Timer and Counter modes** in TMOD (Timer = `C/T̄ = 0`, Counter = `C/T̄ = 1`). **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Wrong vector address** — TF0 is `000BH`, TF1 is `001BH`. Mixing them up is a critical error. **(−2 marks)**
> 7. **Forgetting that Timer 0 Mode 3 disables Timer 1** — a subtle but commonly tested point. **(−1 mark)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Memorize These Points:**

- ✅ The 8051 has **2 hardware timers** (Timer 0, Timer 1) → **2 timer flag interrupts** (TF0, TF1).
- ✅ Timer Flag bits are in **TCON** (SFR `88H`): **TF0 at bit 5, TF1 at bit 7**.
- ✅ TF flags are **set by hardware on overflow, cleared by hardware on ISR entry**.
- ✅ **Vector addresses:** TF0 → **`000BH`**, TF1 → **`001BH`** (both leave 8 bytes for ISR).
- ✅ **Mode 1** is 16-bit (range 1–65,536 ticks) — requires **software reload** in ISR.
- ✅ **Mode 2** is 8-bit auto-reload (range 1–256 ticks) — no reload code needed.
- ✅ **Master formula (Mode 1):** $T_{delay} = (65{,}536 - TH:TL_{initial}) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$
- ✅ **Master formula (Mode 2):** $T_{delay} = (256 - TH_{reload}) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$
- ✅ Always compute **machine cycle = 12 / f<sub>OSC</sub>** first, NOT based on raw oscillator.
- ✅ Interrupt enable sequence: **TMOD config → TH/TL load → `ETx = 1` → `EA = 1` → `TRx = 1`**.
- ✅ ISR **must end with `RETI`** (not `RET`) in assembly; in C, use the `interrupt` keyword with correct vector number (TF0 = 1, TF1 = 3).
- ✅ The 11.0592 MHz crystal is preferred for **UART + Timer** combinations due to clean baud-rate division.
- ✅ Default priority: **External 0 > Timer 0 > External 1 > Timer 1 > Serial**; can be re-prioritized via the **IP register**.
- ✅ For Mode 3, Timer 0 splits into **two independent 8-bit timers**, and **Timer 1 is forced into a fixed count mode** (no interrupt capability).
- ✅ The Timer Flag Interrupt is the **most efficient way** to implement periodic, accurate, CPU-free delays in real-time 8051 systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Serial Port Interrupt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Serial Port Interrupt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Serial Port Interrupt** in the **8051 Microcontroller** is a hardware-triggered interrupt mechanism generated by the on-chip **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver Transmitter)**. This interrupt is the **logical OR** of two distinct hardware events, flagged by the bits **TI (Transmit Interrupt)** and **RI (Receive Interrupt)** located in the **SCON (Serial Control) Register**. Both events share a single, common interrupt vector located at program memory address **0023H**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, mastery of the serial port interrupt requires understanding the four standard operating modes (Modes 0, 1, 2, 3), the configuration of the **Timer 1 overflow** as the baud rate generator, and the architectural handling of the shared ISR (Interrupt Service Routine).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The serial port interrupt is unique because *two distinct hardware flags* (TI and RI) map to a *single interrupt vector*. The programmer MUST check which flag triggered the interrupt inside the ISR — a critical board examination point.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Restaurant Bell" Model)

Imagine you are a chef working in a busy restaurant kitchen:

- **Polling (Without Interrupt):** You must keep walking to the counter to ask, *"Is there a new order? Is the customer ready for the food?"* This wastes your cooking time. This is the **polling method**.
- **Serial Port Interrupt (With Interrupt):** A small bell (the interrupt) is placed on your workstation. The waiter rings the bell (hardware sets TI/RI) **only when** an event actually happens. You stop cooking momentarily, handle the specific task (read SBUF or load SBUF), and return to cooking exactly where you left off. This is the **interrupt-driven method**.

The bell here is the **hardware flag (TI/RI)**, the kitchen is the **main program**, and the action of walking to the counter is the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** at address **0023H**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Frequency:** For serial communication, KTU labs and industry use the crystal frequency **F_osc = 11.0592 MHz** (not 12 MHz) because it yields exact integer values for standard baud rates like **9600, 4800, 2400, 1200** bps, eliminating timing drift.

## 1.3 Why Serial Port Interrupt is Critical in Embedded Systems

In real-world embedded applications, the CPU cannot waste clock cycles polling a slow peripheral like a UART. Devices like **GPS modules, GSM modems, Bluetooth HC-05, and RFID readers** transmit data asynchronously. An interrupt-driven architecture guarantees:

1. **Zero CPU wastage** — CPU executes other tasks until data arrives.
2. **No data loss** — hardware flags ensure no incoming byte is missed.
3. **Deterministic response** — bounded latency for incoming events.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SCON Register (Serial Control) — Address: 98H

The **SCON register** is the master control block for the 8051's serial port. It is a **bit-addressable** SFR, meaning individual bits (e.g., `SCON.0` = RI) can be accessed directly.

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | **SM0** | Serial Port Mode bit 0 (with SM1, selects Mode 0/1/2/3) |
| 6 | **SM1** | Serial Port Mode bit 1 |
| 5 | **SM2** | Multiprocessor Communication Enable (Mode 2/3) |
| 4 | **REN** | Receive Enable (Must be 1 to receive data) |
| 3 | **TB8** | 9th transmitted bit (Modes 2, 3) |
| 2 | **RB8** | 9th received bit (Modes 2, 3) |
| 1 | **TI** | Transmit Interrupt flag (Set by hardware, must be cleared by software) |
| 0 | **RI** | Receive Interrupt flag (Set by hardware, must be cleared by software) |

### Serial Port Modes (SM0, SM1)

| Mode | SM0 | SM1 | Description | Baud Rate Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0 | 0 | 8-bit Shift Register | Fixed: F_osc / 12 |
| **1** | 0 | 1 | 8-bit UART | Variable: Timer 1 Overflow |
| **2** | 1 | 0 | 9-bit UART | Fixed: F_osc / 32 or / 64 |
| **3** | 1 | 1 | 9-bit UART | Variable: Timer 1 Overflow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mode 1 is the most commonly used mode** in KTU lab examinations and real-world applications like UART-to-PC communication (HyperTerminal / Tera Term). It transmits 10 bits per frame: 1 Start bit, 8 Data bits, 1 Stop bit.

## 2.2 The SBUF Register (Serial Buffer) — Address: 99H

Physically, there are **two separate registers** mapped at the same address 99H:
- **Writing** to SBUF loads the **Transmit register** (initiates transmission).
- **Reading** from SBUF reads from the **Receive register** (retrieves received data).

This dual-mapping allows full-duplex communication (simultaneous send/receive) over a single address.

## 2.3 Interrupt Control Registers

### IE Register (Interrupt Enable) — Address: A8H

| Bit | Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | **EA** | Global Interrupt Enable (Master switch — must be 1) |
| 6 | — | Reserved |
| 5 | — | Reserved |
| 4 | **ES** | Enable Serial Port Interrupt |
| 3 | **ET1** | Enable Timer 1 Interrupt |
| 2 | **EX1** | Enable External Interrupt 1 |
| 1 | **ET0** | Enable Timer 0 Interrupt |
| 0 | **EX0** | Enable External Interrupt 0 |

### IP Register (Interrupt Priority) — Address: B8H

| Bit | Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | **PS** | Serial Port Priority (1 = High, 0 = Low) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these formulas — they appear in **almost every KTU exam paper** involving serial port programming.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Baud Rate (Mode 1)** | $Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{F_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | bps (bits per second) |
| 2 | **Baud Rate (Mode 2)** | $Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{64} \times F_{osc}$ | bps |
| 3 | **TH1 Reload Value** | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{F_{osc}}{384 \times Baud}$ | When **SMOD = 0** |
| 4 | **TH1 Reload Value (Double)** | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{F_{osc}}{192 \times Baud}$ | When **SMOD = 1** |
| 5 | **Timer 1 Overflow Rate** | $f_{overflow} = \dfrac{F_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | Hz |
| 6 | **Frame Size (Mode 1)** | 1 Start + 8 Data + 1 Stop = 10 bits | bits/frame |
| 7 | **Time per Bit** | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{Baud\ Rate}$ | seconds |
| 8 | **Interrupt Vector Address** | **0023H** | Program memory |

## 2.5 PCON Register (Power Control) — Address: 87H

The **SMOD bit** (bit 7 of PCON) controls baud rate doubling in Modes 1, 2, 3. Setting SMOD = 1 doubles the baud rate. This is **not bit-addressable**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 8051's serial port interrupt is foundational for:
- **Industrial SCADA systems** for PLC-to-PC data logging.
- **Automotive ECUs** communicating via LIN bus (built upon UART).
- **IoT gateways** using 8051 to bridge sensors to Wi-Fi/BLE modules.
- **Medical devices** transmitting vital signs to monitoring stations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: TH1 Reload Value for Standard Baud Rates

### Problem Setup
Calculate the value to be loaded into **TH1** (with Timer 1 in Mode 2 — 8-bit auto-reload) to generate a baud rate of **9600 bps** using Mode 1 of the 8051 serial port.

**Given:**
- $F_{osc} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz} = 11{,}059{,}200\ \text{Hz}$
- $Baud = 9600\ \text{bps}$
- $SMOD = 0$ (standard, no doubling)

### Step 1: Start with the Mode 1 Baud Rate Formula

$$
Baud = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{F_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute SMOD = 0

When $SMOD = 0$, we have $2^0 = 1$. The formula simplifies to:

$$
Baud = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{F_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

### Step 3: Cross-Multiply to Isolate TH1

$$
32 \times Baud \times 12 \times (256 - TH1) = F_{osc}
$$

$$
384 \times Baud \times (256 - TH1) = F_{osc}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for (256 - TH1)

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{F_{osc}}{384 \times Baud}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute Numerical Values

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 9600}
$$

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400}
$$

$$
256 - TH1 = 3
$$

### Step 6: Solve for TH1

$$
TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253
$$

### Step 7: Convert to Hexadecimal

$$
253_{10} = \text{FD}_{H}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{TH1 = \text{FD}_{H} = 253_{10}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This gives an **exact integer** (3) for the timer count — this is precisely why the 11.0592 MHz crystal is preferred over 12 MHz in real 8051 systems. With a 12 MHz crystal, you would get a non-integer (2.93...), causing **baud rate error** and corrupted data.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Multiple Standard Baud Rates (F_osc = 11.0592 MHz)

| Baud Rate (bps) | SMOD | $384 \times Baud$ | $256 - TH1$ | TH1 (Hex) | TH1 (Dec) | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1200 | 0 | 460,800 | 24 | F8 | 248 | 0 |
| 2400 | 0 | 921,600 | 12 | F4 | 244 | 0 |
| 4800 | 0 | 1,843,200 | 6 | FA | 250 | 0 |
| **9600** | **0** | **3,686,400** | **3** | **FD** | **253** | **0** |
| 19200 | 0 | 7,372,800 | 1.5 | FE | 254 | 0.16% |
| 9600 | 1 | 1,843,200 | 6 | FA | 250 | 0 |

## 3.3 Complete Serial Port Interrupt Program Flow (Analytical Matrix)

The following matrix maps the **Initialization Sequence** — a guaranteed KTU exam question type.

| Step | Action | Register / Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Configure Timer 1 | TMOD = **20H** | Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) |
| 2 | Load TH1 | TH1 = **FDH** | Sets 9600 baud rate |
| 3 | Start Timer 1 | TCON = **40H** (TR1=1) | Begin baud rate clock generation |
| 4 | Configure Serial Port | SCON = **50H** | Mode 1 (SM0=0, SM1=1), REN=1 |
| 5 | Enable Serial Interrupt | IE = **90H** | EA=1, ES=1 |
| 6 | Set Priority (optional) | IP = **10H** | PS=1 (High priority) |
| 7 | CPU begins main loop | — | Waits for interrupt |

## 3.4 Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) Skeleton

The ISR must perform **two distinct actions** based on the flag check:

$$
\text{IF } RI = 1: \text{ Read SBUF, clear RI, process received byte}
$$
$$
\text{IF } TI = 1: \text{ Clear TI, prepare for next transmission}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical:** Both TI and RI are set by **hardware** but must be cleared by **software** inside the ISR. Forgetting to clear them will cause the CPU to re-enter the ISR immediately upon returning (an **infinite interrupt loop**).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: 8051 Serial Port Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU["8051 CPU Core"]
        ACC["Accumulator A"]
        SBUFTX["SBUF TX Buffer"]
        SBUFRX["SBUF RX Buffer"]
    end
    subgraph SREG["Serial Control SCON 98H"]
        SM0["SM0 Mode Bit"]
        SM1["SM1 Mode Bit"]
        REN["REN Receive Enable"]
        TIF["TI Transmit Flag"]
        RIF["RI Receive Flag"]
    end
    subgraph TIMER["Timer 1 Baud Generator"]
        TH1R["TH1 Reload Register"]
        OVFL["Overflow Pulse"]
    end
    subgraph PCON_REG["PCON 87H"]
        SDOUB["SMOD Doubler Bit"]
    end
    subgraph PHYS["Physical Pins"]
        TXD["Pin 11 TXD"]
        RXD["Pin 10 RXD"]
    end
    ACC -->|Write| SBUFTX
    SBUFTX --> TIF
    SBUFRX --> RIF
    ACC -->|Read| SBUFRX
    SM0 --> SM1
    TH1R --> OVFL
    OVFL --> SBUFTX
    OVFL --> SBUFRX
    SDOUB --> OVFL
    SBUFTX --> TXD
    RXD --> SBUFRX
    REN --> SBUFRX
    TIF --> INT23["INT Vector 0023H"]
    RIF --> INT23
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Serial Port Interrupt Handling Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START(["Start Main Program"]) --> INIT["Initialize SCON TMOD TH1 IE"]
    INIT --> LOOP["Execute Main Loop Tasks"]
    LOOP --> WAIT{"Is RI=1 or TI=1?"}
    WAIT -->|No| LOOP
    WAIT -->|Yes| ISR["Jump to Vector 0023H"]
    ISR --> PUSH["Push ACC PSW onto Stack"]
    PUSH --> CHKRI{"Is RI=1?"}
    CHKRI -->|Yes| READ["Read SBUF into A"]
    READ --> CLRR["Clear RI flag manually"]
    CLRR --> PROC["Process Received Byte"]
    PROC --> CHKTI
    CHKRI -->|No| CHKTI{"Is TI=1?"}
    CHKTI -->|Yes| CLRT["Clear TI flag manually"]
    CLRT --> NEXT["Prepare Next Byte in SBUF"]
    NEXT --> POP["Pop PSW ACC from Stack"]
    PROC --> POP
    POP --> RETI["Execute RETI Instruction"]
    RETI --> LOOP
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Byte Transmission with Interrupt

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as 8051 CPU
    participant SBUF as SBUF Register
    participant UART as UART Engine
    participant TXD as TXD Pin 11
    participant EXT as External Device
    CPU->>SBUF: Write Byte to SBUF
    SBUF->>UART: Transfer to Shift Register
    UART->>TXD: Shift Out Frame Start+8bits+Stop
    TXD-->>EXT: Send Bits at Baud Rate
    UART->>CPU: Set TI = 1 Hardware
    CPU->>CPU: Detect Interrupt Jump to 0023H
    CPU->>CPU: ISR Checks TI Software
    CPU->>UART: Clear TI = 0 Software
    CPU->>CPU: RETI Return to Main Loop
```

## 4.4 SCON Register Bit Map Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCON_BITS["SCON Register Address 98H Bit Addressable"]
        B7["Bit 7 SM0"]
        B6["Bit 6 SM1"]
        B5["Bit 5 SM2"]
        B4["Bit 4 REN"]
        B3["Bit 3 TB8"]
        B2["Bit 2 RB8"]
        B1["Bit 1 TI"]
        B0["Bit 0 RI"]
    end
    B7 --- B6 --- B5 --- B4 --- B3 --- B2 --- B1 --- B0
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above bit map shows the physical layout. For **Mode 1 with Receive Enabled**, the initialization byte is: `SM0=0, SM1=1, SM2=0, REN=1, TB8=0, RB8=0, TI=0, RI=0` → Binary `01010000` = **50H**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Explain the role of the SCON register in 8051 serial communication. List the four serial port modes.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **SCON (Serial Control) Register** located at address **98H** is a bit-addressable SFR that controls the operation of the 8051's on-chip UART. It contains key bits:
- **SM0, SM1** (Bits 7, 6): Select the serial mode
- **REN** (Bit 4): Enables the receiver
- **TI, RI** (Bits 1, 0): Interrupt flags set by hardware

**Four Serial Port Modes:**
1. **Mode 0:** 8-bit Shift Register, baud = $F_{osc} / 12$
2. **Mode 1:** 8-bit UART, baud = Timer 1 overflow (most common)
3. **Mode 2:** 9-bit UART, baud = $F_{osc} / 32$ or /64
4. **Mode 3:** 9-bit UART, baud = Timer 1 overflow

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Why must both TI and RI flags be cleared by software inside the serial port ISR? What happens if they are not cleared?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **TI (Transmit Interrupt)** and **RI (Receive Interrupt)** flags in the SCON register are set **automatically by hardware** when a transmission completes or a byte is received, respectively. However, unlike the hardware that sets them, the **8051 hardware does NOT automatically clear them**.

**Reason for Software Clearing:**
- The serial port has only **one interrupt vector (0023H)** shared by both events.
- If not cleared, the interrupt controller will **immediately re-trigger** the ISR as soon as `RETI` executes, creating an **infinite interrupt loop** that freezes the main program.

**Consequence of NOT clearing:** The CPU gets stuck inside the ISR, never returns to the main loop, and the system appears "hung."

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the serial port interrupt mechanism of the 8051 microcontroller. Discuss the SCON register and the role of SBUF.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an 8051 assembly program to transfer the message **"HELLO"** serially at **9600 baud, 8-bit data, 1 stop bit** continuously. Assume an **11.0592 MHz** crystal.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram above for the architecture)

**Explanation:**

The 8051's serial port is a **full-duplex UART** capable of simultaneous transmission and reception. The interrupt mechanism works as follows:

1. **Data Transmission Path:** When the CPU writes a byte to **SBUF (99H)**, it is loaded into the transmit shift register. The hardware automatically appends a **Start bit (0)**, shifts out the 8 data bits (LSB first), and then a **Stop bit (1)** at the rate set by the baud rate generator. Upon completion, the **TI flag (SCON.1)** is set to 1 by hardware.

2. **Data Reception Path:** When the **REN bit (SCON.4)** is set, the receiver monitors the RXD pin (P3.0). On detecting a Start bit, it samples 8 data bits and assembles them into the receive buffer. Upon completion, the **RI flag (SCON.0)** is set to 1 by hardware.

3. **Interrupt Generation:** If the **ES bit (IE.4)** and **EA bit (IE.7)** are both set, setting either TI or RI causes the CPU to push the current PC onto the stack and jump to the interrupt vector at **program address 0023H**.

4. **SCON Register Role:** The SCON register (98H) configures the mode (SM0, SM1), enables the receiver (REN), and houses the TI/RI flags.

5. **SBUF Register Role:** SBUF (99H) is a dual-mapped register — writes access the transmit buffer, reads access the receive buffer.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Baud Rate Calculation (Marks Awarded for Showing Work):**

Given $F_{osc} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz}$, Baud = 9600, SMOD = 0:

$$
TH1 = 256 - \frac{F_{osc}}{384 \times Baud} = 256 - \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400} = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FDH}
$$

**Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0023H           ; Serial Port ISR Vector
        LJMP    SERIAL_ISR
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #20H     ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
        MOV     TH1, #0FDH     ; 9600 baud rate
        MOV     SCON, #50H     ; Mode 1, REN enabled
        SETB    TR1            ; Start Timer 1
        MOV     IE, #90H       ; EA=1, ES=1
        MOV     DPTR, #MSG     ; Point DPTR to message
HERE:   CLR     A
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR     ; Read character from code memory
        JZ      HERE           ; If NULL, loop back
        MOV     SBUF, A        ; Transmit character
        INC     DPTR
        SJMP    HERE           ; Wait for interrupt

SERIAL_ISR:
        CLR     TI             ; Clear TI flag
        RETI                   ; Return from ISR

MSG:    DB      'HELLO', 0     ; Null-terminated string
        END
```

**Explanation of Key Instructions:**
- `MOV TMOD, #20H`: Configures Timer 1 in Mode 2 (auto-reload 8-bit)
- `MOV SCON, #50H`: Mode 1, REN=1 (50H = 0101 0000B)
- `MOV IE, #90H`: 90H = 1001 0000B → EA=1, ES=1
- The TI flag is cleared inside the ISR to allow the next transmission interrupt

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the formula for baud rate generation in **Mode 1** of the 8051 serial port. Calculate the value of **TH1** for a baud rate of **4800 bps** with a crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz** and **SMOD = 0**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of the **PCON register's SMOD bit** in baud rate generation. What is the effect of setting SMOD = 1 on the baud rate? Recalculate TH1 for **9600 baud** with **SMOD = 1**.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of Mode 1 Baud Rate Formula:**

In Mode 1, the baud rate is derived from the **Timer 1 overflow rate** as follows:

**Step 1:** Timer 1 in Mode 2 increments on every machine cycle. The machine cycle period is $12 / F_{osc}$. The timer overflows every $(256 - TH1)$ counts, so the overflow frequency is:

$$
f_{overflow} = \frac{F_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 2:** The UART divides this overflow rate by 32 to generate the baud rate. If SMOD = 1, the rate is doubled by the PCON register:

$$
Baud = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times f_{overflow}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the overflow frequency:

$$
Baud = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{F_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging for TH1 (with SMOD = 0, $2^0 = 1$):

$$
TH1 = 256 - \frac{F_{osc}}{384 \times Baud}
$$

**Calculation for 4800 Baud:**

$$
TH1 = 256 - \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 4800}
$$

$$
TH1 = 256 - \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{1{,}843{,}200}
$$

$$
TH1 = 256 - 6 = 250 = \text{FAH}
$$

$$
\boxed{TH1 = \text{FAH} = 250_{10}}
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Role of SMOD Bit:**

The **SMOD bit** is bit 7 of the **PCON register (87H)**. It is the only bit of PCON that has any effect on serial communication; all other PCON bits are related to power-saving modes (Idle, Power Down).

**Effect of SMOD = 1:**
- The baud rate is **doubled** in Modes 1, 2, and 3.
- This is useful when the standard TH1 reload value would overflow (i.e., become negative or < 1), allowing the use of higher baud rates with the same crystal.

**Recalculation for 9600 Baud with SMOD = 1:**

Using the modified formula:

$$
TH1 = 256 - \frac{F_{osc}}{192 \times Baud}
$$

$$
TH1 = 256 - \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{192 \times 9600}
$$

$$
TH1 = 256 - \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{1{,}843{,}200}
$$

$$
TH1 = 256 - 6 = 250 = \text{FAH}
$$

$$
\boxed{TH1 = \text{FAH} = 250_{10}}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| SMOD | Baud Rate Formula | TH1 for 9600 baud |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $F_{osc} / (384 \times Baud)$ | FDH = 253 |
| 1 | $F_{osc} / (192 \times Baud)$ | FAH = 250 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clear TI/RI inside the ISR** → System hangs in an infinite interrupt loop. (2 marks lost)
> 2. **Using 12 MHz crystal in baud rate calculations** → Non-integer TH1 values cause baud rate error. Always state 11.0592 MHz in answers. (1 mark lost)
> 3. **Setting SCON to 52H instead of 50H** → Accidentally setting TI=1 at initialization triggers a spurious interrupt immediately. (1 mark lost)
> 4. **Not enabling REN (SCON.4)** for reception → The receiver is disabled silently with no compile error. (2 marks lost)
> 5. **Forgetting to set the SMOD bit when using the doubled formula** → Answer will not match the expected TH1. (1 mark lost)
> 6. **Wrong interrupt vector address** → Serial port is **0023H**, NOT 002BH (Timer 1) or 0013H (Ext Int 1). (2 marks lost)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Serial Port Interrupt (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- ☐ **Interrupt Vector Address:** Serial Port = **0023H**
- ☐ **SFR Addresses:** SCON = **98H**, SBUF = **99H**, IE = **A8H**, IP = **B8H**, PCON = **87H**, TMOD = **89H**, TCON = **88H**
- ☐ **Most Common Mode:** Mode 1 (8-bit UART, variable baud via Timer 1)
- ☐ **Standard Crystal:** **11.0592 MHz** (gives exact integer TH1 values)
- ☐ **Key Formula:** $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{F_{osc}}{384 \times Baud}$ (SMOD=0)
- ☐ **Key Formula (SMOD=1):** $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{F_{osc}}{192 \times Baud}$
- ☐ **Initialization Byte:** SCON = **50H** for Mode 1 + REN enabled
- ☐ **Timer Config:** TMOD = **20H** (Timer 1, Mode 2 auto-reload)
- ☐ **Interrupt Enable:** IE = **90H** (EA=1, ES=1)
- ☐ **SBUF is dual-mapped** — write for transmit, read for receive
- ☐ **TI and RI MUST be cleared by software** inside the ISR
- ☐ **Mode 0 baud rate:** $F_{osc} / 12$ (fixed, no Timer 1 needed)
- ☐ **Mode 2 baud rate:** $F_{osc} / 32$ (SMOD=0) or $F_{osc} / 64$ (SMOD=1) — fixed
- ☐ **Frame Format (Mode 1):** 1 Start bit + 8 Data bits + 1 Stop bit = **10 bits/frame**
- ☐ **Two flags, one vector:** Always check both RI and TI inside the ISR

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External Interrupts and Interrupt Priority
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** External Interrupts and Interrupt Priority

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an External Interrupt?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> An **External Interrupt** is a hardware-triggered asynchronous event generated by an external peripheral or digital signal applied to a dedicated microcontroller pin, which forces the CPU to suspend the currently executing program, save its context, and execute a predefined **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. In the 8051 architecture, the external interrupt sources are **INT0̄ (Pin 3.2)** and **INT1̄ (Pin 3.3)**, both of which are sampled on every machine cycle.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Doorbell of the CPU

Imagine you are reading a book (the CPU executing the `main()` program). Suddenly, the **doorbell rings** (an external interrupt signal arrives at INT0̄/INT1̄). You don't wait until you finish the chapter; you:
1. **Mark the page** you were on (Push PC onto the stack).
2. **Get up** and answer the door (Jump to the ISR vector address).
3. **Handle the visitor** (Execute the ISR).
4. **Return to the marked page** (Execute `RETI` → Pop PC from stack).

This is exactly what an external interrupt does. The 8051 has **2 external** interrupts, **2 timer** interrupts, and **1 serial port** interrupt — a total of **5 interrupt sources** and **2 priority levels**.

## 1.2 Interrupt Priority — Why Do We Need It?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority** is the mechanism that resolves conflicts when two or more interrupts arrive **simultaneously**, or when a high-priority interrupt must pre-empt (nest inside) a low-priority ISR that is already running. The 8051 supports a **2-level priority scheme** (High and Low) controlled by the **IP (Interrupt Priority) register**.

### Analogy — The Hospital Triage System

- A **routine check-up patient** (low priority) is in the consultation room.
- Suddenly, a **cardiac arrest patient** (high priority) arrives.
- The doctor (CPU) **pauses** the check-up, **saves** the patient's file (stack), and immediately attends to the cardiac case. After saving the high-priority patient, the doctor **resumes** the low-priority check-up.

This is **interrupt nesting**, and it is enabled by configuring the IP register.

## 1.3 The Three Control Registers Governing External Interrupts

The 8051 uses three special function registers (SFRs) to control external interrupts:

| Register | Address | Purpose |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **TCON** | 88H | Configures trigger type (edge/level) & holds interrupt flags |
| **IE** | A8H | Enables or disables individual interrupts + global master switch |
| **IP** | B8H | Assigns high or low priority to each interrupt source |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TCON Register — Trigger Configuration & Flag Storage

The **TCON (Timer/Counter Control)** register at address **88H** is an **8-bit, bit-addressable** register. The upper 4 bits control the timers; the lower 4 bits govern the external interrupts.

### TCON Bit Layout (Address: 88H)

| Bit | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | **IE1** | **IT1** | **IE0** | **IT0** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Position | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **bold bits (IE0, IT0, IE1, IT1)** are the ones relevant to **External Interrupts**. Bits TF0/TR0 and TF1/TR1 belong to Timers (Module 3 content).

### Meaning of Each External Interrupt Bit

- **IT0 (TCON.0) — Interrupt 0 Type Select:**
  - `IT0 = 0` → **Level-Triggered**: INT0̄ is active **low** and must remain low until the CPU acknowledges it.
  - `IT0 = 1` → **Edge-Triggered**: A **falling edge** (high→low) on INT0̄ sets the flag, even if the signal returns high before acknowledgment.

- **IE0 (TCON.1) — Interrupt 0 Edge Flag:**
  - Set **automatically by hardware** when an external interrupt 0 is detected.
  - **Cleared automatically** when the CPU vectors to the ISR (for edge-triggered mode).
  - For level-triggered mode, the flag follows the pin state and is **not auto-cleared**.

- **IT1 (TCON.2) — Interrupt 1 Type Select:** Same logic as IT0, but for INT1̄ (P3.3).

- **IE1 (TCON.3) — Interrupt 1 Edge Flag:** Same logic as IE0, but for INT1̄.

### The Two Trigger Modes — A Rigorous Comparison

| Property | **Level-Triggered (ITx = 0)** | **Edge-Triggered (ITx = 1)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Activation condition | **Low level** on INTx̄ pin | **Falling edge** (H→L transition) on INTx̄ |
| Flag behavior | Flag = NOT(INTx̄) at end of each cycle | Flag set on H→L transition |
| Flag clearing | **NOT auto-cleared** by hardware | **Auto-cleared** when ISR is entered |
| Re-trigger during ISR | Yes — multiple ISRs if signal stays low | No — only one ISR per edge |
| Use case | Wired-OR interrupt sharing, slowly varying signals | Clean digital pulse, push-button debouncing |
| **KTU High-Yield Point** | **Must ensure the low level is removed before RETI** or ISR will be re-entered | **Safer mode**; widely used in board exams |

## 2.2 The IE Register — Interrupt Enable / Disable

The **IE (Interrupt Enable)** register at address **A8H** is also bit-addressable and acts as the master switch panel for all 5 interrupts.

### IE Register Bit Layout (Address: A8H)

| Bit | **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Position | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |

### Bit Functions

- **EA (IE.7) — Global Enable:** `EA = 1` enables all interrupts (subject to individual bits); `EA = 0` masks all interrupts. **This is the master switch.**
- **EX0 (IE.0) — Enable External Interrupt 0:** Must be `1` along with `EA = 1` to allow INT0̄.
- **ET0 (IE.1) — Enable Timer 0 Interrupt.**
- **EX1 (IE.2) — Enable External Interrupt 1:** Must be `1` along with `EA = 1` to allow INT1̄.
- **ET1 (IE.3) — Enable Timer 1 Interrupt.**
- **ES (IE.4) — Enable Serial Port Interrupt.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often set only `EX0 = 1` and forget `EA = 1`. The result — the interrupt **never fires**. Always set both.

## 2.3 The IP Register — Interrupt Priority Assignment

The **IP (Interrupt Priority)** register at address **B8H** is a bit-addressable register that assigns **HIGH or LOW priority** to each interrupt source.

### IP Register Bit Layout (Address: B8H)

| Bit | — | — | — | **PS** | **PT1** | **PX1** | **PT0** | **PX0** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Position | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |

### Bit Functions

- **PX0 (IP.0) — External Interrupt 0 Priority:** `1` = High priority; `0` = Low priority.
- **PT0 (IP.1) — Timer 0 Priority.**
- **PX1 (IP.2) — External Interrupt 1 Priority.**
- **PT1 (IP.3) — Timer 1 Priority.**
- **PS (IP.4) — Serial Port Priority.**

> [!NOTE]
> After a **Reset**, all bits of IP are cleared to `0`, meaning **all interrupts default to LOW priority**. The programmer must explicitly set the high-priority bits.

## 2.4 The Two-Level Priority Resolution Logic

The 8051 resolves "which interrupt runs first" using this exact 3-step decision ladder:

**Step 1 — Check the Global Flag:** If a new interrupt's priority level is **higher** than the currently executing ISR's priority, the new one is allowed to **pre-empt** (nest inside) the current ISR.

**Step 2 — Check the Natural Priority Poll Order:** If two interrupts of the **same priority level** are detected simultaneously, the 8051 consults a **fixed internal polling sequence**:

| Poll Order | Interrupt Source | Flag |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 (highest) | External Interrupt 0 | **IE0** |
| 2 | Timer 0 Overflow | **TF0** |
| 3 | External Interrupt 1 | **IE1** |
| 4 | Timer 1 Overflow | **TF1** |
| 5 (lowest) | Serial Port | **RI + TI** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "What is the natural priority order in 8051?" The answer is the table above, in this exact sequence. External Interrupt 0 is **always first** in the natural poll.

**Step 3 — A Low-Priority ISR cannot interrupt another Low-Priority ISR.** Similarly, a High-Priority ISR can be pre-empted only by another **higher** priority source — but since there are only 2 levels, a high-priority ISR **cannot be interrupted by any low-priority source**.

## 2.5 Vector Addresses — The Jump Targets

When an interrupt is accepted, the CPU loads the PC with a fixed address (vector). The ISR code must be placed starting at these addresses using `ORG` directives or the compiler's interrupt vector keyword.

| Interrupt | Flag | **Vector Address** | C51 Keyword |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **External 0 (INT0̄)** | IE0 | **0003H** | `void ext0_ISR(void) interrupt 0` |
| Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 | 000BH | `interrupt 1` |
| **External 1 (INT1̄)** | IE1 | **0013H** | `interrupt 2` |
| Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 | 001BH | `interrupt 3` |
| Serial Port | RI/TI | 0023H | `interrupt 4` |

> [!NOTE]
> The vector addresses are spaced only **8 bytes** apart. Since a useful ISR is rarely ≤ 8 bytes, in assembly we either place a short ISR or an `AJMP`/`LJMP` to the actual routine elsewhere. In Keil C, the compiler handles this automatically.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Value / Rule |
| :--- | :--- |
| External Interrupt pins | **P3.2 (INT0̄)**, **P3.3 (INT1̄)** |
| Total number of interrupt sources | **5** (2 external + 2 timer + 1 serial) |
| Priority levels | **2** (High and Low) |
| Edge-trigger detection | **Falling edge** (H→L) when `ITx = 1` |
| Vector address spacing | **8 bytes** |
| External 0 vector | **0003H** |
| External 1 vector | **0013H** |
| Natural priority order | **IE0 $\succ$ TF0 $\succ$ IE1 $\succ$ TF1 $\succ$ RI/TI** |
| Master enable bit | **EA (IE.7)** must be `1` |
| Default IP value after reset | **00H** (all low priority) |
| RETI instruction function | Pop PC from stack + re-enable current priority level logic |
| Low → High pre-emption | **Allowed** |
| High → Low pre-emption | **NOT allowed** |
| Same-level pre-emption | **NOT allowed** (only natural poll breaks tie) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Logical Derivation — How a Single External Interrupt Event is Processed

The 8051 does not respond instantly to an external interrupt. The following is the **complete machine-cycle-by-machine-cycle logical derivation** that every KTU board answer must include.

### Step 1: Hardware Sampling

The 8051 samples the INT0̄ (or INT1̄) pin at the **end of every machine cycle** (each machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods). The CPU holds the sampled value in an internal latch.

### Step 2: Flag Setting Logic

The hardware sets the interrupt flag **IE0** (or **IE1**) under the following exact condition, derived from the TCON configuration:

$$\text{Flag Set} = \begin{cases} \overline{\text{INT0̄}} \cdot \overline{\text{IT0}} & \text{(level-triggered, low level detected)} \\ \text{FallingEdge}(\text{INT0̄}) \cdot \text{IT0} & \text{(edge-triggered, IT0 = 1)} \end{cases}$$

In words:
- If `IT0 = 0` (level mode), the flag IE0 becomes `1` whenever the pin is low.
- If `IT0 = 1` (edge mode), the flag IE0 becomes `1` **only on the high-to-low transition**.

### Step 3: Interrupt Enablement Check (Two AND Gates)

The interrupt request is propagated to the CPU **only if both** of the following are true simultaneously:

$$\text{Request to CPU} = \text{IE0} \cdot \text{EX0} \cdot \text{EA}$$

If any one of these three is `0`, the flag IE0 remains set (waiting) but the CPU **ignores** the request. This is why the KTU examiner always emphasizes: *"Set EA, then EX0, then trigger INT0̄."*

### Step 4: Priority Resolution

The request is forwarded to the priority resolver, which compares the new request's priority bit (PX0) with the priority of the currently running ISR (if any):

- If **no ISR is running** → the request is immediately accepted.
- If an ISR is running and **new priority > current priority** → the request is accepted (pre-emption).
- If an ISR is running and **new priority ≤ current priority** → the request is **latched** and serviced only after the current ISR finishes and executes `RETI`.

### Step 5: Vector Fetch and Context Save (Hardware-Automated)

The CPU automatically executes a **CALL to the vector address** equivalent:

$$\text{PC} \leftarrow 0003\text{H} \quad \text{(for INT0)}$$

The current PC value is **pushed onto the stack** (SP is incremented by 2). The hardware also **clears the IE0 flag** (in edge-triggered mode only). Crucially, the CPU **does NOT automatically save the PSW, ACC, B, or other registers** — this is the **programmer's responsibility** if the ISR modifies them.

### Step 6: ISR Execution

The CPU executes the ISR code from the vector address onward.

### Step 7: Return via RETI

The `RETI` instruction performs two critical actions:
1. **POPs the PC** from the stack (restoring the main program counter).
2. **Informs the priority hardware** that the current ISR has finished, allowing any latched lower-priority (or same-priority waiting) interrupt to be serviced.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** If you use `RET` instead of `RETI` in the ISR, the program returns correctly the first time, but the priority logic is **not re-armed**. Subsequent interrupts will misbehave. **Always use RETI.**

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When is a New Interrupt Accepted?

Let current priority = $P_c$ (the priority of the currently executing ISR, or 0 if main program) and new priority = $P_n$ (the priority level of the newly arriving interrupt). Let $P_H = 1$ (high) and $P_L = 0$ (low). Define the acceptance function $A$:

$$A(P_c, P_n) = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT immediately} & \text{if } P_n > P_c \\ \text{ACCEPT (pre-empt)} & \text{if } P_n = 1 \text{ and } P_c = 0 \\ \text{LATCH (wait)} & \text{if } P_n = P_c \\ \text{REJECT (drop)} & \text{if } P_n < P_c \end{cases}$$

### Truth Table

| $P_c$ (current) | $P_n$ (new) | $A$ (decision) | Reason |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 0 (main) | 0 (low) | **Accept** | Main has no priority, new low-priority runs |
| 0 (main) | 1 (high) | **Accept** | Higher pre-empts main |
| 1 (low ISR) | 0 (low) | **Latch** | Same level, wait for RETI |
| 1 (low ISR) | 1 (high) | **Accept (pre-empt)** | Higher interrupts lower |
| 0 (high ISR) | 0 (low) | **Reject** | Lower cannot interrupt higher |
| 0 (high ISR) | 1 (high) | **Latch** | Same level, wait for RETI |

> [!NOTE]
> A "latched" interrupt is held in its flag (e.g., IE0) and is serviced **only after** the current ISR returns via `RETI`. If a new interrupt of the same or higher priority arrives during the wait, the natural priority poll decides the order.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Interrupt Latency Calculation

> **Problem:** An 8051 system uses an 11.0592 MHz crystal. An external interrupt 0 (edge-triggered) is enabled. Calculate the **minimum interrupt latency** in microseconds.

### Step 1: Determine the machine cycle period

$$T_{cyc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{cyc} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \, \mu\text{s}$$

### Step 2: Identify the latency components

The 8051 does not respond to an interrupt in the **same cycle** it was sampled. The hardware enforces a **1-cycle delay** before vectoring. The actual accepted latency is therefore:

$$T_{latency} = 1 \text{ machine cycle} = 1.085 \, \mu\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **hardware minimum**. In practice, the actual latency is **longer** by the time taken to complete the *currently executing instruction*. If the CPU is executing a `MUL AB` (4 machine cycles) when INT0̄ fires, the ISR does not begin until that `MUL` finishes. The worst-case latency is therefore instruction-dependent.

### Step 3: Worst-case latency for the longest single-cycle instruction

The longest 8051 instruction is `MUL AB` or `DIV AB`, both taking **4 machine cycles**:

$$T_{latency, max} = 5 \times T_{cyc} = 5 \times 1.085 = 5.425 \, \mu\text{s}$$

This completes the derivation.

## 3.4 Complete Initialization Code Block — Both Assembly and C

### Assembly (Intel 8051)

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        SJMP MAIN              ; Skip over ISR vector area

        ; ---- ISR for External Interrupt 0 ----
        ORG 0003H              ; Vector for INT0
EXT0_ISR:
        PUSH PSW
        PUSH ACC               ; Save context
        ; ... user code ...
        POP ACC
        POP PSW
        RETI                   ; Return and re-arm priority

        ORG 0030H
MAIN:
        MOV IE, #10000010B     ; EA=1, EX0=1, rest=0
        MOV IP, #00000001B     ; PX0 = 1 (High priority)
        SETB IT0               ; Edge-triggered on INT0 (P3.2)
HERE:   SJMP HERE              ; Idle loop
        END
```

### Embedded C (Keil C51)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void ext0_ISR(void) interrupt 0   // Vector 0 = External 0
{
    // Save/restore handled by 'using' or by compiler
    P1 ^= 0x01;                   // Toggle P1.0
}

void main(void)
{
    IT0 = 1;                      // Edge-triggered
    EX0 = 1;                      // Enable external 0
    EA  = 1;                      // Global enable
    PX0 = 1;                      // High priority
    while(1);                     // Idle
}
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hardware Block Diagram — External Interrupt Path Inside the 8051

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ExtPins["External World"]
        sw0["Push Button to P3.2 INT0_bar"]
        sw1["Sensor to P3.3 INT1_bar"]
    end

    subgraph PinLogic["Pin Sampling Latch"]
        latch0["Sampler for INT0_bar"]
        latch1["Sampler for INT1_bar"]
    end

    subgraph TCONlogic["TCON Register 88H"]
        it0["IT0 - Trigger Type"]
        ie0["IE0 - Flag for INT0"]
        it1["IT1 - Trigger Type"]
        ie1["IE1 - Flag for INT1"]
    end

    subgraph IEgate["IE Register A8H"]
        ex0["EX0"]
        ex1["EX1"]
        ea["EA - Master Switch"]
    end

    subgraph IPgate["IP Register B8H"]
        px0["PX0"]
        px1["PX1"]
    end

    subgraph CPUcore["8051 CPU Core"]
        priority["Priority Resolver"]
        pcmux["PC Mux to Vector"]
        stack["Stack Push PC"]
        isr["ISR Execution"]
    end

    sw0 --> latch0
    sw1 --> latch1
    latch0 --> ie0
    latch1 --> ie1
    it0 --> ie0
    it1 --> ie1

    ie0 --> ex0
    ie1 --> ex1
    ex0 --> ea
    ex1 --> ea

    ex0 --> priority
    ex1 --> priority
    px0 --> priority
    px1 --> priority

    priority --> pcmux
    pcmux --> stack
    stack --> isr
    isr --> reti["RETI - Return to Main"]
    reti --> priority
```

## 4.2 Functional State Machine — Interrupt Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MainExec: CPU running main program
    MainExec --> Sample: End of every machine cycle
    Sample --> FlagCheck: INTx_bar sampled
    FlagCheck --> Wait: Flag set but EXx or EA = 0
    Wait --> Sample: Continue polling
    FlagCheck --> Priority: EXx = 1 AND EA = 1
    Priority --> MainExec: Lower priority than current ISR
    Priority --> Preempt: Higher priority than current ISR
    Priority --> Service: Main program or no ISR running
    Preempt --> PushPC: Save current PC
    Service --> PushPC: Save current PC
    PushPC --> VectorFetch: Load PC with 0003H or 0013H
    VectorFetch --> ClearFlag: Hardware clears IEx (edge mode)
    ClearFlag --> ISRexec: Execute ISR instructions
    ISRexec --> RETI: Last instruction = RETI
    RETI --> PopPC: Restore PC from stack
    PopPC --> MainExec: Resume main program
```

## 4.3 Priority Resolution Flowchart — Board-Exam Style

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["New Interrupt Request Arrives"] --> B{"EA = 1 ?"}
    B -- No --> Z1["Ignored - Masked by Master Switch"]
    B -- Yes --> C{"Individual enable bit set?"}
    C -- No --> Z2["Ignored - Source Disabled"]
    C -- Yes --> D{"Currently in an ISR?"}
    D -- No --> E["ACCEPT Immediately - Vector to ISR"]
    D -- Yes --> F{"New priority greater than current?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["PRE-EMPT - Push current PC and service new"]
    F -- No --> H{"Same priority level?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["LATCH - Wait for current RETI"]
    H -- No --> Z3["REJECT - Lower priority cannot interrupt"]
    E --> End["Service Complete via RETI"]
    G --> End
    I --> End
```

## 4.4 Memory Map — Interrupt Vector Locations in Code ROM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    addr0000["0000H - Reset Vector<br/>Jump to Main"]
    addr0003["0003H - INT0 Vector<br/>External 0 ISR"]
    addr000B["000BH - Timer 0 Vector"]
    addr0013["0013H - INT1 Vector<br/>External 1 ISR"]
    addr001B["001BH - Timer 1 Vector"]
    addr0023["0023H - Serial Vector"]
    addr0030["0030H onwards - Main Program"]

    addr0000 --- addr0003
    addr0003 --- addr000B
    addr000B --- addr0013
    addr0013 --- addr001B
    addr001B --- addr0023
    addr0023 --- addr0030
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]:**
**List the registers used to control external interrupts in 8051. What is the role of the IT0 bit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 8051 uses three registers to control external interrupts:
1. **TCON (88H)** — Holds the IE0/IE1 flags and configures the trigger type via IT0/IT1 bits.
2. **IE (A8H)** — The Interrupt Enable register, containing the global master bit EA and individual enable bits EX0/EX1.
3. **IP (B8H)** — The Interrupt Priority register, assigning high/low priority via PX0/PX1 bits.

The **IT0 bit (TCON.0)** selects the trigger mode for External Interrupt 0:
- `IT0 = 0` → **Level-triggered**: The interrupt is requested whenever the INT0̄ pin is held LOW.
- `IT0 = 1` → **Edge-triggered**: The interrupt is requested on a **falling edge** (HIGH→LOW) of INT0̄.

*(1 mark each for naming the three registers + 1 mark for the two cases of IT0.)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]:**
**Differentiate between level-triggered and edge-triggered external interrupts in 8051.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Level-Triggered (`ITx = 0`) | Edge-Triggered (`ITx = 1`) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Activation | Pin held **LOW** | **Falling edge** (H→L) |
| Flag auto-clear | Not auto-cleared | Auto-cleared on ISR entry |
| Re-trigger in ISR | Possible (multiple ISRs) | Not possible (one ISR per edge) |
| Typical use | Line-sharing, slow signals | Push buttons, clean pulses |

*(2 marks for clear table + 1 mark for example/justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Between A and B)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]:**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the external interrupt structure of the 8051 microcontroller. List the relevant SFRs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the two-level interrupt priority scheme of 8051. What is the natural priority order? How does an incoming high-priority interrupt pre-empt a low-priority ISR? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The 8051 has two external interrupt pins: **P3.2 (INT0̄)** and **P3.3 (INT1̄)**. The relevant SFRs are:

- **TCON (88H):** Holds the IT0/IT1 trigger-select bits and the IE0/IE1 edge flags. *IT0 = 1* configures edge-triggered mode for INT0̄.
- **IE (A8H):** Master enable EA and individual enables EX0, EX1.
- **IP (B8H):** Priority bits PX0, PX1.

A simplified block diagram:

`P3.2 (INT0̄) → Sampler → IE0 flag in TCON → AND with (EX0 · EA) → Priority Resolver → PC ← 0003H → ISR`

The sequence: the pin is sampled at the end of every machine cycle. On a falling edge, IE0 is set. The signal propagates to the CPU **only if** both `EX0 = 1` and `EA = 1`. The CPU then pushes the current PC onto the stack, clears IE0 (in edge mode), and jumps to vector **0003H**. The ISR ends with `RETI`, which pops the PC and re-arms the priority logic. *(3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for SFR roles + 2 marks for sequence.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

The 8051 supports **2 priority levels** — **High (1)** and **Low (0)** — configured by the IP register. After reset, all interrupts are LOW priority.

**Priority Resolution Rules:**
1. A high-priority interrupt can **pre-empt** (interrupt) a low-priority ISR.
2. A low-priority interrupt **cannot** pre-empt a high-priority ISR.
3. Two interrupts of the **same priority** are resolved by the **natural priority poll order**: **IE0 → TF0 → IE1 → TF1 → RI/TI**. The first in this order wins.

**Pre-emption Mechanism:**
Suppose the CPU is executing a low-priority ISR for INT1̄. At that moment, a high-priority request arrives on INT0̄ (PX0 = 1, PX1 = 0). The priority hardware detects $P_n > P_c$, automatically:
- Pushes the current PC (pointing inside the INT1 ISR) onto the stack.
- Loads PC = 0003H.
- The INT0 ISR runs to completion, ending with `RETI`, which pops the saved PC and resumes the INT1 ISR from where it stopped. *(3 marks for rules + 2 marks for natural order + 2 marks for pre-emption mechanism.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Alternative Choice]:**

**(a)** Write an 8051 C program to configure External Interrupt 0 as **edge-triggered, high priority**. Toggle P1.0 every time INT0̄ occurs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the role of the **EA bit in the IE register** and the **PX0 bit in the IP register**. What happens if EA = 0 even though EX0 = 1 and a valid edge occurs on INT0̄? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;          // P1.0 pin for toggling

void ext0_ISR(void) interrupt 0   // Vector 0 = External Interrupt 0
{
    LED = ~LED;          // Toggle P1.0
}

void main(void)
{
    LED = 0;             // Initialize LED OFF
    IT0 = 1;             // Edge-triggered (falling edge)
    PX0 = 1;             // High priority
    EX0 = 1;             // Enable External 0
    EA  = 1;             // Global enable
    while(1);            // Idle — wait for interrupts
}
```

Explanation:
- `IT0 = 1` → TCON.0 set → falling-edge trigger on P3.2.
- `PX0 = 1` → IP.0 set → high priority assigned.
- `EX0 = 1` and `EA = 1` → both gates open.
- On falling edge of P3.2, IE0 sets, the CPU vectors to 0003H, executes `ext0_ISR` which toggles P1.0, and `RETI` returns. *(2 marks code + 3 marks register-by-register explanation + 2 marks for ISR mechanism.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

- **EA (IE.7) — Global Enable (Master Switch):** When `EA = 1`, all interrupts with their individual enables set are honored. When `EA = 0`, **all interrupts are globally masked**, regardless of EX0, ET0, EX1, ET1, or ES. It is the **single master gate** at the output of the priority resolver.
- **PX0 (IP.0) — External Interrupt 0 Priority:** Sets INT0̄ as **high priority (1)** or **low priority (0)**. After reset, PX0 = 0 (low priority).

**Scenario: EA = 0, EX0 = 1, valid falling edge on INT0̄**
- The sampler detects the edge, IE0 is **set** in TCON.1.
- The AND gate `IE0 · EX0 · EA` evaluates as `1 · 1 · 0 = 0` → the request is **NOT forwarded** to the priority resolver.
- **Effect:** The CPU continues executing the main program. The IE0 flag remains set. The next time EA is set to 1, IF the pin is still low (level mode) or a new edge occurs (edge mode), the request will then be serviced. The interrupt is essentially **held/latched** until EA = 1. *(3 marks EA + 2 marks PX0 + 2 marks scenario analysis.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to set `EA = 1` after configuring `EX0` — ISR **never fires** (very common: 2-mark penalty).
> 2. Using `RET` instead of `RETI` in assembly ISR — priority logic breaks, subsequent interrupts misbehave (2-mark penalty).
> 3. Confusing "edge-triggered" with "rising edge" — the 8051 external interrupts trigger on the **FALLING edge only** (1-mark penalty).
> 4. Not listing the **natural priority order** explicitly when explaining same-level conflicts (lose 2 marks).
> 5. Failing to mention that **level-triggered flags are NOT auto-cleared** — the programmer must clear them, or risk ISR re-entry (1-mark penalty).
> 6. Drawing vector addresses as 0003H/0013H correctly — many students write 0008H or 0004H by mistake.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **External interrupt pins:** P3.2 (INT0̄) and P3.3 (INT1̄); **active LOW**.
- **Three control registers:** TCON (trigger + flag), IE (enable), IP (priority).
- **IT0/IT1 = 0 → level-triggered (low); IT0/IT1 = 1 → edge-triggered (falling edge).**
- **IE0/IE1 flags are set by hardware; auto-cleared only in edge mode on ISR entry.**
- **EA must = 1** to enable any interrupt. **EX0/EX1** are individual enables.
- **Two priority levels only** — High (bit = 1) and Low (bit = 0). **No nested multi-level priority.**
- **Default priority (natural poll):** IE0 → TF0 → IE1 → TF1 → RI/TI.
- **Vector addresses:** External 0 = **0003H**, External 1 = **0013H** (8 bytes apart).
- **Pre-emption rule:** High can pre-empt Low; Low **cannot** pre-empt High; same-level cannot pre-empt.
- **Always end ISR with RETI** — never RET.
- **ISR is responsible for saving/restoring** ACC, B, PSW, DPTR if used (8051 only auto-pushes PC).
- **Worst-case latency** = longest instruction time + 1 machine cycle (e.g., 5 × T_cyc for 11.0592 MHz → 5.425 µs).
- **Memory size of vector region:** 0003H–002AH is 40 bytes reserved for the 5 ISRs — main program usually starts at **0030H** to leave room.
- **High-priority ISR cannot be interrupted by a low-priority source** — fundamental rule in 8051 priority design.
- **8051 has no dedicated Interrupt Controller IC** (unlike 8086's 8259A); the logic is built into the SFRs.
- **Natural priority poll happens at the END of every instruction cycle**, not at every machine cycle.
- **IE0/IE1 edge flags can be set even if EX0/EX1 = 0** — they merely wait for the enable to be applied.
- **PX0 = 1, PX1 = 0** is the typical exam setup to demonstrate pre-emption of INT0̄ over INT1̄.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing of Peripherals - LED
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Interfacing of Peripherals - LED # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of LED Interfacing **Light Emitting Diode (LED) Interfacing** is the process of electrically and logically connecting one or more LEDs to the General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pins of a microcontroller in such a way that the microcontroller can control the ON/OFF (and in advanced cases, brightness via PWM) state of the LED by writing appropriate logic levels (0 or 1) to the corresponding port pins. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context (PBRUT404 - Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems, Module 2), LED interfacing is treated as the **foundational peripheral interfacing problem** that introduces students to real-world I/O hardware, current limiting concepts, GPIO port configuration registers, and basic assembly/C firmware routines. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 specifically requires students to understand *how* a microcontroller "talks" to a simple output device. The LED is the canonical first peripheral because it is non-critical (failure does not damage the MCU), visually verifiable, and exercises both the electrical (resistor sizing) and logical (bit manipulation of SFRs) domains. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **water tap and a sink**: - The **microcontroller pin** is the *hand controlling the tap*. - The **LED** is the *sink that will light up when water flows*. - The **current limiting resistor (R)** is the *narrow pipe* that prevents a dangerous flood (excess current) from destroying the sink. - The **program instruction** (e.g., `SETB P1.0`) is the *decision* to open or close the tap. When the microcontroller "opens the tap" (drives the pin HIGH in active-HIGH mode), conventional current flows from the supply → through the resistor → through the LED → into the pin (or to ground, depending on configuration), and the LED emits light. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard LED forward voltage (V_F):** typically **1.8 V to 3.2 V** depending on color (Red ≈ 1.8–2.2 V, Green ≈ 2.0–2.4 V, Blue/White ≈ 3.0–3.4 V). - **Standard forward current (I_F):** typically **5 mA to 20 mA**; a safe design value is **10 mA**. - **Maximum forward current (I_F(max)):** usually **30 mA** for standard 5 mm LEDs. - **Microcontroller pin output current:** for 8051-family MCUs, typically **10 mA** per pin (sinking) and **60–80 mA** per port. - **Standard logic HIGH voltage (V_OH):** ≈ **5 V** (for TTL-level MCUs like 8051). > [!NOTE] > **Why a resistor is mandatory:** An LED is a *current-driven* device. Without a current limiting resistor, the I-V curve of the LED is nearly vertical after V_F, meaning a tiny voltage increase causes a massive current increase — destroying the LED almost instantly. The resistor linearizes the combined load. ## 1.4 Why LEDs are Interfaced in Two Standard Topologies There are two canonical ways to connect an LED to a microcontroller pin: | Topology | Pin Logic to Turn LED ON | Common Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Active-HIGH (Sourcing)** | Pin = Logic **1** (HIGH) | Most modern MCUs (ARM, PIC) | | **Active-LOW (Sinking)** | Pin = Logic **0** (LOW) | Open-drain buses, 8051 classic style | KTU examiners frequently test the **active-LOW configuration** because it exploits the higher current-sinking capability of 8051 port pins compared to current-sourcing. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Configurations — Operational Theory ### 2.1.1 Active-HIGH Configuration (Current Sourcing) The LED anode connects to the MCU pin through a resistor, and the cathode connects to ground. When the pin is driven HIGH, current flows **out** of the pin, through R, through the LED, to ground. **Step-by-step logic:** 1. MCU program executes `P1.0 = 1` (or `SETB P1.0` in 8051 assembly). 2. Internal output driver of the pin pulls the line up to V_CC (5 V). 3. Voltage drop across the series combination: V_CC − V_F appears across resistor R. 4. Current $I = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{R}$ flows; LED emits light. ### 2.1.2 Active-LOW Configuration (Current Sinking) The LED anode connects to V_CC through resistor R, and the cathode connects to the MCU pin. When the pin is driven LOW, it acts as a path to ground, completing the circuit. **Step-by-step logic:** 1. MCU program executes `P1.0 = 0` (or `CLR P1.0`). 2. Internal output transistor of the pin pulls the line to **0 V** (ground). 3. Voltage drop $V_{CC} - V_F$ appears across resistor R. 4. Current flows from V_CC → R → LED → pin → ground; LED emits light. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** The 8051 GPIO pin can sink more current (typically **20 mA**) than it can source (**a few mA**). Therefore, **active-LOW (sinking) is the preferred industrial standard for 8051-based designs** and is the most frequently asked topology in KTU exams. ## 2.2 GPIO Port Architecture — 8051 Perspective The 8051 has four 8-bit ports (P0, P1, P2, P3). Each pin has: - An **output latch** (1-bit memory cell in an SFR — Special Function Register). - An **output driver** (a totem-pole transistor pair on P1, P2, P3; open-drain on P0). - **Read-back capability** for input operations. To control an LED, the firmware must: 1. **Configure the pin as output** (set the bit in the port register; on 8051 this is implicit for P1/P2/P3 unless overridden). 2. **Write the appropriate logic level** to the port SFR bit. 3. **Include delays** if blinking is required (using software loops or timers — covered in later modules). ## 2.3 Multiple LED Interfacing Strategies For **N LEDs**, there are two strategies: - **Direct Drive (1 LED per pin):** Uses N MCU pins. Simple but pin-inefficient. - **Multiplexed Drive (Charlieplexing / Matrix):** Uses fewer pins. Complex; typically not in KTU Module 2 scope. For Module 2, KTU focuses on **direct drive** of 8 LEDs on a single port (e.g., all 8 bits of P1) — useful for binary counting demonstrations and traffic light mini-projects. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F}$ | Current limiting resistor (active-HIGH) | Ohms (Ω) | | 2 | $R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F}$ | Current limiting resistor (active-LOW) — same math | Ohms (Ω) | | 3 | $I_F = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{R}$ | Forward current through LED | Amperes (A) | | 4 | $P_R = I_F^2 \times R$ | Power dissipated by resistor | Watts (W) | | 5 | $P_{LED} = V_F \times I_F$ | Power dissipated by LED | Watts (W) | | 6 | $P_{total} = (V_{CC} - V_F) \times I_F$ | Total power consumed by LED branch | Watts (W) | | 7 | $R_{min} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_{F(max)}}$ | Minimum safe resistor (boundary) | Ohms (Ω) | | 8 | $I_{port(total)} \le 80\,\text{mA}$ | 8051 port current limit (boundary) | mA | | 9 | $I_{pin(max)} \approx 10\,\text{mA}$ | Per-pin sourcing limit (8051) | mA | | 10 | $I_{pin(max)} \approx 20\,\text{mA}$ | Per-pin sinking limit (8051) | mA | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not just academic — they are used *every day* in PCB design houses in Bangalore, Kochi, and Trivandrum tech parks when an embedded engineer prototypes a new IoT device. Selecting the wrong resistor is the **#1 cause of "magic smoke" releases** in entry-level embedded projects. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility LED interfacing concepts are foundational for: - **Status indicators** (power ON, fault, ready). - **Seven-segment displays** (each segment is essentially an LED). - **Traffic light controllers** (a KTU-favorite mini-project). - **Optical isolation circuits** in industrial PLCs. - **Heartbeat / "device alive" indicators** in IoT edge devices. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Current Limiting Resistor We derive the value of R required to limit the LED current to a safe value $I_F$ given supply voltage $V_{CC}$ and LED forward voltage $V_F$. ### Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) The series circuit contains three elements: the supply $V_{CC}$, the resistor R, and the LED. The sum of voltage drops must equal $V_{CC}$. $$ V_{CC} = V_R + V_F $$ ### Step 2 — Isolate the Resistor Voltage Drop Subtract $V_F$ from both sides to find the voltage that must be dropped across R. $$ V_R = V_{CC} - V_F $$ ### Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law Across the Resistor By Ohm's Law, the current through a resistor equals the voltage across it divided by its resistance. $$ I_F = \frac{V_R}{R} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{R} $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for R Rearrange the equation to make R the subject. $$ R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F} $$ This is the **canonical LED current-limiting resistor formula** used universally in electronics. ### Step 5 — Power Rating of the Resistor The resistor must dissipate $P_R = I_F^2 \cdot R$ safely. Standard resistors come in 1/8 W, 1/4 W, 1/2 W, etc. A design must select a resistor with a power rating at least **2× the calculated dissipation** (safety factor of 2). $$ P_{R(\text{design})} = 2 \times I_F^2 \times R $$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Standard > **Problem (KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 style):** > A red LED ($V_F = 2.0\,\text{V}$, $I_F = 10\,\text{mA}$) is interfaced to an 8051 pin in **active-LOW** configuration with $V_{CC} = 5\,\text{V}$.
