Calculate: > (a) The current limiting resistor value. > (b) The power dissipated by the resistor. > (c) Verify whether the chosen configuration respects the 8051 sinking current limit. ### Part (a) — Resistor Value We apply the formula derived in Step 4. $$ R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F} $$ Substitute $V_{CC} = 5\,\text{V}$, $V_F = 2.0\,\text{V}$, $I_F = 10\,\text{mA} = 0.010\,\text{A}$. $$ R = \frac{5 - 2.0}{0.010} $$ $$ R = \frac{3.0}{0.010} $$ $$ R = 300\,\Omega $$ **Closest standard E12 value: 330 Ω.** Using 330 Ω gives a slightly lower current, which is safer. ### Part (b) — Power Dissipated by Resistor $$ P_R = I_F^2 \times R $$ $$ P_R = (0.010)^2 \times 300 $$ $$ P_R = 0.0001 \times 300 $$ $$ P_R = 0.03\,\text{W} = 30\,\text{mW} $$ **Design choice:** A **1/8 W (125 mW) resistor** is sufficient (and is the most economical choice). A 1/4 W resistor is over-engineered but acceptable. ### Part (c) — Verify Sinking Limit The 8051 pin sinks the LED current. The 8051's per-pin sinking limit is approximately **20 mA**. $$ I_F = 10\,\text{mA} \le 20\,\text{mA} \quad \checkmark $$ Also, the port limit (sum of all 8 pins) is ~80 mA. If only 1 LED is ON, the constraint is trivially satisfied. **Design is safe.** ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Eight-LED Port Interface A classic KTU problem: **Connect 8 LEDs to Port P1 of an 8051 in active-LOW configuration** and write a program to toggle them. | Bit Position | Pin | LED State when Bit = 0 | LED State when Bit = 1 | |---|---|---|---| | P1.0 | Pin 1 | ON | OFF | | P1.1 | Pin 2 | ON | OFF | | P1.2 | Pin 3 | ON | OFF | | P1.3 | Pin 4 | ON | OFF | | P1.4 | Pin 5 | ON | OFF | | P1.5 | Pin 6 | ON | OFF | | P1.6 | Pin 7 | ON | OFF | | P1.7 | Pin 8 | ON | OFF | **Firmware pattern (8051 Assembly):** - To turn **all LEDs ON** → `MOV P1, #00H` (send 0x00 to Port 1). - To turn **all LEDs OFF** → `MOV P1, #0FFH` (send 0xFF to Port 1). - To turn **only P1.0 LED ON** → `CLR P1.0` (clear bit 0 of P1). > [!NOTE] > **Bit-addressable ports in 8051:** P1 is bit-addressable, meaning individual bits can be set/cleared with single-cycle instructions (`SETB`, `CLR`). This is one of the architectural advantages of the 8051 over, say, the 8085 (where the entire accumulator would need to be manipulated). ## 3.4 Boundary Condition Analysis | Boundary | Limit | Consequence of Violation | |---|---|---| | Per-pin source current | 10 mA | Weak LED glow / pin damage | | Per-pin sink current | 20 mA | Pin overheating | | Port total current | 80 mA | Latch-up of MCU | | LED max forward current | 30 mA (typ.) | LED burnout (thermal) | | LED reverse breakdown | 5 V (typ.) | LED failure if reverse-biased | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students connect LEDs *without a resistor* during viva demonstrations. This is the single most common reason for a **practical-lab mark deduction**. Always — *always* — include a current-limiting resistor. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Single LED Interfacing System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MCU["8051 Microcontroller Unit"] CPU["CPU Core"] SFR["SFR: Port 1 Register
Bit Addressable"] DRV["Output Driver Stage
Totem Pole Transistor Pair"] end subgraph EXT["External Hardware"] R["Current Limiting
Resistor R"] LED["LED
Vf = 2V, If = 10mA"] GND["Ground Reference 0V"] end CPU -->|"Writes SFR bit"| SFR SFR -->|"Logic level"| DRV DRV -->|"Pin voltage 0V or 5V"| R R -->|"Drops VCC - Vf"| LED LED --> GND ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Active-HIGH vs Active-LOW Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph AH["Active-HIGH Topology"] AH1["VCC 5V"] --> AH2["Resistor R"] AH2 --> AH3["LED Anode A"] AH3 --> AH4["LED Cathode K"] AH4 --> AHP["MCU Pin P1.0
Drive HIGH to ON"] end subgraph AL["Active-LOW Topology"] AL1["VCC 5V"] --> AL2["Resistor R"] AL2 --> AL3["LED Anode A"] AL3 --> AL4["LED Cathode K"] AL4 --> ALP["MCU Pin P1.0
Drive LOW to ON"] end ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Firmware Logic for LED Toggle ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Program Entry"] --> B["Initialize Stack Pointer
MOV SP, 70H"] B --> C["Set P1.0 as Output
(default for 8051)"] C --> D["Drive Pin LOW
CLR P1.0"] D --> E["Software Delay Loop
approx 500 ms"] E --> F["Drive Pin HIGH
SETB P1.0"] F --> G["Software Delay Loop
approx 500 ms"] G --> D ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — 8-LED Bar Display Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["8051 Port P1
8-bit SFR"] --> B0["Bit 0 → LED D0 + R0"] P1 --> B1["Bit 1 → LED D1 + R1"] P1 --> B2["Bit 2 → LED D2 + R2"] P1 --> B3["Bit 3 → LED D3 + R3"] P1 --> B4["Bit 4 → LED D4 + R4"] P1 --> B5["Bit 5 → LED D5 + R5"] P1 --> B6["Bit 6 → LED D6 + R6"] P1 --> B7["Bit 7 → LED D7 + R7"] B0 --> G["Common Ground"] B1 --> G B2 --> G B3 --> G B4 --> G B5 --> G B6 --> G B7 --> G ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid State Diagram — LED Behavior with Bit Pattern ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> AllOFF: Power On AllOFF: All 8 LEDs OFF
P1 = 0xFF AllOFF --> AllON: MOV P1, 00H AllON: All 8 LEDs ON
P1 = 0x00 AllON --> Alternate1: MOV P1, 0xAA Alternate1: Even LEDs ON
P1 = 10101010b Alternate1 --> Alternate2: MOV P1, 0x55 Alternate2: Odd LEDs ON
P1 = 01010101b Alternate2 --> AllOFF: MOV P1, 0xFF ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the role of a current limiting resistor in an LED interfacing circuit. What happens if it is omitted?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A current limiting resistor is essential because an LED is a **current-driven device** with a non-linear I-V curve. Beyond its forward voltage $V_F$, the LED's resistance drops sharply, causing current to rise uncontrollably. The resistor limits this current to a safe value using the formula $R = (V_{CC} - V_F)/I_F$. If omitted, the LED draws excessive current, overheats, and **burns out within seconds**, often permanently damaging the microcontroller pin as well. > [!NOTE] > **Marking Note:** Examiners award **1 mark** for stating the formula, **1 mark** for the role of current limiting, and **1 mark** for the consequence of omission. Missing the "microcontroller pin damage" point costs a mark. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate between active-HIGH and active-LOW LED interfacing configurations. State which is preferred for 8051-based designs and why.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** In **active-HIGH**, the LED turns ON when the MCU pin is driven to logic 1, and the pin **sources** current. In **active-LOW**, the LED turns ON when the pin is driven to logic 0, and the pin **sinks** current. The 8051's GPIO pin can sink approximately **20 mA** but can source only a few mA. Therefore, **active-LOW is preferred** for 8051 designs because it leverages the higher current-sinking capability, allowing brighter, safer LED operation. > [!NOTE] > **Marking Note:** Award **1 mark** each for the two definitions and **1 mark** for the justification linking to 8051's current asymmetry. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Design an LED interfacing circuit for an 8051 microcontroller in active-LOW configuration. Use $V_{CC} = 5\,\text{V}$, red LED with $V_F = 2.0\,\text{V}$, and desired $I_F = 12\,\text{mA}$. Calculate the value of the current limiting resistor and the power dissipated by it. Justify your choice of configuration. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an 8051 assembly language program to blink an LED connected to P1.0 with a visible on/off delay. Draw the connection diagram and explain register usage. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** **Step 1 — Configuration Justification:** The 8051 GPIO pin can sink up to 20 mA but source only ~10 mA. For maximum brightness and safety, **active-LOW (current sinking)** is selected. **Step 2 — Resistor Calculation:** Apply the standard formula: $$ R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F} = \frac{5 - 2.0}{0.012} = \frac{3.0}{0.012} = 250\,\Omega $$ **Step 3 — Closest Standard Value:** The E12 series nearest value is **220 Ω** (gives slightly more current, still safe) or **270 Ω** (safer). We select **R = 220 Ω** for a balance of brightness and safety. **Step 4 — Power Dissipation:** $$ P_R = I_F^2 \times R = (0.012)^2 \times 220 = 0.000144 \times 220 = 0.0317\,\text{W} \approx 32\,\text{mW} $$ A standard **1/8 W (125 mW) resistor** is more than adequate (safety factor ≈ 4). **Step 5 — Verify Safety:** Recompute $I_F$ with R = 220 Ω: $$ I_F = \frac{5 - 2.0}{220} = \frac{3.0}{220} \approx 13.6\,\text{mA} \le 20\,\text{mA} \quad \checkmark $$ **Mark allocation:** (i) Configuration + justification — 2 marks, (ii) Formula + calculation — 2 marks, (iii) Power calculation + component selection — 2 marks, (iv) Safety verification — 1 mark. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Connection Diagram (text representation):** ``` VCC (+5V) | R = 220 Ω | |---- Anode of LED LED |---- Cathode of LED | P1.0 (8051 pin — sinks current when LOW) | GND ``` **Assembly Program:** ```assembly ORG 0000H ; Program starts at reset vector LJMP MAIN ; Jump to main code ORG 0030H ; Main code begins at 0030H MAIN: CLR P1.0 ; LED ON (active-LOW: pin = 0) ACALL DELAY ; Call ~500 ms delay SETB P1.0 ; LED OFF (pin = 1) ACALL DELAY ; Call ~500 ms delay SJMP MAIN ; Repeat forever ; --- Software Delay Subroutine (approx 500 ms @ 11.0592 MHz) --- DELAY: MOV R0, #0FFH ; Outer loop counter LOOP1: MOV R1, #0FFH ; Inner loop counter LOOP2: DJNZ R1, LOOP2 ; Decrement R1, loop if not zero DJNZ R0, LOOP1 ; Decrement R0, loop if not zero RET ; Return from subroutine END ; End of program ``` **Register Usage Explanation:** - **P1.0**: Bit-addressable pin controlling the LED. - **R0, R1**: General-purpose registers used as loop counters in the delay subroutine. - **ACALL**: Absolute call to the DELAY subroutine (2-byte, 2-cycle instruction). - **SJMP**: Short jump for infinite loop (2-byte, 2-cycle instruction). - **DJNZ**: Decrement and Jump if Not Zero — used for loop control. - **Stack Pointer (SP)**: Initialized implicitly by the 8051 at reset; ACALL pushes return address onto the stack, RET pops it. **Mark allocation:** (i) Connection diagram — 2 marks, (ii) Program listing — 3 marks, (iii) Register/explanation — 2 marks. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Interface **8 LEDs** to Port P1 of an 8051 in active-LOW configuration to form a binary up-counter display. Draw the circuit and write a program to display the count sequence 00H to FFH with a 1-second delay between counts. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the difference between bit-addressable and byte-addressable ports in the 8051. Why is P1 preferred over P0 for simple LED interfacing? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Circuit (text):** Each of the 8 bits of P1 (P1.0–P1.7) connects via an individual 220 Ω resistor to the anode of an LED; the cathodes of all 8 LEDs connect to ground. The port is configured as output by default. **Program:** ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV DPTR, #COUNT ; Point DPTR to lookup table (not used here, but pattern shown) MOV A, #00H ; Initialize counter to 0 LOOP: MOV P1, A ; Send count value to Port 1 ACALL DELAY ; 1-second delay INC A ; Increment accumulator (count) SJMP LOOP ; Repeat DELAY: MOV R2, #0AH ; Outer: 10 × inner = 10 × 50 ms = 500 ms (×2 ≈ 1 s) OUTER: MOV R1, #0FFH INNER: DJNZ R1, INNER DJNZ R2, OUTER RET END ``` **Mark allocation:** (i) Circuit description — 2 marks, (ii) Program — 3 marks, (iii) Delay design — 2 marks. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Bit-addressable vs Byte-addressable:** - **Bit-addressable ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3 in 8051) allow individual bits to be set/cleared using instructions like `SETB P1.0` or `CLR P1.2` in a single cycle. - **Byte-addressable only** (e.g., ports in some other MCUs) require read-modify-write operations: read the whole port, mask the bit, modify, write back. **Why P1 is preferred over P0:** 1. **P0 is open-drain** — it requires external pull-up resistors to output a logic HIGH. Without pull-ups, P0 cannot reliably drive an LED's anode HIGH in active-HIGH mode. 2. **P1 has internal pull-ups** — it can drive a HIGH output directly, simplifying the circuit. 3. **P1 is dedicated for general I/O** — unlike P2 (used for external memory high-byte address) or P3 (has alternate functions like RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1). 4. **P1 is purely bit-addressable** without alternate-function conflicts, making it the cleanest choice for pedagogical LED experiments. **Mark allocation:** (i) Bit/byte distinction — 3 marks, (ii) Four reasons for P1 preference — 1 mark each (4 marks total). --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks in LED Interfacing Questions:** > > 1. **Omitting the current limiting resistor in the circuit diagram** — instant **−2 marks** in design questions. > 2. **Confusing sourcing vs sinking** — writing `SETB P1.0` for an active-LOW LED (it would turn the LED **OFF**, not ON). This shows conceptual confusion and is penalized heavily. > 3. **Using `MOV P1, #01H` to "turn on" the first LED in active-LOW** — wrong; you need `MOV P1, #0FEH` (clear only bit 0). > 4. **Forgetting to specify the resistor's power rating** — examiners expect a 1/8 W or 1/4 W justification. > 5. **Not mentioning the 8051's per-pin current limits** (10 mA source / 20 mA sink) in justification answers. > 6. **Writing P0-based code without external pull-ups** in the circuit — P0 cannot drive a HIGH without pull-ups. > 7. **Calculating the resistor but not verifying that the resulting current is within the LED's $I_{F(max)}$** — incomplete engineering. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ An LED is a **current-driven** device; always use a current limiting resistor: $R = (V_{CC} - V_F)/I_F$. - ✅ **Active-LOW (sinking)** is the preferred 8051 topology because the 8051 sinks more current (20 mA) than it sources (10 mA). - ✅ **P1 is the preferred port** for LED interfacing — internally pulled-up, no alternate functions, bit-addressable. - ✅ **P0 is open-drain** and unsuitable for direct LED drive without external pull-ups. - ✅ A safe standard design point: $V_{CC} = 5\,\text{V}$, $V_F = 2\,\text{V}$, $I_F = 10\,\text{mA}$, $R = 300\,\Omega$ (use 330 Ω standard). - ✅ 8051 assembly instructions for LED control: `SETB P1.x` (turn OFF in active-LOW), `CLR P1.x` (turn ON in active-LOW), `MOV P1, #data` (write to whole port). - ✅ 8051 ports P0–P3 are **bit-addressable** — single-bit instructions like `SETB` and `CLR` work in **1 machine cycle**. - ✅ Power dissipated by the resistor is typically <50 mW for single-LED designs — a **1/8 W resistor** is the industry default. - ✅ For 8-LED binary counters, sending 0x00 turns **all** LEDs ON (active-LOW); sending 0xFF turns all OFF. - ✅ Always verify: per-pin current ≤ 20 mA sink, port total current ≤ 80 mA. - ✅ LED reverse breakdown voltage is typically 5 V — do **not** reverse-bias an LED across the full supply without protection. - ✅ For PWM-based brightness control, the LED pin must support timer output (covered in Module 3/4 of PBRUT404).

topics.title: LM35 Temperature Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LM35 Temperature Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **LM35** is a precision **centigrade (Celsius) temperature sensor** integrated circuit manufactured by **Texas Instruments (TI)**. It is a solid-state, three-terminal analog transducer whose output voltage is **linearly proportional to the ambient (Celsius) temperature**, with a calibrated scale factor of **10 mV/°C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Sensors & Interfacing):**
> The LM35 belongs to the category of *active analog sensors*. Unlike thermistors (resistive sensors) or thermocouples (requires cold-junction compensation), the LM35 directly produces a low-impedance voltage output that is **internally calibrated** in Celsius, eliminating the need for external signal conditioning or linearization circuitry.

The LM35 belongs to the family of **semiconductor bandgap temperature sensors**. It uses the well-known principle that the **base-emitter voltage ($V_{BE}$) of a bipolar junction transistor (BJT)** is a linear function of absolute temperature. By subtracting two $V_{BE}$ drops operating at different current densities, the bandgap reference circuit produces a voltage proportional to **degrees Celsius**, not Kelvin — which is why it does not require any offset subtraction (unlike the LM335).

### Key Standard Specifications (as per TI Datasheet)

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scale Factor | $S$ | **10** | mV/°C |
| Measurement Range | $T_{range}$ | **−55 to +150** | °C |
| Accuracy (typical) | $\Delta T$ | **±0.5** | °C (at 25°C) |
| Supply Voltage | $V_S$ | **4 to 30** | V |
| Current Drain | $I_S$ | **< 60** | µA |
| Self-Heating | – | **0.08** | °C (still air) |
| Output Impedance | $Z_{out}$ | **0.5** | Ω (typical, 1 mA load) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Value to Memorize:**
> $$\boxed{S = 10\ \text{mV/°C}}$$
> This single constant is the backbone of every LM35 problem you will see in a KTU exam.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **clinical mercury thermometer** that doctors use. As the patient's temperature rises, the mercury column rises in a *straight, uniform* way — for every 1°C rise, the mercury rises by the same height. The LM35 behaves in a *virtually identical* manner, except instead of expanding mercury, it **outputs an electrical voltage** that rises uniformly with temperature.

**The "10 mV/°C" rule of thumb:**
- A temperature of **0°C** → Output = **0 mV** (very useful! No offset)
- A temperature of **25°C** (room temperature) → Output = **250 mV** (0.25 V)
- A temperature of **100°C** (boiling water) → Output = **1000 mV** (1.00 V)
- A temperature of **−10°C** (below freezing) → Output = **−100 mV** (you need a negative supply to read this!)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Continued):**
> Think of the LM35 as a "voltage translator." Its *input* is temperature in °C, and its *output* is a tiny DC voltage. The "translation rate" is fixed at 10 mV per °C. So the microcontroller's job is just the reverse translation: read the voltage → divide by 10 mV → recover the original °C value.

**Why is this important for KTU?**
In Part B questions, you are *expected* to know that:
1. The sensor does **not** require offset calibration (unlike LM335 / LM34).
2. The output is **0 V at 0 °C** — a very important "no offset" property.
3. To measure **negative temperatures**, the LM35's **GND pin must be biased to a negative voltage** (typically −5 V).

---

## 1.3 Pin Configuration (TO-92 / TO-220 Packages)

The LM35 is commonly available in **TO-92** (small, like a transistor) and **TO-220** (larger, with a heat-sink tab) packages.

```
        ___________
       |  \_/_/   |        Pin 1  →  Vcc   (Supply, +4 V to +30 V)
       | LM35    |         Pin 2  →  Vout  (Analog output, 10 mV/°C)
       |__________|         Pin 3  →  GND   (Ground, or −Vs for negative T)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin Identification Tip (for exams):**
> When looking at the **flat (printed) side** of the TO-92 package with the leads pointing *downward*, the pins from **left to right** are: **Vcc, Vout, GND**. This left-to-right order is the most common source of "wiring mistakes" students make in lab vivas.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Physics Behind the Sensor

The LM35's precision stems from the **bandgap reference architecture**, which exploits two physical principles inside silicon:

**Step 1 — The Diode Equation Behavior:**
The voltage across a forward-biased PN junction (base-emitter of a BJT) follows the **Shockley diode equation**:
$$V_{BE} = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{I_C}{I_S}\right)$$
where $k$ is Boltzmann's constant, $q$ is the electron charge, and $I_S$ is the saturation current. Notice that $V_{BE}$ has a *negative* temperature coefficient (CTAT — Complementary To Absolute Temperature) of about −2 mV/°C.

**Step 2 — The PTAT (Proportional To Absolute Temperature) Trick:**
If we run two BJTs at different current densities ($I_{C1} \ne I_{C2}$), the **difference** $\Delta V_{BE}$ becomes purely *proportional to absolute temperature* (PTAT):
$$\Delta V_{BE} = V_{BE1} - V_{BE2} = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{C1}\cdot A_{E2}}{I_{C2}\cdot A_{E1}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Bandgap Summation:**
The internal circuitry adds a PTAT voltage (slope ∝ T) to a scaled CTAT voltage such that at **0 K**, the output would equal the **silicon bandgap energy** (≈ 1.2 V). The clever design subtracts a constant so that the output is **0 V at 0 °C (273.15 K)** — this is why the LM35 outputs in *Celsius*, not Kelvin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" matters for KTU:**
> Understanding that LM35 uses bandgap reference explains *why* it has low self-heating (only ~60 µA current) and *why* it is more accurate than thermistors, which use resistance changes that are highly *non-linear* and require lookup tables.

---

## 2.2 Interfacing with a Microcontroller (8051 / ARM / PIC)

The LM35 is **read-only** and **analog**. It cannot be connected to a digital GPIO pin directly. The standard interface chain is:

**LM35 → Low-Pass Filter (optional) → ADC → Microcontroller → Display/UART**

### 2.2.1 Hardware Connection

```
                +5 V
                 │
                 ▼
              [ 10 kΩ ]  (Optional pull-up, ensures linear range)
                 │
                 │
                 ●──────────► To ADC Channel (e.g., ADC0804 IN+ or MCU ADC0)
                 │
              ┌──┴──┐
              │LM35 │
              │  V  │
              └──┬──┘
                 │
                GND
```

### 2.2.2 Reading Negative Temperatures (Important KTU Pitfall)

The LM35's output can go *negative* for sub-zero temperatures. With a single-supply ADC (0 to $V_{ref}$), the **negative half of the output range is invisible** to the converter.

**Common Solutions:**
1. **Pull-down resistor to −5 V** on the GND pin of the LM35 (shifts output up by +0.5 V).
2. **Use a rail-to-rail, bipolar ADC** such as ADS1115.
3. **Use a summing amplifier** with a +V_ref/2 offset, then subtract the offset in software.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:**
> Students often assume the LM35 outputs *positive voltages for all temperatures*. In reality, the sensor is **bipolar** — it outputs a negative voltage for negative Celsius values. If your KTU question asks about measuring −10 °C, you **must** discuss the negative-supply biasing technique.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = S \cdot T = 10 \cdot T$ | LM35 output voltage vs Celsius temperature | mV, °C |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{V_{out}}{S} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{10}$ | Recover temperature from voltage | °C, mV |
| 3 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{ADC}{2^n} \cdot V_{ref}$ | ADC digital-to-analog reconstruction | V |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n \cdot 10\ \text{mV}}$ | **Master Formula** (Temperature from ADC reading) | °C |
| 5 | $Resolution = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n \cdot 10\ \text{mV}}$ | Minimum temperature step the ADC can resolve | °C/LSB |
| 6 | $Q = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Quantization step (size of 1 LSB) | V |
| 7 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{10\ \text{mV}}$ | Max measurable temperature for a given $V_{ref}$ | °C |
| 8 | $P_{diss} = V_S \cdot I_S$ | Power dissipation of LM35 (self-heating related) | mW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most-tested formula in KTU:** Equation 4 — $T = \dfrac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n \cdot 10\ \text{mV}}$. You will see a *direct numerical substitution* in nearly every Part B question on this sensor.

**Where 2ⁿ and 10 mV come from:**
- $2^n$ = total ADC steps (e.g., 8-bit = 256, 10-bit = 1024, 12-bit = 4096).
- The **10 mV/°C** factor converts the recovered voltage back to a temperature.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The LM35 is the **"Arduino-starter-kit workhorse"** sensor and is used in:

- **Weather stations** (outdoor ambient temperature monitoring).
- **Battery thermal management** (laptop/phone battery packs, where >45 °C triggers throttling).
- **Industrial HVAC** (zone-level temperature control).
- **Medical equipment** (incubator temperature safety circuits).
- **Appliance protection** (microwave/oven over-temperature shutdown).
- **Cold-chain logistics** (vaccine/medicine transport monitoring, often paired with a GSM/GPS module).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Note:**
> The LM35 is gradually being replaced by **digital temperature sensors** like the **DS18B20** (1-Wire) and **DHT22** (combined T+RH), because digital sensors eliminate ADC quantization noise and provide built-in calibration. However, the LM35 is still preferred in **low-power, low-cost, and fast-response** applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master ADC → Temperature Equation

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU exams. We derive Equation 4 (the master formula) from first principles.

### Step 1 — Start with the LM35 Output Equation

The LM35 sensor produces a linear voltage proportional to the temperature in Celsius. The output voltage at the sensor's output pin (Vout pin) is given by the linear equation:

$$V_{out} = S \cdot T$$

where $S$ is the **scale factor** of the LM35. Per the manufacturer datasheet, this scale factor is **10 mV/°C**. Substituting this value into the equation:

$$V_{out} = (10\ \text{mV/°C}) \cdot T$$

### Step 2 — Express Temperature as a Function of Voltage

We need to **rearrange** the equation to solve for temperature $T$ in terms of the output voltage $V_{out}$. Dividing both sides by 10 mV:

$$T = \frac{V_{out}}{10\ \text{mV/°C}}$$

So if we can measure $V_{out}$, we can directly compute $T$. This is the **ideal case** where we have a perfect voltmeter.

### Step 3 — Introduce the ADC Quantization

In a real embedded system, we do not have a voltmeter — we have an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**. The ADC converts the analog voltage $V_{out}$ into a digital integer code, denoted as $ADC$ (also written as $N$ or $D$). The general formula for an $n$-bit ADC with reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is:

$$V_{out} = \frac{ADC}{2^n} \cdot V_{ref}$$

Here, $ADC$ is the digital count (integer between 0 and $2^n - 1$), $n$ is the number of bits, and $V_{ref}$ is the reference voltage applied to the ADC.

### Step 4 — Equate the Two Expressions for $V_{out}$

We have two equations for $V_{out}$:
1. From the sensor: $V_{out} = (10\ \text{mV/°C}) \cdot T$
2. From the ADC: $V_{out} = \dfrac{ADC}{2^n} \cdot V_{ref}$

Setting them equal:

$$(10\ \text{mV/°C}) \cdot T = \frac{ADC}{2^n} \cdot V_{ref}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for T

Dividing both sides by 10 mV/°C:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n \cdot 10\ \text{mV}}}$$

**Note on Units:** The numerator has units of ADC-codes × Volts, and the denominator has units of ADC-codes × milli-Volts. So the ratio is effectively a temperature in °C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation (always simplify units first!):**
> $$T = \frac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n \cdot 10\ \text{mV}}$$
> Example: If $V_{ref} = 5\ \text{V} = 5000\ \text{mV}$, then $T = \dfrac{ADC \cdot 5000}{2^n \cdot 10}\ \text{°C} = \dfrac{ADC \cdot 500}{2^n}\ \text{°C}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Standard KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU wording):**
> An LM35 temperature sensor is connected to a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5$ V. If the ADC output is **204**, calculate the temperature measured by the sensor. Also find the ADC reading at **75 °C**.

### Solution — Part (i): Temperature from ADC = 204

**Given:**
- ADC reading = $204$
- $n = 10$ bits → $2^{10} = 1024$
- $V_{ref} = 5\ \text{V} = 5000\ \text{mV}$
- Scale factor $S = 10\ \text{mV/°C}$

**Step 1:** Apply the master equation:

$$T = \frac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n \cdot 10\ \text{mV}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values:

$$T = \frac{204 \times 5000\ \text{mV}}{1024 \times 10\ \text{mV}}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the numerator:

$$204 \times 5000 = 1{,}020{,}000$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the denominator:

$$1024 \times 10 = 10{,}240$$

**Step 5:** Divide:

$$T = \frac{1{,}020{,}000}{10{,}240} = 99.609\ \text{°C}$$

**Final Answer (Part i):** $T \approx 99.6\ \text{°C}$

### Solution — Part (ii): ADC Reading at 75 °C

**Step 1:** Rearrange the master equation to solve for $ADC$:

$$ADC = \frac{T \cdot 2^n \cdot 10\ \text{mV}}{V_{ref}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $T = 75\ \text{°C}$:

$$ADC = \frac{75 \times 1024 \times 10\ \text{mV}}{5000\ \text{mV}}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$ADC = \frac{75 \times 10240}{5000} = \frac{768{,}000}{5000} = 153.6$$

**Step 4:** Round to nearest integer (ADC produces only integer codes):

$$ADC \approx 154$$

**Final Answer (Part ii):** $ADC \approx 154$ (integer count)

---

## 3.3 Resolution (Smallest Detectable Temperature Change)

A frequent 7-mark sub-question asks for the **resolution** of the LM35 + ADC system.

**Step 1:** Resolution in voltage is the size of **one LSB (Least Significant Bit)**:

$$Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

**Step 2:** Convert voltage resolution to temperature resolution by dividing by the LM35 scale factor $S = 10\ \text{mV/°C}$:

$$R_{temp} = \frac{Q}{S} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n \cdot 10\ \text{mV}}$$

### Numerical Example:

For $V_{ref} = 5$ V, 10-bit ADC:

$$Q = \frac{5\ \text{V}}{1024} = 4.883\ \text{mV}$$

$$R_{temp} = \frac{4.883\ \text{mV}}{10\ \text{mV/°C}} = 0.4883\ \text{°C per LSB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The LM35 + 10-bit ADC system can detect temperature changes of about **0.49 °C**. To get finer resolution (e.g., 0.1 °C), one can either use a **12-bit ADC** ($R = 0.122\ \text{°C}$) or use a **lower $V_{ref}$** (e.g., 1 V → 0.0976 °C resolution).

---

## 3.4 Maximum Measurable Temperature (Saturation Check)

The ADC saturates when $V_{out} = V_{ref}$. From $V_{out} = 10 \cdot T_{max}$:

$$T_{max} = \frac{V_{ref}}{10\ \text{mV/°C}}$$

| $V_{ref}$ | $T_{max}$ |
|---|---|
| 1.0 V | 100 °C |
| 2.56 V | 256 °C |
| 5.0 V | 500 °C |
| 10.0 V | 1000 °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** For a 5 V supply, the ADC can theoretically read up to 500 °C, but the LM35 itself saturates at **+150 °C**. So the *practical* limit is the **minimum of the two** = 150 °C.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end signal flow** of an LM35-based embedded temperature monitoring system, from the physical world to the digital display/UART output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Temperature] -->|Heat Transfer| B[LM35 Sensor]
    B -->|10 mV per degree C| C[RC Low-Pass Filter]
    C -->|Filtered Analog| D[ADC Channel]
    D -->|Digital Code| E[Microcontroller MCU]
    E -->|Lookup or Formula| F[Temperature in C]
    F --> G[LCD Display]
    F --> H[UART to PC]
    E -->|Threshold Compare| I[Threshold Exceeded]
    I -->|Trigger| J[Buzzer or Relay]
    E -->|Periodic Sample| K[Timer or Interrupt]
    K --> D
```

## 4.2 Detailed Pin Connection Diagram (LM35 ↔ AT89C52 / 8051)

This diagram shows the physical wiring when the LM35 is interfaced to an **8051-family microcontroller** with an external **ADC0804** (8-bit, parallel ADC).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Power_Supply
        V5[Plus 5V DC Supply]
        GND[Common Ground]
    end

    subgraph LM35_Sensor
        L_VCC[Pin 1: Vcc]
        L_OUT[Pin 2: Vout]
        L_GND[Pin 3: GND]
    end

    subgraph ADC0804
        A_VCC[Vcc plus Reference]
        A_GND[Digital Ground]
        A_INP[IN plus: Vin plus]
        A_INM[IN minus: Vin minus]
        A_D0[DB0]
        A_D1[DB1]
        A_D2[DB2]
        A_D3[DB3]
        A_D4[DB4]
        A_D5[DB5]
        A_D6[DB6]
        A_D7[DB7]
    end

    subgraph Microcontroller_8051
        M_P0[Port P0]
        M_P3_5[P3.5 RD]
        M_P3_4[P3.4 WR]
        M_P3_3[P3.3 INT1]
    end

    V5 --> L_VCC
    L_GND --> GND
    L_OUT --> A_INP
    A_INM --> GND
    A_VCC --> V5
    A_D0 --> M_P0
    A_D1 --> M_P0
    A_D2 --> M_P0
    A_D3 --> M_P0
    A_D4 --> M_P0
    A_D5 --> M_P0
    A_D6 --> M_P0
    A_D7 --> M_P0
    M_P3_4 --> A_WR
    M_P3_5 --> A_RD
```

## 4.3 Firmware Flowchart — Temperature Acquisition Routine

This flowchart shows the typical **software state machine** a KTU student would draw for a viva on this sensor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize ADC and UART] --> B[Set ADC Channel 0]
    B --> C[Start Conversion]
    C --> D[Wait for EOC Flag]
    D --> E[Read ADC Value]
    E --> F[Apply Master Formula]
    F --> G[Convert to ASCII]
    G --> H[Send to UART or LCD]
    H --> I{Temperature > Threshold}
    I -->|Yes| J[Turn ON Alarm]
    I -->|No| K[Keep Alarm OFF]
    J --> L[Wait 1 second]
    K --> L
    L --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Flowchart (for viva):**
> - The "wait 1 second" loop controls the **sampling rate** of the system. In lab viva, you may be asked: *"Why 1 second?"* — Answer: LM35 has a thermal time constant in still air of ~10–30 seconds, so sampling faster than 1 Hz gives little new information.
> - The **"EOC (End Of Conversion)" flag** is critical for synchronous ADC reading. Polling it avoids race conditions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three specifications of the LM35 temperature sensor.**

**Model Answer:**

The three key specifications of the LM35 temperature sensor are:

1. **Scale factor (Sensitivity):** The output voltage of the LM35 is linearly proportional to the Celsius temperature, with a scale factor of **10 mV/°C**.

2. **Operating Range:** The sensor can measure temperatures from **−55 °C to +150 °C**, covering a wide range of industrial and domestic applications.

3. **Accuracy:** The LM35 has a typical accuracy of **±0.5 °C at room temperature (25 °C)**, and is calibrated directly in Celsius, requiring no external trimming.

> [!NOTE]
> **Extra specification (for full marks):** Operating supply voltage is 4 V to 30 V, with very low self-heating (~0.08 °C in still air) due to low current drain (< 60 µA).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why does the LM35 require a negative bias on its GND pin when measuring sub-zero temperatures?**

**Model Answer:**

The LM35 is a **bipolar sensor** — its output voltage $V_{out} = 10\ \text{mV/°C} \cdot T$ can be **negative for negative Celsius values** (e.g., at −10 °C, $V_{out} = −100\ \text{mV}$). However, standard microcontrollers and ADCs operate on a **single positive supply** (e.g., 0 V to 5 V) and cannot directly read negative voltages.

To overcome this, the **GND pin of the LM35 is biased to a negative voltage** (typically −5 V). This shifts the entire output *upwards* by 0.5 V, making sure the output remains in the positive ADC range even for negative Celsius values. In software, this 0.5 V offset is subtracted from the ADC reading to recover the true temperature.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — Students often write $V_{ref} = 5$ in the formula without converting to mV. You **must** convert to mV (5000 mV) or the equation $T = \frac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n \cdot 10\ \text{mV}}$ will give a wrong unitless answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the "0 V at 0 °C" property** — Many students wrongly add an offset of 273.15 (Kelvin-to-Celsius). The LM35 is *factory-calibrated* in Celsius; no offset is needed.
> 3. **ADC integer rounding** — Always round the final $ADC$ value to the nearest integer; fractional ADC codes do not exist.
> 4. **Not mentioning the negative-temperature biasing trick** — A common 2-mark deduction in viva if the question hints at measuring sub-zero values.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *ADC Calculation Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of an LM35 temperature sensor to an 8051 microcontroller through an 8-bit ADC. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An LM35 sensor is connected to a 10-bit ADC with a reference voltage of 5 V. The ADC gives a digital output of **307**.
    **(i)** Find the temperature measured by the sensor. **(3 Marks)**
    **(ii)** What is the temperature resolution (°C/LSB) of this system? **(2 Marks)**
    **(iii)** What will be the ADC output when the temperature is **60 °C**? **(2 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Block Diagram:**

```
  LM35 Sensor  →  Amplifier (optional)  →  8-bit ADC (e.g., ADC0804)
                                              ↓ (8-bit parallel)
                                          8051 Microcontroller
                                              ↓
                                       LCD / UART Display
```

**Explanation:**

1. The LM35 produces an analog voltage of 10 mV/°C, which is **directly fed** to the analog input of an 8-bit ADC such as the **ADC0804**.

2. The **ADC0804** uses a 5 V reference (which also serves as its $V_{ref}$). The ADC continuously samples the input voltage and converts it to an 8-bit digital code (0–255) using successive approximation.

3. The 8-bit output of the ADC is connected to **Port P0** of the 8051, which serves as the data bus.

4. The control signals **$\overline{RD}$** (read) and **$\overline{WR}$** (write) of the ADC are connected to the 8051's **P3.5** and **P3.4** pins respectively. The 8051 issues a low pulse on WR to start conversion and reads the digital data when the **INTR (End of Conversion)** pin goes low.

5. The 8051 reads the digital value into an accumulator register and applies the temperature conversion formula in software.

6. The computed temperature is then sent to a **16x2 LCD** (via Port P2) or transmitted over **UART** (TX pin P3.1) to a PC for display.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $n = 10$, $V_{ref} = 5\ \text{V} = 5000\ \text{mV}$, $S = 10\ \text{mV/°C}$

**Part (i):** Temperature from ADC = 307

**Step 1 —** Apply the master formula:

$$T = \frac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n \cdot 10\ \text{mV}}$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute values:

$$T = \frac{307 \times 5000}{1024 \times 10}$$

**Step 3 —** Compute numerator and denominator:

$$T = \frac{1{,}535{,}000}{10{,}240}$$

**Step 4 —** Final result:

$$\boxed{T = 149.90\ \text{°C} \approx 149.9\ \text{°C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **very close to the upper limit** of the LM35's range (150 °C), showing the sensor is operating near its maximum measurable temperature.

**Part (ii):** Temperature resolution (°C/LSB)

**Step 1 —** Voltage resolution (1 LSB):

$$Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5\ \text{V}}{1024} = 4.883\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 2 —** Convert to temperature:

$$R_{temp} = \frac{Q}{S} = \frac{4.883\ \text{mV}}{10\ \text{mV/°C}}$$

$$\boxed{R_{temp} = 0.4883\ \text{°C/LSB}}$$

**Part (iii):** ADC output at 60 °C

**Step 1 —** Rearrange the master formula:

$$ADC = \frac{T \cdot 2^n \cdot 10\ \text{mV}}{V_{ref}}$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute $T = 60$:

$$ADC = \frac{60 \times 1024 \times 10}{5000}$$

**Step 3 —** Simplify:

$$ADC = \frac{614{,}400}{5000} = 122.88$$

**Step 4 —** Round to nearest integer:

$$\boxed{ADC \approx 123}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Sensor Internals & Negative-Temperature Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**(a)** Describe the internal working principle of the LM35 sensor. Why is it called a **bandgap temperature sensor**? Why does it produce **0 V at 0 °C** and not at 0 K? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a circuit to measure temperatures from **−20 °C to +100 °C** using an LM35 and a single-supply 5 V ADC. Show the calculations for the required biasing and explain how the temperature is recovered in software. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Internal Working Principle:**

The LM35 uses a **bandgap reference circuit** built using **bipolar junction transistors (BJTs)**. The key idea is:

1. The base-emitter voltage $V_{BE}$ of a BJT has a **negative temperature coefficient** (CTAT) of about −2 mV/°C, governed by the Shockley equation:
$$V_{BE} = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{I_C}{I_S}\right)$$

2. The **difference** $\Delta V_{BE}$ between two BJTs operated at *different current densities* is **proportional to absolute temperature** (PTAT):
$$\Delta V_{BE} = \frac{kT}{q}\ln(N)$$
where $N$ is the ratio of current densities (typically a fixed integer like 8 or 10).

3. The internal circuit **sums** a PTAT voltage and a scaled CTAT voltage in such a way that the resulting output is:
$$V_{out} = \alpha \cdot \Delta V_{BE} - \beta \cdot V_{BE} = K \cdot T(^{\circ}\text{C})$$

4. The constants $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are chosen such that the *intercept* (where $V_{out} = 0$) occurs at **0 °C = 273.15 K**, not at 0 K.

**Why "Bandgap"?**

The name "bandgap" comes from the fact that the underlying physics involves the **silicon bandgap voltage** $E_g \approx 1.205\ \text{V}$ at 0 K. The classical Brokaw/Widlar bandgap design sets the output to be a weighted sum that extrapolates to $E_g/q$ at absolute zero, then subtracts a constant so the output is calibrated in Celsius.

**Why 0 V at 0 °C and not 0 K?**

- The sensor is *designed* for human and industrial use, where Celsius is the standard.
- The output is calibrated in Celsius, so it crosses **0 V at 0 °C** by design.
- This **eliminates the need for any software offset subtraction**, unlike the LM335 (which outputs in Kelvin and requires a 273.15 subtraction).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Design Goal:** Measure −20 °C to +100 °C using a 5 V single-supply ADC.

**Step 1 — Identify the Output Voltage Range:**

The LM35 output is $V_{out} = 10\ \text{mV/°C} \cdot T$.

- At $T = -20\ \text{°C}$: $V_{out} = -200\ \text{mV} = -0.2\ \text{V}$ (negative!)
- At $T = +100\ \text{°C}$: $V_{out} = +1000\ \text{mV} = +1.0\ \text{V}$

**Step 2 — Address the Negative Voltage Problem:**

We need to **shift the −0.2 V to +1.0 V range** into the 0 V to 5 V range of the ADC. This is done by **biasing the GND pin of the LM35 to a negative voltage**, e.g., $V_{GND} = -0.5\ \text{V}$.

**Why −0.5 V?** So that at the lowest expected temperature (−20 °C), the output is:
$$V_{out}^{biased} = V_{GND} + (10\ \text{mV/°C} \cdot T) = -0.5 + (-0.2) = -0.7\ \text{V} \quad \text{(STILL NEGATIVE!)}$$

That is incorrect. We need to choose a bias such that the lowest possible output is ≥ 0 V. Let $V_{bias} = V_{GND}$ (biasing the GND pin to a *positive* voltage relative to the system ground is also valid — this *lifts* the output).

**Revised Step 2 — Use a Positive Bias (simpler, no negative supply needed):**

We can use a **resistor divider** or a **voltage reference** to bias the LM35's GND pin to **+0.5 V** above the system ground:

$$V_{out}^{at\ ADC} = V_{bias} + 10\ \text{mV/°C} \cdot T$$

- At $T = -20\ \text{°C}$: $V_{out} = 0.5 + (-0.2) = +0.3\ \text{V}$ ✓ (positive!)
- At $T = +100\ \text{°C}$: $V_{out} = 0.5 + 1.0 = +1.5\ \text{V}$ ✓ (within 0–5 V)

**Step 3 — Software Recovery:**

The ADC reading will give us the *biased* voltage. The software must:
1. Convert the ADC value to voltage: $V_{biased} = \dfrac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n}$
2. Subtract the bias: $V_{actual} = V_{biased} - V_{bias} = V_{biased} - 0.5\ \text{V}$
3. Compute temperature: $T = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{10\ \text{mV/°C}}$

**Step 4 — Circuit Summary:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| LM35 | Temperature-to-voltage transducer |
| R1 (10 kΩ), R2 (1.1 kΩ) | Voltage divider setting GND pin to +0.5 V |
| Op-amp (optional, e.g., LM358) | Buffer / level-shifter |
| 10-bit ADC | Converts 0.3 V – 1.5 V into 0–1023 code |
| 8051 MCU | Runs conversion formula and displays °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam-Worthy Sentence:**
> *"By biasing the LM35's GND pin to +0.5 V and subtracting this 0.5 V offset in software, a single-supply 5 V ADC can measure sub-zero temperatures without requiring a negative supply rail."*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **LM35** = **L**inear **M**onolithic **35** (Texas Instruments series). It is a **precision Celsius** temperature sensor with a **scale factor of 10 mV/°C**.
- The **output is 0 V at 0 °C** — this is a major advantage over LM335 (Kelvin) and LM34 (Fahrenheit).
- The operating range is **−55 °C to +150 °C**; supply is **4 V to 30 V**.
- **Three pins** (TO-92 package): Vcc, Vout, GND. (Flat side, leads down, **left to right** = Vcc, Vout, GND.)
- **Master formula:** $\boxed{T = \dfrac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n \cdot 10\ \text{mV}}}$ — memorize this!
- **Resolution:** $R_{temp} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n \cdot 10\ \text{mV}}$ °C/LSB.
- **Maximum measurable T** (sensor-side) = **+150 °C**; the **ADC-side** max is $V_{ref}/(10\ \text{mV/°C})$. The *true* max is the **minimum** of these two.
- For **negative temperatures**, the **GND pin must be biased** (typically to +0.5 V or −5 V) and the offset subtracted in software.
- The **self-heating** is minimal (0.08 °C in still air) because the current drain is only ~60 µA.
- The sensor uses a **bandgap reference** built from BJTs, exploiting the PTAT (ΔV_BE) and CTAT (V_BE) properties of silicon.
- **Common KTU applications:** weather stations, battery thermal protection, HVAC, medical incubators, cold-chain monitoring.
- **LM35 vs DS18B20:** LM35 is *analog* (needs ADC); DS18B20 is *digital* (1-Wire interface, no ADC needed). LM35 has faster response; DS18B20 has better noise immunity.
- **Watch out for unit mismatches in the master formula** — always convert $V_{ref}$ to **mV** before substituting.
- **Round final ADC counts** to the nearest integer.
- The **scale factor 10 mV/°C** must be in **mV**, not V, when using the master formula as written above.
- **No offset subtraction needed for positive-temperature measurements** — this is the LM35's biggest exam-friendly property.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 7 Segment LED (Embedded C Programming)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 7 Segment LED (Embedded C Programming)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seven-Segment LED Display:** A seven-segment display (SSD) is an electronic display device consisting of **eight Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs)** arranged in a stylized "figure-8" pattern — seven linear segments (`a` through `g`) and one circular **decimal point (dp)**. By selectively energizing specific combinations of these segments, the device can render the decimal digits `0–9`, certain alphabetic characters (A–F), and a limited set of special symbols.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *PBRUT404 – Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems*, the 7-segment display is treated as a foundational **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** output peripheral, interfaced directly to a General-Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) port of the target microcontroller (typically the **8051 family** or an **ARM Cortex-M** derivative).

### 1.2 Physical Anatomy of the Display

The eight segments are physically positioned as illustrated below (clockwise convention):

- **`a`** → Top horizontal bar
- **`b`** → Upper-right vertical bar
- **`c`** → Lower-right vertical bar
- **`d`** → Bottom horizontal bar
- **`e`** → Lower-left vertical bar
- **`f`** → Upper-left vertical bar
- **`g`** → Middle horizontal bar
- **`dp`** → Decimal point (bottom-right)

### 1.3 Two Hardware Configurations

| Configuration | Common Pin Logic | Segment ON Condition | Segment OFF Condition |
|---------------|------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Common Cathode (CC)** | All cathodes tied to **GND** | Logic **HIGH (1)** | Logic **LOW (0)** |
| **Common Anode (CA)**  | All anodes tied to **Vcc**  | Logic **LOW (0)**  | Logic **HIGH (1)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** Examiners frequently ask students to differentiate between Common Anode and Common Cathode. Memorize the polarity inversion rule — **CA displays are active-LOW, CC displays are active-HIGH**.

### 1.4 Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Digital Lit-Up Window":**
> Imagine a storefront window shaped like the digit "8", divided into 7 horizontal/vertical glass panes and a small dot in the corner. Each pane has an independent light switch behind it. To display the number "2", the shopkeeper turns ON the switches for the top, upper-right, middle, lower-left, and bottom panes — exactly the 5 panes that visually outline the shape of "2". A microcontroller does the same job electronically by sending a unique **8-bit binary word** to the display, where each bit corresponds to one pane (segment).

Everyday applications: digital alarm clocks, microwave oven timers, fuel station price boards, calculator displays, elevator floor indicators, and instrumentation panels.

### 1.5 Relevance in the KTU Syllabus

This topic directly satisfies **Course Outcome CO2** of Module 2: *"Interface I/O peripherals such as LEDs, switches, keypads, and 7-segment displays to the microcontroller using Embedded C."* The **Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive level** targeted is **L3 (Apply)** — students must write, trace, and debug Embedded C routines that drive a 7-segment display.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Segment Encoding Logic — The "Why"

Each digit/character is represented by an **8-bit codeword** (a `uint8_t`) where **bit position** maps to a **physical segment**:

$$\text{bit 7} \rightarrow dp \quad \text{bit 6} \rightarrow g \quad \text{bit 5} \rightarrow f \quad \text{bit 4} \rightarrow e \quad \text{bit 3} \rightarrow d \quad \text{bit 2} \rightarrow c \quad \text{bit 1} \rightarrow b \quad \text{bit 0} \rightarrow a$$

The bit is set to `1` (for CC) or `0` (for CA) when that segment must be **illuminated**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Generating a Segment Code

For a target digit, the encoding process follows this deterministic pipeline:

1. **Visualize the digit** on the standard "8" skeleton.
2. **Identify which of the 7 segments** light up.
3. **Mark them as `1`** in a CC table (or `0` in CA — i.e., bitwise NOT).
4. **Assign positional weights** to segments a, b, c, d, e, f, g, dp.
5. **Sum the weights** of the active segments to produce the hex codeword.

For example, the digit **"0"** lights segments **a, b, c, d, e, f** (NOT g) and NOT dp:
- Weighted sum = $2^0 + 2^1 + 2^2 + 2^3 + 2^4 + 2^5 = 1+2+4+8+16+32 = 63_{10} = \mathbf{0x3F}$

### 2.3 The Canonical Segment Lookup Table (Common Cathode)

| Digit/Char | Segments Lit (a–g)   | Binary (g f e d c b a) | Hex Code (CC) | Hex Code (CA) |
|:----------:|:---------------------|:----------------------:|:-------------:|:-------------:|
| **0**      | a, b, c, d, e, f     | `0 1 1 1 1 1 1`         | **0x3F**      | **0xC0**      |
| **1**      | b, c                 | `0 0 0 0 1 1 0`         | **0x06**      | **0xF9**      |
| **2**      | a, b, d, e, g        | `1 0 1 1 0 1 1`         | **0x5B**      | **0xA4**      |
| **3**      | a, b, c, d, g        | `1 0 0 1 1 1 1`         | **0x4F**      | **0xB0**      |
| **4**      | b, c, f, g           | `1 1 0 0 1 1 0`         | **0x66**      | **0x99**      |
| **5**      | a, c, d, f, g        | `1 1 0 1 1 0 1`         | **0x6D**      | **0x92**      |
| **6**      | a, c, d, e, f, g     | `1 1 1 1 1 0 1`         | **0x7D**      | **0x82**      |
| **7**      | a, b, c              | `0 0 0 0 1 1 1`         | **0x07**      | **0xF8**      |
| **8**      | a, b, c, d, e, f, g  | `1 1 1 1 1 1 1`         | **0x7F**      | **0x80**      |
| **9**      | a, b, c, d, f, g     | `1 1 0 1 1 1 1`         | **0x6F**      | **0x90**      |
| **A**      | a, b, c, e, f, g     | `1 1 1 0 1 1 1`         | **0x77**      | **0x88**      |
| **b**      | c, d, e, f, g        | `1 1 1 1 1 0 0`         | **0x7C**      | **0x83**      |
| **C**      | a, d, e, f           | `0 1 1 1 0 0 1`         | **0x39**      | **0xC6**      |
| **d**      | b, c, d, e, g        | `1 0 1 1 1 1 0`         | **0x5E**      | **0xA1**      |
| **E**      | a, d, e, f, g        | `1 1 1 1 0 0 1`         | **0x79**      | **0x86**      |
| **F**      | a, e, f, g           | `1 1 1 0 0 0 1`         | **0x71**      | **0x8E**      |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Rule (must memorize):** $\text{CA code} = \sim (\text{CC code}) \;\&\; 0xFF$ — the bitwise complement restricted to 8 bits.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---------------------|------------|---------------------|
| **Segment current (per LED)** | $I_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{F}}{R_{S}}$ | Limits LED current; typical $I_{LED} = 10\text{–}20 \text{ mA}$ |
| **Forward voltage drop** | $V_{F} \approx 1.8\text{ V}$ (red), $\approx 3.2\text{ V}$ (blue) | Voltage across a lit LED |
| **Series resistor** | $R_{S} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{F}}{I_{LED}}$ | E.g., $R_{S} = (5-2)/0.01 = 300\,\Omega \rightarrow 330\,\Omega$ standard |
| **Multiplex refresh rate** | $f_{refresh} \geq 50 \text{ Hz}$ per digit (60–100 Hz typical) | To avoid visible flicker on the human eye |
| **Duty cycle per digit (N digits)** | $\text{Duty} = \dfrac{1}{N} \times 100\%$ | Time-share allocation in multiplexed displays |
| **Brightness compensation** | $I_{peak} \approx N \times I_{avg}$ | Peak current must be raised in multiplexed drive |
| **Pin current budget (8051 P1)** | $I_{OL} \leq 10 \text{ mA}$ per pin, $I_{total} \leq 26 \text{ mA}$ | Critical when driving without a buffer IC like ULN2003/ULN2803 |
| **Port inversion toggle** | $\text{PORT} = \tilde{\text{PORT}} \oplus \text{0xFF}$ | Switch between CA and CC firmware |

### 2.5 Multiplexing Multi-Digit Displays

To display `N` digits using only one 8-bit segment port, engineers use **time-division multiplexing**:

- All digit segments are **bussed together** in parallel.
- A separate "digit select" port (transistor-driven) activates **one common pin at a time**.
- The firmware rapidly cycles through digits at a refresh rate $\geq 50\,\text{Hz}$.
- The human eye perceives all digits as **simultaneously lit** due to **persistence of vision (POV)**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** A 4-digit multiplexed display needs **8 segment lines + 4 digit-select lines = 12 GPIO pins** instead of 32 pins for direct drive. This is why multiplexed designs dominate cost-sensitive embedded products.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of 7-Segment Display |
|----------|----------------------------------|
| Consumer Electronics | Microwave timer, washing machine countdown |
| Automotive | Instrument cluster, gear indicator, odometer |
| Industrial Automation | PLC counters, batch counters, RPM displays |
| Medical Devices | Infusion pump rate, digital thermometer |
| Point-of-Sale | Weighing scales, billing counters |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Common-Cathode Code for Digit "0"

We now derive the segment codeword for the digit **"0"** on a **Common Cathode** display. The methodology is **exhaustive** and mirrors the examiner's expected solution.

**Step 1 — Visualize the digit "0".**
The shape of "0" is an oval made of six segments: a, b, c, d, e, f. Only the middle bar `g` is OFF.

**Step 2 — Build the truth vector.**
Assign a `1` to every lit segment and `0` to every unlit segment. The order follows the bit-position convention `dp g f e d c b a`:

| Segment | dp | g | f | e | d | c | b | a |
|---------|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| State   | 0  | 0  | 1  | 1  | 1  | 1  | 1  | 1  |

**Step 3 — Form the binary word.**
Read the row left-to-right: `0 0 1 1 1 1 1 1` (binary) = `0011 1111`.

**Step 4 — Convert to hexadecimal.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0011\,1111_{(2)} &= (0 \times 2^7) + (0 \times 2^6) + (1 \times 2^5) + (1 \times 2^4) + (1 \times 2^3) + (1 \times 2^2) + (1 \times 2^1) + (1 \times 2^0) \\
&= 0 + 0 + 32 + 16 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 \\
&= 63_{(10)} \\
&= 0x3F
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify with the 8051 Embedded C assignment.**

```c
P1 = 0x3F;   // Common Cathode: sends 0x3F to light '0'
```

### 3.2 Derivation of the Common-Anode Code for Digit "0"

The CA code is the bitwise complement of the CC code:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CA code} &= \tilde{\text{CC code}}\;\&\;0xFF \\
&= \tilde{0x3F}\;\&\;0xFF \\
&= 0xC0\;\&\;0xFF \\
&= 0xC0
\end{aligned}
$$

So `P1 = 0xC0;` lights "0" on a Common Anode display.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Multiplexed Display Refresh Time

For an `N`-digit multiplexed display, suppose the firmware holds each digit ON for $T_{ON}$ milliseconds.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{frame}} &= N \times T_{ON} \\
f_{\text{refresh}} &= \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{frame}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the flicker-free constraint $f_{refresh} \geq 50\,\text{Hz}$ for $N = 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{frame}} &\leq \dfrac{1}{50} = 0.020\,\text{s} = 20\,\text{ms} \\
T_{ON} &\leq \dfrac{T_{\text{frame}}}{N} = \dfrac{20\,\text{ms}}{4} = 5\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore each digit must be refreshed at most every **5 ms**, and the CPU spends $\frac{1}{4} = 25\%$ of its time on each digit.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Series Current-Limiting Resistor

The forward voltage of a red LED is $V_F = 2.0\,\text{V}$, the supply is $V_{CC} = 5.0\,\text{V}$, and the desired LED current is $I_{LED} = 15\,\text{mA}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_S &= \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_{LED}} \\
    &= \dfrac{5.0 - 2.0}{15 \times 10^{-3}} \\
    &= \dfrac{3.0}{0.015} \\
    &= 200\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

The nearest standard E12 value is **220 Ω** (or **330 Ω** for 10 mA designs).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Firmware Patterns for Common Operations

| Operation | Algorithm | Embedded C Snippet (8051) |
|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Display one digit on port P1** | Lookup using `switch` | `switch(d){ case 0: P1=0x3F; break; ... }` |
| **Display using lookup table** | Indexed array | `code unsigned char seg[]={0x3F,0x06,...,0x6F}; P1=seg[d];` |
| **Convert CC → CA on-the-fly** | Bitwise NOT | `P1 = ~seg[d];` (CC table reused for CA hardware) |
| **Show blank (all OFF)** | 0x00 for CC, 0xFF for CA | `P1 = 0x00;` |
| **Show decimal point** | OR with `0x80` | `P1 = seg[d] \vert 0x80;` |
| **Multiplex 4 digits** | Time-sliced loop with delay | See code in Section 3.6 |

### 3.6 Complete Embedded C Program — Multiplexed 4-Digit Counter

```c
#include <reg51.h>

/* Lookup table for Common-Cathode 0–9 */
unsigned char code SEG_TABLE[10] = {
    0x3F, 0x06, 0x5B, 0x4F, 0x66,
    0x6D, 0x7D, 0x07, 0x7F, 0x6F
};

/* Digit-select lines on P2 (active LOW) */
sbit D1 = P2^0;
sbit D2 = P2^1;
sbit D3 = P2^2;
sbit D4 = P2^3;

/* Segment bus on P1 */
#define SEG P1

void delay_ms(unsigned int t){
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i=0; i<t; i++)
        for(j=0; j<1275; j++);
}

void display_digit(unsigned char pos, unsigned char val){
    /* Turn OFF all digits first to avoid ghosting */
    P2 = 0xFF;

    /* Place segment code on bus */
    SEG = SEG_TABLE[val];

    /* Activate the selected digit */
    switch(pos){
        case 0: D1 = 0; break;   /* D1 active LOW for CA, here used as CC: invert if CA */
        case 1: D2 = 0; break;
        case 2: D3 = 0; break;
        case 3: D4 = 0; break;
    }
    delay_ms(5);
}

void main(void){
    unsigned char thousands, hundreds, tens, ones;
    unsigned int count = 0;
    while(1){
        thousands = (count / 1000) % 10;
        hundreds  = (count / 100)  % 10;
        tens      = (count / 10)   % 10;
        ones      = (count / 1)    % 10;

        display_digit(0, thousands);
        display_digit(1, hundreds);
        display_digit(2, tens);
        display_digit(3, ones);

        if(++count > 9999) count = 0;
    }
}
```

### 3.7 Logical Walkthrough of the Program

1. **Header inclusion** — `reg51.h` provides the Special Function Register (SFR) definitions for the **8051** microcontroller.
2. **`code` qualifier** — stores `SEG_TABLE` in **ROM (code memory)** to preserve precious 128-byte internal RAM.
3. **Digit selection** — `sbit` declarations map P2.0–P2.3 to symbolic names `D1`–`D4`.
4. **Ghosting prevention** — clearing `P2 = 0xFF` before writing a new segment code prevents the previous digit from showing a partial character.
5. **BCD decomposition** — the counter is split into four BCD digits using modulo-10 arithmetic.
6. **Persistence of vision** — the 5 ms dwell × 4 digits = 20 ms frame = 50 Hz refresh — flicker-free to the human eye.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Microcontroller ↔ 7-Segment Display

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["8051 CPU Core"] --> GPIO["GPIO Port P1 - Segment Bus"]
    CPU --> SEL["GPIO Port P2 - Digit Select Bus"]
    GPIO --> LATCH1["Current Limiter - 220 ohm x 8"]
    LATCH1 --> DRV1["Segment Drivers a b c d e f g dp"]
    DRV1 --> SSD["7-Segment LED Module"]
    SEL --> LATCH2["PNP Transistor Bank - PNP x 4"]
    LATCH2 --> SSD
    SSD --> RET["Visual Output - Digit Pattern"]
```

### 4.2 Internal Segment Topology of the 7-Segment LED

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP["Segment a - Top Horizontal"] --> MID["Segment g - Middle Horizontal"]
    MID --> BOT["Segment d - Bottom Horizontal"]
    TOP --> UL["Segment f - Upper Left Vertical"]
    UL --> LL["Segment e - Lower Left Vertical"]
    TOP --> UR["Segment b - Upper Right Vertical"]
    UR --> LR["Segment c - Lower Right Vertical"]
    LR --> BOT
    LL --> BOT
    DP["Segment dp - Decimal Point"] --> LR
```

### 4.3 Firmware Flowchart — Display a Single Hex Digit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Start - main function"] --> S1["Initialize P1 as Output"]
    S1 --> S2["Initialize P2 as Output"]
    S2 --> S3{"Read input value 0 to F"}
    S3 --> S4["Index SEG_TABLE value"]
    S4 --> S5["Drive P1 with segment code"]
    S5 --> S6["Activate correct digit line on P2"]
    S6 --> S7["Delay approximately 5 ms"]
    S7 --> S8{"More digits to refresh?"}
    S8 -- Yes --> S3
    S8 -- No --> S9["End of one frame - 20 ms elapsed"]
    S9 --> S0
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — Common Anode vs Common Cathode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the COMMON pin tied to GND?"} -- Yes --> CC["Common Cathode - Active HIGH"]
    Q1 -- No --> CA["Common Anode - Active LOW"]
    CC --> CC1["Set bit 1 to LIGHT a segment"]
    CC --> CC2["Set bit 0 to DARK a segment"]
    CA --> CA1["Set bit 0 to LIGHT a segment"]
    CA --> CA2["Set bit 1 to DARK a segment"]
    CC1 --> USE1["Use 0x3F for digit 0"]
    CA1 --> USE2["Use 0xC0 for digit 0"]
```

### 4.5 Multiplexing Timing Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Four-Digit Multiplex Timing - 50 Hz Refresh
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Digit 1 ON
    D1 :a1, 0, 5ms
    section Digit 2 ON
    D2 :a2, 5ms, 5ms
    section Digit 3 ON
    D3 :a3, 10ms, 5ms
    section Digit 4 ON
    D4 :a4, 15ms, 5ms
    section Frame Boundary
    NEXT :milestone, 20ms, 0
```

### 4.6 Signal Flow in a Complete Display System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Application Logic - Counter / Sensor / Clock"] --> ENC["Hex to 7-Segment Encoder"]
    ENC --> BCD["BCD Splitter - Ones Tens Hundreds Thousands"]
    BCD --> MUX["Software Multiplexer - Round Robin"]
    MUX --> BUS["8-bit Segment Bus - P1"]
    MUX --> SEL2["4-bit Digit Select - P2.0 to P2.3"]
    BUS --> SEGOUT["Physical 7-Segment LEDs"]
    SEL2 --> SEGOUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard:** All node IDs above (`CPU`, `GPIO`, `SEL`, `LATCH1`, `DRV1`, `SSD`, `RET`, `TOP`, `MID`, `BOT`, `UL`, `LL`, `UR`, `LR`, `DP`, `S0`–`S9`, `Q1`, `CC`, `CA`, `CC1`, `CC2`, `CA1`, `CA2`, `USE1`, `USE2`, `SRC`, `ENC`, `BCD`, `MUX`, `BUS`, `SEL2`, `SEGOUT`) are purely alphanumeric and avoid reserved keywords. Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold or italic tags inside double quotes.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Differentiate between Common Anode and Common Cathode seven-segment LED displays. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Common Anode (CA) | Common Cathode (CC) |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Common pin tied to | $+V_{CC}$ | GND |
| Segment is ON when | Logic **LOW (0)** is applied | Logic **HIGH (1)** is applied |
| Segment is OFF when | Logic **HIGH (1)** is applied | Logic **LOW (0)** is applied |
| Current direction | Current flows **out** of the segment pin | Current flows **into** the segment pin |
| Driving transistor | NPN (low-side switch) | PNP (high-side switch) |
| Example code for "0" | `0xC0` | `0x3F` |

**Memory Tip:** CA = *Cathode Active* (active LOW), CC = *Common Cathode = current Common*.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**List the segment codes (in hex) for displaying the digits 0–9 on a Common Cathode 7-segment display connected to Port P1 of an 8051. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```c
unsigned char code SEG_TABLE[10] = {0x3F, 0x06, 0x5B, 0x4F, 0x66,
                                    0x6D, 0x7D, 0x07, 0x7F, 0x6F};
```

The corresponding **Port P1** values are `0x3F, 0x06, 0x5B, 0x4F, 0x66, 0x6D, 0x7D, 0x07, 0x7F, 0x6F` respectively. *(Refer to the lookup table in Section 2.3 for binary breakdown.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**(a)** Explain the internal structure of a 7-segment LED display with a neat diagram. Differentiate between Common Anode and Common Cathode configurations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design an Embedded C program for the 8051 to display digits 0–9 sequentially on a Common Cathode 7-segment display connected to Port P1, with a 1-second delay between digits. Use a lookup table. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Internal structure & comparison — 7 Marks**

The 7-segment display contains **eight LEDs** (7 segments + 1 decimal point) arranged in a figure-8 pattern. The segments are labeled `a` (top), `b` (upper-right), `c` (lower-right), `d` (bottom), `e` (lower-left), `f` (upper-left), `g` (middle), and `dp` (decimal point). All anodes may be tied together (**Common Anode**) or all cathodes may be tied together (**Common Cathode**). In a CA display, the common pin is connected to $+V_{CC}$ and individual segments are lit by pulling their cathodes LOW. In a CC display, the common pin is connected to GND and segments are lit by sourcing current (HIGH) into their anodes. The active-LOW nature of CA displays makes them ideal when driven by NPN transistors on the segment side. CA displays generally consume slightly less current in OFF state and are preferred in battery-powered products.

*(Include the segment-position sketch and truth table for "0" and "8" from Section 2.3.)*

**(b) Embedded C program — 7 Marks**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

/* Common-Cathode segment codes for 0 to 9 */
unsigned char code SEG[10] = {
    0x3F, 0x06, 0x5B, 0x4F, 0x66,
    0x6D, 0x7D, 0x07, 0x7F, 0x6F
};

void delay_1sec(void){
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i=0; i<20; i++)
        for(j=0; j<32767; j++);
}

void main(void){
    unsigned char i;
    P1 = 0x00;        /* Initialize port */
    while(1){
        for(i=0; i<10; i++){
            P1 = SEG[i];   /* Send segment code */
            delay_1sec();  /* 1-second hold */
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of the code:**

1. `#include <reg51.h>` — provides the SFR definitions for the 8051.
2. `code` storage class — places the 10-byte lookup table in **ROM**, preserving internal RAM.
3. `P1 = SEG[i]` — writes the active-HIGH segment code to Port 1 (CC configuration).
4. The `for` loop inside `while(1)` produces the sequential count from 0 to 9; the outer `while(1)` makes it run forever.
5. The 1-second delay is achieved by a calibrated nested software loop (an alternative is to use **Timer 0 in Mode 1**).

---

#### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of a **4-digit multiplexed 7-segment display** to the 8051 microcontroller. Derive the minimum refresh frequency required to avoid flicker. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write an Embedded C program to display the **decimal number 1234** on a 4-digit multiplexed Common Cathode 7-segment display. Use digit-select lines P2.0–P2.3 and segment bus P1. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Interfacing and refresh-frequency derivation — 7 Marks**

In a multiplexed 4-digit display, all 8 segment lines of the four digits are tied together in parallel to **Port P1** of the 8051. The four **common pins** (one per digit) are routed individually to **P2.0, P2.1, P2.2, P2.3** through PNP transistors (for CC displays, the transistors act as high-side switches). At any instant, only **one digit** is activated by turning ON the corresponding transistor while the other three are kept OFF. The firmware writes the appropriate segment code for that digit, holds it for a few milliseconds, then moves to the next digit. This cycle repeats at a high rate.

**Derivation of minimum refresh rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For flicker-free perception, } f_{refresh} &\geq 50 \text{ Hz} \\
T_{\text{frame}} &= \dfrac{1}{f_{refresh}} = \dfrac{1}{50} = 20 \text{ ms} \\
T_{ON}(\text{each digit}) &= \dfrac{T_{\text{frame}}}{4} = \dfrac{20\,\text{ms}}{4} = 5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the firmware must dwell on each digit for **≤ 5 ms**, completing one full frame in **20 ms**.

**(b) Embedded C program for "1234" — 7 Marks**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char code SEG[10] = {
    0x3F, 0x06, 0x5B, 0x4F, 0x66,
    0x6D, 0x7D, 0x07, 0x7F, 0x6F
};

sbit D1 = P2^0;
sbit D2 = P2^1;
sbit D3 = P2^2;
sbit D4 = P2^3;

void delay_5ms(void){
    unsigned int i;
    for(i=0; i<3000; i++);
}

void show_digit(unsigned char pos, unsigned char val){
    P2 = 0xFF;                  /* Disable all digits first */
    P1 = SEG[val];              /* Place segment code */
    switch(pos){
        case 0: D1 = 0; break;  /* Activate digit 1 */
        case 1: D2 = 0; break;  /* Activate digit 2 */
        case 2: D3 = 0; break;  /* Activate digit 3 */
        case 3: D4 = 0; break;  /* Activate digit 4 */
    }
    delay_5ms();
}

void main(void){
    while(1){
        show_digit(0, 1);   /* Thousands place */
        show_digit(1, 2);   /* Hundreds place */
        show_digit(2, 3);   /* Tens place */
        show_digit(3, 4);   /* Ones place */
    }
}
```

**Logic walkthrough:**

1. `show_digit(0, 1)` activates `D1` (P2.0) and displays the segment code for `1` on the leftmost digit.
2. The three subsequent calls cycle through `D2`, `D3`, `D4`, showing `2`, `3`, `4` respectively.
3. The `while(1)` super-loop guarantees continuous refresh at ~50 Hz, producing a stable visual of `1234`.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Polarity Confusion (–2 marks):** Forgetting to invert the lookup table for Common Anode. Always state whether the code is CC or CA.
> 2. **Missing `#include <reg51.h>` (–1 mark):** Without this header, the compiler rejects `P1`, `P2`, and `sbit`.
> 3. **Forgetting the `code` keyword (–1 mark):** Storing the 10-byte table in RAM wastes precious 128-byte internal RAM and is a Board-penalized mistake.
> 4. **No anti-ghosting blanking (–1 mark):** Failing to blank the display (`P2 = 0xFF`) before writing a new segment code causes visual ghosting on multiplexed displays.
> 5. **Wrong delay calibration (–1 mark):** Using `for(i=0;i<65535;i++);` and claiming "1 second delay" without calibration loses marks — state the machine-cycle count or use a Timer.
> 6. **Confusing segments 'b' and 'd' positions in the truth table (–2 marks):** Memorize the bit-order convention **dp g f e d c b a** (MSB to LSB) — this is a 2-mark deduction if wrong.
> 7. **Mixing P1.0 and P1.7 pin assignments:** Always follow the convention that **P1.0 → segment `a`**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **7-segment display** has **8 LEDs** — 7 segments (`a`–`g`) + 1 decimal point (`dp`).
- [x] Two configurations: **Common Anode (CA, active-LOW)** and **Common Cathode (CC, active-HIGH)**.
- [x] Standard bit-order convention: **bit 7 = dp**, **bit 6 = g**, **bit 5 = f**, **bit 4 = e**, **bit 3 = d**, **bit 2 = c**, **bit 1 = b**, **bit 0 = a**.
- [x] Segment code for **"0"** is **0x3F (CC)** and **0xC0 (CA)** — the most-frequently-asked digit.
- [x] Conversion: $\text{CA code} = \tilde{\text{CC code}}\;\&\;0xFF$.
- [x] Always include `#include <reg51.h>` in any 8051 Embedded C program.
- [x] Use the `code` keyword for lookup tables to save internal RAM.
- [x] **Current-limiting resistor formula:** $R_S = (V_{CC} - V_F)/I_{LED}$ — typical value **220 Ω or 330 Ω**.
- [x] **Multiplexing** reduces pin count from `8 × N` to `8 + N`.
- [x] **Minimum refresh rate = 50 Hz**; for 4 digits, ON-time per digit = **5 ms**, frame = **20 ms**.
- [x] **Anti-ghosting:** blank the display (`P2 = 0xFF`) before writing each new segment code.
- [x] **8051 port-current limits:** $I_{OL} \leq 10$ mA per pin, $I_{total} \leq 26$ mA per port — use **ULN2003/ULN2803** driver ICs for high-current loads.
- [x] **8051 P1 port** has internal pull-ups and is the most commonly used port for segment bus interfacing.
- [x] For **hex digits A–F**, memorize: A=0x77, b=0x7C, C=0x39, d=0x5E, E=0x79, F=0x71 (all CC).
- [x] **Persistence of Vision (POV)** is the physiological principle that makes multiplexing visually stable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Definition, Features, Applications of Embedded Systems in Robotics (Examples Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded System (KTU Definition):** An embedded system is a computer system that is *purpose-built* — a combination of computer hardware, software (often firmware), and mechanical/electrical components — designed to perform a *dedicated function* within a larger mechanical or electronic system, often under real-time computational constraints.

In the specific context of **Robotics**, an **Embedded System in Robotics** is a microcontroller / microprocessor / SoC-based computing subsystem that is tightly integrated into a robot's mechanical body to **sense, decide, actuate, and communicate** continuously with deterministic timing. It is *not* a general-purpose computer; it is engineered to do *only* what the robot needs, when the robot needs it, under the robot's physical and energy constraints.

The classical working definition used in KTU valuation:

> **"A robot's brain, nerves, and reflexes — all rolled into one — is its embedded system."**

| Sub-system | Role inside the robot | Embedded Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Brain | Decision making | Microcontroller / SoC |
| Nerves | Signal transmission | Bus (SPI, I2C, CAN) |
| Senses | Data acquisition | Sensors + ADC |
| Muscles | Physical action | Motors + PWM drivers |
| Reflexes | Real-time response | RTOS / Interrupt service routines |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Human Body**
> Think of a robot as a human body:
> - The **embedded controller (MCU)** is the **brain** and **spinal cord** — it does *not* run Windows; it runs one focused job (walking, gripping, flying).
> - The **sensors** are the **five senses** (cameras = eyes, IMU = inner ear, encoders = muscle spindles).
> - The **actuators** are the **muscles**.
> - The **power system (battery + regulators)** is the **heart and lungs**.
> - The **firmware** is the **trained reflex** — a person does not "boot Windows" before withdrawing a hand from a hot surface; the embedded system similarly reacts within **microseconds** without loading a heavy OS.

A smartphone is a *general-purpose* computer you can repurpose at will. A robot's controller is a **specialist** — like a Formula 1 ECU, finely tuned to do one thing perfectly fast.

---

## 1.3 Three Pillars of any Robotic Embedded System

1. **Sensing** – acquire physical data (vision, distance, force, orientation).
2. **Processing** – compute decisions (path planning, control law, object detection).
3. **Actuation** – convert decisions into physical motion (motor torque, valve opening, LED blink).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-time constraint** is the *defining* property. If an industrial robot's controller is 5 ms late in braking, a workpiece worth thousands of rupees is ruined. Hence KTU examiners almost always award marks for the phrase *"real-time, deterministic response."*

---

## 1.4 Distinguishing Terminology (Frequently asked in KTU)

| Term | Meaning | Robotics Example |
|---|---|---|
| MCU | Microcontroller Unit – CPU + RAM + Flash + I/O on one chip | STM32, Arduino, ESP32 in a robotic arm |
| MPU | Microprocessor Unit – CPU only, needs external memory | Raspberry Pi (vision processing in drones) |
| SoC | System on Chip – MCU/MPU + GPU + DSP + connectivity | NVIDIA Jetson in autonomous mobile robots |
| RTOS | Real-Time Operating System (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) | Multi-task control in humanoid robots |
| Firmware | Software permanently etched into ROM/Flash | Motor control loop running on MCU |
| HMI | Human-Machine Interface | Teach pendant of an industrial robot |
