# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Features of Embedded Systems in Robotics

A robotic embedded system is recognised by the **eight canonical features** below. KTU examiners routinely ask students to list *any four* or *any six* — memorise them with one-line meaning.

### Feature 1 — Single-Purpose, Dedicated Functionality
The system is designed to do *one* job (or a tightly related set of jobs) — for example, balancing an inverted pendulum robot. It is **not** user-programmable in the way a laptop is. This is in contrast to general-purpose computing.

### Feature 2 — Real-Time Deterministic Response
*Determinism* means the **worst-case execution time (WCET)** of a task is *guaranteed* and *bounded*. For a robotic joint controller, the control loop must execute at **1 kHz** (every **1 ms**) without jitter.

$$\text{WCET}_{\text{task}} \leq T_{\text{deadline}}$$

### Feature 3 — Tightly Coupled Hardware–Software Co-Design
In a KUKA robot controller, the firmware, the FPGA timing logic, and the BLDC motor driver are co-designed; one cannot be swapped out independently. The hardware is *optimised* for the algorithm, not the other way round.

### Feature 4 — Constrained Resources (Power, Memory, Size)
A Mars rover (e.g., NASA's Perseverance) operates on a **Multi-Mission Radioisotope Thermoelectric Generator (MMRTG)** producing only ~**110 W** continuously. The embedded payload must compute vision and navigation within this power budget. A humanoid robot cannot carry a desktop GPU; it uses an SoC like the NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano (**7 W – 15 W**).

### Feature 5 — Reliability, Safety & Fault Tolerance
Robots operating near humans (cobots) or in life-critical roles (surgical robots) must be **ISO 13849 / IEC 61508** compliant. Embedded designs use *watchdog timers*, *redundant processors*, and *fail-safe stop circuits*.

### Feature 6 — Rich Sensor & Actuator Interfacing
- **Sensors**: IMU (6/9-axis), LiDAR, encoders, force-torque, cameras, ultrasonic, IR.
- **Actuators**: DC servos, BLDC, stepper motors, pneumatic, hydraulic, voice-coil.
- **Interfaces**: PWM, ADC, DAC, GPIO, SPI, I2C, UART, CAN, EtherCAT, RS-485.

### Feature 7 — Communication & Networking Capability
Modern robotic swarms (warehouse AMRs) communicate over **Wi-Fi 6 / 5G / ROS 2 DDS**. Industrial arms use **EtherCAT** for sub-millisecond cycle times.

### Feature 8 — User Interface (Minimal, Often Diagnostic)
Teach pendants, status LEDs, buzzer, OLED display. A vacuum robot like Roomba has a *single button*; complexity is hidden.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Application in Robotics |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Sampling IMU / encoder signals without aliasing |
| 2 | **PWM Duty Cycle** | $D = \dfrac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{period}}} \times 100\%$ | Controlling average motor voltage |
| 3 | **DC Motor Speed Equation** | $\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$ | Speed control of wheeled mobile robot |
| 4 | **Torque–Inertia Relation** | $\tau = J \ddot{\theta} + b \dot{\theta} + \tau_L$ | Dynamics of a robot joint |
| 5 | **PID Control Law** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Universal robotic joint position controller |
| 6 | **Power Consumption** | $P = V \cdot I$ | Battery budget of a drone |
| 7 | **2R Planar Forward Kinematics** | $x = l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$  $\quad y = l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | 2-DOF arm reachability |
| 8 | **Encoder Resolution** | $\theta_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N \cdot Q}$  (N = lines, Q = quadrature factor = 4) | Position feedback of servos |
| 9 | **LiDAR Range Resolution** | $\Delta r = \dfrac{c}{2 B}$  (c = speed of light, B = chirp bandwidth) | Obstacle detection in AMR |
| 10 | **RTOS Task Utilisation Bound (Liu & Layland)** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(\sqrt[n]{2} - 1)$ | Schedulability test for multi-axis robot |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vertical bar in **$\vert e(\tau) \vert$** style notation is replaced by **`\vert`** in the table above to keep the markdown table parser happy. In your answer script, you may freely use $\vert x \vert$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Why embedded systems are indispensable |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Automation** | A single car assembly line uses 200+ robots, each with ~6-axes of embedded control. |
| **Medical Robotics** | Da Vinci's 1000 Hz force feedback loop is impossible on a non-real-time OS. |
| **Defence** | Quadruped robots (e.g., Boston Dynamics Spot) operate in GPS-denied, comm-denied zones. |
| **Agriculture** | Autonomous tractors use embedded vision to within **±2 cm** row centring accuracy. |
| **Space** | Perseverance's RAD750 radiation-hardened CPU runs at **200 MHz** — slow by phone standards, but ultra-reliable under cosmic radiation. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip:** Whenever you describe an example, *always* mention **which sensor**, **which actuator**, and **which controller** are used. Generic statements like "robots use sensors" fetch **0 marks**. Specifics like "a Roomba uses an IR cliff sensor + bumper switch + wheel encoder on an ARM Cortex-M MCU" fetch full marks.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Models

This section walks through *three* foundational derivations that KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 questions regularly test. The steps are written out **exhaustively** — no algebraic transition is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Loop Position Control of a Robot Joint (PID)

**Scenario:** A single revolute joint of a robot arm is driven by a DC motor. The angular position $\theta(t)$ must track a reference $\theta_{\text{ref}}(t)$. The embedded controller (an MCU) computes the motor voltage $u(t)$ every **1 ms**.

**Step 1 — Define the error signal**

$$e(t) = \theta_{\text{ref}}(t) - \theta(t)$$

The error is the *difference* between where we want the joint to be and where it actually is.

**Step 2 — Apply the generic PID control law**

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- The **$K_p$** term provides *instant* response to current error (proportional).
- The **$K_i$** term eliminates *steady-state* error by accumulating past error.
- The **$K_d$** term damps *future* error by responding to the rate of change.

**Step 3 — Discretise for MCU implementation (MCU cannot integrate continuously)**

The MCU samples at period $T_s$. Replace integrals with *sums* and derivatives with *backward differences*:

$$u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum_{n=0}^{k} e[n] + K_d \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

This is the **discrete-time PID** that the firmware actually executes.

**Step 4 — Anti-windup modification (real engineering necessity)**

The actuator saturates — $u_{\min} \leq u[k] \leq u_{\max}$. If we naively keep summing $e[n]$, the integrator *winds up* even when the actuator is already maxed. We add a clamp:

$$u[k] = \text{sat}\!\left( K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum e[n] + K_d \frac{e[k]-e[k-1]}{T_s} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Z-domain transfer function (for ESE / B.Tech viva)**

Taking Z-transform of the discrete PID:

$$C(z) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i T_s \, z}{z-1} + \dfrac{K_d (z-1)}{T_s z}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This is what the embedded controller's interrupt service routine computes at every PWM tick.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nyquist Sampling Bound for a Robotic IMU

**Scenario:** A quadrotor's flight controller samples an IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) to estimate roll, pitch, yaw. The highest useful mechanical frequency of the airframe is $f_{\max}$. What is the *minimum* sampling rate the MCU's ADC must achieve?

**Step 1 — Identify the highest signal frequency**

For a quadrotor, the airframe vibrates at the motor RPM. A 10 000 RPM motor gives:

$$f_{\max} = \frac{10\,000}{60} \approx 166.7 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Nyquist's theorem**

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$f_s \geq 2 \times 166.7 = 333.3 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Add the engineering safety factor (1.5× to 2×)**

In practice, IMU firmware samples at **500 Hz to 8 kHz** to capture vibration harmonics and to provide bandwidth for the control loop (typically **1 kHz** inner loop, **250 Hz** outer loop).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Sample at 333 Hz and aliasing distorts the accelerometer reading; the Kalman filter on the flight controller produces garbage attitude estimates; the drone flips over. This is the *exact* mechanism behind several real-world drone crashes — a low-cost MCU's ADC was undersampled.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — 2-DOF Planar Arm Forward Kinematics

**Scenario:** A planar 2R robot arm with link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$ and joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$. The embedded controller commands $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$ and must convert this to the *end-effector* position $(x, y)$.

**Step 1 — Position of Joint 2 (i.e., end of link 1)**

$$x_1 = l_1 \cos\theta_1$$

$$y_1 = l_1 \sin\theta_1$$

**Step 2 — Position of End-Effector (relative to joint 2)**

The second link is at *absolute* angle $\theta_1 + \theta_2$ from the X-axis.

$$x_2 = x_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$y_2 = y_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $x_1, y_1$**

$$x = l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$y = l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

**Step 4 — Write in matrix form (cleaner, marks-friendly)**

$$\begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} & = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 \cos\theta_1 & l_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_1 \sin\theta_1 & l_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] & = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \phantom{-}\cos\theta_1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} l_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \phantom{-}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} l_2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation**

- The MCU reads $\theta_1, \theta_2$ from the encoders, applies the above trigonometric functions using a **CORDIC** or a lookup-table co-processor, and outputs $(x,y)$ to the higher-level planner.
- For a 6-DOF industrial arm, this becomes the **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH)** parameter matrix, but the principle is identical.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix: MCU vs MPU vs SoC in Robotics

| Parameter | MCU (e.g., STM32F4) | MPU (e.g., Raspberry Pi 4) | SoC (e.g., Jetson Orin) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clock Speed | 100 – 480 MHz | 1.5 GHz | 1.5 – 2.2 GHz |
| Power | 0.1 – 0.5 W | 3 – 7 W | 7 – 60 W |
| Determinism | High (bare-metal / RTOS) | Low (Linux jitter) | Medium (RT kernel + GPU) |
| Cost | ₹80 – ₹1500 | ₹4000 – ₹7000 | ₹25 000 – ₹1.5 Lakh |
| Best For | Motor control, sensor I/O | Vision preprocessing, gateway | Deep-learning perception |
| Example in Robot | Joint controller in KUKA | Vision hub in AMR | Autonomous driving stack in a car |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's 2024 Scheme emphasises *system-level* thinking. A *single* robot usually contains **all three** in a hierarchy: a Jetson (perception) → a Raspberry Pi (planner) → several STM32s (joint control). This hierarchical embedded architecture is a **very high-yield exam answer**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Block Diagram of a Robotic Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Supply Battery Regulator] --> B[Microcontroller SoC MCU]
    A --> C[Motor Driver H-Bridge ESC]
    B --> D[Sensor Array IMU Lidar Camera Encoder]
    B --> E[Actuator Bank DC Servo Stepper BLDC]
    C --> E
    B --> F[Communication Module WiFi CAN Bluetooth]
    B --> G[User Interface Teach Pendant OLED LEDs]
    D --> B
    F --> H[Remote Operator or Swarm Network]
    B --> I[RTOS Scheduler FreeRTOS VxWorks]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Power flows to *both* logic and driver. Sensors feed data *into* the MCU. The MCU computes and sends low-power control signals to motor drivers, which then supply high-current signals to actuators. Communication links the robot to the outside world, and the RTOS schedules the multi-axis control loop.

---

```mermaid
    end

    subgraph level0 [ACTUATION LAYER]
```

**Why this diagram matters in KTU 2024 Scheme:** Modern robotics questions specifically test whether students can *separate* perception, planning, control, and actuation. Marks are awarded for naming the four layers, and *additional* marks for naming the actual ICs in each layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

The questions below are written in the **exact phrasing style** used in the KTU 2024 Scheme question paper (Career Development Cell, APJAKTU). Mark allocation and choice structure mirror the published ESE pattern.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (Each 3 Marks)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Scheme Model]
> **Define an embedded system in the context of robotics. List any four salient features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An embedded system in robotics is a microcontroller / microprocessor-based computing subsystem that is *integrated into* the robot's mechanical body to perform dedicated, real-time control, sensing, and actuation tasks, generally under constraints of power, size, and determinism.

Four salient features:

1. **Real-time, deterministic response** — bounded worst-case execution time for control loops.
2. **Single-purpose dedicated functionality** — designed to do *one* job, not user-repurposable.
3. **Tightly coupled hardware–software** — firmware and ICs co-designed.
4. **Constrained resources** — low power, small size, limited memory.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for features — ½ mark each.)*

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Scheme Model]
> **Compare microcontroller-based and microprocessor-based embedded systems used in robotic applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Microcontroller (MCU) | Microprocessor (MPU) |
|---|---|---|
| Integration | CPU + RAM + Flash + I/O on *one* chip | CPU only; needs external memory & peripherals |
| Power | 0.1 W – 1 W | 3 W – 15 W |
| Determinism | High (bare-metal/RTOS) | Low (Linux jitter) |
| Use in robots | Joint control, sensor I/O, ESC PWM | Vision, path planning, gateway |
| Example ICs | STM32F4, ATmega328, ESP32 | Raspberry Pi 4, NXP i.MX8 |
| OS | FreeRTOS / bare-metal | Linux, ROS 2 |

A modern robot uses **both** in a hierarchical fashion: an MPU for high-level decisions, MCUs for low-level deterministic control. *(3 marks — 1 each for distinction lines and 1 for combined insight.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (Each 14 Marks, with internal choice)

> *ESE instruction: Attempt **any ONE** full question from the two given.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Scheme Model]

**(a) Explain the role of embedded systems in an industrial 6-DOF robotic arm. Draw and explain the block diagram of its hierarchical controller. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A 6-DOF industrial robotic arm (such as those by KUKA or FANUC) is a *networked, real-time, multi-loop* system. The embedded system forms its **three-tier hierarchy**:

**Tier 1 — Trajectory Planner (High-Level Computer / Industrial PC):**
Runs a non-real-time or soft real-time OS (Linux + ROS 2). Computes the joint trajectory $\{\theta_1(t), \theta_2(t), \dots, \theta_6(t)\}$ using inverse kinematics every **10 – 100 ms**. Communicates to Tier 2 over **EtherCAT**.

**Tier 2 — Motion Controller (DSP or Cortex-M4 MCU):**
Receives the trajectory setpoints. Computes the PID / feedforward torque for each joint at **1 – 4 kHz**. Handles safety interlocks and E-stop.

**Tier 3 — Per-Joint Servo Drive (FPGA + power electronics):**
Generates PWM signals to a three-phase inverter, drives a BLDC motor through a sine-commutation. Current feedback is closed at **20 – 50 kHz**.

**Sensors and actuators involved:**
- Encoders (position) at each joint.
- Resolvers (absolute position) on the wrist.
- 6-axis force-torque sensor at the end-effector.
- BLDC servo motors with harmonic-drive gearboxes.
- 24 V – 48 V DC bus, three-phase inverter bridges.

**Embedded features critical to the arm:**
- **Determinism** — EtherCAT guarantees cycle jitter < 1 µs.
- **Safety** — ISO 13849 PL d / Cat 3 dual-channel E-stop.
- **Reliability** — Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) > 100 000 hours.

*(4 marks for block diagram and explanation of tiers, 2 marks for sensors/actuators, 1 mark for citing determinism/safety/reliability.)*

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR) used in warehouses. Identify the sensors, processors, and communication protocols used. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An AMR (e.g., MiR250, Fetch Robotics Freight) is a wheeled robot that navigates dynamically in a warehouse without fixed tracks. Its embedded architecture is:

- **Perception layer** — 2D/3D LiDAR (SICK, Velodyne), depth camera (Intel RealSense), IMU (BNO055), wheel encoders, optional RFID.
- **Processing layer** — Onboard industrial PC (Intel i7) running ROS 2 Humble with SLAM (Cartographer / RTAB-Map). Sub-controller (STM32) for low-level motor control.
- **Actuation layer** — Two BLDC hub motors with closed-loop torque control, steering servo.
- **Communication layer** — Wi-Fi 6 to fleet manager (WMS), 5G as backup, CAN-bus between MCUs.
- **Power layer** — 24 V or 48 V Li-ion, BMS, hot-swap battery.
- **Safety layer** — Safety PLC (SICK Flexi Soft) certified to PL d, dual-channel E-stop, safety LiDAR.

**Working flow:**
1. WMS sends a "go to bin A12" instruction over Wi-Fi.
2. The PC's SLAM node localises the AMR within ±5 cm using LiDAR + AMCL.
3. The path planner (Nav2) outputs a velocity setpoint.
4. The STM32 motor controller regulates the BLDC speeds at 1 kHz.
5. Safety PLC continuously monitors safety LiDAR; if a person enters the zone, the robot emergency-stops within 80 ms.

**Communication protocols used:** Wi-Fi, 5G, CAN 2.0B, EtherCAT, USB 3.0, ROS 2 DDS (Cyclone DDS / Fast DDS).

*(3 marks block diagram, 2 marks for listing sensors/processors, 2 marks for protocols and safety explanation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Scheme Model]

**(a) Describe the embedded system architecture of a quadrotor UAV drone. Explain the role of its sensors, flight controller, and actuators. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A quadrotor drone (e.g., DJI Matrice, custom Pixhawk build) is a **6-DOF under-actuated aerial vehicle**. It has *four* motors but six degrees of freedom, so it must constantly re-mix motor speeds to maintain attitude — every **1 – 4 ms**.

**Block diagram (refer to Section 4.3 above for the visual).**

**Sensors and their roles:**
- **IMU (6-axis: 3-axis gyroscope + 3-axis accelerometer)** — measures angular rate and linear acceleration at **1 – 8 kHz**. Primary feedback for the inner-loop attitude controller.
- **Barometer (MS5611)** — measures altitude via air pressure at 50 – 200 Hz.
- **GPS / GNSS (u-blox NEO-M8N)** — global position at 1 – 10 Hz for outdoor flights.
- **Magnetometer (compass)** — heading reference, 50 Hz.
- **Optical-flow sensor** — indoor position-hold, 100 Hz.
- **LiDAR / ultrasonic (downward)** — precision altitude hold, 100 Hz.
- **Camera (front / down)** — vision-based obstacle avoidance, 30 fps.

**Flight controller (the heart of the embedded system):**
A Pixhawk-class flight controller uses an **STM32F427 (180 MHz Cortex-M4)** main CPU plus a co-processor (STM32F103) for I/O. The firmware is PX4 or ArduPilot running on **NuttX RTOS**. Inside it runs:
- A **Kalman filter (EKF2)** at 250 Hz that fuses IMU + GPS + baro + mag.
- A **cascade PID** controller: outer loop (position, 50 Hz) → middle loop (velocity, 100 Hz) → inner loop (attitude, 1 kHz).
- A **mixer** that maps the 4 motor commands from the 6-DOF torque vector.

**Actuators and their drivers:**
- **4 × BLDC motors (e.g., 920 kV)** turning propellers.
- **4 × Electronic Speed Controllers (ESCs)** that take PWM (50 – 490 Hz) or DShot (digital, 1 – 600 kHz) commands and commutate the motor.
- **Servo / gimbal** for camera tilt.

**Real-time requirements:** An attitude-loop iteration must complete within **1 ms**; if it overruns, the drone can lose stability within tens of milliseconds.

*(3 marks for naming sensors, 2 marks for flight controller internals, 2 marks for actuators and ESCs.)*

---

**(b) Discuss the applications of embedded systems in surgical robots and space rovers, with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Surgical Robots — Da Vinci Surgical System (Intuitive Surgical)

- **Embedded CPU bank** — three to four redundant Intel Xeon processors in the surgeon's console plus FPGA-based real-time motion controllers in the patient-side cart.
- **Sensors:** 3D HD endoscopes (two CMOS cameras with 1080p output), 7-DOF EndoWrist instrument encoders, force/torque sensors on the tool tips, motion trackers on the surgeon's hand controllers.
- **Actuators:** 4 robotic arms driven by 28 servo motors, all slaved to the surgeon's hand motion with a 1:1, 3:1, or 5:1 motion-scaling factor.
- **Embedded role:** Filters the surgeon's tremor (high-pass filter at ~6 Hz), enforces force limits (< 5 N) to prevent tissue damage, and provides 3-D stereo vision with sub-millisecond latency.
- **Determinism:** Total end-to-end latency (hand motion → instrument motion) < **50 ms**, certified to **IEC 60601-1** medical standards.

### Space Rovers — NASA's Perseverance (Mars 2020)

- **Embedded CPU:** BAE Systems RAD750 radiation-hardened PowerPC, **200 MHz**, **128 MB RAM**, **2 GB Flash** — extremely slow by Earth standards but hardened against cosmic radiation (Single Event Upset mitigation via EDAC).
- **Sensors:** 19 cameras (Mastcam-Z, Navcam, Hazcam, Supercam), MOXIE oxygen-generation spectrometer, ground-penetrating radar RIMFAX, weather station MEDA.
- **Actuators:** 6-wheel rocker-bogie suspension with steering motors, a 2.1 m robotic arm with 5 DOF carrying a coring drill, sample-handling turret.
- **Embedded role:** Real-time visual odometry at **1 – 2 Hz** to localise the rover within **±1 m**; autonomous path planning (AutoNav) up to **200 m** per sol; power-aware scheduling (rover sleeps during dust storms).
- **Communication:** UHF to orbiters (Curiosity/MAVEN), then X-band to Earth — round-trip latency up to **24 minutes**, making the rover *truly* autonomous. No real-time human-in-the-loop.

**Common features of both:**
- Extreme reliability and fault tolerance.
- Real-time deterministic response.
- Power-constrained design.
- Certified safety standards.

*(3 marks for Da Vinci, 3 marks for Perseverance, 1 mark for comparative insight.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in 2024 Scheme ESE answers on this topic:**
> 1. **Generic statements lose marks.** Writing "robots use sensors" gets 0. Writing "Pixhawk's STM32F427 fuses IMU + GPS at 250 Hz via EKF2" gets full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the word "real-time."** Every embedded robotics question demands it. The very first line of your answer should mention *deterministic, real-time response*.
> 3. **Confusing MCU with MPU.** MCU has on-chip memory; MPU does not. Mixing them up in a tier-diagram costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring safety/standards.** Failing to cite **ISO 13849** (industrial) or **IEC 60601-1** (medical) loses 1 mark on every application question.
> 5. **No block diagram = -2 marks minimum.** Even for a 3-mark Part A, draw a small box-and-arrow sketch.
> 6. **Skipping units.** Writing $f_s$ without Hz, $\theta$ without degrees/radians, or power without Watts is a soft -½ mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] An **embedded system in robotics** is a dedicated, real-time, hardware-software co-designed computer that *senses, decides, and actuates* inside a robot.
- [x] Eight canonical features: **single-purpose, real-time, tightly-coupled, resource-constrained, reliable/safe, sensor/actuator-rich, networked, minimal UI**.
- [x] Three pillars: **Sense → Process → Actuate**, with power as the fourth silent pillar.
- [x] The *defining* property is **deterministic, real-time response** — not speed, not power, not size.
- [x] Always use the **hierarchy**: Perception (SoC) → Planning (MPU) → Control (MCU) → Actuation (Power electronics + Motors).
- [x] Industrial arms → **EtherCAT, 1 kHz control loop, BLDC + harmonic drives, ISO 13849**.
- [x] AMRs → **LiDAR + SLAM, ROS 2, Wi-Fi/5G, safety PLC, dual-channel E-stop**.
- [x] Drones → **PX4/ArduPilot on STM32, EKF2 fusion at 250 Hz, BLDC + ESC, attitude loop at 1 kHz**.
- [x] Surgical robots → **Da Vinci, hand-tremor filter, sub-50 ms latency, IEC 60601-1**.
- [x] Space rovers → **RAD750 rad-hard CPU, 200 MHz, AutoNav, 24-min one-way light delay**.
- [x] Formula quick-firing: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$, $D = T_{\text{on}}/T$, $\tau = J\ddot{\theta}$, PID law, 2R forward kinematics.
- [x] Always *always* mention a specific **sensor**, a specific **MCU/SoC**, and a specific **standard or protocol** to get top-band marks.
- [x] Final-block mental model: **Power → MCU → Sensors/Actuators → Communication → HMI, all under an RTOS**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Definition, Features, Applications of Embedded Systems in Robotics.**
> Cross-references: PID derivations in Module 4, RTOS scheduling in Module 5, sensor interfacing in Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded System Architecture: HW - Processor, Controller, SoC, Memory, Peripherals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Embedded System Architecture: HW - Processor, Controller, SoC, Memory, Peripherals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Embedded System Architecture** refers to the *systematic hardware-software organization* of a computing device that is **dedicated to performing a specific function** within a larger electrical or mechanical system. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme for PBRUT404, the hardware architecture of an embedded system is composed of **four tightly-coupled functional blocks**:

1. **Processing Core** – the "brain" executing instructions (Processor / Controller / SoC).
2. **Memory Subsystem** – volatile (RAM) and non-volatile (ROM/Flash) storage of code and data.
3. **Peripherals** – dedicated I/O hardware (ADC, UART, timers, GPIO, sensors, actuators).
4. **Interconnection Fabric** – address bus, data bus, and control bus linking the above three.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** An **Embedded System** is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based system that is *designed for a specific function*, is *part of a larger device*, and operates under *real-time, resource, and power constraints*. The hardware architecture is **optimized for a deterministic, dedicated task**, unlike a general-purpose computer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Human Body as an Embedded System

To make this instantly memorable, let us compare an embedded system to a **human body** (a biological "embedded" control system):

| Embedded System Component | Human Body Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Processor / Controller | Brain (CPU) | Decision-making, computation |
| Memory (RAM/ROM) | Long-term & short-term memory | Stores instructions, facts, data |
| Peripherals (ADC, Sensors) | Eyes, Ears, Skin (Senses) | Collects real-world input |
| Actuators (PWM, Motors) | Hands, Legs, Muscles | Performs physical actions |
| Bus (Address/Data/Control) | Nervous System | Carries signals between parts |
| Power Supply | Heart & Blood (Energy) | Provides energy to operate |

**Take-away analogy:** Just as the brain does *not* try to play music, calculate math, and digest food all at once using separate cores, an embedded controller is **built to do ONE specific job extremely well, in real time, with minimum power**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the distinction matters in KTU exams:** Examiners frequently test whether students understand that embedded systems are *resource-constrained* and *task-specific*, which dictates every architectural choice (e.g., why a microcontroller integrates RAM and ROM, while a PC separates them).

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of Embedded Hardware Architecture

```text
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │      EMBEDDED SYSTEM HW ARCHITECTURE      │
        └───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          │
       ┌──────────┬───────┴────────┬──────────────┐
       ▼          ▼                ▼              ▼
  PROCESSOR   MEMORY         PERIPHERALS     BUS / I/O
  (Brain)    (Storage)      (Senses/Acts)   (Nervous Sys.)
       │          │                │              │
  MPU/MCU     RAM/ROM        ADC/UART/      Address/
  DSP/ASIC    Flash/         PWM/GPIO/      Data/
  SoC/FPGA    Cache          Timers         Control
```

## 1.4 Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 PBRUT404 – Module 3)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Sub-Topics Tested in KTU University Exams:**
> 1. **Processor** – Microprocessor vs. Microcontroller vs. DSP vs. ASIC vs. SoC
> 2. **Memory** – Volatile, Non-volatile, Hierarchy, Address decoding
> 3. **Peripherals** – On-chip (Timers, ADC, UART, I2C, SPI) and Off-chip (Sensors, Actuators)
> 4. **Bus Architecture** – Von Neumann vs. Harvard, AMBA, memory-mapped I/O
> 5. **SoC** – System-on-Chip integration philosophy, IP cores, examples (ARM Cortex, ESP32)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Processing Element: Processor, Controller, SoC

### 2.1.1 Microprocessor (MPU)

A **Microprocessor Unit (MPU)** is *only the Central Processing Unit* of a computer, fabricated on a single integrated circuit. It **does not contain on-chip memory or I/O** – these are external.

- **Examples:** Intel 8085, 8086, Intel Core i7, ARM Cortex-A series.
- **Used in:** Personal Computers, servers, high-end embedded (e.g., Raspberry Pi).
- **Key feature:** Higher clock speed, **CISC** (Complex Instruction Set Computer) or RISC architecture, large external memory address space.

### 2.1.2 Microcontroller (MCU)

A **Microcontroller Unit (MCU)** is a *"computer-on-a-chip"* – it integrates the **CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O peripherals + bus** on a single IC.

- **Examples:** 8051, PIC16F877A, ATmega328 (Arduino), STM32, MSP430.
- **Used in:** Washing machines, microwave ovens, automotive ECUs, IoT.
- **Key feature:** Low power, low cost, deterministic real-time response, **Harvard architecture** typically.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A *favourite* question is *"Differentiate Microprocessor and Microcontroller."* Always give a **minimum 6 points** of difference and draw a block diagram of both.

### 2.1.3 Digital Signal Processor (DSP)

A **DSP** is a specialized processor optimized for **high-speed numeric computation** (especially multiply-accumulate operations on streaming data).

- **Examples:** Texas Instruments TMS320C6000, SHARC ADSP-21060.
- **Used in:** Audio codecs, image processing, radar, modems.
- **Key feature:** Harvard architecture, MAC unit, SIMD, hardware loops.

### 2.1.4 Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC)

An **ASIC** is a chip **custom-designed for one specific application** – not programmable.

- **Examples:** Bitcoin mining chips, smartphone PMIC, custom camera ISP.
- **Used in:** High-volume consumer products where per-unit cost must be minimal.
- **Drawback:** **Non-reconfigurable**; high NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) cost.

### 2.1.5 System-on-Chip (SoC)

A **SoC** integrates *an entire electronic system* – CPU + GPU + DSP + Memory Controller + Peripherals + Radio – on a *single silicon die*.

- **Examples:** Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple M-series, ESP32, Broadcom BCM2837 (Raspberry Pi), NXP i.MX8.
- **Used in:** Smartphones, smartwatches, IoT nodes, automotive ADAS.
- **Key feature:** Highest integration; often uses **IP cores** (licensed processor designs, e.g., ARM Cortex).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question (14 marks):** *"Explain the architecture of a System-on-Chip (SoC) with a neat block diagram. Compare SoC with Microcontroller."* – Prepare this with at least **6 differentiating points**.

### 2.1.6 FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array)

An **FPGA** is a reconfigurable hardware platform where logic is implemented via *hardware description languages* (VHDL/Verilog) rather than software instructions.

- **Examples:** Xilinx Zynq, Intel Cyclone V, Lattice ECP5.
- **Used in:** Prototyping ASICs, DSP pipelines, aerospace.

## 2.2 Memory Subsystem

### 2.2.1 Memory Classification

| Type | Volatility | Use in Embedded | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RAM (SRAM)** | Volatile | Cache, fast scratchpad | On-chip SRAM |
| **RAM (DRAM)** | Volatile | Main working memory (large SoC) | DDR, LPDDR |
| **ROM** | Non-volatile | Mask-programmed firmware | Old 8051 |
| **PROM/EPROM** | Non-volatile | UV-erasable firmware | 27C256 |
| **EEPROM** | Non-volatile | Small config data | AT24C02 |
| **Flash (NOR)** | Non-volatile | Code storage (XIP) | Boot flash |
| **Flash (NAND)** | Non-volatile | Bulk data (SSD, SD cards) | SD card, eMMC |

### 2.2.2 Memory Hierarchy (Speed vs. Size)

```text
REGISTERS   ←  fastest, smallest (bytes), in-CPU
   │
L1 CACHE    ←  ~32 KB, very fast, per-core
   │
L2/L3 CACHE ←  ~256 KB – 8 MB, shared
   │
MAIN RAM    ←  ~MB–GB, slower, off-chip
   │
FLASH/SSD   ←  ~GB, slowest, non-volatile
   │
ARCHIVE     ←  HDD, Cloud (rare in tiny embedded)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Embedded systems often *skip cache* because the workload is predictable. Determinism is preferred over raw speed.

### 2.2.3 Address Decoding & Address Space

For an n-bit address bus, total addressable memory locations = $2^n$.

$$N_{\text{locations}} = 2^{n}$$

For example, an 8051 with **16-bit address** can address:
$$2^{16} = 65{,}536 \; \text{bytes} = 64 \; \text{KB}$$

Memory-mapped I/O uses the same address bus for both memory *and* peripherals, while isolated I/O (used in 8086) uses a separate I/O address space.

## 2.3 Peripheral Subsystem

### 2.3.1 On-Chip Peripherals (integrated within MCU/SoC)

| Peripheral | Purpose | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **GPIO** | Digital input/output | LED, switch, relay |
| **Timer/Counter** | Precise time delays, event counting | PWM generation, debouncing |
| **ADC** | Analog-to-Digital conversion | Sensor reading (temperature) |
| **DAC** | Digital-to-Analog conversion | Audio output |
| **UART** | Asynchronous serial comms | Debug console, GPS |
| **SPI** | Synchronous full-duplex serial | SD card, displays |
| **I²C** | Synchronous 2-wire serial | EEPROM, RTC, sensors |
| **CAN** | Robust automotive bus | Car ECUs |
| **USB** | High-speed comms | PC connectivity |
| **PWM** | Pulse-Width Modulation | Motor speed, LED dimming |
| **Watchdog Timer** | System reset on hang | Reliability |
| **RTC** | Real-Time Clock | Timekeeping |

### 2.3.2 Off-Chip Peripherals

- **Sensors:** Temperature (LM35), Accelerometer (MPU6050), Humidity (DHT22), Gas (MQ-2).
- **Actuators:** DC motor, Stepper motor, Servo, Relay, Buzzer, LED, LCD.
- **Communication Modules:** Bluetooth (HC-05), Wi-Fi (ESP8266), GSM (SIM800), GPS (NEO-6M), RFID (RC522).

## 2.4 Bus Architecture

### 2.4.1 Von Neumann vs. Harvard

| Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory for code & data | **Shared** | **Separate** |
| Bus | Single | Separate instruction & data |
| Speed | Bottleneck (fetch overlaps data) | Faster (parallel fetch) |
| Power | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Used in | 8086, ARM Cortex-A | 8051, PIC, AVR, DSP |

### 2.4.2 AMBA Bus (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)

Standardized by ARM, AMBA defines:
- **AXI** – high-performance, used in SoCs.
- **AHB** – high-speed, for processors and memory.
- **APB** – low-power peripheral bus, for simple peripherals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Examiners often test the **need for multiple bus standards in SoCs** – high-speed cores use AHB/AXI, while slow peripherals use APB, optimizing power and complexity.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Value (Embedded) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Addressable memory | $N = 2^{n}$ bytes | 8051: $2^{16}=64\;$KB |
| 2 | Instruction cycle | $T_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} \times \text{(machine cycles per instruction)}$ | 8051 @ 12 MHz: 1 µs per cycle |
| 3 | Instruction execution time | $T_{\text{inst}} = \dfrac{\text{MC}}{f_{\text{clk}}}$ | AVR @ 16 MHz: 62.5 ns per 1-cycle instr. |
| 4 | ADC resolution | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}-1}$ | 10-bit, $V_{ref}=5\;$V → 4.88 mV |
| 5 | Timer overflow | $T_{\text{ovf}} = \dfrac{(2^{n} - \text{Prescaler} \cdot \text{Initial Value})}{f_{\text{clk}}}$ | 8-bit timer, 1 MHz, prescaler=64: 16.32 ms |
| 6 | Baud rate (UART) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{UBRR} \cdot 16}$ | ATmega: 9600, 115200 |
| 7 | PWM duty cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{period}}} \times 100\%$ | 0 %–100 % |
| 8 | Power dissipation | $P = C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$ | CMOS dynamic power |
| 9 | MIPS rating | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}$ | ARM Cortex-M0 @ 50 MHz: ~45 MIPS |
| 10 | Bus bandwidth | $B = f_{\text{bus}} \times W$ | AHB @ 100 MHz, 32-bit: 400 MB/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the *backbone* of any embedded system design. A 14-mark KTU question often asks the student to *size the timer*, *compute ADC step size*, or *verify baud rate generation* for an MCU of a given clock.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** A modern car has **>100 ECUs** (Electronic Control Units), each an embedded system – engine, airbag, ABS, infotainment.
- **IoT:** An ESP32 SoC integrates Wi-Fi + BT + dual-core CPU + peripherals on a single chip, enabling smart-home nodes.
- **Medical:** Pacemakers and insulin pumps are *life-critical* embedded systems with strict real-time, low-power demands.
- **Industrial:** PLCs control assembly lines using deterministic microcontrollers (e.g., Siemens S7-200).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Addressable Memory from Address Bus Width

**Problem:** An embedded processor has a **32-bit address bus**. Compute (a) the maximum addressable memory, (b) the last valid address, and (c) the address range.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the address bus width.
$$n = 32 \; \text{bits}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the addressability formula.
$$N = 2^{n} = 2^{32}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the exponent.
$$2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \; \text{bytes}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to KB, MB, GB.
$$\frac{4{,}294{,}967{,}296}{1024} = 4{,}194{,}304 \; \text{KB} = 4096 \; \text{MB} = 4 \; \text{GB}$$

**Step 5:** Range of addresses.
$$0 \; \text{to} \; (2^{32} - 1) = 0 \; \text{to} \; \text{0xFFFFFFFF}_{\text{hex}}$$

> **Final Answer:** A 32-bit address bus addresses **4 GB** of memory, ranging from **0x00000000 to 0xFFFFFFFF**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – ADC Resolution & Quantization Step

**Problem:** A 12-bit ADC in a microcontroller uses a reference voltage $V_{\text{ref}} = 3.3\;$V. Find (a) the resolution per LSB, and (b) the digital output for an analog input of $1.65\;$V.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify ADC parameters.
$$n = 12 \; \text{bits}, \quad V_{\text{ref}} = 3.3 \; \text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Total number of quantization levels.
$$L = 2^{n} = 2^{12} = 4096$$

**Step 3:** Quantization step (LSB voltage).
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{4095}$$

$$\Delta V = 8.058 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{V} \approx 0.806 \; \text{mV}$$

**Step 4:** Digital output for $V_{\text{in}} = 1.65\;$V.
$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1.65}{8.058 \times 10^{-4}} \right\rfloor$$

$$D = \lfloor 2047.5 \rfloor = 2047 \; \text{decimal} = 0x7FF_{\text{hex}}$$

**Step 5:** Verification using ideal mid-scale formula.
$$D_{\text{mid}} = \frac{2^{n}}{2} - 1 = 2047$$

Since 1.65 V is **exactly half of 3.3 V**, the output must be mid-scale → **2047** ✓.

> **Final Answer:** Each LSB ≈ **0.806 mV**; the digital output for 1.65 V is **2047 (0x7FF)**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Timer Delay Computation (8051-style MCU)

**Problem:** An 8051 has clock frequency $f_{\text{clk}} = 11.0592 \; \text{MHz}$. Timer 0 is in **Mode 1 (16-bit)**. Compute the value to be loaded into TH0:TL0 to generate a delay of **50 ms**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify machine cycle period.
The 8051 divides the oscillator by 12 to produce the machine cycle clock.
$$T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{\text{mc}} = 1.085 \; \mu\text{s per machine cycle}$$

**Step 2:** Total machine cycles needed.
$$M = \frac{T_{\text{delay}}}{T_{\text{mc}}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$M \approx 46{,}080 \; \text{machine cycles}$$

**Step 3:** Timer counts up to 16-bit overflow at 65,536. So the value to be preloaded:
$$N_{\text{load}} = 2^{16} - M = 65{,}536 - 46{,}080 = 19{,}456$$

**Step 4:** Convert 19,456 to hex.
$$19{,}456 \div 4096 = 4 \; \text{remainder} \; 3072$$
$$3072 \div 256 = 12 \; \text{remainder} \; 0$$
$$19{,}456_{\text{dec}} = 0x4C00_{\text{hex}}$$

**Step 5:** Split into high & low bytes.
$$\text{TH0} = 0x4C, \quad \text{TL0} = 0x00$$

> **Final Answer:** Load **TH0 = 0x4C**, **TL0 = 0x00** to generate ~50 ms delay.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students forget to divide the oscillator by 12 (machine cycle factor) and end up with off-by-12 errors. *Always* convert oscillator frequency to machine-cycle frequency first.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 – UART Baud Rate Calculation (AVR ATmega)

**Problem:** An ATmega328P runs at $f_{\text{clk}} = 16 \; \text{MHz}$. Compute the UBRR value to set UART baud rate to **9600 bps** in normal (non-2x) mode.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Recall the AVR baud rate formula (normal mode).
$$\text{Baud} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{16 \cdot (\text{UBRR} + 1)}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for UBRR.
$$\text{UBRR} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{16 \cdot \text{Baud}} - 1$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.
$$\text{UBRR} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 9600} - 1$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate.
$$\text{UBRR} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{153{,}600} - 1 = 104.166 - 1 = 103.166$$

**Step 5:** Round to nearest integer.
$$\text{UBRR} = 103$$

**Step 6:** Compute the actual achieved baud rate.
$$\text{Baud}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times (103 + 1)} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{1664} \approx 9615.4 \; \text{bps}$$

**Step 7:** Compute the error.
$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{9615.4 - 9600}{9600} \times 100 \approx 0.16 \; \%$$

This error is well within the UART tolerance (±2%), so **UBRR = 103 is valid**.

> **Final Answer:** UBRR = **103 (0x67)**, achieved baud = **9615 bps**, error ≈ **+0.16 %**.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 – Bus Bandwidth (AMBA AHB)

**Problem:** An AHB bus in a SoC has a clock of $100\;$MHz and a 32-bit data width. Compute the peak bus bandwidth.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Recall bandwidth formula.
$$B = f_{\text{bus}} \times W$$

**Step 2:** Substitute (with $W = 32 \; \text{bits} = 4 \; \text{bytes}$).
$$B = 100 \times 10^{6} \times 4 \; \text{bytes/sec}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate.
$$B = 400 \times 10^{6} \; \text{bytes/sec} = 400 \; \text{MB/s}$$

> **Final Answer:** Peak bandwidth = **400 MB/s**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Processing Element

| Selection Criterion | MPU | MCU | DSP | ASIC | SoC | FPGA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit cost (high volume)** | High | Low | Medium | Lowest | Low | High |
| **Flexibility** | High | High | High | None | Medium | Very high |
| **Real-time determinism** | Low | High | High | High | Medium | High |
| **Power consumption** | High | Low | Medium | Lowest | Low–Med | High |
| **Performance (raw)** | Very high | Low | High | Highest | High | High |
| **Time-to-market** | Fast | Fast | Medium | Slow | Fast | Fast |
| **NRE cost** | None | None | None | Very high | Low | None |
| **Re-programmability** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Partial | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a *Washing Machine Controller*, an 8051-class MCU wins. For a *Self-Driving Car ECU*, an SoC with NPU/CPU/GPU combination wins. For a *Hearing Aid* DSP, a custom DSP wins. KTU expects students to *justify the choice* — not just list options.

## 3.7 KTU Numerical Problem Practice (with Hints)

> **Q1:** A processor has 24 address lines and 16 data lines. Find memory capacity.
> *Hint: $2^{24} \times 16 \; \text{bits} = 16 \; \text{MB}$. ✓*

> **Q2:** A 10-bit ADC has $V_{\text{ref}} = 5\;$V. What input voltage gives a digital output of 512?
> *Hint: $V = 512 \times \frac{5}{1023} = 2.503\;$V ✓*

> **Q3:** An 8051 with 12 MHz crystal generates a 1 ms delay. Compute TH0, TL0.
> *Hint: $T_{\text{mc}} = 1\; \mu\text{s}$, so $M = 1000$, load = $65536 - 1000 = 64536 = 0xFC18$ ✓*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram: Generic Embedded System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    Power[Power Supply 3.3V 5V] --> MCU
    CPU[CPU Core ALU Registers Control Unit] --> BusMatrix
    ROM[Non-Volatile Memory Flash ROM Boot Code] --> BusMatrix
    RAM[Volatile Memory SRAM Working Data] --> BusMatrix
    Timer[Timer Counter PWM Watchdog] --> BusMatrix
    Comm[Communication UART SPI I2C CAN USB] --> BusMatrix
    Analog[ADC DAC Comparator] --> BusMatrix
    GPIO[GPIO Ports Pins] --> BusMatrix
    BusMatrix{System Bus AHB APB}
    Sensors[External Sensors LM35 MPU6050] --> GPIO
    Sensors --> Analog
    Actuators[External Actuators Motor Relay LED] --> GPIO
    BusMatrix --> External[External Memory SDRAM NAND]
    Power --> Sensors
    Power --> Actuators
```

## 4.2 Von Neumann vs. Harvard Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph VN[Von Neumann Architecture Shared Bus]
        CPUvn[CPU] --> BusVn{Single Bus}
        BusVn --> MEMvn[Single Memory Code and Data]
    end
    subgraph HV[Harvard Architecture Separate Buses]
        CPUhv[CPU] --> BusHv{Instruction Bus}
        CPUhv --> BusHvD{Data Bus}
        BusHv --> MEMprog[Program Memory ROM]
        BusHvD --> MEMdata[Data Memory RAM]
    end
```

## 4.3 SoC Internal Block Diagram (ARM Cortex-M SoC Example)

```mermaid
graph TB
    AXI[AXI High Performance Bus]
    AHB[AHB Matrix Bus Fabric]
    APB[APB Peripheral Bus]
    BridgeAHBtoAPB[AHB to APB Bridge]
    CoreM4[ARM Cortex M4 Core]
    CoreM4 --> AXI
    AXI --> AHB
    AHB --> RAM[On Chip SRAM 256KB]
    AHB --> Flash[Flash 1MB]
    AHB --> DMA[Direct Memory Access Controller]
    AHB --> ETH[Ethernet MAC]
    AHB --> BridgeAHBtoAPB
    BridgeAHBtoAPB --> APB
    APB --> UART1[UART1 UART2]
    APB --> SPI1[SPI1 SPI2]
    APB --> I2C1[I2C1 I2C2]
    APB --> TIM[TIM1 TIM2 TIM3]
    APB --> ADC1[ADC1 12-bit]
    APB --> GPIOA[GPIOA GPIOB GPIOC]
    APB --> WDT[Watchdog]
    APB --> RTCM[RTC]
```

## 4.4 Memory Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
graph TB
    Reg[REGISTERS Fastest Few Bytes 1 cycle]
    L1[L1 CACHE 32 KB 2-4 cycles]
    L2[L2 CACHE 256 KB 10-20 cycles]
    RAMH[MAIN RAM MB 100 cycles]
    FlashH[FLASH GB 200 cycles]
    DiskH[EXTERNAL STORAGE SD NAND GB ms access]

    Reg --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> RAMH
    RAMH --> FlashH
    FlashH --> DiskH
```

## 4.5 Typical Microcontroller Internal Architecture (8051-class)

```mermaid
graph LR
    CPU8051[CPU ALU Accumulator B Register PSW SP PC]
    Osc[Oscillator Circuit] --> CLK[Clock Generator]
    CLK --> CPU8051
    CLK --> Timer0
    CLK --> Timer1
    ROM8051[ROM 4KB Program] --> CPU8051
    RAM8051[RAM 128B Data 128B SFR] --> CPU8051
    CPU8051 --> P0[Port 0]
    CPU8051 --> P1[Port 1]
    CPU8051 --> P2[Port 2]
    CPU8051 --> P3[Port 3]
    CPU8051 --> Serial[Serial Port UART]
    CPU8051 --> T0B[Timer 0]
    CPU8051 --> T1B[Timer 1]
    CPU8051 --> IntCtrl[Interrupt Controller]
    Bus[8-bit Data Bus 16-bit Address Bus] -.-> CPU8051
```

## 4.6 Power, Clock, and Reset Distribution in SoC

```mermaid
graph TB
    PMU[Power Management Unit PMIC]
    PMU --> Vcore[VDD Core 1.2V]
    PMU --> Vio[VDD IO 3.3V]
    PMU --> Vana[VDD Analog 3.3V LDO]
    ClkSrc[External Crystal or Oscillator]
    ClkSrc --> PLL[PLL Multiplier]
    PLL --> HCLK[AHB Clock HCLK]
    PLL --> PCLK[APB Clock PCLK]
    PLL --> SysTick[SysTick Clock]
    RSTn[Reset Pin POR BOR]
    RSTn --> RSTCtrl[Reset Controller]
    RSTCtrl --> CPU_RST[CPU Reset]
    RSTCtrl --> Peri_RST[Peripheral Reset]
    Vcore --> CPU_RST
    Vio --> Peri_RST
    Vana --> ADC_PWR[ADC Power]
```

## 4.7 Peripheral-to-External-Device Connection Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    MCUblock[Microcontroller / SoC]
    MCUblock -->|GPIO| LED1[LED Status]
    MCUblock -->|PWM| MOT[DC Motor Driver]
    MCUblock -->|ADC| SENS[Analog Sensor LM35]
    MCUblock -->|UART| GPS[GPS Module]
    MCUblock -->|SPI| LCD[SPI LCD Display]
    MCUblock -->|I2C| RTC1[I2C RTC DS1307]
    MCUblock -->|CAN| ECU1[CAN ECU Network]
    MCUblock -->|USB| PC[PC Host]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why These Diagrams Matter in KTU Exams:** Block diagrams fetch *easy marks* if drawn correctly. Always include **labeled arrows** and **explicitly write the bus/interface type** (e.g., "8-bit data bus", "I2C SDA/SCL"). Examiners allocate 1–2 marks purely for a neat, labeled diagram.

---
