- $\overline{Y1}$ → 16 KB ROM #2 (4000H–7FFFH)
- $\overline{Y2}$ → 8 KB RAM (8000H–9FFFH)

Connect A15 → C, A14 → B, A13 → A of the decoder. The 8051 signals `PSEN̄` go to the ROMs' `OE`, and `RD̄, WR̄` go to the RAM. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Detailed address table.**

| Chip | A15 | A14 | A13 | A12…A0 | Start | End |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ROM #1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0…0 to 1…1 | 0000H | 3FFFH |
| ROM #2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0…0 to 1…1 | 4000H | 7FFFH |
| RAM | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0…0 to 1…1 | 8000H | 9FFFH |

*Note: The ROMs use 14 address lines (A0–A13), but the decoder is only 3-input. So the ROM's A13 input is *also* used inside its own decoding, leading to the gap between 3FFFH and 4000H.* **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Address-decoding logic (to be drawn).**

- 74LS138 inputs: A, B, C → A13, A14, A15 respectively
- Enable pins: G1 = +5V, G2A̅ = G2B̅ = GND
- Output $\overline{Y0}$ → $\overline{CS}$ of ROM #1
- Output $\overline{Y1}$ → $\overline{CS}$ of ROM #2
- Output $\overline{Y2}$ → $\overline{CS}$ of RAM **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Strobe assignment.**

- $\overline{PSEN}$ from 8051 → $\overline{OE}$ of both ROMs (program read strobe)
- $\overline{RD}$ from 8051 → $\overline{OE}$ of RAM (data read)
- $\overline{WR}$ from 8051 → $\overline{WE}$ of RAM (data write) **(1 mark)**

**Step 7 — Address Latch.**

Use 74HC573 with ALE to demultiplex Port 0. Connect the latched A0–A7 to A0–A7 of all three chips. A8–A12 of 8051 go directly to all chips. **(0.5 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting PSEN̄ vs RD̄:** Marks are deducted when students use `RD̄` for external program memory. Always use `PSEN̄` for ROM and `RD̄ / WR̄` for RAM. (-2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing internal and external address ranges:** Internal ROM is 0000H–0FFFH for 8051 only. If the question says "interface external ROM starting from 0000H," use the **EA̅ = 0** mode or interface the *external* ROM. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Using wrong number of address lines:** Forgetting that a 32 KB chip needs *15* address lines, not 16. The 16th line is for *chip-select*, not addressing. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Not demultiplexing the address/data bus:** Forgetting to mention the 74HC573 latch and ALE signal in the block diagram. (-2 marks)
> 5. **Incorrect stack pointer initialization:** Many students leave SP at 07H, which collides with Register Bank 0. Always initialize SP to at least **60H** in the startup code. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Treating bit-addressable area as general-purpose:** The 20H–2FH region is *bit-addressable*; using it as a byte array is *possible* but inefficient. Examiners love to test this. (-1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] The 8051 has a **16-bit address bus** → maximum 64 KB addressable in each of program and data spaces.
- [ ] **Internal program memory** = 4 KB Flash (0000H–0FFFH) for 8051; 8 KB for 8052.
- [ ] **Internal data memory** = 128 bytes (00H–7FH) for 8051; 256 bytes for 8052.
- [ ] The **register banks** (4 × 8 bytes) sit at 00H–1FH; default after reset is **Bank 0**.
- [ ] The **bit-addressable area** spans 20H–2FH = 16 bytes = 128 individually-addressable bits.
- [ ] The **SFR area** (80H–FFH) is accessible *only* in direct addressing mode.
- [ ] The **stack grows upward** in 8051. Always reinitialize SP to at least 60H.
- [ ] External **program memory** is read using `PSEN̄`; external **data memory** is read/written using `RD̄ / WR̄`.
- [ ] Use `MOVX` instruction to access external data memory; use `MOVC` to read code memory (e.g., lookup tables).
- [ ] **Volatile memory** (SRAM, DRAM) loses data on power-off; **non-volatile** (ROM family) retains it.
- [ ] **SRAM** = fast, no refresh, 6 T per cell; **DRAM** = dense, needs periodic refresh.
- [ ] **Flash EEPROM** is the *de-facto* program memory of modern microcontrollers.
- [ ] **74HC573** latches the low byte of the address when ALE pulses high.
- [ ] **74LS138** is a 3-to-8 decoder used for clean chip-select generation in multi-chip memory interfacing.
- [ ] Memory capacity formula: $M = 2^{N_{addr}}$. Always express $M$ in **bytes**.
- [ ] Address range of a chip: $A_{end} = A_{start} + M_{chip} - 1$.
- [ ] Incomplete address decoding causes **address aliasing** (one physical location responding to multiple logical addresses).
- [ ] Harvard architecture (used by 8051) has *separate* program and data memory spaces with *separate* control strobes.
- [ ] Reset vector is at 0000H; ISR vectors occupy 0003H–002AH in 8-byte chunks.
- [ ] The 8051's $\overline{EA}$ pin = 1 → internal ROM used for first 4 KB; $\overline{EA}$ = 0 → external ROM from 0000H.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy Tip:** When you see "design a memory interfacing circuit" in the KTU question paper, always start with these four bullets in your answer:
> 1. Draw the address lines calculation.
> 2. Decide the chip-select logic (single chip → direct; multiple → 74LS138).
> 3. Calculate the address range explicitly in hex.
> 4. State the strobe signals (`PSEN̄` for code, `RD̄/WR̄` for data).
> This sequence alone gets you 8–9 marks out of 14 in a typical KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarization of Arduino IDE Programming: Sample Code for LED and Switch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of Arduino IDE Programming: Sample Code for LED and Switch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Arduino Integrated Development Environment (IDE)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Arduino IDE (Integrated Development Environment)** is a cross-platform application (Windows, macOS, Linux) written in Java and C++, that provides a complete software suite for writing, compiling, verifying, and uploading code (called *Sketches*) to Arduino-compatible microcontroller boards. It is the official development tool recommended by KTU for PBRUT404 laboratory and theory modules.

The IDE bundles together:
- A **Text Editor** (with syntax highlighting, auto-indentation, brace matching).
- A **Compiler/Transpiler** (uses `avr-gcc` for AVR boards like UNO).
- A **Library Manager** (handles pre-built C/C++ libraries).
- A **Serial Monitor** (for real-time UART debugging at standard baud rates like **9600 bps**).
- A **Board Manager** (supports UNO, Nano, Mega, ESP32, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology — "Sketch":**
> In Arduino parlance, every program file is called a **Sketch** and is saved with the extension `.ino`. The IDE auto-generates a function prototype section before compiling, so the order of functions in your code does not matter.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mailbox" Model

Imagine the Arduino board is a **post office** and the IDE is the **mail sorting room**:
- You (the developer) write a letter (the sketch) in a specific format.
- The IDE sorts the letter, translates it into machine code (compilation), and addresses it to the right post office box (the correct COM/USB port).
- The postman (the bootloader on the ATmega328P) delivers the letter to the working staff (the CPU), who then run the instructions forever in a loop.

Every time you press the **Upload** button (➡), the IDE performs three invisible steps: **Compile → Verify → Upload via the USB-Serial converter (CH340/ATmega16U2)**.

## 1.3 LED (Light Emitting Diode) and Switch — The Two Fundamental I/O Elements

| Element | Role in Embedded System | Arduino Perspective |
|---|---|---|
| **LED** | Visual Output / Actuator | A `digitalWrite()` target on a GPIO pin configured as `OUTPUT` |
| **Switch (Push Button)** | User Input / Sensor | A `digitalRead()` source on a GPIO pin configured as `INPUT` or `INPUT_PULLUP` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> LED and Switch interfacing form the **Module 4 cornerstone** of PBRUT404 because they teach the *complete* digital I/O cycle: configure pin → read/write logic level → act upon it. Mastery of this cycle is essential for KTU lab exams and the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

## 1.4 The ATmega328P — Heart of Arduino UNO

The Arduino UNO board (the KTU standard reference platform for PBRUT404) is built around the **ATmega328P**, an 8-bit AVR RISC microcontroller from Microchip (formerly Atmel). Relevant specifications for this module:

- **Flash Memory:** 32 KB (0.5 KB used by bootloader)
- **SRAM:** 2 KB
- **EEPROM:** 1 KB
- **Clock Speed:** 16 MHz
- **Digital I/O Pins:** 14 (of which 6 provide PWM)
- **Operating Voltage:** 5 V (logic level)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** When you use `pinMode(13, OUTPUT)`, you are programming **Port B, bit 5 (PB5)** of the ATmega328P via the Arduino abstraction layer. The IDE hides the low-level register manipulation behind a friendly API.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Arduino Sketch — The Three Mandatory Parts

Every Arduino sketch — no matter how complex — must contain **two functions**. A third (the *header comment block*) is a strong convention.

```cpp
/* ===== PART 1: HEADER COMMENT BLOCK (optional but standard) ===== */
 // Project: LED Blink with Switch Control
 // Author : Student Name
 // Date   : DD-MM-YYYY

/* ===== PART 2: setup() — Runs ONCE at power-up / reset ===== */
void setup() {
  // pinMode() calls, Serial.begin(), I2C/SPI init, etc.
}

/* ===== PART 3: loop() — Runs FOREVER, in an infinite while(1) loop ===== */
void loop() {
  // Your main logic goes here
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does `loop()` exist?**
> Embedded systems are **reactive machines** — they must perpetually sense the environment and respond. The Arduino framework hard-codes a `while(1) { loop(); }` after `setup()` returns. If you forget the `loop()` function, the compiler will throw a **compilation error**, not a runtime warning.

## 2.2 The Core Digital I/O API Cheat Sheet

The following four functions are the **only** functions you need to master LED and Switch programming:

| Function | Signature | Purpose | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pinMode(pin, mode)` | `void pinMode(uint8_t pin, uint8_t mode)` | Configures a digital pin as `INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, or `INPUT_PULLUP` | Called in `setup()` |
| `digitalWrite(pin, val)` | `void digitalWrite(uint8_t pin, uint8_t val)` | Drives pin to `HIGH` (5 V) or `LOW` (0 V) | Controlling an LED |
| `digitalRead(pin)` | `int digitalRead(uint8_t pin)` | Returns `HIGH` or `LOW` from a pin | Reading a switch state |
| `delay(ms)` | `void delay(unsigned long ms)` | Pauses execution for `ms` milliseconds | LED blink timing |

### 2.2.1 Logic Levels — The Golden Rule

$$V_{IH} \geq 0.6 \times V_{CC} = 0.6 \times 5\text{ V} = 3.0\text{ V (recognized as HIGH)}$$

$$V_{IL} \leq 0.3 \times V_{CC} = 0.3 \times 5\text{ V} = 1.5\text{ V (recognized as LOW)}$$

Any voltage in the **forbidden zone (1.5 V – 3.0 V)** results in undefined behavior — the pin may read either state. This is why *floating* switch inputs (with no pull-up/pull-down resistor) are dangerous.

## 2.3 The Pull-Up / Pull-Down Resistor Theory

When a switch is in its **open** position, the input pin is **electrically floating** — it can pick up stray electromagnetic noise (50 Hz mains hum, static, RF) and randomly read `HIGH` or `LOW`. To force a defined logic level, we use a **pull-up** (to VCC) or **pull-down** (to GND) resistor.

$$V_{pin} = V_{CC} \times \frac{R_{input}}{R_{input} + R_{pull}}$$

The **ATmega328P** has built-in pull-up resistors (typically **20 kΩ – 50 kΩ**) that can be enabled in software simply by declaring `pinMode(pin, INPUT_PULLUP)`. This eliminates the need for an external resistor — a major advantage in compact designs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:**
> When using `INPUT_PULLUP`, the logic is **inverted**:
> - Switch **OPEN** → Pin reads `HIGH` (pulled up to 5 V)
> - Switch **CLOSED** → Pin reads `LOW` (shorted to GND)
>
> Students frequently lose marks by writing the *opposite* logic in their code.

## 2.4 LED Current Limiting — Ohm's Law Application

An LED is a **non-ohmic** device. It has a forward voltage drop ($V_F$) and a maximum continuous current ($I_{max}$). Exceeding either destroys the LED. The series resistor $R_S$ is mandatory.

$$R_S = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_{LED}}$$

For a typical red LED with $V_F = 2.0\text{ V}$ and desired current $I_{LED} = 10\text{ mA}$ on a 5 V Arduino pin:

$$R_S = \frac{5 - 2.0}{0.010} = \frac{3.0}{0.010} = 300\text{ }\Omega$$

A standard **330 Ω** resistor is the universal KTU lab choice. For the built-in LED on pin 13, this resistor is **already soldered** on the UNO board.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Constants Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| LED Series Resistor | $R_S = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_{LED}}$ | 220 – 330 | Ω |
| LED Forward Voltage (Red) | $V_F$ | 1.8 – 2.2 | V |
| LED Forward Voltage (Blue/White) | $V_F$ | 3.0 – 3.4 | V |
| Arduino Logic HIGH | $V_{IH}$ | ≥ 3.0 | V |
| Arduino Logic LOW | $V_{IL}$ | ≤ 1.5 | V |
| Internal Pull-up Resistance | $R_{PU}$ | 20 – 50 | kΩ |
| Standard Delay Unit | `delay(1000)` | 1000 | ms |
| Default Baud Rate | `Serial.begin(9600)` | 9600 | bps |
| ATmega328P Clock | $f_{CLK}$ | 16 | MHz |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The LED-Switch pattern is the **atomic unit** of every embedded UI:
- **Microwave oven:** Switch (door sensor) + LED (display backlight).
- **Car dashboard:** Switch (button) + LED (indicator).
- **Industrial PLC:** Limit switch + status LED on every machine guard.
- **IoT Smart Home:** Push button (manual override) + LED (status feedback).

Mastering this pattern is the first step toward designing Human-Machine Interfaces (HMI).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Code Analysis & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Sample Code 1 — Blinking the Built-in LED (The "Hello World" of Embedded)

This is the **first program** every Arduino student writes. It blinks the built-in LED connected to **digital pin 13** at 1 Hz (0.5 s ON, 0.5 s OFF).

```cpp
/* ============================================================
   PROGRAM  : LED_Blink.ino
   BOARD    : Arduino UNO (ATmega328P)
   FUNCTION : Toggles the built-in LED on pin 13 every 500 ms
   ============================================================ */

// ---- CONSTANT DEFINITIONS (good practice) ----
const int LED_PIN = 13;        // Built-in LED on Arduino UNO
const int BLINK_DELAY = 500;   // Half-period in milliseconds

void setup() {
  // Step 1: Configure pin 13 as a digital output
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  // Step 2: Drive pin HIGH -> LED ON
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH);
  delay(BLINK_DELAY);          // Wait 500 ms
  
  // Step 3: Drive pin LOW -> LED OFF
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);
  delay(BLINK_DELAY);          // Wait 500 ms
}
```

### 3.1.1 Execution Trace (How the CPU executes this code)

| Time (ms) | Instruction Executed | Pin 13 Voltage | LED State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `pinMode(13, OUTPUT)` | High-Z → Output mode | OFF |
| 0 | `digitalWrite(13, HIGH)` | +5 V | **ON** |
| 500 | `delay(500)` completes | +5 V (held) | **ON** |
| 500 | `digitalWrite(13, LOW)` | 0 V | OFF |
| 1000 | `delay(500)` completes | 0 V (held) | OFF |
| 1000 | `loop()` restarts | — | repeats forever |

**Why one full cycle = 1 second:** The ON phase (500 ms) + OFF phase (500 ms) = 1000 ms = 1 s period, giving a frequency of **1 Hz**.

$$f_{blink} = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2 \times BLINK\_DELAY_{(seconds)}}$$

$$f_{blink} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.5} = 1.0\text{ Hz}$$

## 3.2 Sample Code 2 — External LED Blink with Series Resistor

Same as above, but using a **separate LED on pin 8** wired to GND through a 330 Ω resistor. This teaches the full hardware-software interface.

```cpp
/* ============================================================
   PROGRAM  : External_LED_Blink.ino
   HARDWARE : LED (anode) -> 330 ohm -> Pin 8 (cathode to GND)
   ============================================================ */

const int LED_PIN = 8;

void setup() {
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);    // Configure pin 8 as output
}

void loop() {
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH); // LED ON
  delay(1000);                 // 1 second
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);  // LED OFF
  delay(1000);                 // 1 second
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Pitfall:** Students often write the LED polarity **backwards** on the breadboard. The **anode** (longer lead, +) connects through the resistor to the Arduino pin; the **cathode** (shorter lead, flat side) connects to **GND**. Reversing this either does nothing or damages the LED.

## 3.3 Sample Code 3 — Reading a Push-Button Switch (with Internal Pull-up)

This is the classic **"Switch-controlled LED"** — the most common PBRUT404 lab experiment.

**Wiring:**
- One leg of the push button → **Pin 2**
- Other leg of the push button → **GND**
- LED on pin 13 (built-in) for visual feedback

```cpp
/* ============================================================
   PROGRAM  : Switch_Control_LED.ino
   HARDWARE : Push button between Pin 2 and GND
              Built-in LED on Pin 13
   LOGIC    : Press button -> LED ON  (ACTIVE-LOW)
              Release     -> LED OFF
   ============================================================ */

const int SWITCH_PIN = 2;      // Digital input pin
const int LED_PIN    = 13;     // Built-in LED

void setup() {
  pinMode(SWITCH_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP);  // Enable internal pull-up
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);           // LED as output
}

void loop() {
  // Read the switch state
  int switchState = digitalRead(SWITCH_PIN);
  
  // ACTIVE-LOW logic because of INPUT_PULLUP
  if (switchState == LOW) {
    // Button is pressed (shorted to GND)
    digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH);
  } else {
    // Button is released (pulled HIGH internally)
    digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);
  }
}
```

### 3.3.1 Logic Table (Truth Table) — REQUIRED in KTU Answers

| Switch Physical State | Pin 2 Voltage | `digitalRead()` Returns | LED State |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open (not pressed) | 5 V (pulled up) | `HIGH` | OFF |
| Closed (pressed) | 0 V (shorted to GND) | `LOW` | ON |

### 3.3.2 The `if-else` Decision Flow

$$V_{pin} = \begin{cases} 0\text{ V} & \text{if button is PRESSED (closed)} \\ 5\text{ V} & \text{if button is RELEASED (open)} \end{cases}$$

$$\text{LED} = \begin{cases} \text{ON} & \text{if } V_{pin} = 0\text{ V} \\ \text{OFF} & \text{if } V_{pin} = 5\text{ V} \end{cases}$$

This is a pure **combinational logic** mapping — the output is a direct function of the present input (ignoring mechanical bounce for now).

## 3.4 Switch Debouncing — The Professional Touch

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Debouncing is Necessary:**
> When a mechanical switch is pressed or released, the metal contacts **bounce** against each other for 1–10 ms before settling. Without debouncing, the microcontroller may interpret a single press as **5–50 rapid toggles**, causing the LED to flicker erratically. This is a guaranteed KTU viva question.

```cpp
/* ============================================================
   PROGRAM  : Debounced_Switch.ino
   HARDWARE : Same as Sample 3, plus debounce in software
   ============================================================ */

const int SWITCH_PIN = 2;
const int LED_PIN    = 13;
const unsigned long DEBOUNCE_MS = 50;  // 50 ms is standard

bool ledState = false;          // Tracks current LED state
bool lastStableReading = HIGH;  // Last debounced reading
bool lastRawReading = HIGH;     // Previous raw reading
unsigned long lastDebounceTime = 0;

void setup() {
  pinMode(SWITCH_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP);
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  bool reading = digitalRead(SWITCH_PIN);  // Raw reading
  
  // If reading changed, reset debounce timer
  if (reading != lastRawReading) {
    lastDebounceTime = millis();
  }
  
  // If reading has been stable for DEBOUNCE_MS, accept it
  if ((millis() - lastDebounceTime) > DEBOUNCE_MS) {
    if (reading != lastStableReading) {
      lastStableReading = reading;
      
      // Toggle LED only on a confirmed button press (HIGH -> LOW)
      if (lastStableReading == LOW) {
        ledState = !ledState;
        digitalWrite(LED_PIN, ledState ? HIGH : LOW);
      }
    }
  }
  
  lastRawReading = reading;
}
```

### 3.4.1 The Debounce Time Constant

The debounce interval should be **at least 10× the worst-case bounce time**:

$$T_{debounce} \geq 10 \times T_{bounce,max}$$

$$T_{debounce} \geq 10 \times 10\text{ ms} = 100\text{ ms (safe upper bound)}$$

$$T_{debounce} = 50\text{ ms (typical, balances responsiveness and stability)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardware vs. Software Debouncing:**
> - *Hardware:* Add a **0.1 µF capacitor** in parallel with the switch → forms an RC low-pass filter.
> - *Software:* Use the timer-based approach above, or `Bounce2` library.
> - KTU exams expect you to **mention both** for full marks.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Designing a Two-LED, Two-Switch Toggle System

**Problem Statement (typical KTU lab):**
Design an Arduino system with two switches (SW1, SW2) and two LEDs (LED1, LED2). LED1 should be ON only when SW1 is pressed; LED2 should be ON only when SW2 is pressed. Both switches use `INPUT_PULLUP`. Provide the complete sketch and the truth table.

**Solution:**

```cpp
const int SW1 = 2, SW2 = 3;
const int LED1 = 8, LED2 = 9;

void setup() {
  pinMode(SW1, INPUT_PULLUP);
  pinMode(SW2, INPUT_PULLUP);
  pinMode(LED1, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(LED2, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  // ACTIVE-LOW: pressed = LOW
  digitalWrite(LED1, (digitalRead(SW1) == LOW) ? HIGH : LOW);
  digitalWrite(LED2, (digitalRead(SW2) == LOW) ? HIGH : LOW);
}
```

**Truth Table:**

| SW1 (Pin 2) | SW2 (Pin 3) | LED1 (Pin 8) | LED2 (Pin 9) |
|---|---|---|---|
| HIGH (open) | HIGH (open) | OFF | OFF |
| LOW (pressed) | HIGH (open) | ON | OFF |
| HIGH (open) | LOW (pressed) | OFF | ON |
| LOW (pressed) | LOW (pressed) | ON | ON |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Arduino IDE Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer writes Sketch in IDE Editor] --> B[Click Verify Button]
    B --> C{Compilation Successful?}
    C -- No --> D[Show Error in Console]
    D --> A
    C -- Yes --> E[Generate Hex File in /tmp/build]
    E --> F[Click Upload Button]
    F --> G[IDE sends Reset Pulse via DTR line]
    G --> H[ATmega328P Bootloader starts]
    H --> I[Sketch transferred via UART at 115200 bps]
    I --> J[Bootloader jumps to Application]
    J --> K[setup runs once]
    K --> L[loop runs forever]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Arduino UNO Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER[Power Section]
        USB[USB 5V] --> REG[Voltage Regulator 5V]
        JACK[DC Jack 7-12V] --> REG
        REG --> VCC[+5V Rail]
    end
    
    subgraph MCU[ATmega328P Microcontroller]
        CPU[AVR CPU Core]
        FLASH[32KB Flash]
        RAM[2KB SRAM]
        GPIO[14x Digital I/O]
        ADC[6x 10-bit ADC]
        UART[UART Serial]
    end
    
    VCC --> MCU
    GPIO --> D2[Digital Pin 2]
    GPIO --> D8[Digital Pin 8]
    GPIO --> D13[Digital Pin 13]
    UART --> USB_CHIP[USB-Serial CH340]
    USB_CHIP --> USB_PORT[USB Type-B Port]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — LED Interfacing with Current-Limiting Resistor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIN8[Arduino Pin 8] --> R[330 ohm Resistor]
    R --> ANODE[LED Anode +]
    CATHODE[LED Cathode -] --> GND[Arduino GND]
    
    style PIN8 fill:#4CAF50,color:#fff
    style GND fill:#333,color:#fff
    style R fill:#FF9800,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Switch Interfacing with Internal Pull-up

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIN2[Arduino Pin 2] --> SW[Push Button Switch]
    SW --> GND_SW[GND Rail]
    PIN2 -.-> PU[Internal Pull-up 20-50k]
    PU -.-> VCC[VCC 5V]
    
    PIN2 --> READ[digitalRead returns HIGH when open, LOW when pressed]
    
    style PIN2 fill:#2196F3,color:#fff
    style SW fill:#9C27B0,color:#fff
    style GND_SW fill:#333,color:#fff
```

## 4.5 Mermaid State Diagram — LED State Machine with Debounce

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LED_OFF
    
    LED_OFF --> LED_ON : Switch pressed & debounce confirmed 50ms
    LED_ON --> LED_OFF : Switch pressed & debounce confirmed 50ms
    
    LED_OFF --> DEBOUNCE_WAIT1 : Raw reading changed
    DEBOUNCE_WAIT1 --> LED_OFF : Reading stable for 50ms
    DEBOUNCE_WAIT1 --> DEBOUNCE_WAIT1 : Reading still bouncing
    
    LED_ON --> DEBOUNCE_WAIT2 : Raw reading changed
    DEBOUNCE_WAIT2 --> LED_ON : Reading stable for 50ms
    DEBOUNCE_WAIT2 --> DEBOUNCE_WAIT2 : Reading still bouncing
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Sequence — Program Execution Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer
    participant IDE as Arduino IDE
    participant USB as USB-Serial Bridge
    participant MC as ATmega328P
    
    Dev->>IDE: Write sketch.ino
    Dev->>IDE: Click Upload
    IDE->>IDE: Compile with avr-gcc
    IDE->>USB: Send hex file
    USB->>MC: Pulse DTR to reset
    MC->>MC: Bootloader executes
    MC->>USB: Ready (sync at 115200 bps)
    USB->>MC: Transfer program flash
    MC->>MC: Jump to address 0x0000
    MC->>MC: setup() runs once
    loop Infinite
        MC->>MC: loop() runs
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (3 Marks)
**Explain the functions `pinMode()`, `digitalWrite()` and `digitalRead()` in the Arduino framework. State one typical use case for each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **`pinMode(pin, mode)`:** Configures a specified digital pin to behave either as an `INPUT` (to read sensors/switches), `OUTPUT` (to drive LEDs/motors), or `INPUT_PULLUP` (to enable the internal pull-up resistor). It is called inside `setup()` and takes two arguments: the pin number and the mode. *Typical use case:* `pinMode(13, OUTPUT);` — configures the built-in LED pin as an output.

- **`digitalWrite(pin, value)`:** Drives a previously configured `OUTPUT` pin to either `HIGH` (5 V, logic 1) or `LOW` (0 V, logic 0). *Typical use case:* `digitalWrite(13, HIGH);` — turns ON the LED on pin 13.

- **`digitalRead(pin)`:** Reads the instantaneous logic level on a digital input pin and returns either `HIGH` or `LOW`. *Typical use case:* `int state = digitalRead(2);` — reads the state of a push button connected to pin 2.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (3 Marks)
**What is meant by `INPUT_PULLUP` mode? Why is it preferred over an external pull-down resistor in most Arduino designs?**

**Model Answer:**

`INPUT_PULLUP` is a special pin configuration mode that enables the ATmega328P's **internal pull-up resistor (typically 20–50 kΩ)**, connecting the pin internally to +5 V through that resistor. This means when the pin is left *floating* (no external connection), `digitalRead()` will reliably return `HIGH` rather than an undefined value.

**Why preferred:**
1. **No external component required** — saves breadboard space and cost.
2. **No risk of forgetting the resistor** — the Arduino is internally protected from floating inputs.
3. **Clean wiring** — only two wires needed: pin and GND.
4. **Inverted (active-LOW) logic** — when the switch closes to GND, the pin reads LOW, which is a robust and noise-immune signal because the switch actively drives the line to a known potential.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### (a) Describe the complete architecture of an Arduino sketch. With neat code, write a program to blink an external LED connected to pin 8 (with a 330 Ω series resistor) at a 1-second interval. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Sketch Architecture (3 marks):**
An Arduino sketch consists of three logical sections:
1. **Header comment block** — describes the project, author, and date.
2. **`setup()` function** — executes exactly once at power-up or reset. Used for initialization (pin modes, serial, I²C, etc.).
3. **`loop()` function** — executes continuously in an infinite `while(1)` cycle. Contains the main reactive logic of the program.

The Arduino framework automatically inserts a `main()` function that calls `setup()` once and then repeatedly calls `loop()`.

**Program Code (4 marks):**

```cpp
/* LED_Blink_Pin8.ino
   External LED on Pin 8 with 330 ohm series resistor to GND */

const int LED_PIN = 8;

void setup() {
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);   // Configure pin 8 as output
}

void loop() {
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH); // LED ON
  delay(1000);                 // Wait 1 second
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);  // LED OFF
  delay(1000);                 // Wait 1 second
}
```

**Hardware connection:** Arduino Pin 8 → 330 Ω resistor → LED anode (+); LED cathode (–) → Arduino GND.

**Frequency calculation:**
$$f = \frac{1}{2 \times 1\text{ s}} = 0.5\text{ Hz (one complete ON+OFF cycle every 2 s)}$$

#### (b) Explain the need for switch debouncing. With a neat circuit diagram, write a program using `INPUT_PULLUP` to toggle an LED on pin 13 each time a push button on pin 2 is pressed. Include the debounce logic. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Need for debouncing (2 marks):**
Mechanical push-button switches do not make a single clean electrical contact. The metal contacts physically **bounce** against each other for 1–10 ms before settling, producing multiple rapid open/close transitions. A digital system sampling faster than this bounce duration will interpret one press as **multiple presses**, causing erratic LED toggling. Debouncing (hardware or software) filters these spurious transitions.

**Hardware circuit (2 marks):**
Push button connected between **Pin 2** and **GND**. No external resistor needed (internal pull-up enabled in software). LED on pin 13 (built-in, with onboard 330 Ω resistor) for visual output.

**Program code (3 marks):**

```cpp
const int SW_PIN = 2;
const int LED_PIN = 13;
const unsigned long DEBOUNCE_MS = 50;

bool ledState = false;
bool lastStable = HIGH;
bool lastRaw = HIGH;
unsigned long lastDebounceTime = 0;

void setup() {
  pinMode(SW_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP);
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);
}

void loop() {
  bool reading = digitalRead(SW_PIN);

  if (reading != lastRaw) {
    lastDebounceTime = millis();   // reset debounce timer
  }

  if ((millis() - lastDebounceTime) > DEBOUNCE_MS) {
    if (reading != lastStable) {
      lastStable = reading;
      if (lastStable == LOW) {     // confirmed press
        ledState = !ledState;
        digitalWrite(LED_PIN, ledState);
      }
    }
  }
  lastRaw = reading;
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose up to 4 marks here!):**
> 1. **Forgetting `INPUT_PULLUP`** → switch reads randomly; no credit for "working" code.
> 2. **Using `delay()` inside debounce logic** → blocks the CPU and is academically discouraged in 2024 scheme answers.
> 3. **Wrong active-LOW polarity** → writing `if (reading == HIGH)` when the switch pulls the pin LOW.
> 4. **Not explaining the bounce mechanism** → KTU expects a 1–2 line definition of *why* bounce occurs (mechanical contact chatter).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### (a) With a suitable circuit diagram, explain how to interface an LED to an Arduino UNO. Derive the value of the current-limiting resistor for a red LED ($V_F$ = 2.0 V, $I_{LED}$ = 15 mA) operating from a 5 V pin. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Circuit diagram (3 marks):** [Refer to SECTION 4.3 — LED with 330 Ω resistor in series between pin 8 and GND.]

**Interfacing procedure (2 marks):**
1. Connect the LED's **anode** (longer lead) to the Arduino digital pin through a series resistor.
2. Connect the LED's **cathode** (shorter lead, flat edge) to the Arduino **GND**.
3. Configure the pin as `OUTPUT` in `setup()`.
4. Use `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH)` to illuminate the LED.

**Resistor derivation (2 marks):**

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop:

$$V_{CC} = V_R + V_F$$

$$V_R = V_{CC} - V_F = 5 - 2.0 = 3.0\text{ V}$$

By Ohm's Law:

$$R_S = \frac{V_R}{I_{LED}} = \frac{3.0\text{ V}}{15\text{ mA}} = \frac{3.0}{0.015} = 200\text{ Ω}$$

**Closest standard E12 value: 220 Ω** (provides slightly less current, ~13.6 mA, well within safe limits).

#### (b) Write an Arduino program to read a push button on pin 4 (using `INPUT_PULLUP`) and display its state on the Serial Monitor at 9600 bps. Explain the `setup()` and `Serial.begin()` functions in detail. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Program (4 marks):**

```cpp
/* Switch_SerialMonitor.ino
   Reads a push button on pin 4 and prints state via UART */

const int SW_PIN = 4;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);              // Initialize UART at 9600 bps
  pinMode(SW_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP);   // Enable internal pull-up
  pinMode(13, OUTPUT);             // Status LED
}

void loop() {
  int state = digitalRead(SW_PIN);
  
  if (state == LOW) {
    Serial.println("Button PRESSED");
    digitalWrite(13, HIGH);
  } else {
    Serial.println("Button RELEASED");
    digitalWrite(13, LOW);
  }
  
  delay(300);   // Slow down Serial output to readable rate
}
```

**Explanation of `setup()` (1.5 marks):**
`setup()` is the first function called by the hidden `main()` when the Arduino boots or is reset. It runs **exactly once** and is used for *one-time* initialization: setting pin modes, starting communication protocols (Serial, I²C, SPI), attaching interrupts, and configuring peripherals. Code that should run periodically belongs in `loop()`, not `setup()`.

**Explanation of `Serial.begin(9600)` (1.5 marks):**
`Serial.begin(9600)` initializes the ATmega328P's built-in **UART (USART0)** hardware module at a baud rate of **9600 bits per second**. It configures the baud-rate generator registers (UBRR), enables the transmitter and receiver, and sets the data frame format to 8N1 (8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit) — the standard for Arduino. After this call, `Serial.println()` and `Serial.print()` can be used to send debug text to the PC's Serial Monitor, which must also be set to 9600 bps to display the output correctly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose up to 4 marks here!):**
> 1. **Baud rate mismatch** — forgetting to set the Serial Monitor to 9600 bps in the explanation.
> 2. **Not mentioning the active-LOW logic** — students write `if (state == HIGH)` when the switch is active-LOW.
> 3. **Confusing `Serial.print()` with `Serial.println()`** — the former does not add a newline; output becomes unreadable.
> 4. **Forgetting the explanation** — KTU ESE 14-mark questions **always** require theory alongside code. Pure code with no explanation scores a maximum of 7–8 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This Before the Exam:**

- ☐ Arduino program file is called a **Sketch** with `.ino` extension.
- ☐ Every sketch has **two mandatory functions**: `setup()` (runs once) and `loop()` (runs forever).
- ☐ `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)` → LED; `pinMode(pin, INPUT_PULLUP)` → Switch.
- ☐ **`INPUT_PULLUP` inverts the logic**: switch pressed = `LOW`, released = `HIGH`.
- ☐ `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH)` = +5 V on the pin (LED ON if wired correctly).
- ☐ `digitalRead(pin)` returns either `HIGH` or `LOW` (integer constants, not 1/0).
- ☐ **`delay(ms)` pauses for milliseconds** — 1000 ms = 1 second; avoid inside timing-critical code.
- ☐ LED needs a **series resistor** (220–330 Ω typical) — calculate using $R = (V_{CC} - V_F)/I_{LED}$.
- ☐ **Switch bouncing** lasts 1–10 ms; debounce with 50 ms software delay or 0.1 µF hardware capacitor.
- ☐ `Serial.begin(9600)` opens UART at **9600 bps**; Serial Monitor must match.
- ☐ **ATmega328P clock = 16 MHz**, logic levels: HIGH ≥ 3.0 V, LOW ≤ 1.5 V (for Vcc = 5 V).
- ☐ LED **anode** (long lead) connects toward the Arduino pin; **cathode** (short/flat) toward GND.
- ☐ Board constants: UNO has **14 digital I/O pins** (6 PWM), **6 analog inputs** (10-bit ADC), **32 KB flash**.
- ☐ Compilation pipeline: **Source → avr-gcc → .hex → bootloader → Flash memory → Execution**.
- ☐ KTU exam favourite: always draw a **truth table** when a question mentions "based on input conditions".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** DC Motor # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **DC Motor** (Direct Current Motor) is an electromechanical actuator that converts **direct current (DC) electrical energy** into **rotational mechanical energy** based on the principle of **Lorentz force**, where a current-carrying conductor placed within a magnetic field experiences a mechanical force. In the context of **Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems (PBRUT404)**, the DC Motor is studied primarily as an **actuator peripheral** that is interfaced with a microcontroller (e.g., 8051, ARM, PIC, or Arduino-class devices) via **driver circuits** such as H-bridges and **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** signals to achieve controlled **speed** and **directional rotation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBRUT404 Module 4):** > "A DC motor is a rotary electrical machine that operates on direct current, producing continuous rotation through the interaction of magnetic fields. In embedded systems, DC motors are controlled by microcontrollers using PWM signals and driver ICs like L293D/L298N to vary speed and direction." ## 1.2 Classification of DC Motors (Relevant to Embedded Systems) | Class | Sub-type | Typical Embedded Use | |---|---|---| | **Brushed DC Motor** | Permanent Magnet (PMDC) | Toy cars, small fans, robotic wheels | | **Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)** | Sensorless / Sensored | Drones, hard disk drives, EV auxiliary | | **Servo Motor** | DC Servo (positional) | Robotic arms, CNC, camera gimbals | | **Stepper Motor** | (Often grouped) | 3D printers, linear actuators | > [!NOTE] > For the KTU PBRUT404 syllabus, the focus is on **PMDC (Permanent Magnet DC) motors** and **Servo motors**, with emphasis on **interfacing circuits, PWM control, and driver ICs**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine a **waterwheel in a river**: - The **flowing water** (current) pushes the wheel blades (conductors). - The stronger the flow, the faster the wheel rotates. - If you **reverse the river's flow direction**, the wheel spins the opposite way. A DC motor works on a similar principle: - **Electric current** (water) flows through conductors (blades) inside a **magnetic field** (river banks guiding the flow). - The interaction produces **torque (rotational force)**, causing the shaft to spin. - **Reverse the current** → **reverse the rotation**. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook for KTU Viva:** *"Voltage pushes current, current in a magnetic field produces force, force at a radius produces torque, torque over time produces rotation."* ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Back EMF Constant (Kₑ):** Measured in **V·s/rad** (or V/(rad/s)) - **Torque Constant (Kₜ):** Measured in **N·m/A**; numerically Kₑ = Kₜ in SI units - **Standard Operating Voltage:** **3.3 V, 5 V, 6 V, 12 V, 24 V** - **No-Load Speed (ω₀):** in **RPM** or **rad/s** - **Stall Torque:** Maximum torque at zero speed, in **N·m** --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Lorentz Force A DC motor operates on **two fundamental electromagnetic principles**: 1. **A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force** (Motor Effect). 2. **A rotating conductor in a magnetic field induces a voltage** (Generator Effect — produces back EMF). ### Force on a Conductor When a conductor of length **L** carrying current **I** is placed perpendicular to a magnetic flux density **B**, the force experienced is: $$F = B \cdot I \cdot L$$ Where: - $F$ = Force in **Newtons (N)** - $B$ = Magnetic flux density in **Tesla (T)** - $I$ = Current in **Amperes (A)** - $L$ = Length of conductor in **meters (m)** ### Torque Generation If this force acts at a radius **r** from the shaft axis, the resulting **torque** is: $$T = F \cdot r = B \cdot I \cdot L \cdot r$$ For a motor with **N** conductors and effective radius **r**, the total torque becomes: $$T = K_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$ Where: - $T$ = Torque in **N·m** - $K_t$ = Motor construction constant - $\Phi$ = Magnetic flux per pole in **Weber (Wb)** - $I_a$ = Armature current in **A** ## 2.2 Back EMF (Counter EMF) As the armature rotates inside the magnetic field, it **cuts the magnetic flux lines** and generates a voltage that **opposes the applied voltage** (per Lenz's Law). This is called **Back EMF** ($E_b$). $$E_b = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$ Where: - $E_b$ = Back EMF in **Volts (V)** - $K_e$ = EMF constant (machine constant) - $\Phi$ = Flux per pole in **Wb** - $\omega$ = Angular velocity in **rad/s** ## 2.3 Armature Voltage Equation (KVL of the Motor) Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** to the armature circuit: $$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$$ Rearranging for speed (since $E_b = K_e \Phi \omega$): $$V = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega + I_a \cdot R_a$$ Solving for angular velocity: $$\omega = \frac{V - I_a \cdot R_a}{K_e \cdot \Phi}$$ ## 2.4 Speed Equation (in RPM) Converting $\omega$ (rad/s) to $N$ (RPM) using $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot \omega}{2\pi}$: $$N = \frac{60 \cdot (V - I_a \cdot R_a)}{2\pi \cdot K_e \cdot \Phi}$$ ## 2.5 Power Equations | Quantity | Formula | Unit | |---|---|---| | Electrical Input Power | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_a$ | **Watts (W)** | | Mechanical Output Power | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$ | **Watts (W)** | | Power Lost as Heat (Copper) | $P_{Cu} = I_a^2 \cdot R_a$ | **Watts (W)** | | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | **%** | ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula | Description | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F = B \cdot I \cdot L$ | Force on a conductor | Motor sizing | | 2 | $T = K_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | Torque equation | Torque-speed design | | 3 | $E_b = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$ | Back EMF | Speed estimation | | 4 | $V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$ | Armature KVL | Driver design | | 5 | $\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e \Phi}$ | Speed equation | Speed control | | 6 | $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot (V - I_a R_a)}{2\pi K_e \Phi}$ | Speed in RPM | Practical spec | | 7 | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_a$ | Input power | Power budget | | 8 | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$ | Output power | Mechanical load design | | 9 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ | PWM average voltage | Speed control | | 10 | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | Duty cycle | PWM generation | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL CONSTRAINT:** In SI units, the EMF constant equals the torque constant: $K_e = K_t$ (when $\omega$ is in rad/s and $T$ is in N·m). This is a commonly tested KTU question. ## 2.7 Why DC Motors Are Important in Embedded Systems - **Simplicity of control:** A single DC voltage (or PWM) controls speed; polarity controls direction. - **High starting torque:** Suitable for robotics and conveyor applications. - **Easy interfacing:** Can be controlled by a single GPIO/PWM pin via a driver IC. - **Predictable response:** Linear speed-torque relationship simplifies feedback control. - **Low cost:** Available in standard voltage ranges (3.3 V, 5 V, 12 V). > [!TIP] > **Real-World Application:** In a **line-follower robot** (KTU mini-project favorite), two DC motors drive the wheels, and their **differential speed** (via different PWM duty cycles) steers the robot. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Lorentz Force to Torque Equation We start from the fundamental force on a single conductor inside the motor's magnetic field. ### Step 1: Force on a Single Conductor A straight conductor of active length $L$ carries current $I_a$ in a uniform magnetic field of flux density $B$. The force is: $$F_{single} = B \cdot I_a \cdot L$$ > **Logic:** This is the **Biot-Savart / Lorentz force law** applied to a current element. ### Step 2: Number of Conductors in Parallel Paths For a wave-wound armature with **N** total conductors arranged in **A** parallel paths, the number of conductors per path is $N / A$, and the current per conductor is $I_a / A$. However, the **total tangential force** is the sum of forces from all conductors. ### Step 3: Converting Force to Torque If each conductor is at an effective radius $r$ from the shaft, the torque per conductor is $F \cdot r$. Summing over all $N$ conductors: $$T = N \cdot B \cdot I_a \cdot L \cdot r / A$$ Grouping all motor-construction-dependent terms into a single constant $K_t$: $$T = K_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$ > **Logic:** Since $\Phi = B \cdot A_{pole}$ (where $A_{pole}$ is the pole area), the flux $\Phi$ replaces $B$, and the remaining geometric factors merge into $K_t$. ### Step 4: Back EMF Derivation By **Faraday's Law**, the EMF induced in a rotating armature is proportional to the rate of change of flux linkage: $$E_b = \frac{d\Psi}{dt}$$ For a motor rotating at angular velocity $\omega$, the flux cut per unit time is $\Phi \cdot \omega$. Hence: $$E_b = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$ ### Step 5: Combining to Get the Speed Equation Apply KVL to the armature loop (supply = back EMF + IR drop): $$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$$ $$V = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega + I_a \cdot R_a$$ Solving for $\omega$: $$\omega = \frac{V - I_a \cdot R_a}{K_e \cdot \Phi}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: PWM Average Voltage A PWM signal has period $T$, ON time $T_{on}$, and OFF time $T_{off}$ such that $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$. The **duty cycle** is: $$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$ The **average DC voltage** delivered to the motor is: $$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T_{on}} V_{supply} \, dt + \frac{1}{T} \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt$$ $$V_{avg} = V_{supply} \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T} = D \cdot V_{supply}$$ > **Logic:** The motor's mechanical inertia filters the high-frequency switching, so it "sees" only the average voltage. Hence, **higher duty cycle → higher average voltage → higher speed**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: No-Load vs. Loaded Condition | Parameter | No-Load Condition | Loaded Condition | |---|---|---| | Armature current $I_a$ | Very small (just to overcome friction) | Large (proportional to load torque) | | Back EMF $E_b$ | Maximum (since $\omega$ is high) | Reduced (since $\omega$ drops) | | $I_a \cdot R_a$ drop | Negligible | Significant | | Speed $\omega$ | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_e \Phi}$ (theoretical max) | $\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e \Phi}$ | | Torque $T$ | Near zero (friction only) | $T = K_t \Phi I_a$ (load-dependent) | | Power output | Zero | Maximum (at $\approx 50\%$ of no-load speed) | > [!TIP] > **KTU Conceptual Question:** "Why does a DC motor draw maximum current at the moment of starting?" > **Answer:** At start, $\omega = 0$, so $E_b = 0$. The entire supply voltage drops across $R_a$, giving $I_a = V / R_a$ — typically **6 to 10 times** the rated current. This is why **inrush current limiting** and **soft-start PWM ramping** are critical in embedded designs. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A 12 V DC motor has an armature resistance $R_a = 1.2 \, \Omega$ and a torque constant $K_t = 0.05 \, \text{N·m/A}$. At a load, the armature draws $I_a = 1.5 \, \text{A}$. Find: (a) The torque developed. (b) The back EMF. (c) The speed in RPM. (d) The mechanical output power. (e) The efficiency. ### Solution **(a) Torque developed:** $$T = K_t \cdot I_a = 0.05 \times 1.5 = 0.075 \, \text{N·m}$$ **(b) Back EMF:** $$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 12 - (1.5 \times 1.2) = 12 - 1.8 = 10.2 \, \text{V}$$ **(c) Speed in RPM:** Since $K_e = K_t = 0.05$ in SI units, $E_b = K_e \Phi \omega$ but here we use the equivalent form: $$\omega = \frac{E_b}{K_e} = \frac{10.2}{0.05} = 204 \, \text{rad/s}$$ Converting to RPM: $$N = \frac{60 \cdot \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 204}{2\pi} = \frac{12240}{6.2832} \approx 1948 \, \text{RPM}$$ **(d) Mechanical output power:** $$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = 0.075 \times 204 = 15.3 \, \text{W}$$ **(e) Efficiency:** $$P_{in} = V \cdot I_a = 12 \times 1.5 = 18 \, \text{W}$$ $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{15.3}{18} \times 100\% = 85\%$$ > **Verification check:** Power loss $= I_a^2 R_a = 1.5^2 \times 1.2 = 2.7 \, \text{W}$. Total $= 15.3 + 2.7 = 18 \, \text{W} = P_{in}$. ✓ --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — DC Motor in an Embedded System The following **Mermaid functional block diagram** illustrates the complete signal and power flow from a microcontroller to a DC motor load. ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU["Microcontroller
8051 or ARM Cortex"] PWM["PWM Module
Duty Cycle D"] DIR1["GPIO Direction Pin 1"] DIR2["GPIO Direction Pin 2"] DRV["Motor Driver IC
L293D or L298N"] HBR["H-Bridge
4 x MOSFETs"] MOT["DC Motor
PMDC 12V"] PSU["DC Power Supply
12V 2A"] FB["Speed Sensor
Optional Encoder"] subgraph CPWR["Power Section"] PSU end subgraph CTRL["Control Section"] MCU PWM DIR1 DIR2 end subgraph DRV_S["Driver Section"] DRV HBR end subgraph ACT["Actuator Section"] MOT end subgraph SENS["Feedback Section"] FB end PSU -->|Vmot 12V| DRV MCU -->|PWM signal| DRV MCU -->|DIR1| DRV MCU -->|DIR2| DRV DRV -->|Switched PWM| HBR HBR -->|Bidirectional current| MOT MOT -->|Rotation| FB FB -->|Tach pulses| MCU ``` ## 4.2 H-Bridge Operation — Forward and Reverse Modes The H-bridge is a 4-switch topology that allows **bidirectional current** through the motor by selectively activating diagonal switch pairs. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HB["H-Bridge Topology"] V1["Q1 TOP LEFT
Switch"] V2["Q2 TOP RIGHT
Switch"] V3["Q3 BOTTOM LEFT
Switch"] V4["Q4 BOTTOM RIGHT
Switch"] end M["DC Motor
Armature"] SUP["V+ Supply"] GND["Ground GND"] SUP --- V1 SUP --- V2 V1 --- M V2 --- M M --- V3 M --- V4 V3 --- GND V4 --- GND FWD["FORWARD Mode
Q1 ON, Q4 ON
Current: V+ to GND via M"] REV["REVERSE Mode
Q2 ON, Q3 ON
Current: V+ to GND via M opposite direction"] BRAKE["BRAKE Mode
Q1 ON, Q3 ON or Q2 ON, Q4 ON
Both terminals shorted"] COAST["COAST Mode
All switches OFF
Motor freewheels"] FWD -.Controls.-> V1 FWD -.Controls.-> V4 REV -.Controls.-> V2 REV -.Controls.-> V3 ``` > [!WARNING] > **NEVER** turn ON **Q1 and Q2 simultaneously** (or **Q3 and Q4**) — this creates a **shoot-through short circuit** across the supply, destroying the driver. This is the **#1 killer** of H-bridge motor drivers in student projects. ## 4.3 PWM Speed Control — Timing Diagram (Textual Schematic) The figure below describes the **PWM signal generation and motor response**: | Time Interval | PWM Pin State | Switch Driver | Motor Terminal | Effect | |---|---|---|---|---| | $0 \to T_{on}$ | HIGH | ON | Energized | Motor accelerates | | $T_{on} \to T$ | LOW | OFF | Disconnected | Motor decelerates (coast) | | Cycle repeats | Period $T$ | — | — | Average voltage = $D \cdot V$ | **Duty Cycle vs. Speed (for constant load):** | Duty Cycle D | Average Voltage | Approx. Speed (% of max) | |---|---|---| | 0 % | 0 V | 0 % (stopped) | | 25 % | 3 V (for 12 V) | ~22 % | | 50 % | 6 V | ~48 % | | 75 % | 9 V | ~73 % | | 100 % | 12 V | ~95 % (limited by load) | ## 4.4 Microcontroller Interfacing — L293D Motor Driver Pinout ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L293D["L293D Dual H-Bridge Driver IC"] P1["Pin 1 Enable 1 2"] P2["Pin 2 Input 1A"] P3["Pin 3 Output 1Y"] P4["Pin 4 GND"] P5["Pin 5 GND"] P6["Pin 6 Output 2Y"] P7["Pin 7 Input 2A"] P8["Pin 8 Vcc2 Motor Supply"] P9["Pin 9 Enable 2 2"] P10["Pin 10 Input 3A"] P11["Pin 11 Output 3Y"] P12["Pin 12 GND"] P13["Pin 13 GND"] P14["Pin 14 Output 4Y"] P15["Pin 15 Input 4A"] P16["Pin 16 Vcc1 Logic 5V"] end MC["Microcontroller
PWM and GPIO"] M1["Motor A"] M2["Motor B"] PS["12V Supply"] L5V["5V Logic"] MC -->|PWM| P1 MC -->|DIR A| P2 MC -->|DIR B| P7 MC -->|PWM2| P9 P3 --> M1 P6 --> M1 P11 --> M2 P14 --> M2 PS --> P8 L5V --> P16 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **L293D** can drive **2 DC motors bidirectionally** at up to **600 mA continuous** per channel, with **built-in flyback diodes** — the most popular KTU lab component for motor interfacing. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Back EMF in a DC Motor. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Model Answer:** Back EMF (Counter Electromotive Force) is the voltage generated in the armature of a DC motor due to the rotation of the armature conductors within the magnetic field, in **opposition** to the applied supply voltage, as per **Lenz's Law**. **Mathematical Expression:** $$E_b = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$ **Significance:** - It is **directly proportional** to motor speed and flux. - At **starting** (ω = 0), $E_b$ = 0, so armature current is maximum. - It acts as a **natural feedback** mechanism — as load increases, speed drops, $E_b$ drops, and more current flows to produce more torque. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU One-liner to memorize:** *"Back EMF is the generator effect inside a motor — it opposes the supply and regulates the current."* --- ### Question 2: What is the role of PWM in DC motor speed control? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** controls the **average voltage** delivered to the DC motor by rapidly switching the supply ON and OFF at a fixed frequency. The ratio of ON time to total period is called the **Duty Cycle (D)**. **Average Voltage:** $$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$$ **Why PWM (and not variable DC voltage)?** 1. **Efficiency:** The driver switches are either fully ON or fully OFF → minimal power loss (unlike linear regulators). 2. **Heat reduction:** No dissipative voltage drop across the driver. 3. **Easy generation:** Microcontroller timers (e.g., 8051 Timer 0, STM32 TIM1) natively generate PWM. 4. **Precise control:** Digital duty cycle values are exact and repeatable. > [!NOTE] > Typical PWM frequency for DC motors: **1 kHz to 25 kHz** (above audible range to avoid whining noise). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Design & Analysis **(a)** Derive the **torque equation** and **back EMF equation** of a DC motor from first principles, clearly stating the role of **Lorentz force** and **Faraday's Law**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **12 V, 0.8 Ω armature resistance** PMDC motor draws **2 A** at a certain load. Calculate the **(i)** back EMF, **(ii)** speed in RPM (assume $K_e \Phi = 0.05 \, \text{V·s/rad}$), **(iii)** developed torque, and **(iv)** efficiency. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Derivation:** **Step 1 — Lorentz Force on a Conductor:** A current-carrying conductor of length $L$ in a magnetic field of density $B$ experiences a force: $$F = B \cdot I \cdot L$$ **Step 2 — Force on Armature:** The armature of a DC motor contains **N** conductors arranged around the periphery. The total tangential force on all conductors is: $$F_{total} = N \cdot B \cdot I_a \cdot L / A$$ where $A$ is the number of parallel paths (2 for wave winding, $P$ for lap winding). **Step 3 — Torque Generation:** Each conductor acts at a mean radius $r$ from the shaft, so total torque is: $$T = F_{total} \cdot r = \left(\frac{N \cdot B \cdot L \cdot r}{A}\right) \cdot I_a$$ Defining $K_t = \dfrac{N \cdot B \cdot L \cdot r}{A \cdot \Phi_{pole}}$ and noting that $B \cdot A_{pole} = \Phi$: $$\boxed{T = K_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a}$$ **Step 4 — Back EMF via Faraday's Law:** As the armature rotates at angular velocity $\omega$, each conductor cuts flux at a rate $\Phi \cdot \omega$. Summing over all conductors: $$E_b = \left(\frac{N \cdot \Phi}{A}\right) \cdot \omega = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$ **Step 5 — Key Relationship:** In SI units: $K_e = K_t$, and the **electromagnetic power conversion** is conserved: $$E_b \cdot I_a = T \cdot \omega$$ --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $V = 12 \, \text{V}$, $R_a = 0.8 \, \Omega$, $I_a = 2 \, \text{A}$, $K_e \Phi = 0.05 \, \text{V·s/rad}$ **(i) Back EMF:** $$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 12 - (2 \times 0.8) = 12 - 1.6 = 10.4 \, \text{V}$$ **(ii) Speed in RPM:** Angular velocity: $$\omega = \frac{E_b}{K_e \Phi} = \frac{10.4}{0.05} = 208 \, \text{rad/s}$$ Converting to RPM: $$N = \frac{60 \times 208}{2\pi} = \frac{12480}{6.2832} \approx 1986 \, \text{RPM}$$ **(iii) Developed Torque:** Since $K_t = K_e = 0.05$ in SI units: $$T = K_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a = 0.05 \times 2 = 0.1 \, \text{N·m}$$ **(iv) Efficiency:** Input power: $$P_{in} = V \cdot I_a = 12 \times 2 = 24 \, \text{W}$$ Output power: $$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = 0.1 \times 208 = 20.8 \, \text{W}$$ Efficiency: $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{20.8}{24} \times 100\% \approx 86.67\%$$ **Verification:** Power loss $= I_a^2 R_a = 4 \times 0.8 = 3.2 \, \text{W}$. Then $P_{in} - P_{loss} = 24 - 3.2 = 20.8 \, \text{W} = P_{out}$. ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain the **H-bridge circuit** for DC motor direction control. With the help of a **truth table**, describe Forward, Reverse, Brake, and Coast modes. What is **shoot-through fault**? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Interface a **5 V PMDC motor** with an **8051 microcontroller** using the **L293D driver IC**. Draw the interfacing circuit and write an **embedded C program** (using Keil/SDCC) to: - Rotate the motor **forward** for 2 seconds, - **Stop** for 1 second, - Rotate **reverse** for 2 seconds, - Repeat continuously using a **PWM duty cycle of 60 %**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — H-Bridge Theory:** An **H-bridge** consists of 4 switches (typically BJT/MOSFET/IGBT) arranged in an "H" configuration, with the motor forming the crossbar. The four switches are labeled **Q1, Q2 (top)** and **Q3, Q4 (bottom)**. **Truth Table:** | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Motor State | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **FORWARD** — current flows V+ → Q1 → Motor → Q4 → GND | | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | **REVERSE** — current flows V+ → Q2 → Motor → Q3 → GND | | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **BRAKE (dynamic)** — motor shorted via Q1 and Q3 | | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **BRAKE (dynamic)** — motor shorted via Q2 and Q4 | | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **COAST (freewheel)** — motor terminals floating | | 1 | 1 | X | X | **SHOOT-THROUGH (FAULT)** — direct V+ to GND short | **Shoot-through fault** occurs when **both top and bottom switches on the same leg are ON simultaneously**, creating a direct short from V+ to GND. This draws enormous current, causes **instantaneous destruction** of the switches, and may damage the power supply. > [!WARNING] > **Anti-shoot-through protection:** Use **dead-time insertion** (a small delay of ~1 µs between turning OFF one switch and turning ON the complementary switch) in software or hardware. **Part (b) — Interfacing Diagram and Code:** **Interfacing Connections (8051 to L293D):** | 8051 Pin | L293D Pin | Function | |---|---|---| | P1.0 | Pin 2 (1A) | Motor A direction | | P1.1 | Pin 7 (2A) | Motor A direction | | P1.2 | Pin 1 (1,2EN) | PWM enable for Motor A | | P3.0 (RxD) | (Tied to 1,2EN) | Alternate PWM via Timer | | Vcc (5 V) | Pin 16 (Vcc1) | Logic supply | | 12 V external | Pin 8 (Vcc2) | Motor supply | | Motor terminals | Pin 3, Pin 6 | Output to motor | **Embedded C Program (8051, using Keil C51 syntax):** ```c #include // Pin definitions sbit MOTOR_IN1 = P1^0; // L293D pin 2 (1A) sbit MOTOR_IN2 = P1^1; // L293D pin 7 (2A) sbit MOTOR_PWM = P1^2; // L293D pin 1 (1,2EN) - PWM enable // Software delay function (~1 ms per iteration at 11.0592 MHz crystal) void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) { unsigned int i, j; for (i = 0; i < ms; i++) for (j = 0; j < 120; j++); } // Generate PWM with given duty cycle (0-100) at ~1 kHz void set_pwm(unsigned char duty) { unsigned int on_time, off_time; on_time = (unsigned int)duty * 10; // ON time in microseconds off_time = (unsigned int)(100 - duty) * 10; MOTOR_PWM = 1; delay_ms(on_time / 1000 + 1); // Approximate ON delay MOTOR_PWM = 0; delay_ms(off_time / 1000 + 1); // Approximate OFF delay } void main(void) { unsigned char duty = 60; // 60% duty cycle while (1) { // FORWARD for 2 seconds MOTOR_IN1 = 1; MOTOR_IN2 = 0; set_pwm(duty); delay_ms(2000); // STOP for 1 second MOTOR_IN1 = 0; MOTOR_IN2 = 0; delay_ms(1000); // REVERSE for 2 seconds MOTOR_IN1 = 0; MOTOR_IN2 = 1; set_pwm(duty); delay_ms(2000); // STOP for 1 second before repeating MOTOR_IN1 = 0; MOTOR_IN2 = 0; delay_ms(1000); } } ``` **Program Logic Explanation:** 1. **MOTOR_IN1 = 1, MOTOR_IN2 = 0** → current flows from V+ to GND through the motor in one direction → **FORWARD** rotation. 2. **MOTOR_IN1 = 0, MOTOR_IN2 = 1** → current reverses → **REVERSE** rotation. 3. **MOTOR_IN1 = 0, MOTOR_IN2 = 0** → both bottom switches OFF → **COAST** (natural stop). 4. The **set_pwm(60)** function toggles the EN pin rapidly to achieve **60 % duty cycle**, giving an average voltage of $0.6 \times 12 = 7.2 \, \text{V}$ to the motor. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. Forgetting to include **flyback diodes** across the motor terminals in the circuit diagram (L293D has internal diodes, but mention it). > 2. **Wrong PWM frequency:** Below 100 Hz → audible whine; above 50 kHz → switching losses dominate. Stick to **1–25 kHz**. > 3. **No dead-time:** Switching direction without stopping the motor first causes **shoot-through** and can destroy the L293D in milliseconds. > 4. **Confusing stall current with running current:** A 12 V motor with $R_a = 1 \, \Omega$ can draw **12 A at stall** — always check the driver's current rating (L293D = 600 mA, L298 = 2 A). > 5. Forgetting **decoupling capacitors** (100 nF) on the Vcc pins of L293D. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Summary for KTU PBRUT404 Module 4 — DC Motor** ### Core Concepts - ✅ DC motor converts **electrical energy → mechanical rotation** using **Lorentz force** ($F = BIL$). - ✅ **PMDC (Permanent Magnet DC) motor** is the most common type in embedded applications. - ✅ **Back EMF** ($E_b = K_e \Phi \omega$) opposes the supply and acts as self-regulating feedback. - ✅ **Armature equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ — fundamental KVL. - ✅ **Torque equation:** $T = K_t \Phi I_a$ — linear with armature current. - ✅ **Speed equation:** $\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e \Phi}$ — speed drops with load. ### Control Methods - ✅ **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** is the **preferred speed control method** in embedded systems. - ✅ **Duty cycle** $D = T_{on} / T$ → Average voltage $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$. - ✅ **H-Bridge** (4 switches in H-config) provides **bidirectional rotation**. - ✅ **L293D / L298N** are standard **motor driver ICs** for microcontroller interfacing. - ✅ Always include **dead-time** between direction changes to prevent **shoot-through**. ### Key Numerical Relationships - ✅ **Stall current** $I_{stall} = V / R_a$ — can be **6 to 10× rated current**; use soft-start. - ✅ **No-load speed** $\omega_0 = V / (K_e \Phi)$ — theoretical maximum. - ✅ In SI units, $K_e = K_t$ — always quote the correct unit. - ✅ **Power balance:** $V \cdot I_a = T \cdot \omega + I_a^2 R_a$. ### Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid - ❌ Don't forget the **sign of back EMF** — it **opposes** the supply. - ❌ Don't confuse **servo motors** (positional, 0–180°) with **continuous rotation DC motors** (variable speed, full 360°). - ❌ Don't omit the **enable pin** in the L293D circuit — without it, PWM control is impossible. - ❌ Don't forget **heat sinks** for L298N at high currents. - ❌ Don't use **analogWrite** in 8051 — 8051 needs **timer-based PWM** generation (unlike Arduino). ### Practical / Lab Points - 🔧 Use **optocouplers** (e.g., PC817) to isolate the noisy motor circuit from the microcontroller. - 🔧 Use **snubber diodes** or **TVS diodes** across motor terminals for inductive kickback protection. - 🔧 Use **current-sense resistors** + ADC for closed-loop current control. - 🔧 Use **encoders** (quadrature) for closed-loop speed control in robotics applications. ### Sample Question Patterns - 📝 **Define:** Back EMF, Duty Cycle, H-Bridge, PWM, Torque constant. - 📝 **Derive:** Torque equation, Back EMF, Speed equation, $V_{avg}$ of PWM. - 📝 **Numericals:** Given $V, R_a, I_a, K_e \Phi$ → find $E_b, N, T, \eta$. - 📝 **Design:** Interface diagram + embedded C code for motor control using 8051/ARM. - 📝 **Compare:** Brushed vs. Brushless DC motor, Servo vs. Stepper. ---

topics.title: Stepper Motor Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Stepper Motor Control # Module 4 — Stepper Motor Control ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Stepper Motor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **stepper motor** is a *brushless, synchronous electromagnetic actuator* that converts a stream of digital input pulses into discrete mechanical angular displacements (steps). Each input pulse advances the rotor by a fixed angular increment known as the **step angle (α)**. Because movement is synchronized directly to the pulse train, the motor can be operated in an *open-loop* configuration without requiring position sensors or feedback encoders. In the context of **PBRUT404 – Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, stepper motor control refers to the technique of generating the *precise, time-staggered excitation pattern* (the *stepping sequence*) on the motor's stator windings using a general-purpose microcontroller (8051 / ARM / PIC) and a current-amplifying **driver IC** such as the **ULN2003 / ULN2803** or the dedicated **L293D / L298 H-bridge**. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — The "Staircase Motor"** > Imagine you are climbing a spiral staircase where every step is at a fixed height. You don't need a speedometer or a GPS to know your exact position — you just count the steps you've climbed. A stepper motor behaves exactly like this staircase: > - Each electrical pulse = "one stair" the rotor climbs. > - The size of each stair = **step angle α** (commonly **1.8°** or **7.5° / 90°**). > - No feedback sensor is required — the controller "knows" the position by simply counting the pulses it issued. **Practical visibility:** Open any inkjet printer, CNC plotter, 3-D printer, floppy-disk drive, or robotic arm — the silent, jerky-precise rotation of a small cylindrical motor visible in the gear train is a stepper motor in action. ### 1.3 Classification of Stepper Motors (KTU Syllabus Highlight) > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus (PBRUT404 Module 4) specifically tests the *Permanent Magnet* and *Variable Reluctance* families. The *Hybrid* motor is mentioned as a higher-performance combination. | Family | Rotor Construction | Step Angle Range | Key Feature | |---|---|---|---| | **Variable Reluctance (VR)** | Soft-iron toothed rotor (no magnet) | 5° – 15° | Rotation is driven by *minimizing* magnetic reluctance path | | **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Axially magnetized ferrite rotor | 45° – 90° | Rotor aligns with the energized stator pole; high *holding torque* | | **Hybrid (HB)** | Toothed rotor with embedded magnets | 0.9° – 5° | Combines PM holding torque with VR fine resolution | ### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall) - **Step Angle (α):** Fixed angular displacement per pulse — typically **1.8°**, **7.5°**, or **15°**. - **Steps per Revolution (N):** $N = \dfrac{360°}{\alpha}$ — for a **1.8°** motor, $N = 200$. - **Stepping Frequency (f):** Number of pulses per second (Hz). - **Holding Torque (T_h):** Maximum *static* torque the motor can sustain while energized without losing synchronism. - **Pull-out Torque (T_out):** Maximum *dynamic* torque before the rotor skips steps. - **Phase (m):** Number of independent stator windings — most common 4-phase unipolar motor used with **ULN2003**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic A *4-phase unipolar stepper motor* has its stator wound into **four independent coils** typically brought out on **5 or 6 wires** (with a common tap). The rotor is a multi-toothed permanent magnet. Rotation is produced by sequentially energizing the coils in a *predetermined binary pattern*. **Step 1 — Coils Energized in a Coded Sequence** A microcontroller (e.g., 8051, P89V51) writes a *4-bit nibble* on one of its ports (say **P1.0 → P1.3**). Each bit drives one power transistor inside the **ULN2003** Darlington array, which in turn energizes one stator coil. **Step 2 — Rotor Aligns with the Resultant Magnetic Field** The energized coils produce a magnetic field vector pointing in a particular direction. The permanent-magnet rotor experiences a torque proportional to $\sin(\theta)$ of the *angle error* and snaps to the nearest position of *minimum magnetic potential energy* — i.e., aligns with the field. **Step 3 — Sequencer Advances the Field Vector** By shifting the energized coil to the next in the sequence, the magnetic field vector rotates by one *step angle* $\alpha$. The rotor is dragged along — producing a precise incremental rotation. **Step 4 — Pulse Train ⇒ Continuous Rotation** Repeating Step 3 in a tight loop produces continuous rotation. **Direction** is selected by choosing *forward* or *reverse* sequence; **speed** is selected by the *delay* between successive outputs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Why" matters for KTU answers:** > Examiners award marks for explaining that *each pulse* ⇒ *one step* ⇒ *no feedback needed*. Open-loop control is the conceptual heart of stepper systems. State this explicitly to secure the *understanding* marks. ### 2.2 The Three Canonical Stepping Sequences (KTU Hot-Spot) For a 4-phase motor with coils labeled **A, B, C, D**: | Sequence | Coils ON at Any Time | Step Angle | Smoothness | Torque | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Wave Drive (1-phase ON)** | One coil at a time | $\alpha$ (full) | Least smooth | Lowest | | **Full-Step Drive (2-phase ON)** | Two adjacent coils | $\alpha$ (full) | Smooth | ~40 % higher than wave | | **Half-Step Drive (1-then-2)** | Alternates between 1 and 2 coils | $\alpha/2$ | Smoothest | Variable (peaks & troughs) | #### Truth Table (Binary Pattern on P1.3:P1.0) | Step | A | B | C | D | Wave (0x) | Full (1x) | Half (0x/1x) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0x08 | 0x0C | 0x08 | | 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x04 | 0x06 | 0x0C | | 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0x02 | 0x03 | 0x04 | | 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x01 | 0x09 | 0x06 | | 5 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0x08 | 0x0C | 0x02 | | 6 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x04 | 0x06 | 0x03 | | 7 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0x02 | 0x03 | 0x01 | | 8 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x01 | 0x09 | 0x09 | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > All quantities below are **board-tested**. Memorize the *units* column to avoid easy mark loss in the numerical sections. | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Variable Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Step angle (mechanical) | $\alpha = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$ | degrees (°) | $m$ = no. of stator phases, $N_r$ = no. of rotor teeth | | 2 | Steps per revolution | $N = \dfrac{360°}{\alpha}$ | steps/rev | Total discrete steps in one full turn | | 3 | Electrical angle per step | $\theta_e = \dfrac{360°}{m}$ | degrees (electrical) | Always fixed by phase count | | 4 | Mechanical angle per step | $\alpha = \dfrac{\theta_e}{N_r}$ | degrees (mechanical) | The *visible* rotation per pulse | | 5 | Rotational speed | $n = \dfrac{f_{step} \cdot 60}{N}$ | RPM | $f_{step}$ in Hz, $N$ in steps/rev | | 6 | Step frequency for desired RPM | $f_{step} = \dfrac{n \cdot N}{60}$ | Hz | Used to compute the software delay | | 7 | Software delay per step | $T_d = \dfrac{1}{f_{step}} = \dfrac{60}{n \cdot N}$ | seconds | Time between successive coil switches | | 8 | Steps required for angle $\phi$ | $S = \dfrac{\phi}{\alpha}$ | steps | Total pulses to rotate through angle $\phi$ | | 9 | Holding torque (PM motor) | $T_h \propto N_r \cdot B_r \cdot A$ | N·m | $B_r$ = remnant flux density, $A$ = air-gap area | | 10 | Resolution multiplier (half-step) | $\alpha_{half} = \dfrac{\alpha}{2}$ | degrees | Doubles effective resolution | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility of these formulas:** > - In **CNC plotters** — $S$ determines the X-Y coordinate precision. > - In **robotic joints** — $\alpha$ and $T_h$ define the *minimum controllable displacement* and *load-bearing capacity*. > - In **automated syringe pumps** — $f_{step}$ directly gives the *flow rate*. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Step Angle from Motor Construction **Premise:** A *Variable Reluctance* stepper motor has $m$ stator phases evenly distributed around the stator. The rotor has $N_r$ uniformly spaced salient teeth. **Step 1 — Establish the electrical periodicity.** When the stator field rotates by one *complete electrical cycle* ($360°$ electrical), every stator phase has been energized once. Because the $m$ phases are equally spaced, the field vector physically advances by: $$ \Theta_{e \to m} = \frac{360°}{m} \quad \text{(mechanical degrees per electrical cycle)} $$ **Step 2 — Relate electrical to mechanical angle on the rotor.** The rotor has $N_r$ teeth, so it possesses $N_r$-fold rotational symmetry. Hence, one full mechanical rotation of the rotor (360°) corresponds to $N_r$ electrical cycles: $$ \theta_{e, total} = N_r \cdot 360° \quad \text{(electrical degrees per mechanical revolution)} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the mechanical displacement per electrical pulse (per step).** One pulse causes the field to advance by one *stator-pole pitch*, i.e., $\frac{1}{m}$ of an electrical cycle. This corresponds to a mechanical rotation of: $$ \alpha = \frac{1}{m} \cdot \frac{360°}{N_r} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\alpha = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}\;} $$ **Step 4 — Verification with a numerical example (PM motor, $m = 4$, $N_r = 50$):** $$ \alpha = \frac{360°}{4 \times 50} = \frac{360°}{200} = 1.8° $$ This matches the *de-facto industry standard* of 200 steps/rev — confirming the formula is correct. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stepping Frequency Required for a Given RPM **Premise:** A stepper motor has $N$ steps per revolution. The user requires a rotational speed of $n$ RPM. **Step 1 — Convert RPM to revolutions per second.** $$ f_{rev} = \frac{n}{60} \;\; \text{rev/s} $$ **Step 2 — Multiply by steps per revolution to get steps per second (= step frequency).** $$ f_{step} = f_{rev} \times N = \frac{n \cdot N}{60} \;\; \text{Hz} $$ **Step 3 — Invert to obtain the inter-step software delay $T_d$.** $$ T_d = \frac{1}{f_{step}} = \frac{60}{n \cdot N} \;\; \text{seconds} $$ **Step 4 — Worked example: $n = 60$ RPM, $N = 200$ steps/rev.** $$ f_{step} = \frac{60 \times 200}{60} = 200 \;\; \text{Hz} $$ $$ T_d = \frac{1}{200} = 5 \;\; \text{ms} $$ This means the microcontroller must output a new coil pattern every **5 ms**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The above $T_d$ is the *physical* time between transitions. In 8051 C / Assembly code, $T_d$ is generated by a *software delay loop* (e.g., a `for(i=0;i ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (Microcontroller → Driver → Motor) ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU["MICROCONTROLLER
8051 OR ARM OR PIC
P1 PORT 4 BIT OUTPUT"] DRV["DRIVER IC
ULN2003 OR ULN2803
DARLINGTON ARRAY"] MOT["4 PHASE STEPPER
PERMANENT MAGNET
UNIPOLAR 5 WIRE"] PWR["DC SUPPLY
12 V OR 24 V"] PWR --> DRV MCU -- 4 BIT LOGIC
P1.0 P1.1 P1.2 P1.3 --> DRV DRV -- HIGH CURRENT
COIL DRIVE --> MOT MOT -- SHAFT ROTATION
DISCRETE STEPS --> LOAD["MECHANICAL LOAD
GEAR OR WHEEL OR ARM"] style MCU fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style DRV fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style MOT fill:#e0ffd6,stroke:#1f7a1f,color:#000 style PWR fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#800000,color:#000 style LOAD fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** The microcontroller cannot directly source the *several hundred milliamps* required by a stepper coil — its I/O pin is typically rated at only ~20 mA. The **ULN2003** therefore acts as a *current amplifier* (Darlington pair, 500 mA per channel) and also provides the *flyback diodes* needed to safely switch off the inductive coils. --- ### 4.2 Detailed Pin-Interface Schematic (ULN2003 ↔ 8051 ↔ Stepper) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CPU_BLOCK["8051 MICROCONTROLLER SECTION"] P10["P1.0"] P11["P1.1"] P12["P1.2"] P13["P1.3"] VCC1["VCC 5V"] GND1["GND"] end subgraph DRV_BLOCK["ULN2003 DRIVER IC"] IN1["IN1 PIN 1"] IN2["IN2 PIN 2"] IN3["IN3 PIN 3"] IN4["IN4 PIN 4"] OUT1["OUT1 PIN 16"] OUT2["OUT2 PIN 15"] OUT3["OUT3 PIN 14"] OUT4["OUT4 PIN 13"] COM["COM PIN 9
FREE WHEEL DIODE"] end subgraph MOT_BLOCK["4 PHASE STEPPER MOTOR"] COILA["COIL A ORANGE"] COILB["COIL B YELLOW"] COILC["COIL C PINK"] COILD["COIL D BLUE"] COMW["COMMON RED"] end PWR2["12V OR 24V DC SUPPLY"] GND2["GROUND"] P10 --> IN1 P11 --> IN2 P12 --> IN3 P13 --> IN4 OUT1 --> COILA OUT2 --> COILB OUT3 --> COILC OUT4 --> COILD COM -.-> PWR2 PWR2 --> COMW COMW --> COILA COMW --> COILB COMW --> COILC COMW --> COILD style CPU_BLOCK fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style DRV_BLOCK fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000 style MOT_BLOCK fill:#e0ffd6,stroke:#1f7a1f,color:#000 style PWR2 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#800000,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - *Left bank* (8051 port pins) provides TTL logic levels (0–5 V). - *Middle bank* (ULN2003) inverts the logic: an input HIGH pulls the corresponding output to GND, sinking the coil current. - *Right bank* (motor coils) are tied together at the *Common* (red) wire, which is held permanently at the +V supply. - Activating an output (e.g., P1.0 = 1) sinks current from Coil A through the ULN2003 to ground — energizing that phase. --- ### 4.3 Stepping-Sequence State Transition Flow (Full-Step, 2-Phase ON) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> S1 S1: S1 PHASE AB ON
COIL A AND B
CODE 0X0C S1 --> S2: NEXT PULSE S2: S2 PHASE BC ON
COIL B AND C
CODE 0X06 S2 --> S3: NEXT PULSE S3: S3 PHASE CD ON
COIL C AND D
CODE 0X03 S3 --> S4: NEXT PULSE S4: S4 PHASE DA ON
COIL D AND A
CODE 0X09 S4 --> S1: NEXT PULSE
REPEAT CYCLE S1: S1 PHASE AB ON S2: S2 PHASE BC ON S3: S3 PHASE CD ON S4: S4 PHASE DA ON ``` > [!NOTE] > Each transition corresponds to **one pulse** from the microcontroller and **one step** of the rotor. The total cycle of 4 states yields 4 full steps = $4 \times 1.8° = 7.2°$ per cycle. After 200/4 = 50 full cycles, the rotor completes exactly one revolution. --- ### 4.4 Flowchart — Microcontroller Software Algorithm (Conceptual Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD ST["START"] INI["INITIALIZE PORT P1
AS OUTPUT"] CFG["LOAD STEP SEQUENCE
ARRAY 4 BYTES"] DIR["READ DIRECTION
INPUT SWITCH"] DLY["INITIALIZE TIMER
FOR STEP DELAY TD"] OUT["OUTPUT CURRENT
PATTERN TO P1"] WAIT["WAIT FOR TIMER
ELAPSE EQUAL TO TD"] ADV["ADVANCE INDEX
FORWARD OR REVERSE"] LOOP["REPEAT FOR TOTAL
STEP COUNT S"] STOP["STOP AND TURN
OFF ALL COILS"] ENDX["END"] ST --> INI --> CFG --> DIR --> DLY --> OUT --> WAIT --> ADV --> LOOP --> OUT LOOP -->|S REACHED| STOP --> ENDX style ST fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333 style ENDX fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333 style OUT fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366 style WAIT fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600 ``` ## 5.
