3. **Measured service** (1 mark) — Cloud metering enables *pay-per-byte* billing, making large-scale IoT economically feasible (as shown in Worked Example 1).

*(Any three of the five NIST characteristics are acceptable.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS in the context of IoT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):** Provides raw VMs, storage, and networking. IoT example: hosting a private MQTT broker on AWS EC2. (1 mark)
- **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service):** Provides a managed platform including OS, middleware, and IoT-specific services. IoT example: AWS IoT Core manages device shadows, rules engine, and registry. (1 mark)
- **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service):** End-user application accessed via browser/app. IoT example: ThingSpeak dashboard for visualising sensor data. (1 mark)

> [!NOTE]
> The *direction of management responsibility* is the key differentiator — IaaS user manages the most, SaaS user manages the least.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **(14 Marks)**

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of IoT integrated with cloud computing. Discuss any *six* key reasons why cloud computing is essential for IoT applications. Mention two real-world use cases.**

#### Part (a) — IoT-Cloud Architecture (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

An IoT-Cloud architecture typically has **four logical layers**, as shown in the diagram below.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeL1[LAYER 1 PERCEPTION Sensors Actuators RFID Microcontroller]
    nodeL2[LAYER 2 NETWORK Gateways Routers WiFi BLE LoRa 5G]
    nodeL3[LAYER 3 CLOUD Storage Compute Analytics ML Database]
    nodeL4[LAYER 4 APPLICATION Dashboard Mobile Alerts ERP Integration]
    nodeL1 --> nodeL2
    nodeL2 --> nodeL3
    nodeL3 --> nodeL4
    nodeL4 --> nodeL1
```

**Layer-by-layer description (7 marks distribution):**

1. **Perception Layer (1 mark):** The physical layer comprising sensors (temperature, pressure, gas, motion), actuators (relays, motors, solenoids), RFID tags, and embedded microcontrollers (ESP32, STM32). It performs *sense and actuate*.

2. **Network Layer (1.5 marks):** The communication layer using protocols such as **Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, and 5G**. Gateways aggregate data from multiple low-power nodes and forward it to the cloud using **MQTT, CoAP, or HTTPS**.

3. **Cloud Layer (2.5 marks):** The *brain* of the system. Provides:
   - **Storage:** Object stores (S3), time-series DBs (InfluxDB, Timescale).
   - **Compute:** VMs, containers, serverless functions.
   - **Analytics:** Batch (Spark), stream (Kafka + Flink), ML (SageMaker).
   - **Identity & Security:** Device certificates, OAuth2, TLS mutual auth.

4. **Application Layer (1.5 marks):** End-user interface — web dashboards (Grafana), mobile apps, alert systems (SMS/email), and integration with enterprise systems (SAP, Salesforce).

5. **Feedback path (0.5 marks):** Closing the loop — cloud issues actuation commands back to the perception layer, enabling remote control and automation.

#### Part (b) — Six Key Reasons + Two Use Cases (7 marks)

**Six Essential Reasons (1 mark each):**

1. **Virtually unlimited storage** — Edge nodes store KBs; cloud provides petabytes.
2. **High-performance compute on demand** — ML/DL workloads require GPUs/TPUs unavailable on edge.
3. **Big data aggregation** — Cross-device, cross-location analytics yield insights impossible locally.
4. **Scalability & elasticity** — Resources scale with the deployment; no upfront CapEx.
5. **Cost efficiency (pay-per-use)** — Opex model replaces heavy CapEx on local servers.
6. **Remote accessibility, visualization, and API integration** — Dashboards, mobile apps, third-party integrations.

**Two Real-World Use Cases (0.5 mark each):**
- **Smart Agriculture (1 use case):** 5,000 soil-moisture sensors stream to AWS IoT; ML predicts irrigation schedules, saving 30% water.
- **Predictive Maintenance in IIoT (1 use case):** Vibration sensors on 500 motors stream to cloud; anomaly detection reduces unplanned downtime by 45%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - Drawing the diagram *without labelling the protocols* (MQTT, HTTPS) at each layer → loses 1 mark.
> - Listing reasons *without justifying with an example* → loses 0.5 mark per reason.
> - Confusing *fog* with *cloud* → fog is the *intermediate* layer; cloud is the *central* back-end.
> - Writing "cloud is fast" without latency numbers → examiners expect **quantitative reasoning** (e.g., "Cloud round-trip = 50–500 ms").

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **(14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**Q: Discuss the limitations of resource-constrained IoT devices that necessitate the use of cloud computing. With a worked numerical example, compute the cloud storage required and the monthly cost for a smart-city deployment of 2,000 noise-pollution sensors, each generating 32-byte packets every 5 seconds, running for 60 days. Assume 2:1 compression and 3× replication, with storage cost \$0.02/GB/month.**

#### Part (a) — Limitations of IoT Devices (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

IoT endpoints are deliberately built as **resource-constrained** to minimize cost, power, and form-factor. This creates seven fundamental limitations:

1. **Limited storage (1 mark):** Microcontrollers have KB to a few MB of flash. A 60-day smart-city deployment generates GBs — physically impossible to retain locally.

2. **Limited compute (1 mark):** MCUs run at 80–240 MHz with no FPU. They cannot perform ML inference, Fourier transforms, or compression beyond trivial levels.

3. **Limited RAM (1 mark):** A few hundred KB. In-memory analytics, large buffers, and real-time stream processing are infeasible.

4. **Power budget (1 mark):** Battery-powered nodes must sleep >99% of the time. Heavy processing or long wireless transmissions deplete the battery rapidly.

5. **Network bandwidth (1 mark):** Low-power radios (LoRa, NB-IoT) are limited to a few Kbps; high-throughput data (video, raw audio) must be compressed or pre-filtered.

6. **No user interface (1 mark):** Devices are headless; users cannot visualize, configure, or analyse data without an external platform.

7. **Weak security posture (1 mark):** No anti-virus, no patch cycle, no UI for credential management — making them vulnerable. Cloud provides centralized, hardware-rooted security.

> [!NOTE]
> These limitations collectively enforce the **offloading principle**: heavy work migrates to the cloud; the edge remains a *sensor-and-actuator* node.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $N = 2{,}000$ sensors
- $S = 32\,\text{bytes}$
- $F = 1/5\,\text{Hz} = 0.2\,\text{Hz}$
- $t = 60\,\text{days} = 60 \times 24 \times 3600 = 5{,}184{,}000\,\text{s}$
- Compression $C = 2{:}1$
- Replication $R = 3$
- Cost $P = \$0.02/\text{GB}/\text{month}$

**Step 1 — Total samples**

$$
N_{samples} = N \cdot F \cdot t = 2{,}000 \times 0.2 \times 5{,}184{,}000
$$

First, $0.2 \times 5{,}184{,}000 = 1{,}036{,}800$. Then $2{,}000 \times 1{,}036{,}800 = 2{,}073{,}600{,}000$.

$$
N_{samples} = 2.0736 \times 10^{9}
$$

**Step 2 — Raw data in bytes**

$$
D_{raw} = N_{samples} \times S = 2.0736 \times 10^{9} \times 32 = 6.6355 \times 10^{10}\,\text{bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to GB (1 GB = 1024³ bytes = 1.0737 × 10⁹ bytes)**

$$
D_{raw\,GB} = \frac{6.6355 \times 10^{10}}{1.0737 \times 10^{9}} = 61.8\,\text{GB}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply 2:1 compression**

$$
D_{compressed\,GB} = \frac{61.8}{2} = 30.9\,\text{GB}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply 3× replication**

$$
D_{stored\,GB} = 30.9 \times 3 = 92.7\,\text{GB}
$$

**Step 6 — Monthly cost**

$$
Cost = 92.7\,\text{GB} \times \$0.02/\text{GB} = \$1.854
$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **92.7 GB** of replicated storage is required, costing **\$1.85/month** on the cloud.

> [!NOTE]
> **Economic insight:** The total ownership cost of an *on-premise* server to handle 2,000 sensors (electricity, cooling, rack space, IT staff, RAID, backup) easily exceeds **\$200/month**, making the cloud **>100× cheaper** for this workload.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in Numerical Answers:**
> - Forgetting to convert **bytes → GB** (1 GB = $1024^3$ bytes, not $10^9$) → 1 mark lost.
> - Applying compression *after* replication instead of *before* → wrong final value.
> - Writing "the answer is X" without showing the equation → 2 marks lost for missing steps.
> - Confusing **bits vs bytes** when working with network bandwidth questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize Before Exam)**

- **Definition (1-liner):** Cloud computing in IoT = on-demand, scalable, pay-per-use delivery of compute, storage, and analytics to IoT endpoints over the Internet.

- **NIST's 5 Essential Characteristics:** On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service.

- **3 Service Models:** IaaS (you manage OS+), PaaS (you manage apps only), SaaS (you manage data only). FaaS/Serverless is the modern addition.

- **4 Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community. Hybrid is most common in industrial IoT.

- **4-Layer IoT-Cloud Architecture:** Perception → Network → Cloud → Application. Always close the feedback loop in the diagram.

- **7 Reasons Cloud is Needed:** Storage asymmetry, Compute asymmetry, Energy budget, Big Data & AI, Cross-device orchestration, Remote access, Security & compliance.

- **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $D_{total} = N \cdot S \cdot F \cdot t$
  - $B_{uplink} = (N \cdot S \cdot F) / C_{compression}$
  - $T_{total} = T_{sense} + T_{tx} + T_{net} + T_{cloud\,proc} + T_{rx} + T_{actuate}$

- **Latency Rule of Thumb:** Pure cloud = 50–500 ms; Fog = 1–20 ms; Pure edge = <1 ms. Hard real-time control **cannot** rely on cloud alone.

- **Popular IoT Cloud Platforms:** AWS IoT Core, Microsoft Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT, IBM Watson IoT, ThingSpeak (open-source for academic use).

- **Common Protocols:** MQTT (most popular for IoT), CoAP (constrained), HTTP/HTTPS (legacy), AMQP (enterprise), DDS (real-time).

- **Examiner Pet Topics:** Draw the architecture diagram with **labelled protocols**, mention **Digital Twin** at least once, and quote a **latency number** to demonstrate quantitative understanding.

- **Pitfall to Avoid:** "Cloud is needed because it is fast and cheap" — examiners want *specific* reasons (storage, compute, scalability, analytics, security) with *quantitative* justification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Protocols: UART, SPI and USB
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Communication Protocols: UART, SPI and USB

# Communication Protocols: UART, SPI and USB

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Communication Protocols** are standardized sets of rules that govern the exchange of data between two or more embedded devices. They define voltage levels, timing, frame structure, and physical connections to ensure reliable inter-device communication.

### 1.1 UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)

**Formal Definition:**
UART is a hardware communication protocol that uses **asynchronous serial** communication with configurable baud rate (typically 9600, 115200 bps). Data is transmitted without a shared clock signal, relying instead on pre-agreed timing parameters between transmitter and receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **Asynchronous** means there is **NO shared clock line** between the sender and receiver. Both sides must independently agree on the same baud rate (bits per second) before communication begins.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine two people (Tx and Rx) whispering to each other through a long pipe. There is no metronome to keep them in sync. Before they begin, they must agree: *"I will speak exactly 1 word every second."* Each word (frame) starts with a soft tap (START bit) to grab attention, contains the message, and ends with a pause (STOP bit). This is precisely how UART operates — the START bit acts as a synchronization trigger, and the STOP bit provides a small recovery window.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Asynchronous** (no clock line)
- **Full-duplex** (separate Tx and Rx lines)
- **Point-to-point** (one master, one slave)
- **Configurable parameters**: Baud rate, data bits (5–9), parity (None/Even/Odd), stop bits (1/1.5/2)

---

### 1.2 SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)

**Formal Definition:**
SPI is a **synchronous, full-duplex, master-slave** serial communication protocol developed by Motorola. It uses **4 wires** (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS) and operates at clock speeds typically from **1 MHz to 100 MHz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SPI is **synchronous** because it has a dedicated clock line (SCLK) generated by the master device. Every bit transferred is clocked in/out on the rising or falling edge of SCLK.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of SPI as a teacher (master) with a group of students (slaves) sitting in a row. The teacher rings a bell (SCLK) to signal *"now speak!"* and addresses one specific student by pointing at them (SS line goes LOW). That student whispers back the answer through a return channel (MISO), while the teacher simultaneously lectures them (MOSI). Multiple students can be in the room, but only one is addressed at a time. The bell keeps everyone perfectly synchronized — no one speaks "out of turn."

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Synchronous** (shared clock)
- **Full-duplex**
- **Master-slave** architecture (1 master, N slaves)
- **4 standard signals**: SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS (active LOW)

---

### 1.3 USB (Universal Serial Bus)

**Formal Definition:**
USB is a **synchronous, polled, host-centric** serial bus standard that uses **differential signaling** (D+ and D− lines) and **NRZI bit encoding** with bit stuffing. It supports plug-and-play, hot-plugging, and power delivery (up to **5V/500mA** for USB 2.0).

> [!NOTE]
> USB stands for **Universal Serial Bus**. It is the de-facto standard for connecting peripherals (keyboards, mice, flash drives) to PCs and embedded hosts (e.g., STM32 with USB OTG).

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine USB as a **highly disciplined postal system** between a post office (host) and a citizen (device). Every letter (packet) must follow a strict format: a header telling you what type of mail it is (PID), a body (data payload), and a checksum to verify no damage occurred (CRC). The post office always initiates conversation; the citizen can only reply when asked. Specially encoded "talking patterns" (NRZI with bit stuffing) ensure the post office can always detect the start of new words even after long silences.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Differential signaling** (D+, D−) for noise immunity
- **NRZI encoding** with **bit stuffing**
- **Host-Device** architecture (host initiates all transactions)
- **Hot-pluggable** and supports up to **127 devices** via hubs
- **Plug-and-Play** with automatic enumeration

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic is a core part of Module 4, covering external peripheral interfacing. The CO mapping is typically **CO4**: *Design embedded systems using communication protocols and peripheral interfaces.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 UART: Deep Dive

### Operational Logic Steps

1. **Idle State:** The UART line stays HIGH (logic 1, typically **3.3V or 5V** depending on standard) when no transmission occurs.
2. **Start Bit:** The transmitter pulls the line LOW for 1 bit period. This falling edge alerts the receiver: *"data is coming."*
3. **Data Bits:** 5 to 9 bits of actual data are transmitted LSB-first (Least Significant Bit first). This is unique to UART.
4. **Parity Bit (Optional):** Used for single-bit error detection. Even parity → total number of 1s (including parity) is even.
5. **Stop Bit(s):** Line returns to HIGH for 1, 1.5, or 2 bit periods, marking the end of the frame.
6. **Baud Rate Tolerance:** Receiver samples at the center of each bit period (~1.5 bit periods after the falling start edge).

### UART Frame Structure

```
| IDLE | START (0) | D0 D1 D2 D3 D4 D5 D6 D7 | PARITY | STOP (1) | IDLE |
```

> [!NOTE]
> **LSB-First Transmission** is a hallmark of UART. ARM Cortex-M based microcontrollers (STM32, LPC2148) transmit the LSB of the data register first.

### Standard UART Variants

| Standard | Voltage Levels | Logic Inversion | Use Case |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|----------|
| **TTL UART** | 0V (LOW) / 3.3V or 5V (HIGH) | No inversion | Direct MCU-to-MCU |
| **RS-232** | +3V to +25V (space) / −3V to −25V (mark) | Inverted | Legacy PC COM ports |
| **RS-485** | Differential ±1.5V to ±6V | No inversion | Industrial, multi-drop |

---

## 2.2 SPI: Deep Dive

### Operational Logic Steps

1. **Master initializes SS** (Slave Select) line HIGH for inactive slaves and LOW for the active slave.
2. **Master generates SCLK** (serial clock) — frequency is a multiple of APB clock divided by BR (baud rate) prescaler (e.g., `f_SCLK = f_PCLK / (BR)`).
3. **Master writes to MOSI** while simultaneously **reading from MISO** — full duplex.
4. **Data is shifted in/out** on the configured clock edge (rising or falling).
5. **Transfer ends** when master de-asserts SS back to HIGH.
6. **Slave holds MISO in high-impedance** when its SS is inactive (to avoid bus contention).

### SPI Signal Roles

| Signal | Full Name | Direction | Function |
|--------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **SCLK** | Serial Clock | Master → Slave | Synchronization clock |
| **MOSI** | Master Out Slave In | Master → Slave | Data from master to slave |
| **MISO** | Master In Slave Out | Slave → Master | Data from slave to master |
| **SS / NSS** | Slave Select (active LOW) | Master → Slave | Selects active slave |

### SPI Modes (Clock Polarity & Phase)

SPI has **4 modes** defined by CPOL (Clock Polarity) and CPHA (Clock Phase):

| Mode | CPOL | CPHA | Idle Clock | Sampling Edge | Setup Edge |
|------|------|------|-----------|---------------|------------|
| **0** | 0 | 0 | LOW | Rising | Falling |
| **1** | 0 | 1 | LOW | Falling | Rising |
| **2** | 1 | 0 | HIGH | Falling | Rising |
| **3** | 1 | 1 | HIGH | Rising | Falling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master and slave MUST operate in the same SPI mode** for communication to succeed. Mismatched CPOL/CPHA is the #1 cause of "garbage data" in SPI debugging.

---

## 2.3 USB: Deep Dive

### USB Architecture (Tiered Star Topology)

- **Host** (root) → **Hub(s)** → **Devices**
- Only **one host per bus** (unless using USB OTG, which is dual-role)
- Maximum of **127 devices** addressable via 7-bit address field

### USB Physical Layer

- **D+ and D−** differential pair
- **Low-speed / Full-speed**: 1.5 Mbps / 12 Mbps
- **High-speed**: 480 Mbps (USB 2.0)
- **Pull-up resistor** on D+ (full-speed) or D− (low-speed) signals device attachment
- **NRZI Encoding:** Logic 0 → toggle, Logic 1 → no toggle

### USB Data Flow Types

| Transfer Type | Purpose | Examples |
|---------------|---------|----------|
| **Control** | Enumeration, configuration | Device descriptors |
| **Bulk** | Large, non-time-critical data | Printers, USB drives |
| **Interrupt** | Small, periodic, low-latency | Mouse, keyboard |
| **Isochronous** | Real-time, guaranteed bandwidth | Audio, video |

### USB Packet Structure (USB 2.0)

Every USB transaction consists of **3 packets**:

1. **Token Packet** (from host) — IN, OUT, SETUP, SOF
2. **Data Packet** (optional) — DATA0, DATA1 (with data toggle synchronization)
3. **Handshake Packet** (from device) — ACK, NAK, STALL

```
[SYNC][PID][ADDR][ENDP][CRC5]  → Token
[SYNC][PID][DATA (0–1024 bytes)][CRC16]  → Data
[SYNC][PID]  → Handshake
```

> [!NOTE]
> **SYNC** is a fixed 8-bit pattern (`01111100` in NRZI) used for clock synchronization. **PID** is a 4-bit type field complemented with its 4-bit inverse for error detection.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Notes |
|---------|---------------------|-------|
| **UART Bit Period** | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{Baud\ Rate}$ | E.g., at 9600 bps → $T_b \approx 104.17\ \mu s$ |
| **UART Frame Time** | $T_f = (1 + D + P + S) \cdot T_b$ | $D$ = data bits, $P$ = parity (0 or 1), $S$ = stop bits |
| **UART Throughput** | $\eta = \dfrac{D}{1 + D + P + S} \cdot 100\%$ | Efficiency drops with more overhead bits |
| **SPI SCLK Frequency** | $f_{SCLK} = \dfrac{f_{APB}}{BR \cdot (Prescaler\ div)}$ | STM32 example: $BR = f_{APB} / f_{SCLK}$ |
| **SPI Throughput** | $R_{SPI} = f_{SCLK} \times bits$ | E.g., 1 MHz × 8 bits = 1 MB/s |
| **NRZI Encoding** | Bit 0 → toggle; Bit 1 → no toggle | Maintains DC balance |
| **USB Bit Stuffing** | Insert 0 after every 6 consecutive 1s | Receiver removes stuffed bit |
| **USB Frame Time (FS)** | $T_{frame} = 1\,ms$ | Contains 1500 bytes (12 Mbps) |
| **USB Max Devices** | $2^7 - 1 = 127$ | 7-bit address (0 reserved) |
| **RS-232 Max Speed** | Typically $\leq 115.2\,kbps$ | Distance $\leq 15\,m$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** UART is the **debugging workhorse** of embedded engineers (used by `printf()` redirected via ITM or UART). SPI dominates **high-speed sensor communication** (SD cards, ADCs, displays). USB is the standard for **PC-to-device connectivity** in modern products.

---

## 2.5 Protocol Comparison Matrix

| Feature | UART | SPI | USB |
|---------|------|-----|-----|
| **Type** | Asynchronous | Synchronous | Synchronous, polled |
| **Clock** | Not shared | Shared (SCLK) | Embedded (via SYNC) |
| **Duplex** | Full | Full | Half (with scheduling) |
| **Wires** | 2 (Tx, Rx) | 4 (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS) | 2 differential (D+, D−) + VBUS/GND |
| **Speed** | Up to ~1 Mbps | Up to ~100 Mbps | Up to 480 Mbps (HS) |
| **Topology** | Point-to-point | Master + N slaves | Tiered star (host + hubs) |
| **Max Devices** | 2 | N (limited by GPIO) | 127 |
| **Encoding** | NRZ | NRZ | NRZI + bit stuffing |
| **Error Detection** | Parity (optional) | None (software) | CRC5 + CRC16 |
| **Power Delivery** | No | No | Yes (up to 100W PD) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: UART Frame Time Calculation

### Problem
Given: Baud rate = 9600 bps, Data bits = 8, Parity = None, Stop bits = 1. Calculate the **time to transmit one frame** and the **theoretical throughput efficiency**.

### Step 1: Calculate the Bit Period

The bit period $T_b$ is the reciprocal of the baud rate:

$$
T_b = \frac{1}{Baud\ Rate} = \frac{1}{9600}\ \text{seconds}
$$

Converting to microseconds:

$$
T_b = \frac{10^6}{9600}\ \mu s = 104.1667\ \mu s
$$

> This means **each bit lasts 104.17 μs** on the wire.

### Step 2: Identify Frame Components

A complete UART frame contains:
- 1 Start bit
- 8 Data bits ($D = 8$)
- 0 Parity bits ($P = 0$)
- 1 Stop bit ($S = 1$)

**Total bits per frame:**

$$
N_{frame} = 1 + D + P + S = 1 + 8 + 0 + 1 = 10\ \text{bits}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Frame Duration

Multiplying the total bits by the bit period:

$$
T_f = N_{frame} \times T_b = 10 \times 104.1667\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_f = 1041.667\ \mu s \approx 1.042\ \text{ms}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Throughput Efficiency

The efficiency is the ratio of useful data bits to total transmitted bits:

$$
\eta = \frac{D}{1 + D + P + S} \times 100\% = \frac{8}{10} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta = 80\%
$$

> **Insight:** 20% of the bandwidth is overhead (start + stop). Adding parity drops efficiency to 8/11 ≈ 72.7%.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: SPI SCLK Configuration (STM32 Example)

### Problem
The APB1 peripheral clock on an STM32F4 is $f_{APB1} = 50\ \text{MHz}$. We need $f_{SCLK} = 1\ \text{MHz}$. The baud rate prescaler (BR) can take values 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256. Determine the **minimum prescaler** that gives an SCLK ≤ 1 MHz.

### Step 1: Write the SCLK Formula

$$
f_{SCLK} = \frac{f_{APB1}}{BR}
$$

Rearranging for $BR$:

$$
BR = \frac{f_{APB1}}{f_{SCLK}} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^6} = 50
$$

### Step 2: Select Nearest Valid Prescaler

Since $BR$ must be from {2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, ...}, and 50 is not in the list, we choose the **smallest BR ≥ 50**:

$$
BR_{selected} = 64
$$

### Step 3: Recompute Actual SCLK

$$
f_{SCLK,actual} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{64} = 781.25\ \text{kHz}
$$

### Step 4: Verify Bit Time

$$
T_{bit,SPI} = \frac{1}{f_{SCLK,actual}} = \frac{1}{781250} \approx 1.28\ \mu s\ \text{per bit}
$$

> **Insight:** In 8-bit SPI transfer, one byte takes $8 \times T_{bit} = 10.24\ \mu s$, giving a throughput of ~97.66 KB/s — over 100× faster than typical 9600 baud UART.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: NRZI Encoding with Bit Stuffing (USB)

### Problem
Encode the byte `0x3F` = `00111111` using USB NRZI encoding, and apply bit stuffing if needed.

### Step 1: Pre-Encode the Byte

The data byte `00111111` has **six consecutive 1s** at the end — triggering the bit-stuffing rule.

### Step 2: Apply Bit Stuffing

USB rule: after **6 consecutive 1s**, a **stuffed 0** is inserted.

Original:
$$
0011\ 1111
$$

After stuffing a `0` after the 6th `1`:
$$
0011\ 1110\ 1
$$

> The receiver **removes** this stuffed 0 to recover the original data.

### Step 3: Apply NRZI Encoding

NRZI rule: 
- Bit **0** → toggle the line level
- Bit **1** → hold the line level

Assume line starts HIGH:

| Bit | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 (stuffed) | 1 |
|----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|----|---|
| **Line Level** | LOW (toggle) | HIGH (toggle) | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | LOW (toggle) | LOW |

> **Insight:** The stuffed `0` forces a toggle, preventing the receiver's PLL from losing lock during long runs of 1s.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Protocol

| Application | Recommended Protocol | Justification |
|-------------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Debug console (`printf`)** | UART | Simple, 1 wire, ubiquitous in IDEs |
| **SD card interface** | SPI | High speed, full duplex, simple hardware |
| **Temperature sensor (slow)** | I²C or UART | Low pin count, low bandwidth |
| **LCD/OLED display** | SPI | High frame rate, fast updates |
| **PC peripheral (mouse, keyboard)** | USB | Standard, plug-and-play, polling model |
| **Industrial sensor (long cable)** | RS-485 (UART variant) | Differential, noise immune, multi-drop |
| **Wireless module (XBee)** | UART | AT command interface, simple framing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to *compare* protocols, always tabulate the differences and justify with a **numerical throughput or application argument**, not vague qualitative statements.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three differences between UART and SPI communication protocols.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | UART | SPI |
|---------|------|-----|
| **Clock** | Asynchronous (no clock line) | Synchronous (uses SCLK) |
| **Wires Required** | 2 (Tx, Rx) | 4 (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS) |
| **Speed** | Typically up to 115.2 kbps | Up to tens of MHz |
| **Topology** | Point-to-point | Master with N slaves |
| **Duplex** | Full duplex | Full duplex |

(Any 3 valid differences earn full 3 marks)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is NRZI encoding? Why is it used in USB?**

**Model Answer:**

**NRZI (Non-Return-to-Zero Inverted)** is a line encoding scheme where:
- A **logical '0'** causes a **transition (toggle)** in the signal level
- A **logical '1'** causes **no transition** (level held constant)

**Why USB uses NRZI:**
1. **Maintains DC balance** — no long-term DC bias on the line.
2. **Combined with bit stuffing** (a 0 inserted after every 6 consecutive 1s), it guarantees frequent clock transitions for the receiver's PLL to recover the clock.
3. **Reduces EMI** compared to simple NRZ.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the **SPI protocol**. List the functions of the four signals SCLK, MOSI, MISO, and SS. **[7 Marks]**

**b)** A microcontroller communicates with an **MCP3208 ADC** using SPI at $f_{SCLK} = 2\ \text{MHz}$. The APB clock is $32\ \text{MHz}$. Calculate the **minimum baud rate prescaler (BR)** and the **time to convert and read one 12-bit sample**. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Architecture Block Diagram:**

```
                    SPI MASTER (MCU)
                    +-----------+
                    |  SPI      |
   +----+   SS1     | Peripheral|    SCLK ----> Slave 1
   |GPIO|----------->|           |    MOSI ----> Slave 1
   +----+            |           |    MISO <---- Slave 1
   |GPIO|----------->|  Control  |    SS2  ----> Slave 2
   +----+  (optional)|           |    MOSI ----> Slave 2
                    +-----------+    MISO <---- Slave 2
                          |
                    (Internal shift registers)
```

**Signal Functions:**

| Signal | Function |
|--------|----------|
| **SCLK** | Clock generated by master; synchronizes data shift on both lines |
| **MOSI** | Master Out Slave In — data from master to selected slave |
| **MISO** | Master In Slave Out — data from selected slave to master |
| **SS / NSS** | Active LOW chip-select; only one slave is selected at a time |

**Operating Sequence:**
1. Master asserts SS (LOW) for the target slave.
2. Master generates SCLK (CPOL/CPHA configured as per slave's mode).
3. Bits are shifted out on MOSI and shifted in on MISO simultaneously (full duplex).
4. After N bits (8/16 typically), master de-asserts SS.
5. Non-selected slaves keep MISO in high-impedance (Hi-Z).

(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for signal functions, 2 for operating sequence)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Calculate Required Prescaler**

$$
BR_{required} = \frac{f_{APB}}{f_{SCLK}} = \frac{32 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^6} = 16
$$

Since SPI prescaler values are 2, 4, 8, **16**, 32, 64... → the minimum valid $BR = 16$ (exact match).

**Step 2: Verify Actual SCLK**

$$
f_{SCLK,actual} = \frac{32\ \text{MHz}}{16} = 2\ \text{MHz} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3: Bit Time**

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.5\ \mu s\ \text{per bit}
$$

**Step 4: MCP3208 Conversion Time**

The MCP3208 needs **24 SCLK cycles** to perform one 12-bit conversion:
- 1 START bit + 1 SGL/DIFF bit + 3 channel select bits + null bit + 12 data bits = **24 SCLKs total** (some datasheets: 24 SCLKs with one more bit of resolution)

$$
T_{conversion} = 24 \times T_{bit} = 24 \times 0.5\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{conversion} = 12\ \mu s
$$

**Step 5: Sample Rate**

$$
f_{sample} = \frac{1}{T_{conversion}} = \frac{1}{12\ \mu s} \approx 83.33\ \text{kSPS}
$$

The MCP3208's rated maximum is 100 kSPS at 5V — well within spec.

(7 marks: 2 for BR calculation, 2 for SCLK verification, 2 for conversion time, 1 for sample rate)

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**a)** Explain the **UART frame format** with a neat timing diagram. Define **baud rate** and explain how to calculate the time taken to transmit the string `"HELLO"` (5 characters, 8-bit ASCII, no parity, 1 stop bit) at **9600 bps**. **[7 Marks]**

**b)** Describe the **USB architecture** in brief. What is **NRZI encoding**? Explain **bit stuffing** in USB with a suitable example. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**UART Frame Format (One Byte: 0x48 = 'H' = 01001000b):**

```
Line:  _____|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|______
       IDLE   0  0  0  1  0  0  1  0    1     IDLE
            START D0 D1 D2 D3 D4 D5 D6 D7  STOP
            (LSB transmitted FIRST)
```

**Baud Rate Definition:**
**Baud rate** is the number of **signal changes (symbols) per second** on a communication line. For UART with binary signaling, baud rate = bits per second (bps).

**Calculation:**

Each character frame has:
- 1 Start + 8 Data + 0 Parity + 1 Stop = **10 bits**

Bit period at 9600 bps:

$$
T_b = \frac{1}{9600} = 104.17\ \mu s
$$

Bits to transmit for 5 characters:

$$
N_{total} = 5 \times 10 = 50\ \text{bits}
$$

Total transmission time:

$$
T_{total} = 50 \times 104.17\ \mu s = 5208.5\ \mu s \approx 5.21\ \text{ms}
$$

(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for baud rate definition, 3 for calculation)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**USB Architecture:**

USB uses a **tiered star topology**:

```
                    [ROOT HUB in HOST]
                          |
            +-------------+-------------+
            |             |             |
         [HUB 1]      [HUB 2]       [Device 1]
         /  |  \         |
       D1  D2  D3       D4
```

- One **host** (PC or OTG device) initiates all transactions.
- **Hubs** expand a single port into multiple.
- **Devices** (slaves) only respond when addressed.
- Maximum **127 devices** (7-bit address, 0 reserved for newly attached devices during enumeration).

**NRZI Encoding:**

- Logical **0** → signal **toggles** (level flips)
- Logical **1** → signal **holds** (no change)

This ensures DC balance and aids clock recovery.

**Bit Stuffing Rule:**

After every **six consecutive 1s** in the data stream, a **stuffed 0** is automatically inserted by the transmitter. The receiver detects and removes this stuffed 0.

**Example:** Original data → `...0111111...` (seven 1s in a row)
- After stuffing: `...01111101...` (a 0 inserted after the 6th 1)
- The stuffed 0 forces a transition in NRZI, preventing the receiver's clock recovery PLL from drifting.

(7 marks: 2 for architecture diagram, 2 for NRZI, 3 for bit stuffing example)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common mistakes costing 2–4 marks:**
> 1. **Confusing baud rate with bit rate** — they are equal for binary signaling but conceptually distinct.
> 2. **Forgetting start/stop bits** in UART frame size — leads to wrong frame time calculations.
> 3. **Drawing UART MSB-first** — UART transmits **LSB-first**, opposite of SPI/MSB.
> 4. **Missing the active-LOW nature of SS** in SPI — examiners deduct marks if shown as active HIGH.
> 5. **Omitting CRC fields** in USB packet diagrams — both CRC5 (tokens) and CRC16 (data packets) must be shown.
> 6. **Not justifying protocol choice** with application context — a "use SPI because it's fast" answer lacks depth; explain *why* (e.g., "SPI is preferred for SD cards because of its 24 MHz SCLK capability, enabling 3 MB/s read speeds, which UART at 115200 bps cannot match").

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ✅ Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **UART** = **Asynchronous**, 2 wires, **LSB-first**, baud-rate-based, full duplex. Frame = Start + Data + Parity + Stop.
- [ ] **UART bit period** $T_b = 1/\text{baud rate}$. Total frame time includes overhead bits.
- [ ] **SPI** = **Synchronous**, 4 wires (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS), **MSB-first** (typical), full duplex, master-slave.
- [ ] **SPI has 4 modes** (CPOL × CPHA) — master and slave must match.
- [ ] **SS is active LOW** — multiple slaves connected via independent SS lines from master.
- [ ] **USB** = host-centric, polled, differential (D+, D−), supports up to **127 devices**.
- [ ] **USB encoding** = NRZI + bit stuffing (0 inserted after 6 consecutive 1s).
- [ ] **USB packets** = SYNC + PID + (ADDR/ENDP or Data) + CRC.
- [ ] **USB transfer types** = Control, Bulk, Interrupt, Isochronous.
- [ ] **USB speed tiers**: Low (1.5 Mbps), Full (12 Mbps), High (480 Mbps), SuperSpeed (5 Gbps+).
- [ ] **RS-232** uses inverted voltage levels (±3V to ±25V) versus TTL UART (0/3.3V/5V).
- [ ] **Bit stuffing is mandatory** in USB — without it, long runs of 1s break clock recovery.
- [ ] **Speed ranking**: SPI > USB (HS) > USB (FS) > UART.
- [ ] **NRZ (UART) vs NRZI (USB)** — UART has no DC balance guarantee; USB does via bit stuffing.

### 🎯 High-Yield Formulas to Memorize

$$
T_b\ (UART) = \frac{1}{Baud\ Rate}
$$

$$
T_f\ (UART) = (1 + D + P + S) \times T_b
$$

$$
f_{SCLK} = \frac{f_{APB}}{BR\ (prescaler)}
$$

$$
T_{SPI,byte} = 8 \times \frac{1}{f_{SCLK}}
$$

$$
USB\ Max\ Devices = 2^7 - 1 = 127
$$
